name: ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS

topics.title: Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing: Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing, Descriptive Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing: Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing, Descriptive Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing** are formal mathematical and statistical frameworks used to represent, analyze, and extract clinically meaningful information from physiological signals such as ECG (Electrocardiogram), EEG (Electroencephalogram), EMG (Electromyogram), and PPG (Photoplethysmogram). These models convert raw biological waveforms into quantifiable descriptors and transform-domain representations that can be used for diagnosis, monitoring, and pattern recognition.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PEBMT635, the syllabus decomposes this into two principal pillars:

1. **Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing** — formal tools (Linear Time-Invariant systems, Convolution, Fourier/Laplace/Z-Transforms, Correlation, Spectral Density) used to characterize how biological signals are generated, propagated, and observed.
2. **Descriptive Methods** — statistical and morphological tools (mean, variance, higher-order moments, histograms, probability distributions, envelope extraction) that summarize signal behavior in compact numerical or graphical form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The fundamental objective is to build a **mathematical scaffold** so that noisy, non-stationary biological signals can be reduced to **interpretable parameters** (e.g., heart rate variability indices, dominant EEG rhythms, EMG median frequency) suitable for engineering analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope vs. The Engineer's Equation":**
> A clinician listens to a heartbeat through a stethoscope and qualitatively says "the rhythm is irregular." An engineer, however, needs a *number*, *graph*, or *equation* that captures the same intuition. Interpretative models are precisely the **"mathematical stethoscope"** — they translate a squiggly waveform like an ECG into:
> - A **model equation** (the mathematical instrument), e.g., a Fourier series showing the dominant frequencies.
> - A **summary statistic** (the descriptive method), e.g., the mean RR interval = **0.85 s**, equivalent to a heart rate of approximately **70.6 bpm**.
> 
> Just as a stethoscope amplifies and filters sound, these models **amplify the diagnostic content** hidden inside a noisy biological signal and **filter out** irrelevant variability.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

The following constants and standard metrics recur throughout this topic:

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** typically **250 Hz – 1000 Hz** for ECG, **128 Hz – 512 Hz** for EEG, **1000 Hz – 2000 Hz** for EMG.
- **Nyquist Frequency ($f_N$):** equal to **$f_s / 2$** — the maximum unambiguously representable frequency.
- **Heart Rate Range:** **40 bpm – 200 bpm** (normal adult at rest ≈ **60–100 bpm**).
- **EEG Band Limits:** Delta (**0.5–4 Hz**), Theta (**4–8 Hz**), Alpha (**8–13 Hz**), Beta (**13–30 Hz**), Gamma (**>30 Hz**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars — Logic Steps

### Pillar A: Mathematical Instruments (Transform & System Tools)

These tools answer the question: *"How does the biological system transform an internal physiological process into an observable electrical signal?"*

1. **Model the source as a deterministic or stochastic process.** A nerve firing train can be modeled as a point process; the heart's pacemaker as a periodic impulse train.
2. **Characterize the propagation medium (the body tissue) as a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system.** Tissues attenuate and distort signals; this distortion is captured by an impulse response $h(t)$.
3. **Apply transforms** to convert convolution in time into multiplication in frequency (Fourier), or differential equations into algebraic equations (Laplace), or discrete sequences into rational functions (Z).
4. **Compute correlation and spectral density** to quantify similarity between two signals (e.g., ECG and respiration) or the power distribution across frequencies.

### Pillar B: Descriptive Methods (Statistical & Morphological Tools)

These tools answer the question: *"What is a single representative number that summarizes this complex biological signal?"*

1. **Compute central tendency (mean, median, mode).**
2. **Compute dispersion (variance, standard deviation, IQR).**
3. **Compute higher-order moments (skewness, kurtosis)** to detect non-Gaussianity — common in pathology.
4. **Construct histograms and estimate probability density functions (PDFs)** to visualize the amplitude distribution.
5. **Compute envelope, RMS value, peak-to-peak amplitude** for time-domain morphology.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory:** In the tables below, absolute value and set-membership symbols are written using `\vert` (NOT `|`) to preserve Markdown table integrity.

### Table 2.1 — Mathematical Instruments (Transforms & Systems)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Linear Convolution | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$ | Continuous-time LTI | Models how tissue filtering distorts an ECG waveform |
| 2 | Discrete Convolution | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,h[n-k]$ | Discrete-time LTI | Digital filtering of sampled biosignals |
| 3 | Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt$ | Absolutely integrable $x(t)$ | Identifies dominant frequency content of EEG |
| 4 | Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,e^{-j\omega n}$ | $-\pi \le \omega \le \pi$ | Spectral analysis of sampled biosignals |
| 5 | Laplace Transform | $X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-s t}\,dt$, $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | $\text{Re}\{s\} > \sigma_0$ | Solves ODE models of biological systems |
| 6 | Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$ | ROC: annulus $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ | Analyzes discrete biological filters & AR models |
| 7 | Autocorrelation | $R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\,dt$ | Energy / power signals | Detects periodicity in ECG (RR regularity) |
| 8 | Cross-Correlation | $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,y(t+\tau)\,dt$ | Jointly stationary processes | Coupling between respiration & heart rate |
| 9 | Power Spectral Density (Wiener–Khinchin) | $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$ | Wide-sense stationary | PSD of HRV in autonomic assessment |

### Table 2.2 — Descriptive Methods (Statistical Descriptors)

| # | Descriptor | Formula | What it Reveals |
|---|------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]$ | DC offset / baseline |
| 2 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x[n]-\mu)^2$ | Signal power / variability |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Same as variance, in original units |
| 4 | Skewness (3rd moment) | $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{E[(x-\mu)^3]}{\sigma^3}$ | Asymmetry of amplitude distribution |
| 5 | Kurtosis (4th moment) | $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{E[(x-\mu)^4]}{\sigma^4}$ | "Tailedness"; spike detection (e.g., QRS) |
| 6 | RMS Value | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}x^2[n]}$ | Equivalent power, used in EMG amplitude |
| 7 | Peak-to-Peak | $x_{pp} = \max(x) - \min(x)$ | Signal dynamic range |
| 8 | Histogram | $H(b) = \#\{x[n] : b \le x[n] < b+1\}$ | Empirical PDF of amplitudes |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology:** Mean and SDNN (standard deviation of NN intervals) quantify heart rate variability (HRV), a marker of autonomic nervous system health.
- **Neurology:** PSD separates EEG into clinically meaningful bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma).
- **Rehabilitation:** RMS of EMG estimates muscle activation level for prosthetic control.
- **Sleep Medicine:** Skewness & kurtosis of beat-to-beat intervals detect obstructive sleep apnea events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Time-Domain Convolution to Frequency-Domain Multiplication

**Goal:** Prove that convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency — the foundation of why the Fourier transform is so useful for biological signal interpretation.

**Step 1 — Start with the convolution integral.**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of the CTFT to both sides.**

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y(t)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt
$$

Substitute the convolution expression for $y(t)$:

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau\right] e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Swap the order of integration.** This is allowed if $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ are absolutely integrable (true for finite-energy biosignals).

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t-\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt\right] d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Perform a change of variable in the inner integral.** Let $u = t - \tau$, so $t = u + \tau$ and $dt = du$.

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t-\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(u)\,e^{-j2\pi f (u+\tau)}\,du
$$

**Step 5 — Separate the exponential.**

$$
= e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(u)\,e^{-j2\pi f u}\,du = e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,H(f)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $Y(f)$.**

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,H(f)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the remaining integral as $X(f)$.**

$$
Y(f) = H(f)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,d\tau = H(f)\,X(f)
$$

**Final Result (Convolution Theorem):**

$$
\boxed{\,\mathcal{F}\{x(t) * h(t)\} = X(f)\,H(f)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A biological tissue that filters an ECG is described by $h(t)$. Rather than performing a difficult convolution in time, we **multiply two spectra** in the frequency domain. This is the central reason Fourier analysis is the workhorse of biomedical signal processing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean, Variance & Higher-Order Moments of a Discrete Biosignal

**Step 1 — Define the sample mean** as the first raw moment $m_1$:

$$
m_1 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]
$$

This represents the **DC component** or baseline wander of the biosignal.

**Step 2 — Define the $k$-th central moment** about the mean:

$$
\mu_k = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x[n] - m_1)^k
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the variance** as the second central moment ($k=2$):

$$
\sigma^2 = \mu_2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x[n] - m_1)^2
$$

For an unbiased estimator, divide by $(N-1)$ instead of $N$.

**Step 4 — Skewness** is the third standardized moment:

$$
\gamma_1 = \frac{\mu_3}{\sigma^3} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x[n]-m_1)^3}{\left(\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x[n]-m_1)^2\right)^{3/2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Kurtosis** is the fourth standardized moment:

$$
\gamma_2 = \frac{\mu_4}{\sigma^4} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x[n]-m_1)^4}{\left(\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x[n]-m_1)^2\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Excess kurtosis** (subtract 3 to make a Gaussian signal have $\gamma_2^{excess} = 0$):

$$
\gamma_2^{excess} = \gamma_2 - 3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why higher moments matter clinically:** A clean sinus rhythm has a roughly **symmetric, low-kurtosis** RR-interval distribution. Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) introduce **heavy tails** (high kurtosis) and **asymmetry** (non-zero skewness), making these descriptors powerful screening tools.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Spectral Density via Wiener–Khinchin Theorem

**Step 1 — Autocorrelation of a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process $x(t)$:**

$$
R_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)] = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — Take the CTFT of $R_{xx}(\tau)$** with respect to $\tau$:

$$
S_{xx}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{xx}(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — The result is the Power Spectral Density.** This is the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**:

$$
\boxed{\,S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning:** $S_{xx}(f)\,df$ gives the **average signal power** contained in the frequency band between $f$ and $f+df$. For EEG analysis, integrating $S_{xx}(f)$ over the alpha band (8–13 Hz) yields the **absolute alpha power**, a clinical biomarker of relaxed wakefulness.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Tools to Biosignal Applications

| Biological Signal | Mathematical Instrument Used | Descriptive Method Used | Diagnostic Insight |
|-------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| ECG | Fourier Transform, Autocorrelation | Mean RR interval, SDNN | Heart rate variability |
| EEG | PSD (Wiener–Khinchin) | Band power (delta/theta/alpha/beta) | Sleep stage, seizure detection |
| EMG | RMS, Fourier Transform | Median frequency, RMS amplitude | Muscle fatigue, prosthetic control |
| PPG | Cross-correlation with ECG | Pulse transit time statistics | Blood pressure estimation |
| Heart Sound (PCG) | Envelope detection, Spectrogram | Peak intervals, spectral centroid | Murmur classification |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Interpretative Models

The following Mermaid diagram maps how a raw biological signal flows through mathematical instruments and descriptive methods to yield a clinical interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Physiological Source] --> srcB[Signal Propagation Tissue]
    srcB --> srcC[Sensor and Electrode]
    srcC --> srcD[Acquired Biosignal x of t]
    srcD --> miA[Mathematical Instruments Branch]
    srcD --> dmA[Descriptive Methods Branch]

    miA --> mi1[LTI System Model h of t]
    miA --> mi2[Fourier Transform X of f]
    miA --> mi3[Laplace and Z Transforms]
    miA --> mi4[Correlation Rxx and Rxy]
    miA --> mi5[Power Spectral Density Sxx]

    dmA --> dm1[Central Tendency Mean Median]
    dmA --> dm2[Dispersion Variance SD IQR]
    dmA --> dm3[Higher Moments Skewness Kurtosis]
    dmA --> dm4[Histogram and PDF Estimate]
    dmA --> dm5[Envelope RMS Peak to Peak]

    mi2 --> intp[Clinical Interpretation]
    mi5 --> intp
    dm3 --> intp
    dm5 --> intp
    intp --> diag[Diagnosis Monitoring Prognosis]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biomedical Signal Interpretation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Raw Biosignal] --> blk1[Pre Processing Block]
    blk1 --> blk2[Feature Extraction Block]
    blk2 --> blk3[Mathematical Instrument Block]
    blk2 --> blk4[Descriptive Method Block]
    blk3 --> blk5[Decision and Classification Block]
    blk4 --> blk5
    blk5 --> out1[Clinical Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functionality:**
> - **Pre-Processing Block:** Analog front-end, anti-alias filter, baseline removal, notch filter for 50/60 Hz powerline interference.
> - **Feature Extraction Block:** Segments the signal into epochs (e.g., 1-s EEG windows, beat-to-beat ECG intervals).
> - **Mathematical Instrument Block:** Computes Fourier/Laplace/Z transforms, correlations, PSDs.
> - **Descriptive Method Block:** Computes moments, histograms, envelopes, RMS.
> - **Decision Block:** Thresholding, rule-based logic, or ML classifier producing a clinical label.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
Define the **autocorrelation function** of a wide-sense stationary random process. Why is it useful in analyzing ECG signals?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** For a wide-sense stationary process $x(t)$, the autocorrelation is defined as $R_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]$, i.e., the expected value of the product of the signal with a time-shifted version of itself.
- **(1 mark) Property:** $R_{xx}(0) = E[x^2(t)]$ equals the average power; $R_{xx}(\tau)$ is maximum at $\tau=0$ and is an even function.
- **(1 mark) Application:** In ECG analysis, peaks in $R_{xx}(\tau)$ occur at integer multiples of the dominant RR interval, enabling automatic detection of periodicity and measurement of heart rate variability (HRV).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
What is **kurtosis**? Mention its significance in biosignal analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Kurtosis is the fourth standardized moment, $\gamma_2 = E[(x-\mu)^4] / \sigma^4$. Excess kurtosis subtracts 3 so that a Gaussian signal has value 0.
- **(1 mark) Interpretation:** High kurtosis indicates a **heavy-tailed** distribution, often caused by sharp transient spikes such as QRS complexes in ECG or K-complexes in EEG.
- **(1 mark) Application:** Used to detect epochs containing epileptiform spikes, premature beats, or motion artifacts in biosignal recordings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units mistake:** When computing frequency from Fourier variables, students often forget to include the $2\pi$ factor. Always state whether you are using $f$ (Hz) or $\omega$ (rad/s).
> 2. **DC offset omission:** The mean of a real biosignal is rarely zero. Forgetting to subtract the mean before computing variance or PSD inflates low-frequency components — a guaranteed loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Convolution vs. correlation mix-up:** Convolution flips the kernel; correlation does not. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the Wiener–Khinchin hypothesis:** PSD via Fourier is valid only for **wide-sense stationary** processes. State this assumption explicitly.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **convolution theorem** stating that convolution in the time domain corresponds to multiplication in the frequency domain. Discuss its significance in interpreting filtered biosignals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete ECG segment sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz contains $N = 1000$ samples with sample mean $m_1 = 0.02$ mV. The sum of squared deviations $\sum(x[n]-m_1)^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$ mV², the sum of cubed deviations $\sum(x[n]-m_1)^3 = -1.1 \times 10^{-4}$ mV³, and the sum of fourth-power deviations $\sum(x[n]-m_1)^4 = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}$ mV⁴. Compute the **variance, standard deviation, skewness, and kurtosis**. Comment on the shape of the amplitude distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Convolution Theorem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the convolution integral (1 mark):**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Take the CTFT of $y(t)$ (1 mark):**

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau\right]e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Interchange integration order (1 mark):**

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t-\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt\right]d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u = t-\tau$ in the inner integral (1 mark):**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(u)\,e^{-j2\pi f (u+\tau)}\,du = e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,H(f)
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the remaining integral as $X(f)$ (1 mark):**

$$
Y(f) = H(f)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau = H(f)\,X(f)
$$

**Step 6 — State significance (2 marks):**
- Filtering of biosignals (e.g., low-pass smoothing of noisy EMG) becomes simple **multiplication** of spectra rather than complex time-domain integration.
- Allows frequency-selective design: a **notch filter at 50 Hz** is specified by its $H(f)$, and the filtered output's spectrum is simply $Y(f) = X(f)\,H(f)$.

**(b) Computation of Statistical Descriptors (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Variance (2 marks):** Using unbiased estimator,

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum(x[n]-m_1)^2}{N-1} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{999} \approx 4.805 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mV}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{4.805 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.192 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Skewness (2 marks):** First compute third central moment,

$$
\mu_3 = \frac{-1.1 \times 10^{-4}}{1000} = -1.1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mV}^3
$$

Then,

$$
\gamma_1 = \frac{\mu_3}{\sigma^3} = \frac{-1.1 \times 10^{-7}}{(2.192 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{-1.1 \times 10^{-7}}{1.053 \times 10^{-8}} \approx -10.45
$$

**Step 4 — Kurtosis (2 marks):** Compute fourth central moment,

$$
\mu_4 = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1000} = 7.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mV}^4
$$

$$
\gamma_2 = \frac{\mu_4}{\sigma^4} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-8}}{(4.805 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-8}}{2.309 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 3118
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (built into the marks above):**
- Large **negative skewness** ($\gamma_1 \approx -10.45$) indicates a **left-tailed (negatively skewed)** distribution, consistent with the deep S-wave and dominant negative deflection in the QRS complex of a typical ECG lead.
- Very **high kurtosis** ($\gamma_2 \approx 3118 \gg 3$) confirms a **heavy-tailed, sharply peaked** distribution, characteristic of the impulsive QRS spike superimposed on a low-amplitude P-T baseline.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, ESE Module 1 Alternate]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**. Show that the **autocorrelation** and **power spectral density (PSD)** form a Fourier transform pair. Mention two clinical applications of PSD in EEG analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EMG signal of length $N = 2000$ samples is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. Compute the **mean**, **variance**, **RMS value**, and **peak-to-peak amplitude** given: $\sum x[n] = 10.4$ mV, $\sum x^2[n] = 0.452$ mV², $\max(x) = 0.18$ mV, $\min(x) = -0.21$ mV. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement (2 marks):** For a wide-sense stationary random process $x(t)$ with autocorrelation $R_{xx}(\tau)$, the power spectral density $S_{xx}(f)$ is the Fourier transform of $R_{xx}(\tau)$:

$$
S_{xx}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}R_{xx}(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of autocorrelation (1 mark):**

$$
R_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]
$$

**Step 3 — Demonstration that they form a Fourier pair (2 marks):** Starting from the CTFT definition and substituting $R_{xx}(\tau)$:

$$
S_{xx}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]\,e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau
$$

Exchanging expectation and integration (justified by linearity of expectation):

$$
= E\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\,d\tau\right]
$$

Letting $u = t + \tau$:

$$
= E\left[x(t)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(u)\,e^{-j2\pi f(u-t)}\,du\right] = E\left[x(t)\,e^{j2\pi ft}\right]\,X(f) = X(f)\,X^*(f) = \vert X(f)\vert^2
$$

Hence $S_{xx}(f) = \vert X(f)\vert^2 \ge 0$ — a real, non-negative function.

**Step 4 — Two clinical applications in EEG (2 marks):**
- **(i) Band-power quantification:** Integrating $S_{xx}(f)$ over the alpha band (8–13 Hz) yields absolute alpha power, used to assess relaxed wakefulness vs. drowsiness.
- **(ii) Seizure detection:** During an epileptic seizure, the PSD shows a sharp peak in the high-frequency beta/gamma range, contrasting with the normal 1/f-like spectrum.

**(b) Descriptive Statistics of EMG (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean (1 mark):**

$$
\mu = \frac{\sum x[n]}{N} = \frac{10.4}{2000} = 5.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mV} = 0.0052 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Variance (2 marks):** Using $\sum(x-\mu)^2 = \sum x^2 - N\mu^2$:

$$
\sum x^2 - N\mu^2 = 0.452 - 2000 \times (5.2\times 10^{-3})^2
$$

$$
= 0.452 - 2000 \times 2.704 \times 10^{-5} = 0.452 - 0.05408 = 0.39792 \text{ mV}^2
$$

Sample variance:

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{0.39792}{1999} \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mV}^2
$$

**Step 3 — RMS value (2 marks):**

$$
x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum x^2[n]}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.452}{2000}} = \sqrt{2.26 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.01503 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak-to-peak amplitude (2 marks):**

$$
x_{pp} = \max(x) - \min(x) = 0.18 - (-0.21) = 0.39 \text{ mV}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The crest factor $x_{pp} / x_{rms} = 0.39 / 0.015 \approx 26$ is very high, which is typical of surface EMG signals due to the sparse, impulsive nature of motor unit action potentials. The small mean (0.0052 mV) confirms a near-zero DC offset, indicating proper baseline correction in the pre-processing stage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Mathematical Instruments** transform biosignals into alternate domains where their information content becomes interpretable (Fourier → frequency, Laplace → s-plane, Z → z-plane, Correlation → similarity).
- **The Convolution Theorem** $\mathcal{F}\{x * h\} = X(f)\,H(f)$ is the cornerstone — biological tissue filtering is modeled as LTI convolution.
- **Descriptive methods** (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, RMS, peak-to-peak, histogram) compress a complex waveform into a few diagnostic numbers.
- **Skewness** captures asymmetry; **kurtosis** captures tail-heaviness — both critical for detecting abnormal beats or spikes.
- **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** links autocorrelation to PSD: $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$. PSD is valid only for **wide-sense stationary** signals.
- **Always subtract the mean** before computing variance or PSD to avoid low-frequency bias.
- **Sampling theorem constraint:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing. ECG: $f_s \ge 250$ Hz; EEG: $f_s \ge 250$ Hz; EMG: $f_s \ge 1000$ Hz.
- **Clinical biomarkers by signal:** ECG → SDNN (HRV); EEG → Band power; EMG → RMS & median frequency; PPG → Pulse transit time.
- **Excess kurtosis** = (kurtosis) − 3; a Gaussian signal has excess kurtosis = 0.
- **Crest factor** = peak-to-peak / RMS, a useful EMG descriptor.
- **Fourier variables:** always specify $f$ (Hz) vs. $\omega$ (rad/s); the $2\pi$ factor differs by definition.
- **Riemann–Lebesgue lemma:** for absolutely integrable signals, $X(f) \to 0$ as $f \to \infty$ — useful for justifying finite-bandwidth biosignals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Black-Box Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Black-Box Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Black-Box Model** is a mathematical representation of a system in which the internal physiological, physical, or mechanistic structure is completely unknown, ignored, or deliberately abstracted away. The model is constructed purely from measurable **input–output (I/O) data pairs** by identifying a suitable parametric or non-parametric functional relationship that maps inputs to outputs.

In the context of biomedical signal processing, a black-box model treats the human body, an organ, or a signal-generating physiological process as an *opaque operator* $\mathcal{H}$ such that:

$$y(t) = \mathcal{H}\{u(t)\} + v(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the input (stimulus/sensor excitation), $y(t)$ is the observed output (biomedical signal), and $v(t)$ represents additive measurement noise and unmodelled dynamics. The operator $\mathcal{H}$ is identified *solely* by fitting parameters to recorded data — no a priori physiological equations are enforced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Black-box models are *data-driven* representations belonging to the class of **System Identification** models. They include parametric linear structures (AR, MA, ARMA, ARX, ARMAX, OE, BJ) and non-parametric/nonlinear structures (Neural Networks, Fuzzy Systems, Volterra Series, Wiener/Hammerstein models). They form the foundation of Narmax and subspace identification used clinically in cardiology, neurology, and rehabilitation engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are handed a sealed cardboard box with an input knob and an output meter. You have absolutely no idea what is inside — it could be a circuit, a fluid system, or a tiny dancing gnome. Your only strategy is to **twist the knob to many positions and record the meter's reading**. By plotting these readings, you mathematically *fit* a function that best describes the relationship between knob angle and meter deflection.

That fitted function is your **black-box model**.

In biomedicine:
- The **input** $u(t)$ might be an electrical stimulus applied to muscle tissue.
- The **output** $y(t)$ might be the resulting EMG signal.
- The **black box** is the neuromuscular junction, which is far too complex (involving ion channels, calcium dynamics, etc.) to model mechanistically for every patient.
- A **black-box model** (e.g., an ARX model) captures the *behaviour* between stimulus and response using only recorded data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Model Paradigms in KTU Context:**
> 1. **White-Box Model** — fully derived from first principles (e.g., Hodgkin–Huxley neuron model).
> 2. **Grey-Box Model** — partial physics + partial data fitting (e.g., compartmental PK models with one fitted clearance rate).
> 3. **Black-Box Model** — purely data-driven; no internal physiological knowledge is required (e.g., NARX neural network modelling of ECG).

### 1.3 Core Physical & Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Biomedical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency | **250 Hz – 1000 Hz** (ECG), **100–500 Hz** (EEG), **1 kHz–10 kHz** (EMG) |
| $N$ | Number of recorded I/O samples | **$10^3$ – $10^6$** samples per record |
| $T$ | Record duration | **30 s – 24 h** (Holter ECG); **1–10 min** (clinical EEG) |
| $n_a, n_b$ | ARX polynomial orders | Typically **2 – 10** for biomedical signals |
| SNR | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **10 dB – 40 dB** for clean clinical recordings |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Black-Box Modelling Pipeline

The black-box modelling philosophy in KTU's framework rests on the following operational steps:

1. **Experiment Design** — Choose informative input excitation $u(t)$. For biomedical systems, pseudo-random binary sequences (PRBS) or band-limited Gaussian noise are commonly used to excite the relevant frequency band.
2. **Data Acquisition** — Synchronously sample input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$ at rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion).
3. **Pre-processing** — Remove baseline wander, 50/60 Hz powerline interference, and high-frequency noise. Apply segmentation (epoching) for non-stationary signals.
4. **Model Structure Selection** — Choose from AR, ARX, ARMAX, OE, BJ, or nonlinear variants based on prior knowledge of noise characteristics and system delay.
5. **Parameter Estimation** — Use least-squares, recursive least-squares (RLS), instrumental variables, or maximum-likelihood methods to fit model coefficients.
6. **Model Validation** — Cross-validate using *one-step-ahead prediction*, *simulation prediction*, and residual whiteness tests.
7. **Deployment** — Use the validated model for prediction, diagnosis, anomaly detection, or control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this "Why & How" so important for KTU?**
> Board questions often ask: *"Justify why a black-box approach is preferred over a physiological model for this application."* The answer must invoke:
> - **Practicality**: Physiological models require 10–100 parameters, many of which are unmeasurable in vivo.
> - **Patient-specific tuning**: Black-box models can be re-identified per patient using routine clinical data.
> - **Real-time feasibility**: Linear black-box models are computationally inexpensive (matrix inversion in $O(n^3)$).

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Black-Box Models

Black-box models are classified along two orthogonal axes:

- **Linear vs. Nonlinear**
- **Parametric vs. Non-parametric**

| Family | Mathematical Form | Parametric? | Linear? | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AR** (Auto-Regressive) | $A(q)\,y(t) = e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | ECG beat-to-beat, EEG spectral |
| **MA** (Moving Average) | $y(t) = C(q)\,e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Noise modelling |
| **ARMA** | $A(q)\,y(t) = C(q)\,e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Stationary processes |
| **ARX** | $A(q)\,y(t) = B(q)\,u(t) + e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Drug response, evoked potentials |
| **ARMAX** | $A(q)\,y(t) = B(q)\,u(t) + C(q)\,e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Systems with coloured noise |
| **OE** (Output Error) | $y(t) = \dfrac{B(q)}{F(q)}u(t) + e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Open-loop identification |
| **BJ** (Box–Jenkins) | $y(t) = \dfrac{B(q)}{F(q)}u(t) + \dfrac{C(q)}{D(q)}e(t)$ | Yes | Yes | Most general linear parametric |
| **NARX** (Nonlinear ARX) | $y(t) = f\!\left(y(t-1),\ldots,u(t-d)\right)$ | Yes | No | Heart-rate variability, EMG |
| **Neural Network** | $y(t) = \mathrm{NN}\{u(t), \theta\}$ | Yes | No | EEG classification, seizure detection |
| **Volterra / Wiener** | Quadratic/cubic kernel expansions | Yes | No | Nonlinear system dynamics |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials** for the topic "The Black-Box Models".

| # | Formula (using shift operator $q^{-1}$) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q^{-1} y(t) = y(t-1)$ | Unit-delay operator definition |
| 2 | $A(q) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + a_2 q^{-2} + \cdots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}$ | Autoregressive polynomial of order $n_a$ |
| 3 | $B(q) = b_1 q^{-1} + b_2 q^{-2} + \cdots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$ | Input polynomial of order $n_b$ |
| 4 | $C(q) = 1 + c_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + c_{n_c} q^{-n_c}$ | Noise polynomial |
| 5 | $F(q), D(q)$ | Denominator polynomials for OE and BJ structures |
| 6 | **AR model:** $\; y(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n_a} a_i\, y(t-i) + e(t)$ | Pure output regression |
| 7 | **MA model:** $\; y(t) = e(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{n_c} c_i\, e(t-i)$ | Pure noise regression |
| 8 | **ARMA model:** $\; y(t) = -\sum a_i y(t-i) + \sum c_i e(t-i) + e(t)$ | Combined AR + MA |
| 9 | **ARX model:** $\; y(t) = -\sum a_i y(t-i) + \sum b_i u(t-i) + e(t)$ | Linear-in-parameters |
| 10 | **ARMAX model:** $\; y(t) = -\sum a_i y(t-i) + \sum b_i u(t-i) + \sum c_i e(t-i) + e(t)$ | Coloured noise |
| 11 | **OE model:** $\; y(t) = \dfrac{B(q)}{F(q)}u(t-n_k) + e(t)$ | Output-error form |
| 12 | **BJ model:** $\; y(t) = \dfrac{B(q)}{F(q)}u(t-n_k) + \dfrac{C(q)}{D(q)}e(t)$ | Most general linear form |
| 13 | **Least-Squares Estimator:** $\; \hat{\theta} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y$ | ARX parameter solution |
| 14 | **Prediction Error:** $\; \varepsilon(t,\theta) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid t-1;\theta)$ | One-step-ahead PE |
| 15 | **FIT (%):** $\; \mathrm{FIT} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert y - \hat{y} \vert}{\vert y - \bar{y} \vert}\right) \times 100\%$ | Model-accuracy metric |
| 16 | **AIC:** $\; \mathrm{AIC} = 2k + N \ln(\mathrm{RSS}/N)$ | Model order selection |
| 17 | **MDL:** $\; \mathrm{MDL} = k \ln N + N \ln(\mathrm{RSS}/N)$ | Minimum Description Length |
| 18 | **FPE:** $\; \mathrm{FPE} = \dfrac{N+k}{N-k}\,\sigma_e^2$ | Final Prediction Error |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology:** Black-box ARX models of cardiovascular dynamics predict blood pressure from ECG-derived features in ICU.
- **Neurology:** EEG-based NARMAX models identify seizure precursors non-invasively.
- **Rehabilitation:** EMG-driven black-box models power intent-detection controllers in prosthetic limbs.
- **Pharmacology:** PK/PD models with black-box error correction enable personalised drug dosing.
- **Sleep Medicine:** Nonlinear black-box models classify sleep stages from polysomnographic signals with > 90 % accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the ARX Least-Squares Estimator

The ARX model is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU papers on black-box models. We derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Write the ARX model in scalar form.**

For a single-input single-output (SISO) system with orders $n_a$ and $n_b$ and an input–output delay $n_k$ (in KTU notation often $d$), the ARX structure is:

$$y(t) = -a_1 y(t-1) - a_2 y(t-2) - \cdots - a_{n_a} y(t-n_a) + b_1 u(t-n_k) + b_2 u(t-n_k-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(t-n_k - n_b + 1) + e(t)$$

This equation says: the current output sample $y(t)$ depends linearly on the past $n_a$ output samples, the past $n_b$ input samples (lagged by $n_k$), and a white-noise disturbance $e(t)$.

**Step 2 — Group the unknown coefficients into a parameter vector.**

Define the parameter vector $\theta$ of dimension $n = n_a + n_b$:

$$\theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 & a_2 & \cdots & a_{n_a} & b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_{n_b} \end{bmatrix}^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Define the regression (data) vector.**

Define the corresponding regressor (row) vector $\varphi^T(t)$:

$$\varphi^T(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(t-1) & -y(t-2) & \cdots & -y(t-n_a) & u(t-n_k) & u(t-n_k-1) & \cdots & u(t-n_k-n_b+1) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite the model in compact linear-in-parameters form.**

Using $\theta$ and $\varphi(t)$:

$$y(t) = \varphi^T(t)\,\theta + e(t)$$

This is the **fundamental linear regression form** that makes ARX estimation a least-squares problem.

**Step 5 — Stack the equations for $N$ data samples.**

For $N$ observations $t = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, stack vertically:

$$\begin{aligned}
y(1) &= \varphi^T(1)\,\theta + e(1) \\
y(2) &= \varphi^T(2)\,\theta + e(2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
y(N) &= \varphi^T(N)\,\theta + e(N)
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$Y = \Phi\,\theta + E$$

with

$$Y = \begin{bmatrix} y(1) \\ y(2) \\ \vdots \\ y(N) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} \varphi^T(1) \\ \varphi^T(2) \\ \vdots \\ \varphi^T(N) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times n}, \quad E = \begin{bmatrix} e(1) \\ e(2) \\ \vdots \\ e(N) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Formulate the quadratic cost function.**

The least-squares criterion minimises the sum of squared residuals:

$$J(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \left( y(t) - \varphi^T(t)\theta \right)^2 = (Y - \Phi\theta)^T(Y - \Phi\theta)$$

**Step 7 — Set the gradient to zero (Normal Equation derivation).**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -2 \Phi^T (Y - \Phi\theta) = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve the Normal Equation.**

Assuming $\Phi^T \Phi$ is non-singular (full column rank, i.e., $N \geq n$ and the data is persistently exciting):

$$\Phi^T \Phi \,\theta = \Phi^T Y$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y}$$

This is the **blueprint formula** of linear black-box identification.

**Step 9 — Verify the result.**

The covariance of the estimate (under $E\{E E^T\} = \sigma_e^2 I$):

$$\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\theta}_{LS}) = \sigma_e^2 (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1}$$

The smaller the eigenvalues of $\Phi^T \Phi$, the larger the parameter uncertainty — this is why **persistently exciting** inputs are mandatory for biomedical experiments.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Black-Box Model

This matrix is what a KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce in a 14-mark question on "Justify the choice of black-box structure".

| Criterion | AR | ARX | ARMAX | OE | BJ | NARX |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inputs used | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Noise model | White only | White | Coloured | None | Coloured | Flexible |
| Closed-loop safe? | Yes | Limited | Limited | No | No | No |
| Computational cost | Very low | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| Bias with coloured noise | High | High | Low | High | Very low | Low |
| Robust to outliers? | Poor | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Good |
| Biomedical suitability | EEG/HRV | Drug/EP | Cardiovascular | Respiratory | Sleep EEG | Nonlinear EMG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When the noise enters at the system output (e.g., sensor noise in EEG), the **OE or BJ model is preferred** over ARX. When the noise is part of the dynamics (e.g., muscle tremor with neural drive), **ARMAX is appropriate**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Black-Box Identification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Design] --> B[Acquire I/O Data]
    B --> C[Pre-processing and Segmentation]
    C --> D[Select Model Structure]
    D --> E[Parameter Estimation]
    E --> F{Validation Pass?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Deploy Model]
    G --> H[Monitor and Re-identify]
    H --> D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Black-Box Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Black Box Models]
    Root --> Linear[Linear]
    Root --> NonLinear[Nonlinear]

    Linear --> Param[Parametric]
    Linear --> NonParam[Non Parametric]

    Param --> AR[AR]
    Param --> ARX[ARX]
    Param --> ARMAX[ARMAX]
    Param --> OE[OE]
    Param --> BJ[BJ]

    NonParam --> Correl[Correlation Analysis]
    NonParam --> Spec[Spectral Estimation]

    NonLinear --> NARX[NARX]
    NonLinear --> NN[Neural Network]
    NonLinear --> Volterra[Volterra Wiener]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARX Black-Box Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UT[Input u of t] --> DEL[Delay nk]
    DEL --> BFIL[B q Filter]
    BFIL --> SUM1[Summer]

    YT[Output y of t] --> AFIL[A q Filter]
    AFIL --> SUM2[Inverter Summer]
    SUM2 --> SUM1

    NOISE[e of t] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> YT
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Biomedical Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App[Biomedical Application]
    App --> Card[Cardiology: BP from ECG]
    App --> Neuro[Neurology: EEG Seizure]
    App --> Musc[Muscle: EMG Prosthetic]
    App --> Pulm[Pulmonary: Respiratory Drive]

    Card --> ARX1[ARX Model]
    Neuro --> NARX1[NARX Neural Net]
    Musc --> NN1[NN Hammerstein]
    Pulm --> OE1[OE Model]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Define a black-box model. How does it differ from a white-box model in biomedical system identification?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A black-box model is a purely data-driven mathematical representation of a system where the internal physiological structure is unknown or ignored. It maps inputs to outputs using parameters fitted to observed data, e.g., AR, ARX, ARMAX, NARX models. A white-box model, in contrast, is derived from first principles of physics, chemistry, or physiology — e.g., the Hodgkin–Huxley equations for neuronal membrane dynamics. Black-box models require only measurable I/O records, are patient-specific, and are computationally efficient, making them practical in real-time clinical applications where the underlying physiology is too complex to model mechanistically. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*List any four parametric black-box model structures used in biomedical signal processing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four parametric black-box model structures are:
1. **AR** (AutoRegressive) — used for EEG and HRV spectral analysis.
2. **ARX** (AutoRegressive with eXogenous input) — used for drug-response and evoked-potential modelling.
3. **ARMAX** — used for systems with coloured noise such as cardiovascular dynamics.
4. **OE** (Output Error) — used for open-loop respiratory-system identification.
**(3 marks — 0.75 marks each)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the least-squares estimator for an ARX model. State the normal equation and discuss the role of the persistency-of-excitation condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an EEG signal, an AR model of order $p=4$ yields the parameters $a_1 = -1.35$, $a_2 = 0.75$, $a_3 = -0.20$, $a_4 = 0.05$. Compute the residual variance and the AIC for $N = 1000$ samples, given the sum of squared residuals $\mathrm{RSS} = 4.8$. Comment on model adequacy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
Refer to the step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y$$

**Explanation steps a student must write in the exam:**
1. Write ARX model in scalar form (1 mark)
2. Define parameter vector $\theta$ and regressor $\varphi(t)$ (2 marks)
3. Stack into matrix form $Y = \Phi\theta + E$ (1 mark)
4. Formulate the quadratic cost function $J(\theta) = (Y - \Phi\theta)^T(Y - \Phi\theta)$ (1 mark)
5. Differentiate and equate to zero — obtain the normal equation (1 mark)
6. Solve and state the estimator; discuss the persistency-of-excitation condition (1 mark)

**Persistency of excitation:** A persistent input ensures that $\Phi^T \Phi$ is positive definite, that the parameter estimate is unique, and that the covariance $\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\theta}) = \sigma_e^2(\Phi^T\Phi)^{-1}$ remains bounded. For biomedical signals, PRBS or band-limited Gaussian noise with sufficient bandwidth is typically used.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given $p = 4$, $N = 1000$, $\mathrm{RSS} = 4.8$, $k = 4$:

**Step 1 — Residual variance estimate:**

$$\hat{\sigma}_e^2 = \frac{\mathrm{RSS}}{N} = \frac{4.8}{1000} = 0.0048$$

**Step 2 — AIC computation:**

$$\mathrm{AIC} = 2k + N \ln(\hat{\sigma}_e^2) = 2(4) + 1000 \cdot \ln(0.0048) = 8 + 1000 \times (-5.339) = 8 - 5339 = -5331$$

**Step 3 — Model adequacy comment:**
Since $\hat{\sigma}_e^2$ is very small (0.0048) and the AR parameters yield a stable polynomial (eigenvalues within the unit circle can be checked by the Jury test), the model adequately captures the dominant EEG dynamics. AIC of $-5331$ is highly negative, indicating a well-fitting model with appropriate parsimony. **(7 marks — 2 for variance, 2 for AIC, 3 for comment)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Compare the AR, ARX, OE, and BJ black-box model structures using a comparison matrix. Justify which structure is most appropriate for **modelling the relationship between electrical muscle stimulation (EMS) input and the resulting EMG output** in a rehabilitation setup. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ARX(2,2,1) model fitted to a blood-pressure record gives the regression matrix and observation vector:

$$\Phi^T \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & -800 & 0 & 50 \\ -800 & 1000 & -50 & 0 \\ 0 & -50 & 500 & -200 \\ 50 & 0 & -200 & 500 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Phi^T Y = \begin{bmatrix} 12.5 \\ -8.0 \\ 6.0 \\ 3.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the parameter vector $\hat{\theta}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The student must reproduce a comparison matrix (refer to Section 3.2) and then justify.

**Justification for EMS-to-EMG modelling:**
- The EMS input $u(t)$ is a known electrical stimulus and the EMG output $y(t)$ is the measured muscle response.
- Measurement noise arises at the EMG electrodes (output-side noise) — hence **ARX is suboptimal** (it assumes white noise entering the system equation).
- The **OE (Output Error) model** is most appropriate because it explicitly models the noise as an additive disturbance at the system output and the noise does not corrupt the regressors.
- The plant dynamics $B(q)/F(q)$ capture the muscle's second-order behaviour (activation–contraction dynamics).
- For linearised, time-invariant muscle behaviour, OE provides lower bias than ARX and is computationally cheaper than BJ.
- Therefore, **OE is the most suitable structure for EMS-EMG modelling in rehabilitation.** **(7 marks — 3 for matrix, 4 for justification)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

We must solve:

$$\hat{\theta} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y$$

**Step 1 — Compute the determinant of $\Phi^T \Phi$.**

Using cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$\det(\Phi^T \Phi) = 1000 \cdot M_{11} - (-800)\cdot M_{12} + 0 - 50 \cdot M_{14}$$

Computing the $3 \times 3$ minors:

$$M_{11} = \det\begin{bmatrix} 1000 & -50 & 0 \\ -50 & 500 & -200 \\ 0 & -200 & 500 \end{bmatrix} = 1000(500\cdot 500 - (-200)(-200)) - (-50)((-50)(500) - (-200)(0)) + 0$$

$$= 1000(250000 - 40000) - (-50)(-25000) = 1000 \times 210000 - 1250000 = 210000000 - 1250000 = 208750000$$

$$M_{12} = \det\begin{bmatrix} -800 & -50 & 0 \\ 0 & 500 & -200 \\ 50 & -200 & 500 \end{bmatrix} = -800(500\cdot 500 - (-200)(-200)) - (-50)(0\cdot 500 - (-200)\cdot 50) + 0$$

$$= -800(210000) - (-50)(0 + 10000) = -168000000 + 500000 = -167500000$$

$$M_{14} = \det\begin{bmatrix} -800 & 1000 & -50 \\ 0 & -50 & 500 \\ 50 & 0 & -200 \end{bmatrix} = -800((-50)(-200) - 500\cdot 0) - 1000(0\cdot(-200) - 500\cdot 50) + (-50)(0\cdot 0 - (-50)\cdot 50)$$

$$= -800(10000) - 1000(-25000) + (-50)(2500) = -8000000 + 25000000 - 125000 = 16875000$$

Therefore:

$$\det = 1000(208750000) + 800(-167500000) - 50(16875000) = 208750000000 - 134000000000 - 843750000 = 73906250000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the adjugate (or solve the linear system directly using Gaussian elimination).**

Using Gaussian elimination on the augmented matrix $[\Phi^T\Phi \mid \Phi^T Y]$:

Row 1: $[1000, -800, 0, 50 \mid 12.5]$
Row 2: $[-800, 1000, -50, 0 \mid -8.0]$
Row 3: $[0, -50, 500, -200 \mid 6.0]$
Row 4: $[50, 0, -200, 500 \mid 3.0]$

Divide Row 1 by 1000: $[1, -0.8, 0, 0.05 \mid 0.0125]$
Eliminate $R_2$: $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 + 800 R_1 \Rightarrow [0, 360, -50, 40 \mid 2.0]$
Eliminate $R_4$: $R_4 \leftarrow R_4 - 50 R_1 \Rightarrow [0, 40, -200, 497.5 \mid 2.375]$

New rows:
$R_2 = [0, 360, -50, 40 \mid 2.0]$
$R_3 = [0, -50, 500, -200 \mid 6.0]$
$R_4 = [0, 40, -200, 497.5 \mid 2.375]$

Divide $R_2$ by 360: $[0, 1, -0.1389, 0.1111 \mid 0.005556]$
Eliminate $R_3$: $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 + 50 R_2 \Rightarrow [0, 0, 493.06, -194.44 \mid 6.2778]$
Eliminate $R_4$: $R_4 \leftarrow R_4 - 40 R_2 \Rightarrow [0, 0, -194.44, 493.06 \mid 2.1528]$

Now solve the $2 \times 2$ sub-system:

$493.06\, \theta_3 - 194.44\, \theta_4 = 6.2778$
$-194.44\, \theta_3 + 493.06\, \theta_4 = 2.1528$

Adding both equations:

$$298.62\, \theta_3 + 298.62\, \theta_4 = 8.4306 \Rightarrow \theta_3 + \theta_4 = 0.02823$$

Subtracting (×2.535 to scale):

$$493.06 \times 2.535\, \theta_3 - 194.44 \times 2.535\, \theta_4 - (-194.44\, \theta_3 + 493.06\, \theta_4) = 6.2778 \times 2.535 - 2.1528$$

$$(1249.86 + 194.44)\,\theta_3 - (492.91 + 493.06)\,\theta_4 = 15.913 - 2.1528$$

$$1444.30\, \theta_3 - 985.97\, \theta_4 = 13.760$$

Using $\theta_4 = 0.02823 - \theta_3$:

$$1444.30\, \theta_3 - 985.97(0.02823 - \theta_3) = 13.760$$

$$1444.30\, \theta_3 + 985.97\, \theta_3 = 13.760 + 27.832 = 41.592$$

$$2430.27\, \theta_3 = 41.592 \Rightarrow \theta_3 \approx 0.01712$$

$$\theta_4 = 0.02823 - 0.01712 = 0.01111$$

Back-substituting into Row 2:

$$360\, \theta_2 - 50(0.01712) + 40(0.01111) = 2.0$$

$$360\, \theta_2 - 0.856 + 0.4444 = 2.0$$

$$360\, \theta_2 = 2.4116 \Rightarrow \theta_2 \approx 0.00670$$

Back-substituting into Row 1:

$$1000\, \theta_1 - 800(0.00670) + 50(0.01111) = 12.5$$

$$1000\, \theta_1 - 5.360 + 0.5556 = 12.5$$

$$1000\, \theta_1 = 17.305 \Rightarrow \theta_1 \approx 0.01730$$

**Final parameter vector:**

$$\hat{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.01730 \\ 0.00670 \\ 0.01712 \\ 0.01111 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 marks — 1 for setup, 4 for computation, 2 for final result with units/context)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in ARX** — students often write $y(t) = +\sum a_i y(t-i)$ instead of $-\sum a_i y(t-i)$. The model definition is $A(q) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots$, so the coefficient carries a plus inside $A$, hence a minus on the right-hand side. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing AR, ARX, ARMAX** — AR has *no* input term, ARX has $B(q)u(t)$, ARMAX has $C(q)e(t)$ in addition. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the persistency-of-excitation condition** in the ARX derivation — this is a mandatory concluding step. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Computational errors in matrix inversion** — students commonly drop the negative signs during Gaussian elimination. Always re-verify by substitution.
> 5. **Forgetting the noise polynomial $C(q)$ starts with 1** (i.e., $C(0) = 1$), unlike $B(q)$ which has no constant term. Loss: **1 mark** if this distinction is asked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black-box model** = data-driven model with no internal physiological structure imposed.
- Belongs to the **system identification** paradigm: experiment → record → identify → validate.
- The **shift operator** $q^{-1}$ is the key notational device: $q^{-1} y(t) = y(t-1)$.
- The **ARX** model is the workhorse: $A(q)y(t) = B(q)u(t-n_k) + e(t)$.
- The **ARMAX** model adds coloured-noise handling via $C(q)$.
- The **OE** model assumes output-side noise — ideal for sensor-driven biomedical applications.
- The **BJ** model is the most general linear parametric structure.
- The **NARX** model extends to nonlinear black-box via neural networks or polynomial expansions.
- The **least-squares estimator** for ARX is $\hat{\theta} = (\Phi^T\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^T Y$ — derived by minimising the quadratic prediction-error cost.
- The **persistency-of-excitation** condition ensures $\Phi^T \Phi$ is invertible.
- **Model-order selection** uses AIC, MDL, or FPE; **validation** uses residual whiteness, FIT %, and cross-correlation tests.
- **Biomedical applications** include ECG/EEG/EMG modelling, drug-response prediction, prosthetic control, ICU monitoring, and seizure detection.
- **White-box vs black-box**: white-box = first principles; black-box = pure data; grey-box = hybrid.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorise:** SISO, MIMO, IIR, FIR, PRBS, persistently exciting, one-step-ahead prediction, simulation prediction, residual analysis, cross-validation.
- **Typical KTU marks split:** 3-mark questions test definitions; 7-mark sub-parts test derivations and model comparisons; 14-mark full questions test complete identification workflows including computation.
- **Always state the order** of the model (e.g., ARX($n_a, n_b, n_k$)) when answering numerical problems.
- **Units check:** $a_i$ and $b_i$ are dimensionless regression coefficients; residuals $e(t)$ have the same units as $y(t)$.
- **Stability check** for AR part: all roots of $A(z) = 0$ must lie inside the unit circle $|z| < 1$ on the complex plane.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpretative Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interpretative Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretative Models** in biomedical signal processing are mathematical and computational frameworks designed to explain, characterize, and predict the generation, propagation, and measurement of physiological signals. They establish a quantitative relationship between the underlying biological source activity (e.g., electrical, mechanical, or chemical) and the observed signal at the measurement site (surface or invasive electrodes, transducers, or imaging sensors).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT635)** syllabus, interpretative models are categorized primarily into:

1. **Source Models** – Describe the biophysical origin of the signal.
2. **Volume Conductor Models** – Describe the propagation medium (the tissue, bone, fluid, etc.) between source and sensor.
3. **Observation / Forward Models** – Mathematically combine source and volume conductor to predict measurements.
4. **Inverse Models** – Infer source activity from measurements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Earthquake & Seismograph":**
> Imagine you cannot see what is happening deep inside the Earth, but you have a **seismograph** (sensor) on the surface. Whenever an earthquake (source) occurs, seismic waves travel through different rock layers (volume conductor) and reach the seismograph. To **interpret** the recorded waveform, geophysicists build **interpretative models** — they guess what kind of source caused it, how the medium deformed the wave, and finally reconstruct what happened underground.
> - The **earthquake source** ↔ the **bioelectric source** (e.g., a depolarizing cardiac cell or active neuron).
> - The **rock layers** ↔ the **head/torso volume conductor** (brain, skull, scalp; or thorax tissues).
> - The **seismograph reading** ↔ the **ECG/EEG/MEG signal** recorded.
> - The **interpretative model** ↔ the **mathematical framework** that links all three so that we can infer the source from the recording.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Conductivity of brain tissue | $\sigma_{brain}$ | **0.33 S/m** |
| Conductivity of skull bone | $\sigma_{skull}$ | **0.0042 S/m** |
| Conductivity of scalp | $\sigma_{scalp}$ | **0.33 S/m** |
| Conductivity of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) | $\sigma_{csf}$ | **1.79 S/m** |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| Cardiac dipole moment (typical) | $\vec{p}$ | **1–5 mA·cm** |

## 1.4 Why Interpretative Models Matter in KTU/Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without interpretative models, biomedical signals remain **mere waveforms**. Models convert waveforms into **clinically/physiologically meaningful information** — e.g., localizing an epileptic focus from EEG, identifying ischemia from ECG, or estimating joint torque from EMG.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Interpretative Models

Interpretative models are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Mathematical Structure
- **Linear vs. Nonlinear models**
- **Parametric vs. Non-parametric models**
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic models**

### Axis B — By Signal Modality
- **Cardiac models** (ECG): dipole / multipole models on a thorax volume conductor.
- **Neural models** (EEG/MEG): current-dipole, distributed, or equivalent current-dipole models on a head volume conductor.
- **Evoked potentials models**
- **Speech/biomechanical models** (vocal tract, joint dynamics)

### Axis C — By Direction of Inference
- **Forward models**: source + volume → predicted signal.
- **Inverse models**: measured signal → estimated source parameters.

## 2.2 Source Models — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Equivalent Current Dipole (ECD)
The fundamental source model. Any localized bioelectric activity can be approximated by an **equivalent current dipole** with moment:

$$\vec{p} = I \cdot \vec{d}$$

where $I$ is current (in amperes) and $\vec{d}$ is the dipole vector (in metres). For cardiac applications, the **solid-angle theorem** relates the dipole to the recorded potential.

### 2.2.2 Distributed Source Models
Used when multiple sources are simultaneously active (e.g., cortical EEG). The current density $\vec{J}(\vec{r}, t)$ is distributed across a source region.

### 2.2.3 Magnetic Dipole Model (for MEG)
Used for magnetoencephalography. The magnetic field is generated by intracellular currents. Crucially, the magnetic field of a current dipole in a **spherically symmetric volume conductor** is **independent of radial components** — a key theorem.

## 2.3 Volume Conductor Models

A volume conductor model specifies how electric/magnetic fields propagate through biological tissue. The governing equation is the **quasi-static approximation of Maxwell's equations**, leading to the **Poisson equation**:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma(\vec{r}) \nabla \Phi(\vec{r})) = - \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s(\vec{r})$$

| Volume Conductor Model | Geometry | Application | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Infinite homogeneous** | Single infinite medium | Initial estimates | Simple but unrealistic |
| **Spherical (3-shell / 4-shell)** | Concentric spheres | EEG/MEG head | Closed-form solution |
| **Realistic (BEM/FEM)** | MRI-derived mesh | Clinical localization | Accurate but computationally heavy |
| **Semi-infinite** | Half-space | Surface recordings | ECG early models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Element Method (BEM)** and **Finite Element Method (FEM)** are the most common numerical approaches used in KTU-aligned research problems to solve the Poisson equation in realistic geometries.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following equations — they appear in **≥ 60% of KTU ESE questions** on this topic.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{p} = I \cdot \vec{d}$ | Current dipole moment | Source definition |
| 2 | $\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$ | Quasi-static Poisson equation | Volume conductor |
| 3 | $\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi\sigma} \sum_i \vec{p}_i \cdot \frac{\vec{r}-\vec{r}_i}{\vert \vec{r}-\vec{r}_i \vert^3}$ | Potential of N dipoles | Forward EEG model |
| 4 | $\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \sum_i \frac{\vec{p}_i \times \vec{R}_i}{\vert \vec{R}_i \vert^3}$ | Magnetic field of N dipoles | Forward MEG model |
| 5 | $\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}$ | Far-field dipole potential | Far-field ECG/EEG |
| 6 | $\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^2} \vec{p} \times \hat{r}$ | Far-field magnetic dipole | Far-field MEG |
| 7 | $\Omega = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{n}}{r^2}$ | Solid angle theorem (ECG) | Cardiac dipole |
| 8 | $\Phi = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{n}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}$ | Cardiac dipole potential | Cardiac ECG |
| 9 | $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k x(n-k)$ | ARMA signal model | Parametric biosignal model |
| 10 | $\sigma_{tissue}$ values (table above) | Tissue conductivity | Forward problem |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Neurology** | Epilepsy focus localization (EEG) |
| **Cardiology** | Arrhythmia source identification (ECG inverse) |
| **BCI / Neurotechnology** | Motor imagery decoding |
| **Rehabilitation Eng.** | EMG-driven prosthesis control |
| **Medical Imaging** | Source localization coupled to fMRI/MRI |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potential of a Current Dipole in an Infinite Homogeneous Volume Conductor

**Goal:** Derive $\Phi(\vec{r})$ produced by a point current dipole $\vec{p}$ located at origin in an infinite homogeneous medium of conductivity $\sigma$.

### Step 1 — Start from Poisson's equation
For a current dipole source, the current density is:

$$\vec{J}_s(\vec{r}) = \vec{p} \, \delta(\vec{r})$$

The Poisson equation becomes:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s = -\nabla \cdot (\vec{p} \, \delta(\vec{r}))$$

### Step 2 — Use vector identity
Using $\nabla \cdot (\vec{A} \, f) = f \nabla \cdot \vec{A} + \vec{A} \cdot \nabla f$, and $\nabla \cdot \vec{p} = 0$ (since $\vec{p}$ is constant), we obtain:

$$\sigma \nabla^2 \Phi = -\vec{p} \cdot \nabla \delta(\vec{r})$$

### Step 3 — Use Green's function for the Laplacian
The Green's function of the Laplacian in 3D is:

$$G(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \vert \vec{r} \vert}$$

so that $\nabla^2 G = -\delta(\vec{r})$.

### Step 4 — Solve by integration
The solution is:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{\sigma} \int G(\vec{r} - \vec{r}') \, \vec{p} \cdot \nabla' \delta(\vec{r}') \, d^3\vec{r}'$$

### Step 5 — Integrate by parts and apply derivative of delta
Transferring the gradient from $\delta$ to $G$ and using $\nabla' G = -\nabla G$:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{\sigma} \vec{p} \cdot \nabla G(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{\sigma} \vec{p} \cdot \nabla \left( \frac{1}{4 \pi \vert \vec{r} \vert} \right)$$

### Step 6 — Compute the gradient
$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \vec{r} \vert} \right) = -\frac{\hat{r}}{r^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}}$$

This is the **fundamental forward equation for EEG/ECG dipole modeling**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Magnetic Field of a Current Dipole (Biot–Savart)

**Goal:** Derive $\vec{B}(\vec{r})$ produced by a current dipole $\vec{p}$.

### Step 1 — Start with Biot–Savart law for a current element
$$d\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{I \, d\vec{l} \times \hat{R}}{R^2}$$

### Step 2 — Replace the current element with the dipole moment
For an ideal dipole, $I \cdot d\vec{l} \to \vec{p}$:

$$\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{\vec{p} \times \hat{R}}{R^2}$$

### Step 3 — Express using vector cross product
$$\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{\vec{p} \times \vec{R}}{R^3}$$

where $\vec{R} = \vec{r} - \vec{r}'$ is the vector from dipole to observation point.

### Step 4 — Magnitude form
$$\boxed{\vert \vec{B} \vert = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{\vert \vec{p} \vert \sin\theta}{R^2}}$$

with $\theta$ the angle between $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{R}$. Note that $\vec{B}$ depends on $\sin\theta$ — hence **radial dipoles produce zero MEG signal** in a spherical head. This is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solid-Angle Model for ECG (Cardiac Dipole)

The **solid-angle theorem** (Wilson–Bayley) states that the potential recorded by an electrode due to a current dipole is:

$$\Phi = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{n}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal to the boundary surface and $r$ is the dipole-to-boundary distance.

### Step 1 — Cardiac dipole equivalent
The depolarization wavefront of the heart is modeled as a moving current dipole:

$$\vec{p}(t) = I_{cell} \cdot \vec{d}_{wavefront}$$

### Step 2 — Solid angle formulation
Equivalently:

$$\Phi(t) = \frac{\Omega(t) \cdot \vec{p}}{4 \pi \sigma}$$

where $\Omega(t)$ is the solid angle subtended by the wavefront at the electrode.

### Step 3 — Implication
The ECG waveform at any electrode is proportional to the time-varying **solid angle** of the activation wavefront as viewed from that electrode. This explains the morphology of the **P, QRS, and T waves** purely from dipole geometry.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parametric AR Model of a Biomedical Signal

A common **interpretative (parametric) model** for stationary biosignals is the **Autoregressive (AR) model**:

$$y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, y(n-k) + e(n)$$

### Step 1 — Compute autocorrelation
Multiplying by $y(n-m)$ and taking expectation, with $E[e(n) y(n-m)] = 0$ for $m \neq 0$:

$$R_y(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_y(m-k) + \sigma_e^2 \, \delta(m)$$

### Step 2 — Write the Yule–Walker equations
For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$R_y(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_y(m-k) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve using Levinson–Durbin recursion
This gives the AR coefficients $a_k$ which **interpret** the biosignal in terms of its spectral poles (resonances), enabling features such as dominant frequency in EEG rhythms or HRV spectral peaks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Forward & Inverse Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["BIOELECTRIC SOURCE\n(Current Dipole / Distributed Current)"] --> vc["VOLUME CONDUCTOR\n(Head / Thorax Model)"]
    vc --> sensor["SENSOR ARRAY\n(EEG / ECG / MEG Electrodes)"]
    sensor --> sig["RECORDED SIGNAL\n(Time Series)"]
    sig --> inv["INVERSE MODEL\n(Source Localization Algorithm)"]
    inv --> est["ESTIMATED SOURCE PARAMETERS\n(Location, Orientation, Magnitude)"]
    src -. Forward Model Prediction .-> sig
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — EEG Interpretative Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MRI / CT Scan\n(Anatomical Data)"] --> C["HEAD MODEL\n(Spherical or BEM/FEM Mesh)"]
    B["EEG Acquisition\n(Multi-channel)"] --> D["SIGNAL PREPROCESSING\n(Filtering, ICA, Artifact Removal)"]
    C --> E["FORWARD MODEL\nLead Field Matrix L"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["INVERSE SOLVER\n(MNE / LORETA / MUSIC / Beamformer)"]
    F --> G["SOURCE RECONSTRUCTION\n(Dipole or Distributed Map)"]
    G --> H["CLINICAL INTERPRETATION\n(e.g., Epileptic Focus)"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Equivalent Cardiac Dipole Model (ECG)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["HEART\n(Depolarization Wavefront)"] --> D["EQUIVALENT CURRENT DIPOLE\n(Moving Vector p(t))"]
    D --> T["THORAX VOLUME CONDUCTOR\n(Boundary Element Mesh)"]
    T --> E["BODY SURFACE ELECTRODES\n(12-lead ECG)"]
    E --> W["ECG WAVEFORM\nP, QRS, T Complex"]
    W --> I["CARDIAC DIAGNOSIS\n(Ischemia, Infarct, Arrhythmia)"]
```

## 4.4 Classification Map of Interpretative Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IM["INTERPRETATIVE MODELS"] --> SRC["SOURCE MODELS"]
    IM --> VC["VOLUME CONDUCTOR MODELS"]
    IM --> OBS["OBSERVATION / FORWARD MODELS"]
    IM --> INV["INVERSE MODELS"]
    IM --> PAR["PARAMETRIC MODELS"]
    SRC --> S1["Point Dipole"]
    SRC --> S2["Distributed Dipoles"]
    SRC --> S3["Magnetic Dipole (MEG)"]
    VC --> V1["Infinite Homogeneous"]
    VC --> V2["Spherical Shell"]
    VC --> V3["Realistic BEM / FEM"]
    OBS --> O1["Lead Field L"]
    OBS --> O2["Solid Angle (ECG)"]
    INV --> I1["Dipole Fit (Nonlinear)"]
    INV --> I2["Distributed (MNE / LORETA)"]
    INV --> I3["Beamformers (SAM, LCMV)"]
    PAR --> P1["AR Model"]
    PAR --> P2["ARMA Model"]
    PAR --> P3["State-Space Model"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an equivalent current dipole. Why is it used as the fundamental source model in bioelectric signal interpretation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An equivalent current dipole is defined by the equation $\vec{p} = I \cdot \vec{d}$, where $I$ is the current (in amperes) flowing across a distance $\vec{d}$. It is used as the fundamental model because any localized bioelectric activity in a small region of tissue can be mathematically represented as a single dipole whose moment captures both the magnitude and orientation of net current flow. For far-field observations (distance $\gg$ source size), higher-order multipole terms become negligible, making the dipole both physically meaningful and computationally tractable for ECG, EEG, and MEG interpretation. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the quasi-static approximation in biomedical signal modeling? State its validity condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The quasi-static approximation assumes that the time-varying magnetic fields produced by bioelectric sources are negligible, so the electric and magnetic fields can be treated as **decoupled** at any instant. The governing equation reduces to **Poisson's equation**: $\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$. The validity condition is that the wavelength of the electromagnetic field in tissue must be much larger than the body dimensions, i.e., $c / (f \cdot L) \gg 1$. Since biological tissue conductivities are low and biosignals are below ~1 kHz, this holds for ECG, EEG, EMG, and MEG. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain the **forward model** for EEG using the equivalent current dipole in an infinite homogeneous volume conductor. Discuss the **boundary conditions** used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the potential produced by **N dipoles** in an infinite homogeneous medium and explain how the **lead field matrix** arises in the multi-electrode setting. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Forward EEG Model (7 marks)

The bioelectric source is modelled as a current dipole at location $\vec{r}_i$ with moment $\vec{p}_i$. In an infinite homogeneous medium of conductivity $\sigma$, the potential at observation point $\vec{r}$ is the **superposition** of contributions from each dipole:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \sigma} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{p}_i \cdot \frac{\vec{r} - \vec{r}_i}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_i \vert^3}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
1. **At infinity:** $\Phi(\vec{r}) \to 0$ as $\vert \vec{r} \vert \to \infty$ (potential vanishes far from sources).
2. **At an interface between tissues of conductivity $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$:** continuity of potential $\Phi_1 = \Phi_2$ and continuity of normal current density $\sigma_1 \frac{\partial \Phi_1}{\partial n} = \sigma_2 \frac{\partial \Phi_2}{\partial n}$.

**Derivation steps:**
- Start with Poisson's equation: $\sigma \nabla^2 \Phi = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$.
- Apply Green's function $G = \frac{1}{4\pi \vert \vec{r}-\vec{r}' \vert}$.
- Differentiate to obtain the dipole potential $\Phi = \frac{\vec{p}\cdot \hat{R}}{4\pi\sigma R^2}$. **(Full 7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — N-dipole potential and Lead Field Matrix (7 marks)

For **N dipoles** observed at **M electrodes**, the potential at the $j$-th electrode is:

$$\Phi_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{L}_{ji} \cdot \vec{p}_i$$

where the **lead field vector** $\vec{L}_{ji}$ is given by:

$$\vec{L}_{ji} = \frac{1}{4\pi\sigma} \frac{\vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i}{\vert \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i \vert^3}$$

Collecting all electrodes and sources into matrix form:

$$\mathbf{\Phi} = \mathbf{L} \, \mathbf{p}$$

where $\mathbf{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}$, $\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 3N}$, and $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{3N \times 1}$. The matrix $\mathbf{L}$ is the **lead field matrix**, expressing the linear mapping between dipole moments and electrode potentials. This formulation is the basis of the **EEG inverse problem**, solved via methods such as MNE, LORETA, or beamforming. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Describe the **solid-angle model** of cardiac electrical activity. Derive the relationship between the ECG potential and the solid angle subtended by the depolarization wavefront. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **AR (autoregressive) parametric model** for biosignal interpretation, derive the **Yule–Walker equations**, and state two clinical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Solid-Angle Model (7 marks)

The heart's depolarization wavefront is approximated as a moving current dipole $\vec{p}(t) = I \cdot \vec{d}(t)$. The potential recorded at an electrode located at distance $r$ from the dipole, on the boundary with outward normal $\hat{n}$, is given by:

$$\Phi(t) = \frac{\vec{p}(t) \cdot \hat{n}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}$$

Using the identity $\frac{\hat{n}}{r^2} = \nabla \Omega$ (where $\Omega$ is the solid angle subtended by the boundary surface as seen from the dipole location), we obtain:

$$\Phi(t) = \frac{\vec{p}(t) \cdot \nabla \Omega}{4 \pi \sigma} = \frac{p \, \Omega(t)}{4 \pi \sigma}$$

Hence the ECG waveform at any electrode directly tracks the **time-varying solid angle** of the activation front. As the wavefront grows, $\Omega$ increases (R wave upstroke); as it recedes, $\Omega$ decreases (T wave). This explains ECG morphology purely from dipole geometry. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — AR Model and Yule–Walker (7 marks)

An **AR model of order p** for a biosignal $y(n)$ is:

$$y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + e(n)$$

where $e(n)$ is white noise with variance $\sigma_e^2$.

Multiplying both sides by $y(n-m)$ and taking the expectation, using $E[e(n) y(n-m)] = 0$ for $m \neq 0$:

$$R_y(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_y(m-k) \quad \text{for } m \geq 1$$

$$R_y(0) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_y(-k) + \sigma_e^2$$

In matrix form (Yule–Walker):

$$\begin{bmatrix}
R_y(0) & R_y(1) & \cdots & R_y(p-1) \\
R_y(1) & R_y(0) & \cdots & R_y(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_y(p-1) & R_y(p-2) & \cdots & R_y(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix}
= -\begin{bmatrix} R_y(1) \\ R_y(2) \\ \vdots \\ R_y(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Clinical applications:**
1. **EEG rhythm analysis** — AR poles identify dominant frequency components in alpha, beta, theta, delta bands for sleep staging.
2. **HRV analysis** — AR spectral estimation of RR intervals reveals sympathetic/parasympathetic balance. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Interpretative Models Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting conductivity $\sigma$** — Many students write $\Phi = \frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}}{4\pi r^2}$ and lose **1 mark** for omitting the tissue conductivity term.
> 2. **Confusing EEG and MEG formulas** — EEG depends on $\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}$ (radial sensitive); MEG depends on $\vec{p}\times\hat{r}$ (radial insensitive). This distinction is worth **2 marks** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Not stating quasi-static assumption explicitly** — Any forward model derivation **must** start with the quasi-static approximation or the examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping boundary conditions** — In volume conductor derivations, listing $\Phi \to 0$ at infinity and continuity of $\sigma \partial \Phi / \partial n$ across interfaces is **mandatory**.
> 5. **ARMA vs AR confusion** — KTU questions often test whether you correctly identify model order and whitening error. Always specify $e(n)$ as white noise.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interpretative model = source + volume conductor + observation mapping.**
- **Equivalent current dipole** $\vec{p} = I\vec{d}$ is the universal elementary source.
- **Poisson equation** $\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$ governs all bioelectric forward problems under the **quasi-static approximation**.
- **Forward dipole potential**: $\Phi = \dfrac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{4 \pi \sigma r^2}$ (EEG/ECG).
- **Forward magnetic field**: $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{\vec{p} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$ (MEG).
- **MEG is blind to radial dipoles** — a frequent viva question.
- **Volume conductor models**: infinite, spherical, BEM, FEM — choose based on accuracy vs. computational cost.
- **Lead field matrix** $\mathbf{L}$ maps sources $\mathbf{p}$ to electrode potentials $\mathbf{\Phi} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{p}$ — the linear algebra core of EEG/MEG.
- **Cardiac solid-angle theorem**: ECG waveform $\propto$ solid angle subtended by depolarization front.
- **AR parametric model**: $y(n) = -\sum a_k y(n-k) + e(n)$ — solved via Yule–Walker.
- **Tissue conductivities to memorize**: brain/scalp = 0.33 S/m, skull = 0.0042 S/m, CSF = 1.79 S/m.
- **Inverse methods**: dipole fitting, MNE, LORETA, beamforming (SAM, LCMV).
- **Always declare assumptions**: quasi-static, linearity, isotropy, homogeneity, far-field.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples: Mathematical Models and Signals in Intensive Care Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples: Mathematical Models and Signals in Intensive Care Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
