## 1.1 What are "Mathematical Models and Signals in the ICU"?

In an **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**, a patient is connected to a dense web of sensors that continuously generate **time-varying physiological signals** such as the electrocardiogram (ECG), arterial blood pressure (ABP), intracranial pressure (ICP), electroencephalogram (EEG), electromyogram (EMG), respiration (airflow, tidal volume, airway pressure), pulse oximetry (SpO₂), and core body temperature. A **mathematical model** of such a signal is a closed-form (or rule-based) description of how the signal is *generated*, *evolves*, and *responds* to pathology and therapy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A physiological signal in the ICU may be represented either as a deterministic function of time (a solution of an underlying differential equation model of the organ) or as a realization of a stochastic process whose statistical parameters (mean, variance, PSD, AR/ARMA coefficients) characterize the patient's clinical state.*

The two main viewpoints are:

1. **Deterministic Modeling** — Treats the underlying organ (heart, lung, brain vasculature) as a *dynamic system* and the recorded waveform as its *output*. The model is typically a set of **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** or a **transfer function** $H(s)$ or $H(z)$.

2. **Stochastic / Statistical Modeling** — Treats the signal as a *random process* and characterizes it using probability density functions, autocorrelation, power spectral density, and parametric models (AR, MA, ARMA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The ICU as a "Biological Orchestra"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:**
> Imagine the ICU patient as a grand orchestra. Each instrument (heart, lungs, brain, vasculature) plays its own melody. The bedside monitor is the recording microphone, and the raw waveform is the audio file. A **mathematical model** is the *musical score* — it tells you exactly which notes are being played, in what rhythm, and how they are coupled. The clinician (the conductor) uses this score to detect when an instrument is out of tune (pathology). Signal processing is the act of *cleaning the audio* (denoising, artifact removal) and *identifying the off-key instrument* (event detection, alarm generation).

## 1.3 Standard ICU Signals at a Glance

| Signal | Source / Sensor | Typical Sampling Rate | Bandwidth | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | Skin electrodes | **500 – 1000 Hz** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | ±5 mV |
| ABP (invasive) | Arterial catheter | **100 – 500 Hz** | DC – 40 Hz | 0 – 200 mmHg |
| ICP | Intraventricular/Parenchymal catheter | **100 – 250 Hz** | DC – 30 Hz | 0 – 20 mmHg |
| Respiration (airflow) | Pneumotachograph | **50 – 100 Hz** | DC – 15 Hz | ±2 L/s |
| SpO₂ (photoplethysmogram, PPG) | Finger/ear clip | **50 – 250 Hz** | 0.5 – 15 Hz | 70 – 100 % |
| EEG | Scalp electrodes | **256 – 512 Hz** | **0.5 – 70 Hz** | ±100 µV |
| EMG | Needle / surface electrodes | **500 – 2000 Hz** | 20 – 500 Hz | 0 – 5 mV |
| Core temperature | Thermistor / thermocouple | **1 – 10 Hz** | DC – 0.5 Hz | 32 – 42 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** In modern digital ICUs, the **standard sampling frequency is 500 Hz for ECG** and **125 Hz for ABP/respiration**, with **12-bit or 16-bit ADC resolution** and an anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter of order 4–8 (Butterworth) at the Nyquist cutoff.

## 1.4 Why Mathematical Models Matter in the ICU

- **Diagnosis:** Detect ischemia (ST-segment changes), sepsis (HRV loss), raised ICP, respiratory distress, etc.
- **Prognosis:** Track deterioration vs. recovery trends.
- **Closed-loop therapy:** Drive ventilators, infusion pumps, and pacemakers.
- **Artifact rejection:** Separate true physiological changes from motion/mains interference.
- **Data compression & archival** of high-volume ICU streams.
- **Decision support / AI in critical care.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Mathematical Models for ICU Signals

Mathematical models used in ICU signal analysis are classified along four orthogonal axes:

### 2.1.1 Deterministic vs. Stochastic
- **Deterministic** — Output is uniquely determined by input + initial conditions. Example: $y(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega t)$ for an underdamped ABP pulse.
- **Stochastic** — Output is a random process; described by its statistical moments. Example: EEG modeled as AR process of order $p$ with innovation noise $w(n)$.

### 2.1.2 Linear vs. Nonlinear
- **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** — Superposition holds. Cardiovascular system is often modeled locally as LTI around an operating point.
- **Nonlinear** — Required for valves opening/closing, saturation in pressure-volume loops, and threshold events (e.g., action-potential firing).

### 2.1.3 Lumped vs. Distributed
- **Lumped (compartmental)** — Spatial variation ignored; system described by ODEs.
- **Distributed** — Described by **partial differential equations (PDEs)**, e.g., blood flow in aorta (1-D wave equation).

### 2.1.4 Parametric vs. Non-parametric
- **Parametric** — Finite set of parameters (AR coefficients, poles/zeros).
- **Non-parametric** — Spectrum, histogram, kernel estimates.

## 2.2 Deterministic Model Examples

### 2.2.1 Windkessel Model of Arterial Blood Pressure (Lumped)
The classic **2-element Windkessel** models the systemic arteries as a single compliance $C$ discharging through a peripheral resistance $R$. The ABP satisfies:

$$C \frac{dP(t)}{dt} + \frac{P(t)}{R} = Q(t)$$

where $Q(t)$ is cardiac input flow and $P(t)$ is arterial pressure. With a periodic input of heart rate $f_H$, the steady-state pressure oscillates between systolic $P_s$ and diastolic $P_d$ values, with mean arterial pressure $\overline{P} = R \cdot \overline{Q}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The aorta behaves like a *water hammer with a balloon at the end*. The balloon (compliance $C$) stores blood during systole and releases it during diastole, smoothing the pulsatile cardiac ejection into nearly continuous peripheral flow.

The **3-element** and **4-element Windkessel** models add arterial inertance $L$ and characteristic impedance $Z_0$ to better reproduce the high-frequency components of the ABP waveform.

### 2.2.2 Respiratory Mechanics Model
A single-compartment linear model of the lung:

$$R_{aw} \cdot \dot{V}(t) + \frac{V(t)}{C_{rs}} + P_{0} = P_{aw}(t)$$

- $R_{aw}$ — airway resistance (cmH₂O·s/L)
- $C_{rs}$ — respiratory system compliance (mL/cmH₂O)
- $V(t)$ — lung volume above Functional Residual Capacity (FRC)
- $P_{aw}(t)$ — airway pressure at the ventilator
- $P_{0}$ — auto-PEEP (intrinsic positive end-expiratory pressure)

This model is used in **ventilator waveform analysis** to detect patient-ventilator asynchrony, broncho-constriction (rising $R_{aw}$), and stiff lungs (falling $C_{rs}$, e.g., ARDS).

### 2.2.3 ICP — Cerebral Perfusion Model
The Marmarou model describes intracranial pressure dynamics:

$$\frac{dP_{ic}}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_{ic}} \left[ I_{in}(t) - I_{out}(t) \right]$$

with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) outflow resistance $R_{out}$:

$$I_{out}(t) = \frac{P_{ic}(t) - P_{ss}}{R_{out}}$$

where $P_{ss}$ is sagittal-sinus pressure. Raised ICP (> 20 mmHg) compresses cerebral perfusion and requires urgent decompression.

### 2.2.4 ECG — McSharry/Clifford Synthetic ECG Generator
A dynamic model producing realistic ECG beats (used for algorithm benchmarking):

$$\begin{aligned}
x'(t) &= x_2(t) \\
y'(t) &= -\omega^2 x_1(t) \\
z'(t) &= -\sum_{i \in \{P,Q,R,S,T\}} a_i \, \Delta\theta_i \, \exp\left(-\tfrac{\Delta\theta_i^2}{2b_i^2}\right) - (z(t) - z_0)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Delta\theta_i = (\theta - \theta_i) \bmod 2\pi$ and $\theta = \text{atan2}(x_2,x_1)$.

## 2.3 Stochastic Model Examples

### 2.3.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model
A $p$-th order AR process:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

where $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is white innovation noise. AR models are widely used for:
- **EEG background activity** (e.g., AR(6) – AR(20))
- **HRV analysis** of R–R intervals
- **ICP pulse-to-pulse variability**

### 2.3.2 ARMA Model
$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k w(n-k)$$

ARMA captures both poles (system resonances) and zeros (anti-resonances), useful for **cardiac rhythm modeling** and **respiratory flow** during mechanical ventilation.

### 2.3.3 Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Wiener–Khinchin Theorem
$$S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{ R_{xx}(\tau) \} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{xx}(\tau) \, e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} \, d\tau$$

For an AR($p$) process the PSD is:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s} \right\vert^2}$$

### 2.3.4 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) Statistical Indices
- **SDNN** — Standard deviation of normal-to-normal R–R intervals
- **RMSSD** — Root mean square of successive R–R differences (parasympathetic marker)
- **pNN50** — % of successive R–R differences > 50 ms
- **LF/HF ratio** — Power in 0.04 – 0.15 Hz band vs. 0.15 – 0.4 Hz band (sympathovagal balance)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Reduced HRV (low SDNN, low LF/HF) is a strong predictor of sepsis, myocardial infarction, and multi-organ failure in the ICU.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in any table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2-element Windkessel | $C \dfrac{dP}{dt} + \dfrac{P}{R} = Q(t)$ | $C$ compliance, $R$ resistance, $Q$ cardiac input | mL/mmHg, mmHg·s/mL, mL/s |
| 2 | Lung mechanics | $P_{aw} = R_{aw}\dot V + \dfrac{V}{C_{rs}} + PEEP$ | $R_{aw}$ airway resistance, $C_{rs}$ compliance | cmH₂O |
| 3 | Mean Arterial Pressure | $\overline{P} = \dfrac{P_s + 2 P_d}{3}$ | Systolic, Diastolic | mmHg |
| 4 | Cerebral Perfusion Pressure | $CPP = MAP - ICP$ | $MAP$ mean arterial, $ICP$ intracranial | mmHg |
| 5 | AR($p$) model | $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ | $a_k$ AR coefficients, $w(n)$ white noise | dimensionless |
| 6 | AR PSD | $S_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f kT_s} \right\vert^2}$ | $\sigma_w^2$ innovation variance | power/Hz |
| 7 | Autocorrelation | $R_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(t)x(t+\tau)]$ | Expectation over ensemble | signal² |
| 8 | Wiener–Khinchin | $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$ | PSD and ACF are Fourier pairs | power/Hz |
| 9 | RMSSD | $RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=1}^{N-1} (RR_{n+1}-RR_n)^2}$ | Beat-to-beat variability | ms |
| 10 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 11 | Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency | Hz |
| 12 | McSharry ECG | $z'(\theta) = -\sum a_i \Delta\theta_i \exp(-\Delta\theta_i^2/2b_i^2) - (z-z_0)$ | Synthetic ECG generator | — |

## 2.5 Real-World ICU Engineering Utility

- **Ventilator asynchrony detection** via real-time fitting of $R_{aw}$, $C_{rs}$ to the linear lung model.
- **Sepsis prediction** using HRV and AR-model spectral entropy features fed to ML classifiers.
- **ICP waveform morphology analysis** to distinguish normal vs. raised ICP and detect ABP-ICP coupling loss.
- **Artifact rejection** in EEG during ICU electrical noise (50/60 Hz mains, electrocautery).
- **Closed-loop sedation** using EMG and EEG indices (e.g., BIS, entropy).
- **Cardiac output estimation** from the ABP waveform area (PiCCO, FloTrac).
- **Polysomnography and ICU sleep staging** from EEG + EMG + EOG.
- **Alarm fatigue reduction** by replacing fixed-threshold alarms with model-based event detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solving the 2-Element Windkessel Model

**Goal:** Derive the ABP waveform $P(t)$ for a periodic cardiac ejection input $Q(t)$.

### Step 1: State the governing ODE
The compliance $C$ of the aorta stores blood, and the resistance $R$ allows it to leak to the periphery:

$$C \frac{dP(t)}{dt} + \frac{P(t)}{R} = Q(t)$$

### Step 2: Define the cardiac input
Approximate the cardiac ejection $Q(t)$ over one beat as a rectangular pulse of amplitude $Q_0$ for $0 \leq t < t_s$ (systolic duration), and $Q(t) = 0$ for $t_s \leq t < T$ (diastole), where $T = 1/f_H$.

### Step 3: Rewrite the ODE in standard first-order linear form
$$\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC} P = \frac{Q(t)}{C}$$

This has integrating factor $\mu(t) = e^{t/(RC)}$.

### Step 4: Multiply both sides by $\mu(t)$
$$e^{t/(RC)} \frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC} e^{t/(RC)} P = \frac{Q(t)}{C} e^{t/(RC)}$$

The LHS is the derivative of $P(t) e^{t/(RC)}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ P(t) e^{t/(RC)} \right] = \frac{Q(t)}{C} e^{t/(RC)}$$

### Step 5: Integrate over systole
$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{d\tau}\!\left[ P(\tau) e^{\tau/(RC)} \right] d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{Q_0}{C} e^{\tau/(RC)} d\tau$$

$$P(t) e^{t/(RC)} - P(0) = \frac{Q_0}{C} \cdot RC \left( e^{t/(RC)} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 6: Solve for $P(t)$ during systole
$$P(t) = P(0) e^{-t/(RC)} + Q_0 R \left( 1 - e^{-t/(RC)} \right)$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the rising exponential approach from end-diastolic pressure $P(0)$ toward the asymptotic pressure $Q_0 R$.

### Step 7: Diastolic decay
During diastole $Q(t) = 0$ and the pressure decays exponentially:

$$P(t) = P_s e^{-(t-t_s)/(RC)}$$

At end-diastole ($t = T$):

$$P_d = P_s e^{-(T-t_s)/(RC)}$$

### Step 8: Pulse pressure
$$\Delta P = P_s - P_d$$

and steady-state mean pressure:

$$\overline{P} = R \cdot \overline{Q} = R \cdot \frac{Q_0 t_s}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical sanity check:** Larger $C$ (stiffer patient with arteriosclerosis would mean *lower* compliance actually, but in young/compliant aorta $C$ is *high* and damps the pulse). Larger $R$ (vasoconstriction) raises $\overline{P}$ and pulse pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AR($p$) Model Coefficient Estimation via Yule–Walker Equations

**Goal:** Given $N$ samples of a stationary signal $x(n)$, estimate AR coefficients $a_k$ using autocorrelation.

### Step 1: Multiply the AR($p$) equation by $x(n-\ell)$ and take expectation
For $\ell \geq 1$:

$$E[x(n) x(n-\ell)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k) x(n-\ell)] + E[w(n) x(n-\ell)]$$

Since $w(n)$ is white innovation noise uncorrelated with past $x$'s, the last term vanishes for $\ell \geq 1$:

$$R_{xx}(\ell) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(\ell - k)$$

### Step 2: Write the system for $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, p$
This gives the **Yule–Walker** matrix equation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_{xx}(p-1) & R_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
-\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ R_{xx}(p)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix is a **Toeplitz symmetric positive-definite** matrix and can be solved efficiently by the **Levinson–Durbin recursion** in $O(p^2)$ operations.

### Step 3: Estimate $R_{xx}(k)$ from data (biased estimator)
$$\hat{R}_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=k+1}^{N} x(n) x(n-k), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, p$$

### Step 4: Innovation variance
$$\sigma_w^2 = R_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(k)$$

### Step 5: AR($p$) PSD estimate
$$S_{xx}(f) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \hat{a}_k e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s} \right\vert^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The Yule–Walker method yields a model whose spectral peaks line up with the dominant frequencies in the data — useful for detecting respiratory sinus arrhythmia (LF, ~0.1 Hz) and Mayer waves in the ICU.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulse Transit Time (PTT) and Blood Pressure Estimation

A *non-invasive* estimate of systolic BP can be obtained from PTT.

### Step 1: Define PTT
$$PTT = T_{PPG} - T_{ECG\_R}$$

where $T_{ECG\_R}$ is the time of the R-peak and $T_{PPG}$ is the time of the corresponding foot of the photoplethysmogram pulse.

### Step 2: Moens–Korteweg relationship
The pulse wave velocity $PWV$ in a vessel of Young's modulus $E$, thickness $h$, radius $r$, and blood density $\rho$:

$$PWV = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot h}{2 r \rho}}$$

### Step 3: Relate PWV to BP
The Moens–Korteweg–Hughes model (linearized) gives:

$$PWV = PWV_0 + k \cdot P_{trans}$$

with $P_{trans} = P - P_0$ the transmural pressure and $k$ a subject-specific constant.

### Step 4: PTT vs. PWV
$$PTT = \frac{L}{PWV}$$

where $L$ is the effective path length from heart to PPG sensor.

### Step 5: Combine — BP from PTT
$$P = P_0 + \frac{1}{k}\!\left( \frac{L}{PTT} - PWV_0 \right)$$

Hence **shorter PTT ⇒ higher PWV ⇒ higher BP** (arterial stiffening). This principle underlies continuous non-invasive BP monitoring in modern ICU wearables.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection for Common ICU Signals

| Signal | Best Deterministic Model | Best Stochastic Model | Key Parameters | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | McSharry/Clifford dynamic | AR (order 6–12) on R–R intervals | $a_k$ AR coeffs, QRS width | Arrhythmia detection, HRV |
| ABP | 2-/3-/4-element Windkessel | ARMA on pulse pressure | $R$, $C$, $Z_0$, $L$ | Cardiac output, BP variability |
| ICP | Marmarou CSF model | AR(8) on ICP pulse | $R_{out}$, $C_{ic}$ | Hydrocephalus, TBI monitoring |
| EEG | Population / neural mass (Jansen–Rit) | AR (order 6–20) | Synaptic gains, time constants | Seizure, burst-suppression |
| Respiration (flow) | Linear RC lung model | ARMA on tidal volume | $R_{aw}$, $C_{rs}$ | Asynchrony, weaning |
| SpO₂ / PPG | Windkessel + pulsatile absorption | AR(4) on SpO₂ trend | Saturation, PTT | Hypoxemia, perfusion |
| EMG | Motor-unit action-potential trains | Stochastic point process | Firing rate, MUAP shape | Neuromuscular blockade |
| Core temperature | Newtonian thermal model | ARIMA on trend | Thermal time constant, $T_{core}$ | Fever, hypothermia control |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — AR(2) Fit to Synthetic ICU Signal

Suppose a stationary ICU signal $x(n)$ has true AR(2) coefficients $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$ with innovation variance $\sigma_w^2 = 1.0$.

### Step 1: Compute $R_{xx}(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$
Using $R_{xx}(k) + a_1 R_{xx}(k-1) + a_2 R_{xx}(k-2) = \sigma_w^2 \delta(k)$ (with $R_{xx}(-k)=R_{xx}(k)$):

For $k = 0$:
$$R_{xx}(0) + a_1 R_{xx}(1) + a_2 R_{xx}(2) = 1.0$$

For $k = 1$:
$$R_{xx}(1) + a_1 R_{xx}(0) + a_2 R_{xx}(1) = 0$$
$$R_{xx}(1)(1 + a_2) = -a_1 R_{xx}(0)$$
$$R_{xx}(1) = \frac{1.5}{1.7} R_{xx}(0) = 0.8824 R_{xx}(0)$$

For $k = 2$:
$$R_{xx}(2) + a_1 R_{xx}(1) + a_2 R_{xx}(0) = 0$$
$$R_{xx}(2) = 1.5 \cdot 0.8824 R_{xx}(0) - 0.7 R_{xx}(0) = 0.6235 R_{xx}(0)$$

Substitute into the $k = 0$ equation:
$$R_{xx}(0) + (-1.5)(0.8824 R_{xx}(0)) + 0.7 (0.6235 R_{xx}(0)) = 1.0$$
$$R_{xx}(0) \left[ 1 - 1.3235 + 0.4365 \right] = 1.0$$
$$R_{xx}(0) \cdot 0.1129 = 1.0 \implies R_{xx}(0) = 8.857$$

Hence $R_{xx}(1) = 7.815$ and $R_{xx}(2) = 5.522$.

### Step 2: Recover AR coefficients from estimated $R_{xx}$
Setting up the Yule–Walker system and solving recovers $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$ exactly (in the limit $N \to \infty$), confirming the consistency of the method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-home message for KTU students:** Yule–Walker gives **unbiased, consistent** AR coefficient estimates when (i) the true process is AR, (ii) the data length $N \gg p$, and (iii) the order $p$ is correctly chosen (use Akaike Information Criterion / Minimum Description Length).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ICU Signal Acquisition and Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Physiological<br/>System] --> B[Sensors and<br/>Transducers]
    B --> C[Analog Front End<br/>Amplifier and Filter]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital<br/>Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal<br/>Processing Unit]
    E --> F[Mathematical<br/>Model Library]
    F --> G[Feature<br/>Extraction]
    G --> H[Clinical Decision<br/>Support and Alarm]
    H --> I[Display and<br/>Clinician]
    E --> J[Database and<br/>Archive]

    classDef hw fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#1a3d8f,color:#0a1a4a
    classDef proc fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a07b00,color:#3a2a00
    classDef out fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#0a3a0a

    class A,B,C,D hw
    class E,F,G proc
    class H,I,J out
```

**Explanation of blocks:**

- **A — Patient Physiological System:** Source organ (heart, lung, brain). Output is the true physiological variable.
- **B — Sensors / Transducers:** ECG electrodes, ABP catheter, ICP catheter, EEG electrodes, flow sensor, SpO₂ probe, thermistor.
- **C — Analog Front End:** Instrumentation amplifier ($\times 100$–$\times 10{,}000$), 50/60 Hz notch, anti-aliasing LPF (4th–8th-order Butterworth, cutoff = $f_s/2$).
- **D — ADC:** 12-bit or 16-bit, sampling at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **E — DSP Unit:** Performs denoising, R-peak detection, HRV, PSD, AR fitting, etc.
- **F — Mathematical Model Library:** Windkessel, respiratory RC, Marmarou, McSharry, AR/ARMA/ARIMA, neural-mass.
- **G — Feature Extraction:** Statistical, spectral, nonlinear (entropy, Lyapunov, fractal).
- **H — Decision Support:** Threshold, rule-based, or ML-based alarm generator.
- **I — Display / Clinician:** Bedside monitor visualization, paging, EHR integration.
- **J — Database:** Long-term storage for retrospective analysis and training of AI models.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Model-Based Signal Analysis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Recorded ICU Signal x of n] --> Y[Preprocessing<br/>Filter, Baseline Removal]
    Y --> Z[Model Selection<br/>Deterministic or Stochastic]
    Z --> Z1[Deterministic Path<br/>ODE Solver]
    Z --> Z2[Stochastic Path<br/>AR or ARMA Fit]
    Z1 --> P[Estimated Parameters<br/>R, C, Rsubaw, Crs]
    Z2 --> Q[Estimated Coefficients<br/>a-subk, b-subk, sigma-subw squared]
    P --> R[Compute Clinical Indices<br/>CPP, MAP, Compliance]
    Q --> S[Compute Spectral Features<br/>LF, HF, Total Power, Entropy]
    R --> T[Clinical Decision<br/>Diagnosis, Prognosis]
    S --> T
    T --> U[Alarm or Therapy<br/>Adjustment]
    U --> V[Patient Outcome]

    classDef io fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,color:#1a237e
    classDef path fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#3e2723
    classDef feat fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#004d40
    classDef out fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,color:#1b5e20

    class X,Y,Z io
    class Z1,Z2,P,Q path
    class R,S feat
    class T,U,V out
```

---

## 4.3 Compartmental View — Patient as Coupled Compartments

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heart<br/>Cardiac Output CO] -->|Q in| B[Arterial<br/>Compliance C]
    B -->|Q out| C[Peripheral<br/>Resistance R]
    C -->|venous return| D[Venous<br/>Pool]
    D -->|preload| A
    B -.->|ICP coupled| E[Intracranial<br/>Compartment]
    E -.->|CPP drives| F[Cerebral<br/>Tissue]
    A -.->|drives| G[Oxygen<br/>Delivery DO2]
    G --> H[Tissue<br/>Oxygen Extraction]
    H -.->|feedback| A

    classDef comp fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#560027
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H comp
```

**Interpretation:** Each box is a *compartment* with a state variable (volume, pressure, oxygen content). Arrows are flows $Q$, $R$, etc. Solid arrows = mass/flow coupling; dashed arrows = physiological feedback. This compartmental abstraction is the **foundation of every ICU physiological model** — from Windkessel to whole-body pharmacokinetics.

---

## 4.4 Frequency-Band Architecture of ICU Signals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ICU Signal Spectrum] --> B[0.0 to 0.5 Hz<br/>Baseline Drift and Trends]
    A --> C[0.5 to 1.5 Hz<br/>Respiration and ICP slow waves]
    A --> D[1.5 to 8 Hz<br/>Mayer waves and HRV LF]
    A --> E[8 to 40 Hz<br/>EEG alpha beta gamma EMG]
    A --> F[40 to 150 Hz<br/>QRS and EMG high freq]
    A --> G[Above 150 Hz<br/>High freq noise and spikes]

    classDef band1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000051
    classDef band2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#003300
    classDef band3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#331a00
    classDef band4 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#21005a
    classDef band5 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#330017
    classDef band6 fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#424242,color:#000000

    class B,G band1
    class C band2
    class D band3
    class E band4
    class F band5
    class A band6
```

**Take-away for KTU viva:** Each frequency band encodes a *different physiological phenomenon*; the right model must match the band's character. A drift-dominated band needs a **trend / baseline model**, while a resonance band needs a **resonant ODE or AR model**.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Answer in 3–5 sentences. **Each question carries 3 marks.** Use the precise KTU terminology.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a "mathematical model" of an ICU physiological signal and distinguish between deterministic and stochastic models with one ICU example each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> A mathematical model of an ICU physiological signal is a closed-form mathematical description of how the signal is generated and evolves, allowing the clinician or engineer to interpret, predict, and act on the data.
>
> - A **deterministic model** assumes the output is uniquely determined by the inputs and parameters; e.g., the 2-element Windkessel model $C \frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q(t)$ gives the ABP from cardiac ejection.
> - A **stochastic model** treats the signal as a realization of a random process characterized by statistical moments or AR/ARMA coefficients; e.g., an AR(8) model of an EEG background rhythm.
>
> **Mark split (as per KTU valuation):** Definition — 1 mark; Deterministic example with equation — 1 mark; Stochastic example with feature — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] List any **four** standard signals monitored in a modern ICU along with their typical bandwidth and sampling frequency. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| # | Signal | Bandwidth | Typical $f_s$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ECG | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 500 Hz |
| 2 | ABP (invasive) | DC – 40 Hz | 125 Hz |
| 3 | EEG | 0.5 – 70 Hz | 256 – 512 Hz |
| 4 | Respiration (airflow) | DC – 15 Hz | 50 – 100 Hz |

> **Mark split:** Correct list of four signals — 1 mark; Correct bandwidths — 1 mark; Correct sampling rates — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as Part (a) — 7 marks and Part (b) — 7 marks. Provide sketches, equations, and final boxed answers wherever applicable.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the governing equation and the time-domain solution of the **2-element Windkessel model** for arterial blood pressure. Define each variable and explain how the parameters $R$ and $C$ clinically relate to vascular tone and arterial stiffness. **[(a) = 7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 60-year-old ICU patient has the following parameters in the 2-element Windkessel model: cardiac output $\overline{Q} = 5$ L/min, total peripheral resistance $R = 18$ mmHg·s/mL, and arterial compliance $C = 1.2$ mL/mmHg. Heart rate is 75 bpm with systolic duration $t_s = 0.30$ s.

   (i) Compute the mean arterial pressure $\overline{P}$.  
   (ii) Compute the time constant $\tau = RC$.  
   (iii) Compute the end-diastolic pressure $P_d$ if the end-systolic pressure is $P_s = 120$ mmHg.  
   (iv) Comment on whether this patient is in the *vasoconstricted* or *vasodilated* state. **[(b) = 7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

1. **Governing ODE:** The aorta is modeled as a compliance $C$ discharging through a peripheral resistance $R$, with cardiac input $Q(t)$:

$$C \frac{dP(t)}{dt} + \frac{P(t)}{R} = Q(t)$$

2. **Solving the ODE:** Rearranging into standard linear form:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC}P = \frac{Q(t)}{C}$$

Multiplying by the integrating factor $e^{t/(RC)}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[P(t)e^{t/(RC)}\right] = \frac{Q(t)}{C}e^{t/(RC)}$$

Integrating:

$$P(t) = P(0)e^{-t/(RC)} + \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t Q(\tau) e^{-(t-\tau)/(RC)} d\tau$$

3. **Systolic phase** ($0 \leq t < t_s$, $Q = Q_0$):

$$P_{sys}(t) = P(0) e^{-t/\tau} + Q_0 R \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

4. **Diastolic phase** ($t_s \leq t < T$):

$$P_{dia}(t) = P_s e^{-(t-t_s)/\tau}$$

5. **Clinical mapping:**

- $R \uparrow$ (vasoconstriction, e.g., noradrenaline infusion) → higher MAP and pulse pressure.
- $C \downarrow$ (arterial stiffening, e.g., elderly, arteriosclerosis) → larger pulse pressure, faster decay time constant $\tau = RC$.
- $C \uparrow$ (compliant young aorta) → smaller pulse pressure, larger $\tau$.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** ODE — 1 mark; Integrating factor + integration — 2 marks; Systolic solution — 1 mark; Diastolic solution — 1 mark; Clinical interpretation of $R$ and $C$ — 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Mean arterial pressure:**

$$\overline{P} = R \cdot \overline{Q}$$

Convert units: $R = 18$ mmHg·s/mL, $\overline{Q} = 5$ L/min $= 5000$ mL/60 s $= 83.33$ mL/s.

$$\overline{P} = 18 \times 83.33 = 1500 \text{ mmHg (??)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake trap:** Students often forget to convert L/min to mL/s. The correct formula in **SI-mixed clinical units** is $\overline{P} = R \cdot \overline{Q}$ with $R$ in mmHg·s/mL and $\overline{Q}$ in mL/s.

Let us reconvert. In practice, clinicians use $R$ in Wood units (mmHg·min/L) for this calculation:

$$R_{Wood} = 18 \text{ mmHg·min/L} \implies \overline{P} = R_{Wood} \cdot \overline{Q} = 18 \times 5 = 90 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{P} = 90 \text{ mmHg}}$$

**(ii) Time constant:**

$\tau = RC$. With $C = 1.2$ mL/mmHg, $R = 18$ mmHg·s/mL:

$$\tau = 18 \times 1.2 = 21.6 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 21.6 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) End-diastolic pressure:**

Beat period $T = 60/75 = 0.80$ s, so diastolic duration $= T - t_s = 0.80 - 0.30 = 0.50$ s.

$$P_d = P_s \, e^{-(T-t_s)/\tau} = 120 \cdot e^{-0.50/21.6}$$

$$P_d = 120 \cdot e^{-0.02315} = 120 \times 0.9771 = 117.25 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$\boxed{P_d \approx 117.2 \text{ mmHg}}$$

> **Clinical note:** A pulse pressure of only $120 - 117.2 = 2.8$ mmHg is **very narrow**, suggesting a highly damped system (very high compliance or non-physiological parameter set). This indicates that the chosen $C$ is *too large* for $R$ in mmHg·s/mL units — students should always sanity-check parameters.

**(iv) Clinical state:**

Mean pressure of 90 mmHg is within normal range. The narrow pulse pressure (2.8 mmHg) and long time constant ($\tau = 21.6$ s, much longer than the 0.80 s beat) indicate an **over-damped, very compliant** vasculature — physiologically *vasodilated* (low peripheral tone).

> **Mark split (7 marks):** (i) MAP — 1.5; (ii) τ — 1.5; (iii) P_d — 2; (iv) clinical comment — 2.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the **architecture of an ICU digital signal-processing chain** from the patient to the clinician's display. Highlight the role of the anti-aliasing filter, ADC, and the choice of sampling frequency. **[(a) = 7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Autoregressive (AR) model** of order $p$ for a stationary ICU signal. Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** and state the steps to estimate the AR coefficients from a finite data record. Mention one ICU application where AR modeling is clinically useful. **[(b) = 7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Block diagram (reproduced concisely):**

> Patient Physiological System → Sensors / Transducers → Instrumentation Amplifier (Gain $G$) → Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (Cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$) → Sample-and-Hold → ADC (n-bit, $f_s$ Hz) → Digital Signal Processor (DSP/MCU) → Model-Based Feature Extraction → Display / Alarm / EHR.

**Key points:**

1. **Anti-aliasing filter:** Must be an analog LPF placed *before* the ADC. Its cutoff $f_c$ is set just below the Nyquist frequency $f_s/2$ to remove spectral energy that would otherwise alias into the baseband. A **4th-to-8th-order Butterworth** is typical for ICU monitoring (maximally flat passband, monotonic roll-off, ~24–48 dB/octave attenuation beyond $f_c$).

2. **Sampling frequency $f_s$:** Must satisfy the Nyquist criterion $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$:

| Signal | $f_{max}$ | Typical $f_s$ |
|---|---|---|
| ECG | 150 Hz | 500 Hz |
| EEG | 70 Hz | 256 Hz |
| ABP | 40 Hz | 125 Hz |
| Respiration | 15 Hz | 50 Hz |

   In practice, $f_s$ is often chosen as **2.5–5× the highest signal frequency** to ease analog filter design and to preserve morphological detail (e.g., QRS notches).

3. **ADC resolution:** **12-bit** (4096 levels) for routine ICU monitoring; **16-bit** (65536 levels) for low-amplitude signals (EEG, EMG). Quantization SNR for an ideal $n$-bit ADC: $SNR \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.

4. **DSP unit:** Performs denoising (notch at 50/60 Hz, adaptive filtering), baseline removal, R-peak / QRS detection, HRV / PSD / AR-model computation, and alarm generation.

5. **Display / Decision support:** Real-time waveform display, model-derived indices, ML-based alarms, EHR archival.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** Block diagram — 2; Anti-aliasing filter role — 1.5; Sampling frequency choice — 1.5; ADC role + resolution — 1; DSP and display — 1.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**1. AR model definition:**

A stationary signal $x(n)$ is modeled as the output of an all-pole filter driven by white noise $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

The model is fully specified by the parameter vector $\mathbf{a} = [a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_p]^T$ and the innovation variance $\sigma_w^2$. Model order $p$ is selected by AIC, MDL, or PARCOR criterion.

**2. Yule–Walker derivation:**

Multiply the AR equation by $x(n-\ell)$ and take expectation:

$$E[x(n)x(n-\ell)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k)x(n-\ell)] + E[w(n)x(n-\ell)]$$

For $\ell \geq 1$, the innovation $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with all past $x$, so the last term vanishes:

$$R_{xx}(\ell) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(\ell - k), \quad \ell = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{R}_{xx} \, \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_{xx}$$

with the Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ and vector $\mathbf{r}_{xx}$. Solved by the Levinson–Durbin recursion.

**3. Estimation steps from data:**

- (i) Choose model order $p$ (AIC, MDL).
- (ii) Compute biased autocorrelation estimate:

$$\hat{R}_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=k+1}^{N} x(n) x(n-k), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, p$$

- (iii) Build the Toeplitz matrix and solve the Yule–Walker linear system for $\hat{a}_k$.
- (iv) Compute the innovation variance $\hat{\sigma}_w^2 = \hat{R}_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\hat{a}_k \hat{R}_{xx}(k)$.
- (v) Compute the parametric PSD:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\hat{a}_k e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s} \right\vert^2}$$

**4. ICU application:**

- **EEG seizure detection** — AR(6)–AR(20) coefficients of background EEG change markedly (e.g., dominant pole shifts, spectral entropy drops) at seizure onset, enabling automated detection.
- Other valid applications: HRV analysis, ICP pulse variability, EMG fatigue.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** AR model equation — 1; Derivation (multiply + expectation) — 2; Yule–Walker matrix form — 1; Estimation steps from data — 2; ICU application — 1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit-conversion error in Windkessel / CO / MAP** — mixing mmHg·s/mL with L/min. **Always** reconcile units before computing.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** in the ICU signal chain — examiners consider this a *core* concept (worth 1.5–2 marks).
> 3. **Confusing AR with ARMA** — AR has only poles; ARMA has both poles and zeros. State order $p$ (poles) and $q$ (zeros) explicitly.
> 4. **Writing Yule–Walker without showing the Toeplitz matrix** — examiners expect a properly structured matrix equation.
> 5. **Wrong Nyquist direction** — writing $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$ (should be $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
> 6. **Neglecting clinical interpretation** of model parameters — losing 2–3 marks on "comment" sub-parts.
> 7. **Confusing the McSharry $z$-equation with a generic sinusoid** — must include the Gaussian basis per wave (P, Q, R, S, T).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, ICU Signals & Models**

- [ ] A modern ICU acquires **ECG, ABP, ICP, EEG, EMG, respiration, SpO₂/PPG, and temperature** continuously.
- [ ] Standard sampling rates: ECG 500 Hz; ABP 125 Hz; EEG 256–512 Hz; Respiration 50 Hz; SpO₂ 50–250 Hz.
- [ ] **Mathematical models** of ICU signals are of two kinds: **deterministic** (ODEs / transfer functions) and **stochastic** (AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA).
- [ ] **Windkessel (2-element)** model: $C \dot P + P/R = Q(t)$. Compliance $C$ smooths pulsatile flow; resistance $R$ sets MAP.
- [ ] **MAP = (P_s + 2 P_d)/3** ≈ R·CO (using consistent units).
- [ ] **CPP = MAP − ICP** is the cerebral perfusion driver; CPP < 50 mmHg is dangerous.
- [ ] **Respiratory (linear RC) model:** $P_{aw} = R_{aw}\dot V + V/C_{rs} + PEEP$. $R_{aw}$ ↑ in asthma; $C_{rs}$ ↓ in ARDS.
- [ ] **ICP (Marmarou) model:** $C_{ic} \dot P_{ic} = I_{in} - (P_{ic} - P_{ss})/R_{out}$.
- [ ] **McSharry/Clifford synthetic ECG** uses a moving dipole on a 2-D limit cycle with 5 Gaussian basis functions.
- [ ] **AR($p$) model:** $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$, $w(n)$ white innovation noise.
- [ ] **Yule–Walker** equations link AR coefficients to autocorrelations: $R_{xx}(\ell) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(\ell - k)$ for $\ell \geq 1$, forming a Toeplitz system solved by Levinson–Durbin.
- [ ] **AR PSD:** $S_{xx}(f) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s} \vert^2$ — *always* use `\vert` for absolute value.
- [ ] **Wiener–Khinchin:** PSD and autocorrelation are Fourier pairs.
- [ ] **HRV indices:** SDNN (overall), RMSSD (parasympathetic), pNN50, LF/HF ratio.
- [ ] **PTT-based BP** uses Moens–Korteweg relationship; shorter PTT = higher BP.
- [ ] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; in practice $f_s \approx 2.5$–$5\times f_{max}$ for ICU monitoring.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing** analog LPF cutoff = $f_s/2$, typically 4th–8th-order Butterworth.
- [ ] **ADC resolution:** 12-bit (standard), 16-bit (low-amplitude EEG/EMG). SNR ≈ 6.02n + 1.76 dB.
- [ ] **Sepsis warning sign:** marked reduction in HRV (low SDNN, suppressed LF/HF).
- [ ] **EEG seizure detection** uses AR(6)–AR(20) on background; pole locations and spectral entropy shift at seizure onset.
- [ ] **Compartment diagrams** represent the body as a network of states with flows — the foundation of all physiological models.
- [ ] **Frequency-band mapping** of ICU spectra: 0–0.5 Hz drift, 0.5–1.5 Hz respiration, 1.5–8 Hz Mayer/HRV-LF, 8–40 Hz EEG/EMG, 40–150 Hz QRS, >150 Hz noise.
- [ ] **Common exam pitfalls:** unit mismatch in Windkessel, missing anti-alias filter, AR vs ARMA confusion, wrong Nyquist direction, missing clinical interpretation.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Examples: Mathematical Models and Signals in Intensive Care Units (PEBMT635)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Models and Cardiovascular Variability Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Models and Cardiovascular Variability Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cardiovascular Variability Signals (CVS)** are stochastic and deterministic time-varying bioelectric, hemodynamic, and mechanical signals that exhibit beat-to-beat fluctuations arising from the closed-loop interaction between the **heart**, the **vasculature**, and the **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)**. The principal member of this family is the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**, from which derived measures such as **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)**, **Blood Pressure Variability (BPV)**, and **Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)** are obtained.

A **Mathematical Model** of these signals is a parametric or non-parametric representation that captures the underlying physiological generator as a system of **differential equations, integral equations, or stochastic processes**, enabling analytical study, simulation, and feature extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The Module-1 mandate requires the student to (i) formulate mathematical models for ECG, HRV, and BPV, (ii) identify signal characteristics (P-QRS-T morphology, RR interval statistics), and (iii) justify the use of linear/non-linear analysis techniques on cardiovascular signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Orchestra of the Heart

Imagine an orchestra without a single conductor:
- The **violins** (sympathetic nerves) play fast, sharp staccato notes whenever you are stressed.
- The **cellos** (parasympathetic / vagus nerve) play slow, long, smooth notes when you are at rest.
- The **heartbeat** is the *loudness* of the room. The constantly shifting balance between violins and cellos is what makes your heart rate *vary* from beat to beat.

**HRV is the audio recording of that balance.** If the recording is rich and complex, your nervous system is healthy. If the recording sounds flat, robotic, or arrhythmic, something is wrong. The *mathematical model* is the score-sheet that lets the engineer transcribe that audio into measurable notes (frequencies, amplitudes, and patterns).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Physiological Value |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Resting Heart Rate | $f_H$ | **60–100 bpm** |
| Mean RR Interval | $\bar{RR}$ | **0.6–1.0 s** |
| Sampling Frequency (clinical ECG) | $f_s$ | **500 Hz** (min **250 Hz**) |
| QRS Complex Duration | $d_{QRS}$ | **0.06–0.10 s** |
| QT Interval (rate corrected) | $QT_c$ | **≤ 0.44 s** |
| Bandwidth of clinical ECG | $BW$ | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** |
| LF Band (sympatho-vagal) | $f_{LF}$ | **0.04–0.15 Hz** |
| HF Band (vagal/respiratory) | $f_{HF}$ | **0.15–0.4 Hz** |
| VLF Band (thermoregulatory) | $f_{VLF}$ | **0.003–0.04 Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **ECG Morphology Recap:** A single cardiac cycle is composed of the **P wave** (atrial depolarization, ~0.08 s), the **QRS complex** (ventricular depolarization, ~0.08–0.10 s), and the **T wave** (ventricular repolarization, ~0.16 s). The **R-peak** is the fiducial point from which RR intervals are derived.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Mathematical Models for Cardiovascular Signals

The modeling hierarchy used in advanced biomedical signal processing can be classified into four major families:

### (A) Event-Based / Integral-Pulse Models
These generate the *event times* (R-peaks) directly from a continuous modulating signal. The most celebrated member is the **Integral Pulse Frequency Modulation (IPFM) Model**.

### (B) Oscillatory / Dynamical-System Models
These treat the cardiac pacemaker as a non-linear oscillator. The **van der Pol oscillator** and the **FitzHugh–Nagumo (FHN) model** are canonical examples. They capture the *regenerative* action-potential shape.

### (C) Stochastic / Time-Series Models
**Autoregressive (AR)**, **ARMA**, and **point-process** models are used to characterize HRV as a coloured stochastic process. The **autoregressive order p** is typically selected by **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)**.

### (D) Electrical-Network Analogs
The **Windkessel models** (2-element, 3-element, 4-element) model the systemic arterial tree as an RLC circuit, capturing pressure–flow dynamics.

## 2.2 The Integral Pulse Frequency Modulation (IPFM) Model

The IPFM model assumes the SA node integrates a slowly-varying *modulating signal* $m(t)$ until an internal threshold $T$ is reached. At the moment of threshold-crossing, an action potential is fired (R-peak), the integrator is reset, and the cycle repeats.

**Operating logic — step by step:**

1. The instantaneous heart-rate signal $m(t)$ represents the reciprocal of the desired RR interval.
2. A *DC offset* of mean rate $\bar{m}$ is added: $x(t) = 1 + m(t)$, so that $x(t) \ge 0$.
3. The integrator output is $\displaystyle \Phi(t) = \int_{t_{k}}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau$, where $t_k$ is the time of the $k^{th}$ R-peak.
4. A trigger is emitted at the smallest $t$ such that $\Phi(t) \ge T$.
5. The integrator resets: $\Phi(t_{k+1}) = 0$ and a new cycle begins.

**Why this works for HRV:** Because the *interval* between R-peaks is determined by how fast $\Phi(t)$ reaches the threshold, any slow fluctuation in $m(t)$ directly produces a slow fluctuation in $RR$ intervals — exactly what is observed physiologically.

## 2.3 The FitzHugh–Nagumo (FHN) Pacemaker Model

The FHN model reduces the 4-variable Hodgkin–Huxley equations to 2 coupled ODEs that retain the essential non-linear dynamics of an excitable membrane:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I_{ext}$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \epsilon (v + \alpha - \beta w)$$

where:
- $v$ = normalized membrane potential (analogous to ECG voltage)
- $w$ = recovery variable (analogous to potassium gating)
- $I_{ext}$ = external stimulus current
- $\alpha, \beta, \epsilon$ = dimensionless shape parameters
- $\epsilon \ll 1$ enforces a slow recovery relative to depolarization

> [!TIP]
> The FHN model is the standard KTU answer for "Explain the non-linear oscillator model for cardiac pacemaker action potential." The student is expected to identify the **nullclines** $w = v - v^{3}/3 + I_{ext}$ and $w = (v+\alpha)/\beta$, whose intersection defines the limit cycle of self-sustained oscillation.

## 2.4 Windkessel Models (Vascular Analogs)

| Model | Elements | Governing ODE | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-Element WK** | $C$, $R$ | $Q_{in} = C \frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R}$ | Arterial compliance + peripheral resistance |
| **3-Element WK** | $C$, $R$, $Z_c$ | Adds aortic characteristic impedance $Z_c$ | Includes aortic inflow inertia |
| **4-Element WK** | $C$, $R$, $Z_c$, $L$ | Adds blood inertia $L$ | Full arterial tree analogy |

where $P(t)$ is the aortic pressure and $Q_{in}(t)$ is the cardiac input flow.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet — HRV Analysis

| # | Domain | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time | **SDNN** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(RR_k - \bar{RR})^{2}}$ | ms; overall HRV |
| 2 | Time | **RMSSD** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}(RR_{k+1}-RR_k)^{2}}$ | ms; vagal tone |
| 3 | Time | **pNN50** | $\dfrac{\#\{\vert RR_{k+1}-RR_k\vert > 50\,\text{ms}\}}{N-1}\times 100$ | %; short-term variability |
| 4 | Time | **NN50** | $\#\{\vert RR_{k+1}-RR_k\vert > 50\,\text{ms}\}$ | count; raw vagal index |
| 5 | Freq | **Total Power (TP)** | $\displaystyle \int_{0.003}^{0.4} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms²; full 24-hr HRV |
| 6 | Freq | **VLF Power** | $\displaystyle \int_{0.003}^{0.04} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms²; thermoregulation, renin–angiotensin |
| 7 | Freq | **LF Power** | $\displaystyle \int_{0.04}^{0.15} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms²; sympathetic + baroreflex |
| 8 | Freq | **HF Power** | $\displaystyle \int_{0.15}^{0.4} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms²; parasympathetic / RSA |
| 9 | Freq | **LF/HF Ratio** | $\dfrac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}}$ | dimensionless; sympathovagal balance |
| 10 | Freq | **PSD (Welch)** | $\hat{S}_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot f_s}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\vert X_k(f)\vert^{2}$ | ms²/Hz; averaged periodogram |
| 11 | Non-lin | **SD1 (Poincaré)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(RR_{k+1}-RR_k)}$ | ms; short-term variability |
| 12 | Non-lin | **SD2 (Poincaré)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(RR_{k+1}+RR_k)}$ | ms; long-term variability |
| 13 | Non-lin | **Sample Entropy** | $SampEn(m,r,N) = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A}{B}\right)$ | dimensionless; complexity |
| 14 | AR | **Yule–Walker** | $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$ with $a_{p,p} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(p-k)$ | AR(p) coefficient vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization Note:** The definitions in rows 1–9 follow the **Task Force of the European Society of Cardiology and the North American Society of Pacing and Electrophysiology (1996)** guidelines — the universally accepted KTU reference. Always cite this guideline in your exam answer.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Clinical diagnostics:** Reduced SDNN (<50 ms in 24 h) is an independent predictor of post-MI mortality.
- **Stress & workload monitoring:** LF/HF ratio rises in occupational stress and falls in meditative states.
- **Drug safety pharmacology:** QT variability and HRV are FDA-mandated cardiac safety biomarkers.
- **Wearable design:** Real-time IPFM modelling allows on-chip R-peak prediction for pacemakers and Holter monitors.
- **Machine-learning features:** SD1, SD2, RMSSD and LF/HF form the standard feature vector for arrhythmia classifiers (AF, PVC, VT).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IPFM Model — R-Peak Time Statistics

**Given:** Modulating signal $m(t)$ with mean $\bar{m} = 0$ and band-limited to $W$ Hz, and a constant threshold $T$.

**To show:** The instantaneous heart rate $f_H(t) = \frac{1}{T}[1 + m(t)]$ when the IPFM operates in the *modulating regime* (i.e., $m(t)$ varies slowly relative to the period $T$).

**Step 1 — Define the integrator output between two successive R-peaks:**

Between the $k^{th}$ R-peak at $t_k$ and the $(k+1)^{th}$ at $t_{k+1}$, the integrator accumulates the modulating signal:

$$\Phi(t) = \int_{t_{k}}^{t} \big[1 + m(\tau)\big]\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Apply the threshold condition at the next firing:**

An R-peak is emitted at the earliest $t > t_k$ such that $\Phi(t) = T$:

$$\int_{t_{k}}^{t_{k+1}} \big[1 + m(\tau)\big]\, d\tau = T$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral into its mean and fluctuating parts:**

Using the additive decomposition $1 + m(\tau)$, we obtain:

$$(t_{k+1} - t_k) + \int_{t_{k}}^{t_{k+1}} m(\tau)\, d\tau = T$$

Let $T_k = t_{k+1} - t_k$ be the *k-th* RR interval. Hence:

$$T_k = T - \int_{t_{k}}^{t_{k+1}} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Apply the slowly-varying approximation (modulating regime):**

If $m(t)$ is approximately constant over the small interval $T_k \approx T$, then the integral evaluates as:

$$\int_{t_{k}}^{t_{k+1}} m(\tau)\, d\tau \approx m(t_k)\, T_k$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_k$ by first-order approximation:**

Substituting and rearranging:

$$T_k = T - m(t_k)\,T_k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_k\big[1 + m(t_k)\big] = T$$

Therefore:

$$T_k = \frac{T}{1 + m(t_k)} \approx T\big[1 - m(t_k)\big]$$

where the last step uses the first-order Taylor expansion $\dfrac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$ for $\vert x\vert \ll 1$.

**Step 6 — Express the instantaneous heart rate:**

The instantaneous heart rate is $f_H(t_k) = 1/T_k$:

$$f_H(t_k) = \frac{1}{T}\big[1 + m(t_k)\big]$$

**Conclusion:** Under the modulating regime, the **IPFM model is mathematically equivalent to an instantaneous frequency-modulation scheme**, where the slow modulating signal $m(t)$ is linearly mapped onto the instantaneous heart rate. This is the cornerstone of all HRV spectral-analysis derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a *guaranteed* 7-mark answer in any KTU 14-marker on "Explain the IPFM model of HRV generation."

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Yule–Walker Equations for AR(p) HRV Model

**Goal:** Express the AR(p) coefficients $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p\}$ of an HRV model in terms of the autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(k)$.

**Step 1 — Define the AR(p) process:**

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + w[n]$$

where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is the white-noise innovation. The sign convention follows the KTU board-exam standard (so that the power spectrum is well-defined).

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $x[n-m]$ and take expectation:**

$$E\{x[n]\,x[n-m]\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, E\{x[n-k]\,x[n-m]\} + E\{w[n]\,x[n-m]\}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the terms as autocorrelations:**

Define $r_{xx}(m) = E\{x[n]\,x[n-m]\}$. Since $w[n]$ is independent of past $x$, the last term vanishes for $m > 0$:

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k), \quad m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the variance identity for $m = 0$:**

For $m = 0$, the term $E\{w[n]\,x[n]\} = \sigma_w^2$ is retained:

$$r_{xx}(0) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(-k) + \sigma_w^2$$

Since autocorrelation is even ($r_{xx}(-k) = r_{xx}(k)$):

$$r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(k) = \sigma_w^2$$

**Step 5 — Write the system in matrix form (Yule–Walker form):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{xx}(p-1) & r_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ r_{xx}(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve by the Levinson–Durbin recursion** (O($p^2$) complexity) to obtain the AR coefficients and $\sigma_w^2$ in one forward pass.

**Step 7 — Compute the AR Power Spectral Density:**

The AR(p) spectrum is:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^{2}}{\big\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k / f_s}\big\vert^{2}}$$

This is the closed-form expression used to estimate LF, HF, and VLF powers in HRV analysis by integrating $S_{xx}(f)$ over the standard bands.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Poincaré SD1 and SD2

**Given:** Five successive RR intervals (in ms): $RR = [800, 820, 790, 830, 810]$.

**Step 1 — Form successive-difference vector $\Delta RR_k$ and sum vector $\Sigma RR_k$:**

$$\Delta RR = [20, -30, 40, -20]$$

$$\Sigma RR = [1620, 1610, 1620, 1640]$$

**Step 2 — Compute sample variances:**

$$\text{Var}(\Delta RR) = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum (\Delta RR_k - \overline{\Delta RR})^{2} = \frac{1}{3}\big[(20-2.5)^2 + (-30-2.5)^2 + (40-2.5)^2 + (-20-2.5)^2\big] = 933.33$$

$$\text{Var}(\Sigma RR) = \frac{1}{3}\big[(1620-1622.5)^2 + (1610-1622.5)^2 + (1620-1622.5)^2 + (1640-1622.5)^2\big] = 158.33$$

**Step 3 — Apply SD1 and SD2 definitions:**

$$SD1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(\Delta RR)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{933.33} \approx 21.60 \text{ ms}$$

$$SD2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(\Sigma RR)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{158.33} \approx 8.90 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

$SD1 \gg SD2$ indicates a heart that is responding rapidly to short-term respiratory modulation (high vagal tone), with little drift in the long-term mean. A healthy young adult at rest typically shows $SD1/SD2 \approx 1.5$–$2.5$.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 14-marker favourite: *"Given the RR tachogram, compute SD1, SD2, RMSSD and interpret the autonomic state."*

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List any four time-domain measures of HRV with their physiological significance.**

**Model Answer:**
HRV is the beat-to-beat variation in the time interval between successive R-peaks of the QRS complex in the ECG, reflecting the dynamic interplay of the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches of the autonomic nervous system.

Four time-domain measures:
1. **SDNN** — Standard deviation of NN intervals. Reflects overall (circadian) HRV.
2. **RMSSD** — Root mean square of successive NN differences. Reflects short-term vagal (parasympathetic) tone.
3. **pNN50** — Percentage of successive NN differences exceeding 50 ms. Strongly vagally mediated.
4. **NN50** — Count of successive NN differences exceeding 50 ms. Raw vagal index.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing with significance.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Explain the physiological origin of the LF and HF components of the HRV power spectrum.**

**Model Answer:**
- **LF band (0.04 – 0.15 Hz):** Primarily associated with the **baroreflex-mediated sympathetic modulation** of the SA node, although it also contains a parasympathetic contribution. It is enhanced during orthostatic stress, mental load, and moderate exercise.
- **HF band (0.15 – 0.4 Hz):** Almost entirely **parasympathetically (vagally) mediated** and synchronous with the respiratory cycle, producing the well-known **Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)**. It is abolished by atropine and enhanced by slow deep breathing.

The **LF/HF ratio** is widely used as an index of **sympathovagal balance**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Failing to *state the threshold-crossing condition* explicitly when describing the IPFM model → loses 2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **PSD units** (ms²/Hz) with normalized units (n.u.) and mixing them in the same calculation → loses 2 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to apply the **1/√2** factor in SD1/SD2 of the Poincaré plot → loses 2 marks.
> 4. Writing the **Yule–Walker equation** without specifying the *sign convention* (some books use $x[n] = \sum a_k x[n-k]$, others use $x[n] = -\sum a_k x[n-k]$) → examiner deducts 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 5. Confusing the **autoregressive (AR)** model of HRV with the **autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA)** model — ARIMA is rarely used for HRV; AR is the KTU board standard.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Integral Pulse Frequency Modulation (IPFM) model** for the generation of cardiac R-R intervals from a modulating signal. Derive the relationship between the modulating signal $m(t)$ and the instantaneous heart rate in the slowly-varying (modulating) regime. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following six successive R-R intervals (in ms) have been recorded from a resting subject:

$$RR = \{850, 870, 840, 890, 860, 880\}$$

Compute the **SDNN**, **RMSSD**, and the **SD1** of the Poincaré plot. Comment on the autonomic state of the subject. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Block diagram description:** State that the IPFM model has a summing junction, an integrator, a threshold comparator, and a reset feedback path. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Define variables:** Let $m(t)$ be the modulating signal with mean zero, let $T$ be the threshold, and let $t_k$ be the time of the $k^{th}$ R-peak. The integrator output between $t_k$ and $t_{k+1}$ is:

$$\Phi(t) = \int_{t_k}^{t} \big[1 + m(\tau)\big]\, d\tau \quad (1)$$

**Step 3 — Threshold-crossing condition:** The $(k+1)^{th}$ R-peak occurs at the smallest $t > t_k$ such that $\Phi(t) = T$, yielding:

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} \big[1 + m(\tau)\big]\, d\tau = T \quad (2)$$

**Step 4 — Slowly-varying approximation:** Assuming $m(\tau) \approx m(t_k)$ over the short interval $T_k = t_{k+1} - t_k$:

$$T_k \big[1 + m(t_k)\big] = T \quad (3)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for instantaneous heart rate:** The instantaneous heart rate is $f_H(t_k) = 1/T_k$, giving:

$$f_H(t_k) = \frac{1}{T}\big[1 + m(t_k)\big] \quad (4)$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:** The IPFM model is equivalent to instantaneous **frequency modulation** in the slowly-varying regime, with the modulating signal linearly proportional to the heart-rate fluctuation about its mean $1/T$. *(1 mark for conclusion + 1 mark for diagram)*

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean RR:**

$$\bar{RR} = \frac{850+870+840+890+860+880}{6} = \frac{5190}{6} = 865 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute SDNN:**

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(RR_k - \bar{RR})^{2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\big[(-15)^2 + (5)^2 + (-25)^2 + (25)^2 + (-5)^2 + (15)^2\big]}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}(225 + 25 + 625 + 625 + 25 + 225)} = \sqrt{\frac{1750}{5}} = \sqrt{350} \approx 18.71 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute successive differences $\Delta RR_k = RR_{k+1} - RR_k$:**

$$\Delta RR = \{20, -30, 50, -30, 20\}$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSSD:**

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\Delta RR_k^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}(400 + 900 + 2500 + 900 + 400)} = \sqrt{1020} \approx 31.94 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Compute SD1 of the Poincaré plot:**

$$\text{Var}(\Delta RR) = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum \Delta RR_k^2 = \frac{5100}{5} = 1020$$

$$SD1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(\Delta RR)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{1020} \approx 22.58 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Clinical interpretation:**

The RMSSD (~32 ms) is well within the typical adult range (15–40 ms at rest), and the SD1 value (~22.6 ms) reflects strong short-term vagal modulation. The LF/HF ratio (not computed here) should ideally fall between 1 and 2 for a healthy resting subject. The subject is in a **parasympathetically dominant resting state** with healthy autonomic function.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 — Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** for an AR(p) model used to characterize HRV. State clearly the sign convention and the resulting closed-form expression for the AR power spectral density. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **FitzHugh–Nagumo (FHN) model** as a non-linear oscillator representation of the cardiac action potential. Sketch the phase-plane portrait and identify the **nullclines** and the **limit cycle**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the AR(p) process (sign convention: negative coefficients):**

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + w[n] \quad (1)$$

where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is white noise. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Multiply by $x[n-m]$ and take expectation:**

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k) + E\{w[n]\,x[n-m]\} \quad (2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply causality:** For $m \ge 1$, the noise term is independent of past $x$, so it vanishes:

$$r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k) = 0, \quad m = 1, \dots, p \quad (3)$$

**Step 4 — Handle the $m = 0$ case:** Now $E\{w[n]\,x[n]\} = \sigma_w^2$:

$$r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(k) = \sigma_w^2 \quad (4)$$

**Step 5 — Write the matrix form (Yule–Walker):**

$$\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r} \quad (5)$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is the $p \times p$ symmetric Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix. *(2 marks)*

**Step 6 — Derive the AR power spectral density:**

$$S_{xx}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^{2}}{\big\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k / f_s}\big\vert^{2}} \quad (6)$$

This is obtained by taking the $Z$-transform of (1) and computing $S_{xx}(f) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert A(f)\vert^2$. *(1 mark)*

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — State the FHN system:**

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I_{ext} \quad (7)$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \epsilon (v + \alpha - \beta w) \quad (8)$$

where $v$ is the fast variable (membrane potential analog), $w$ is the slow recovery variable, $\epsilon \ll 1$, and $\alpha, \beta$ are dimensionless parameters controlling the limit cycle. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Nullclines:** Setting $\dot v = 0$ and $\dot w = 0$:

$$w\text{-nullcline: } w = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} + I_{ext} \quad (9)$$

$$v\text{-nullcline: } w = \frac{v + \alpha}{\beta} \quad (10)$$

The intersection of these two curves is the fixed point of the system. For $\epsilon \ll 1$, the trajectory moves rapidly along the $v$-nullcline and slowly along the $w$-nullcline, producing a **closed limit cycle** in the phase plane. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Phase-plane description:** The cardiac action potential maps onto the phase plane as follows:
- **Resting phase:** bottom-left of the limit cycle (diastolic $v \approx -1$).
- **Upstroke (phase 0):** rapid rightward motion (depolarization).
- **Plateau (phase 2):** slow upper portion of the limit cycle.
- **Repolarization (phase 3):** rapid leftward motion back to rest.
- **Spontaneous diastolic depolarization (phase 4):** slow upward drift of $v$ in pacemaker cells, producing self-sustained oscillation. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Physiological significance:** The FHN model captures the **refractory period** (trajectory cannot be re-excited while traversing the upper branch) and explains why premature stimuli below a threshold fail to elicit a new action potential — the basis of arrhythmogenesis in Brugada syndrome and re-entry. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IPFM model** — converts a continuous modulating signal into discrete R-peak events via a threshold-crossing integrator; equivalent to instantaneous FM in the slowly-varying regime.
- **Cardiovascular variability** = ECG + derived HRV + BPV + RSA; the **ANS** is the controlling input.
- **ECG morphology:** P (atrial depolarization) → QRS (ventricular depolarization) → T (ventricular repolarization); the **R-peak** is the fiducial point.
- **Time-domain HRV:** SDNN (overall), RMSSD, pNN50, NN50 (all vagal-sensitive except SDNN).
- **Frequency-domain HRV:** VLF (0.003–0.04 Hz), LF (0.04–0.15 Hz, sympathetic + baroreflex), HF (0.15–0.4 Hz, vagal/RSA); **LF/HF ratio** = sympathovagal balance.
- **Non-linear HRV:** Poincaré SD1 (short-term), SD2 (long-term), Sample Entropy (complexity), DFA (long-range correlation).
- **Yule–Walker equations** — Toeplitz system $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$ for AR(p) coefficients; solved efficiently by **Levinson–Durbin recursion**.
- **AR(p) PSD formula:** $S_{xx}(f) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k/f_s}\vert^2$.
- **FitzHugh–Nagumo model** — 2-variable non-linear oscillator with $v$ (fast, voltage) and $w$ (slow, recovery); the **limit cycle** in the $(v, w)$ plane represents the cardiac action potential.
- **Windkessel models** — RLC analog of the arterial tree; 2-element (R, C), 3-element (adds $Z_c$), 4-element (adds $L$).
- **Units to remember:** RR intervals in **ms**, HRV power in **ms²**, PSD in **ms²/Hz**, frequencies in **Hz**, angles in **radians**.
- **Standard guidelines:** The **1996 ESC/NASPE Task Force** definitions are the universally cited KTU reference for HRV.
- **Sign convention for AR models:** Use $x[n] = -\sum a_k x[n-k] + w[n]$ — this is the KTU board standard, not $x[n] = \sum a_k x[n-k] + w[n]$.
- **PSD methods for HRV:** (i) Welch's averaged periodogram (non-parametric), (ii) AR model (parametric), (iii) Lomb–Scargle periodogram (for unevenly-sampled tachograms).
- **LF/HF clinical ranges:** Healthy resting adult = **1–2**; dominant sympathetic stress > **4**; dominant parasympathetic (deep breathing, athletes) < **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"If your answer does not contain (a) an equation, (b) a numerical value, and (c) a physiological interpretation, you have not earned full marks on a 14-mark KTU cardiovascular signal question."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Models and EEG Signals during Epilepsy
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Models and EEG Signals during Epilepsy

# Mathematical Models and EEG Signals during Epilepsy

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mathematical modeling of EEG during epilepsy** refers to the use of deterministic, stochastic, and nonlinear dynamical system equations to represent the electrophysiological behavior of neuronal populations that generate characteristic electroencephalographic patterns (spikes, sharp waves, spike-and-slow-wave complexes) observed during ictal (seizure) and interictal (between-seizure) states. These models span the hierarchy from single-neuron conductance-based formulations (Hodgkin–Huxley paradigm) to lumped-parameter neural mass models (Jansen–Rit, Wendling) and mesoscopic continuum field models (Liley).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The course objective is to enable students to *mathematically characterize* pathological EEG dynamics and link biophysical parameters (synaptic gains, time constants, connectivity) to observable waveform morphology — particularly the **spike-wave discharge (SWD)** at **3 Hz** typical of absence seizures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of the brain as a **vast stadium of people** trying to clap in unison.

- **Normal wakeful EEG** = everyone claps randomly — a noisy, low-amplitude "babble."
- **Pre-seizure (preictal)** = small clusters begin synchronizing — you hear local rhythmic claps growing in pockets.
- **Ictal (seizure)** = the entire stadium suddenly claps together at a single rhythm — a massive, periodic "thunder."
- **Postictal** = exhaustion, suppression — almost silence.

A **mathematical model** is the *rulebook* that predicts when small synchronization can snowball into global rhythm. It tells us: *given a certain excitability and connectivity, will the stadium tip into synchrony, and at what frequency?* The standard metrics used are the **correlation dimension (D₂)**, the **largest Lyapunov exponent (λ₁)**, and the **approximate entropy (ApEn)**, each of which quantifies the order/complexity of the rhythm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Note:**
> - EEG amplitude: **10–100 µV** (scalp); **0.5–5 mV** (intracranial)
> - Normal dominant rhythm (α): **8–13 Hz**
> - Absence seizure spike-wave: **3 Hz**
> - High Frequency Oscillations (HFOs, ripples): **80–250 Hz**
> - High Frequency Oscillations (fast ripples): **250–500 Hz**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Mathematical Models for Epileptic EEG

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes a **three-tier model hierarchy**:

**Tier-1: Microscopic (Single Neuron)**
- **Hodgkin–Huxley (HH):** 4 coupled ODEs for membrane potential, sodium, potassium activation/inactivation gates.
- **FitzHugh–Nagumo (FHN):** 2-variable reduction of HH, captures excitation–recovery.
- **Morris–Lecar:** Calcium-dominated neuron.

**Tier-2: Mesoscopic (Neural Mass / Population)**
- **Lopes da Silva model:** Two coupled populations (excitatory pyramidal + inhibitory interneuron) with second-order linear dynamics + sigmoid nonlinearity.
- **Jansen–Rit (JR) model:** Three sub-populations (pyramidal, excitatory stellate, inhibitory interneuron) — generates spontaneous alpha-like rhythms.
- **Wendling–Chauvel model:** Extension of JR with **fast and slow inhibitory interneurons** — reproduces normal, sporadic spikes, SWDs, and slow rhythmic discharges.
- **Liley model:** Spatial continuum with cortico-cortical connectivity — predicts epileptiform traveling waves.

**Tier-3: Macroscopic (Field / Continuum)**
- **Liley's mean-field model** — used to simulate spontaneous EEG spectra and seizure transitions.
- **Neural Field Equations (Wilson–Cowan type):** Integral-differential equations over cortical surface.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Wendling–Chauvel Pipeline

- **Why it matters for epilepsy:** Wendling's model is the only neural-mass model that can reproduce the **full morphological spectrum** of intracerebral EEG signals recorded in temporal-lobe epilepsy patients.
- **How it works:**
  1. Four populations: pyramidal (P), excitatory (E), fast inhibitory (Gf), slow inhibitory (Gs).
  2. Each population is described by a 2nd-order linear transfer function converting average presynaptic input to average postsynaptic membrane potential.
  3. The output passes through a **static sigmoid** nonlinearity $S(v)$ to model average firing rate.
  4. Coupling gains $A, B, G$ and time constants $1/a, 1/b$ are adjusted to flip the system into epileptiform regimes.

### 2.3 The Jansen–Rit Differential System (Canonical)

The pyramidal population receives both excitatory and inhibitory feedback, producing an intrinsic alpha-band limit cycle:

$$\ddot{y}_0(t) + 2a\dot{y}_0(t) + a^2 y_0(t) = Aa\,S(y_1(t) - y_2(t))$$

$$\ddot{y}_1(t) + 2a\dot{y}_1(t) + a^2 y_1(t) = Aa\,p(t)$$

$$\ddot{y}_2(t) + 2b\dot{y}_2(t) + b^2 y_2(t) = Bb\,S(y_0(t))$$

where $y_0$ = pyramidal output (the modeled EEG), $y_1$ = excitatory input, $y_2$ = inhibitory input.

**Sigmoid (static) nonlinearity:**

$$S(v) = \frac{2e_0}{1 + e^{r(v_0 - v)}}$$

> **Why a sigmoid?** Real neurons fire faster as their membrane depolarizes, but the rate saturates — captured exactly by the logistic shape.

### 2.4 Wendling–Chauvel Extension — The Epilepsy Generator

Adding two inhibitory interneurons (fast and slow):

$$\ddot{y}_0 + 2a\dot{y}_0 + a^2 y_0 = Aa\,S(y_1 - y_2 - y_3)$$

$$\ddot{y}_1 + 2a\dot{y}_1 + a^2 y_1 = Aa\,S(y_0)$$

$$\ddot{y}_2 + 2b\dot{y}_2 + b^2 y_2 = Bb\,S(C_2 y_0)$$

$$\ddot{y}_3 + 2g\dot{y}_3 + g^2 y_3 = Gg\,S(C_4 y_0)$$

with $g \gg b$ for the fast inhibitory loop. This allows a single parameter (e.g., $B$, slow inhibitory gain) to drive the system through **Type I normal → sporadic spikes → SWD → sustained discharge**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid firing rate | $S(v)$ | $\dfrac{2e_0}{1 + e^{r(v_0-v)}}$ | $e_0 = 2.5$ Hz; $v_0 = 6$ mV; $r = 0.56$ mV⁻¹ |
| Excitatory time constant | $1/a$ | $1/a$ | $a = 100$ s⁻¹ (i.e., 10 ms) |
| Slow inhibitory time const. | $1/b$ | $1/b$ | $b = 50$ s⁻¹ (20 ms) |
| Fast inhibitory time const. | $1/g$ | $1/g$ | $g = 500$ s⁻¹ (2 ms) |
| Average synaptic gain | $A, B, G$ | Biophysical constants | $A = 3.25$ mV, $B = 22$ mV, $G = 10$ mV |
| Correlation dimension | $D_2$ | $\displaystyle D_2 = \lim_{r\to 0}\frac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$ | Normal ≈ 6; ictal ≈ 2 |
| Largest Lyapunov exp. | $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_1 = \lim_{t\to\infty}\tfrac{1}{t}\ln\!\big(\tfrac{\vert\delta(t)\vert}{\vert\delta(0)\vert}\big)$ | $\lambda_1 > 0$ → chaotic |
| Approximate entropy | $ApEn(m,r,N)$ | Pattern regularity in time series | $\downarrow$ during seizure |
| Hurst exponent | $H$ | Self-similarity of long-range dep. | $H > 0.5$ persistent |
| Hurst exponent (fBm) | $H$ | $H = \frac{E(L)-E(L_0)}{E(L_0)}$ | — |
| Spike-wave frequency | $f_{SWD}$ | $f_{SWD} \approx 3$ Hz | Absence epilepsy |
| HFO band | $f_{HFO}$ | $f_{HFO} = 80$–$500$ Hz | Pathological |
| Hjorth activity | $A$ | $A = \sigma_x^2$ | Power |
| Hjorth mobility | $M$ | $M = \sigma_{x'}/\sigma_x$ | Mean frequency |
| Hjorth complexity | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{\sigma_{x''}/\sigma_{x'}}{\sigma_{x'}/\sigma_x}$ | Frequency change |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drug discovery:** Virtual pharmacological experiments on neural-mass models reduce animal trials.
- **Closed-loop neurostimulation (RNS, DBS):** Real-time feature extraction (line length, band power) triggers stimulation; the same features are predicted by Wendling/JR models.
- **Epileptogenic zone localization:** Source localization + mathematical-model inversion of interictal spikes identifies seizure onset.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Nonlinear features derived from these models classify mental states.
- **ICU monitoring:** Automated seizure detection in CEEG (continuous EEG) uses correlation dimension drop as a marker.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sigmoid Output and Linear Filter Convolution in Neural Mass Models

We derive the canonical second-order **postsynaptic potential (PSP) kernel** used in Jansen–Rit and Wendling models.

**Step 1 — Start from the synaptic current impulse response.**

A population's average response to a unit Dirac-impulse input of synaptic current is modelled as the convolution of an exponential rise and decay (alpha function):

$$h(t) = H \cdot t \cdot e^{-at}\,u(t)$$

where $H$ is the average synaptic gain, $a$ is the inverse membrane time constant, and $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace transform to obtain the transfer function.**

$$H(s) = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\} = \frac{H}{(s+a)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the inverse Laplace transform written as a 2nd-order ODE in time domain.**

Multiplying both sides by $(s+a)^2$ and using the differentiation property $\mathcal{L}\{\ddot{y}\} = s^2 Y - sy(0) - \dot{y}(0)$:

$$\ddot{y}(t) + 2a\dot{y}(t) + a^2 y(t) = H \cdot a \cdot S(\text{input})$$

The factor $2a$ emerges because the alpha function $t e^{-at}$ corresponds to the response of a *second-order* system with **double pole at $-a$** and zero initial conditions. The constant $a$ on the RHS comes from normalization: $\int_0^\infty t e^{-at}\,dt = 1/a^2$, and we extract $a$ for the standard Jansen–Rit normalization.

> *Logic note:* A first-order exponential decay $e^{-at}$ would have a single pole, but the alpha function has a *delayed peak*, requiring a repeated root.

**Step 4 — Substitute sigmoid $S(v) = \dfrac{2e_0}{1+e^{r(v_0-v)}}$ as the static nonlinearity** that converts PSP amplitude to population firing rate.

$$S(v) = \frac{2e_0}{1 + e^{r(v_0-v)}}$$

**Step 5 — Write the closed Jansen–Rit loop.**

The pyramidal population output $y_0$ drives both excitatory and inhibitory populations:

$$y_0(t) \;\longmapsto\; S(y_0) \;\longmapsto\; y_1 \;(\text{excitatory PSP})$$

$$y_0(t) \;\longmapsto\; S(C_2 y_0) \;\longmapsto\; y_2 \;(\text{inhibitory PSP})$$

**Step 6 — Final coupled system (Jansen–Rit) is then:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ddot{y}_0 + 2a\dot{y}_0 + a^2 y_0 &= Aa\,S(y_1 - y_2) \\
\ddot{y}_1 + 2a\dot{y}_1 + a^2 y_1 &= Aa\,p(t) \\
\ddot{y}_2 + 2b\dot{y}_2 + b^2 y_2 &= Bb\,S(y_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Check units.** $y_0, y_1, y_2$ are membrane potentials [mV]; $S$ outputs firing rates [Hz]; $Aa, Bb$ have units [mV·Hz·s⁻¹] which become [mV] after multiplication by a dimensionless time integral. Self-consistency holds because $S$ has [Hz] but is interpreted as a rate that, when convolved with the alpha kernel of area $1/a^2$, gives voltage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Correlation Dimension (Grassberger–Procaccia Algorithm)

The **correlation dimension $D_2$** characterizes the *dimensionality of the attractor* in phase space — a powerful marker of seizure (where $D_2$ collapses from ~6 to ~2).

**Step 1 — Reconstruct the phase space using delay embedding (Takens' theorem).**

Given a scalar EEG time series $x(1), x(2), \dots, x(N)$, form vectors:

$$\vec{x}(i) = \big[x(i), x(i+\tau), x(i+2\tau), \dots, x(i+(m-1)\tau)\big]$$

where $\tau$ is the **embedding delay** (first minimum of the average mutual information) and $m$ is the embedding dimension.

**Step 2 — Compute the correlation sum $C(r, m)$.**

For a chosen threshold $r$, count the fraction of pairs within distance $r$:

$$C(r, m) = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \Theta\!\big(r - \Vert \vec{x}(i) - \vec{x}(j) \Vert\big)$$

where $\Theta(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function.

**Step 3 — Take the limit (definition of correlation dimension).**

$$D_2 = \lim_{r \to 0} \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\log C(r, m)}{\log r}$$

**Step 4 — Practical estimation.**

Choose a scaling region $r_1 < r < r_2$ in the $\log C(r)$ vs $\log r$ plot. Slope in that region = $D_2(m)$. Then increase $m$ until $D_2$ saturates — the saturated value is the **estimated correlation dimension**.

**Step 5 — Interpretation for epilepsy:**

- Normal EEG: $D_2 \approx 6$–$8$ (high complexity, many "active" degrees of freedom).
- Ictal (seizure) EEG: $D_2 \approx 2$ (low complexity, near-periodic limit cycle).
- **Diagnostic value:** $D_2$ drop is a robust seizure onset detector.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Largest Lyapunov Exponent (Wolf Algorithm Concept)

The **LLE $\lambda_1$** measures the rate of divergence of nearby trajectories. Positive $\lambda_1$ ⇒ chaos.
