## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle as applicable to time-frequency analysis of biomedical signals.**

**Model Answer:**
The Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle states that for any finite-energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier transform $X(f)$, the product of its RMS time duration $\sigma_t$ and RMS frequency bandwidth $\sigma_f$ is bounded below:

$$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \;\geq\; \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

This means that a signal cannot be localized with arbitrarily high resolution in *both* time and frequency simultaneously. The Gaussian function $x(t) = A\, e^{-\alpha t^2}$ is the unique signal that achieves equality and is therefore the optimal kernel for time-frequency concentration. In biomedical signal processing, this principle forces a trade-off: a short window gives good time resolution (needed for sharp QRS detection) but poor frequency resolution, while a long window gives the opposite.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between uniform tiling of STFT and adaptive tiling of the Wavelet Transform in terms of time-frequency resolution.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | STFT | Wavelet (CWT) |
|---------|------|---------------|
| Window | Fixed in size | Variable (scales with frequency) |
| Tiling | Uniform rectangles | Adaptive — narrow at high $f$, tall at low $f$ |
| Time resolution | Constant | Better at high frequencies |
| Frequency resolution | Constant | Better at low frequencies |
| Best for | Quasi-stationary signals | Non-stationary signals with transients |
| Example | Steady-state EEG alpha rhythm | ECG QRS complex, EEG spikes |

The STFT provides uniform resolution throughout the time-frequency plane because it uses a fixed window $w(t-\tau)$. The CWT overcomes this by using scaled wavelets $\psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$, achieving logarithmic tiling that is naturally suited for signals with a wide dynamic range of frequencies.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the Heisenberg–Gabor uncertainty principle for a finite-energy signal. Show clearly all intermediate steps and state the equality condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Gaussian window $w(t) = \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/4} e^{-\alpha t^2/2}$ used in STFT analysis of an EEG signal, compute (i) the RMS duration $\sigma_t$, (ii) the RMS bandwidth $\sigma_f$, and (iii) the time-bandwidth product. Verify the uncertainty bound is satisfied. Take $\alpha = 100$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The proof proceeds in five steps (full expansion shown in Section 3.1):

**Step 1 — Definitions.** For an energy-normalized signal $x(t)$ centered so that the mean time $\mu_t = 0$, define:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \int t^2\, \vert x(t)\vert^2\, dt, \quad \sigma_f^2 = \int f^2\, \vert X(f)\vert^2\, df$$

**Step 2 — Quadratic in $\alpha$.** Consider the non-negative quantity:

$$\int \bigl\vert \alpha\, t\, x(t) + x'(t) \bigr\vert^2 dt \;\geq\; 0$$

Expanding and applying Parseval's theorem ($\int \vert x'(t)\vert^2 dt = (2\pi)^2 \sigma_f^2$):

$$\alpha^2 \sigma_t^2 \;-\; 2\alpha \mu_t \;+\; (2\pi)^2 \sigma_f^2 \;\geq\; 0$$

**Step 3 — Discriminant condition.** A non-negative quadratic has discriminant $\leq 0$:

$$4\mu_t^2 - 4\sigma_t^2(2\pi)^2 \sigma_f^2 \leq 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sigma_t^2 \sigma_f^2 \geq \frac{\mu_t^2}{4\pi^2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification.** Setting $\mu_t = 0$:

$$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Equality condition.** Equality holds iff the quadratic has a double root, i.e., $\alpha\, t\, x(t) + x'(t) = 0$, whose solution is $x(t) = A\, e^{-\alpha t^2/2}$ (Gaussian). ∎

**(b) Gaussian window computation (7 marks):**

For $w(t) = \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/4} e^{-\alpha t^2/2}$ with $\alpha = 100$:

**(i) RMS duration:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/2} e^{-\alpha t^2}\, dt = \frac{1}{2\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\alpha}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{200}} \approx 0.0707\,\text{s} = 70.7\,\text{ms}}$$

**(ii) RMS bandwidth:** Fourier transform is also Gaussian. Using Parseval:

$$\sigma_f^2 = \int f^2\, \vert W(f)\vert^2 df = \frac{\alpha}{8\pi^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha}}{2\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{2\pi\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.128\,\text{Hz}}$$

**(iii) Time-bandwidth product:**

$$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 100}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{100}}{2\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{4\pi} \approx 0.0796 \;\checkmark$$

The bound $\sigma_t \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ is satisfied with equality, confirming that the Gaussian window achieves optimal joint time-frequency localization — ideal for analyzing EEG rhythms around 1 Hz (delta) where high resolution in both domains is critical.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for computing the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of a non-stationary biomedical signal. Discuss how window size affects time-frequency resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG signal sampled at $F_s = 500\,\text{Hz}$ is analyzed using STFT with a Hamming window of length $N = 128$ samples and a hop size of $H = 32$ samples. Calculate: (i) time resolution, (ii) frequency resolution, (iii) number of spectrogram frames for a 10-second recording, and (iv) the percentage overlap between successive windows. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) STFT procedure and window effects (7 marks):**

**Block diagram description:**

- **Step 1:** Acquire the discrete biomedical signal $x[n]$ at sampling rate $F_s$.
- **Step 2:** Choose window length $N$ and window function $w[n]$ (Hamming, Hann, Blackman, etc.).
- **Step 3:** Initialize time index $\tau = 0$. Multiply $x[n]$ by the window centered at $\tau$: $x_\tau[n] = x[n]\, w[n - \tau]$.
- **Step 4:** Compute the DFT of the windowed segment: $X[\tau, k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, w[n-\tau]\, e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.
- **Step 5:** Store the magnitude squared $\vert X[\tau, k]\vert^2$ as one column of the spectrogram.
- **Step 6:** Shift $\tau$ by the hop size $H$ and repeat Steps 3–5 until the signal end is reached.

**Effect of window size on resolution:**

- **Short window (small $N$):** Better time resolution ($\Delta t = N/F_s$ small) because the window covers fewer samples — sharp events like the QRS complex are localized. Worse frequency resolution because $\Delta f = F_s/N$ is large — fine spectral details are smeared.
- **Long window (large $N$):** Worse time resolution but better frequency resolution — useful for analyzing sustained oscillations like EEG alpha rhythms.
- The product $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 1$ (for rectangular window) is constant, confirming the uncertainty principle in discrete form.

For biomedical applications: ECG typically uses 50–100 ms windows; EEG uses 1–4 second windows for delta/theta analysis and 250–500 ms for beta/gamma.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Given: $F_s = 500\,\text{Hz}$, $N = 128$, $H = 32$, recording duration $T = 10\,\text{s}$.

**(i) Time resolution:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{N}{F_s} = \frac{128}{500} = 0.256\,\text{s} = 256\,\text{ms}$$

**(ii) Frequency resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N} = \frac{500}{128} \approx 3.906\,\text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Number of spectrogram frames:**

Total samples: $T \cdot F_s = 10 \cdot 500 = 5000$ samples. With hop size $H = 32$:

$$\text{Frames} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Total samples} - N}{H} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{5000 - 128}{32} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 152.25 \right\rfloor + 1 = 152 + 1 = 153$$

**(iv) Percentage overlap:**

$$\text{Overlap} = \frac{N - H}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{128 - 32}{128} \times 100\% = \frac{96}{128} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

A 75% overlap provides smooth temporal continuity in the spectrogram without excessive computational cost — standard for clinical ECG/EEG displays.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Time-Frequency Resolution Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing rad/s and Hz units** — the bound is $\frac{1}{4\pi}$ in rad/s but $\frac{1}{2}$ in Hz. Many students lose 1 mark for using the wrong constant.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization constant** $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a\vert}}$ in the CWT definition — examiners specifically test this in Part B.
> 3. **Mixing up $\sigma_t$ with window length $N$** — $\sigma_t$ is the RMS duration, NOT the support length. For a Hamming window, the effective $\sigma_t \approx 0.3 N$.
> 4. **Neglecting to state the equality condition** (Gaussian) when asked to derive the uncertainty principle — this carries 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Computing $\Delta f$ using DFT bin count instead of $F_s/N$** — these give different numerical answers.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Parseval's theorem application** in the derivation — a key intermediate step worth 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the overlap calculation** when given hop size — the formula $\frac{N-H}{N} \times 100\%$ is frequently tested.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Uncertainty Principle:** $\sigma_t \sigma_f \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ (rad/s) or $\geq \dfrac{1}{2}$ (Hz) — equality only for Gaussian signals.
- **STFT** uses a fixed window → **uniform tiling** → constant resolution across all frequencies.
- **CWT/Wavelets** use scaled wavelets → **logarithmic (adaptive) tiling** → better time resolution at high frequencies, better frequency resolution at low frequencies.
- **Time-bandwidth product** is a *constant* — improving one axis worsens the other. This is a fundamental physical limit, not a technological one.
- **Discrete STFT formulas:** $\Delta t = N/F_s$, $\Delta f = F_s/N$, Frames $= \lfloor(T \cdot F_s - N)/H\rfloor + 1$, Overlap $\% = (N-H)/N \times 100$.
- **Pseudo-frequency relation:** $f_a = \dfrac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta_t}$, where $f_c$ is the wavelet center frequency and $a$ is the scale.
- **Parseval's theorem** is the bridge between time-domain and frequency-domain energy — essential in uncertainty derivations.
- **Gaussian window achieves optimal joint resolution** — basis of the Gabor transform and the optimal Short-Time Fourier kernel.
- **Constant-Q property** of wavelets: $Q = f_c / \Delta f$ is preserved across scales — analogous to the human auditory system.
- **Clinical applications:** ECG QRS → short window (high time resolution); EEG alpha rhythm → long window (high frequency resolution); heart murmurs → wavelet multi-resolution.
- **Most important equation to memorize for KTU:** the uncertainty principle and the CWT definition with the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a\vert}}$ normalization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiresolution Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiresolution Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiresolution Analysis (MRA)**, also known as **Multi-Resolution Approximation**, is a mathematical framework introduced by **Stephane Mallat (1989)** that represents a square-integrable function $f(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ as a limit of successive approximations at progressively finer resolutions. Formally, an MRA is a sequence of closed subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ satisfying the following six axioms:

1. **Nested (Monotonic) Subspaces:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j \subset V_{j-1} \subset \cdots \subset L^2(\mathbb{R})$
2. **Density (Completeness in Limit):** $\overline{\bigcup_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
3. **Separation (Triviality in Limit):** $\bigcap_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} V_j = \{0\}$
4. **Scale Invariance (Self-Similarity):** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$
5. **Translation Invariance:** $f(t) \in V_j \implies f(t - 2^j k) \in V_j$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$
6. **Existence of Basis:** There exists a function $\phi(t) \in V_0$ such that $\{\phi(t-k) \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ forms a **Riesz basis** (orthonormal basis) for $V_0$. This $\phi(t)$ is called the **scaling function** (or father wavelet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** MRA is the foundational pillar of the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT). Every DWT computation in biomedical engineering — denoising ECG, detecting QRS complexes, decomposing EEG bands — is fundamentally an application of MRA theory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a cardiologist looking at an **ECG signal** displayed on a monitor:

- At a **coarse resolution** (zoomed out), you can see the overall heart rhythm and identify general R-peaks. You don't need fine detail.
- At a **medium resolution**, you can see the P-wave, QRS complex, and T-wave distinctly.
- At a **fine resolution** (zoomed in), you can see micro-features like the notches, baseline wander, and high-frequency muscle noise.

**Multiresolution Analysis does exactly this with mathematics.** Instead of looking at the signal with a single fixed "magnification," MRA builds a "mathematical zoom lens" that decomposes a signal into a hierarchy of resolutions simultaneously. Each resolution level captures information appropriate to that scale — coarse levels capture slow trends (baseline wander), and fine levels capture sharp transients (QRS spikes, EMG noise).

Think of it as a **musical score**: instead of hearing all instruments at once (time-domain), MRA separates them into bass (low frequency, large scale), mid (medium), and treble (high frequency, small scale) — and you can analyze each independently before reconstructing the symphony.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MRA over Fourier?** The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) uses a FIXED window size — same resolution for all frequencies. MRA uses VARIABLE resolution — good time resolution for high frequencies and good frequency resolution for low frequencies. This is the **Heisenberg uncertainty optimization** for signals like ECG that have both slow (ST segment) and fast (QRS) components.

## 1.3 Key Physical and Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Property |
|---|---|---|
| $j$ | Resolution index (level) | $j \in \mathbb{Z}$ (positive $j$ = coarse, negative = fine) |
| $V_j$ | Approximation subspace at level $j$ | Closed subspace of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ |
| $W_j$ | Detail subspace at level $j$ | Orthogonal complement: $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ |
| $\phi(t)$ | Scaling function (father wavelet) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\, dt = 1$ |
| $\psi(t)$ | Wavelet function (mother wavelet) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ |
| $h[n]$ | Scaling (low-pass) filter coefficients | Real, finite energy |
| $g[n]$ | Wavelet (high-pass) filter coefficients | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ (QMF relation) |
| $2^j$ | Dyadic scale factor | Powers of 2 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the MRA Axioms

### Axiom 1 — Nested Subspaces (The "Zooming" Property)
$$V_{-\infty} \subset \cdots \subset V_{-2} \subset V_{-1} \subset V_0 \subset V_1 \subset V_2 \subset \cdots \subset V_{\infty}$$

As $j$ increases, the resolution becomes **coarser** (more averaging). As $j$ decreases (more negative), the resolution becomes **finer**. The deepest level $V_{-\infty} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$ contains all possible signals with infinite resolution.

### Axiom 2 & 3 — Completeness and Separation
These two together guarantee that **every** $L^2$ function can be approximated arbitrarily well at fine resolution, while no information is "stuck" in the limit. This makes the MRA a **complete framework**.

### Axiom 4 — Scale Invariance (The Dyadic Property)
$$f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$$
This is the heart of the MRA. It tells us that all subspaces are related by **simple scaling by 2**. So if we know $V_0$, we know all other $V_j$.

### Axiom 5 — Translation Invariance
The function is translated by integer multiples of $2^j$ at level $j$. This ensures the basis functions tile the time axis without overlap.

### Axiom 6 — Existence of Scaling Function
The scaling function $\phi(t) \in V_0$ is the **"atom"** of the analysis. It satisfies:
$$\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \, \phi(2^{-j}t - k)$$
The set $\{\phi_{j,k}(t)\}_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$ forms an **orthonormal basis** for $V_j$.

## 2.2 The Two-Scale Relation (Dilation Equation)

Since $\phi(t) \in V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, and $\{\phi_{-1,k}(t) = \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-k)\}$ forms a basis for $V_{-1}$, the scaling function can be expressed in terms of itself at the next finer level:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)}$$

Taking the Fourier transform gives the **frequency-domain dilation equation**:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = H\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \hat{\phi}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

Iterating this $N$ times:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \left[\prod_{k=1}^{N} H\left(\frac{\omega}{2^k}\right)\right] \hat{\phi}\left(\frac{\omega}{2^N}\right)$$

As $N \to \infty$, $\hat{\phi}(\omega/2^N) \to \hat{\phi}(0) = 1$ (using the admissibility condition), yielding:
$$\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} H\left(\frac{\omega}{2^k}\right)$$

## 2.3 The Wavelet Function (Mother Wavelet)

The detail space $W_j$ captures the "difference" between $V_{j-1}$ and $V_j$:
$$V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j \quad \text{(orthogonal direct sum)}$$

The wavelet function $\psi(t) \in W_0 \subset V_{-1}$ satisfies its own two-scale relation:
$$\boxed{\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} g[n]\, \phi(2t - n)}$$

where $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ (the **Quadrature Mirror Filter** relationship).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ | Subspace decomposition | Core MRA relationship |
| 2 | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$ | Scaling equation (time) | Finding scaling function |
| 3 | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n g[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$ | Wavelet equation (time) | Finding mother wavelet |
| 4 | $\hat{\phi}(\omega) = H(\omega/2)\,\hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$ | Scaling equation (frequency) | Filter design |
| 5 | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ | QMF condition | High-pass from low-pass |
| 6 | $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | Normalization (DC gain) | Valid filter check |
| 7 | $\sum_n h[n]\,h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ | Orthogonality | Perfect reconstruction |
| 8 | $a_{j,k} = \langle f, \phi_{j,k} \rangle$ | Approximation coefficients | Coarse-scale coefficients |
| 9 | $d_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$ | Detail coefficients | Wavelet coefficients |
| 10 | $f(t) = \sum_k a_{J,k}\,\phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_k d_{j,k}\,\psi_{j,k}(t)$ | MRA expansion | Signal reconstruction |
| 11 | $E_j = \sum_k \vert a_{j,k} \vert^2$ | Energy at scale $j$ | Energy distribution |
| 12 | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(t)\,dt = 1$ | Admissibility (DC pass) | Low-pass property |
| 13 | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t)\,dt = 0$ | Wavelet admissibility | Band-pass property |
| 14 | $\vert H(\omega)\vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi)\vert^2 = 2$ | Power complementarity | PR filter bank |
| 15 | $W_f(j,k) = \int f(t)\,\psi_{j,k}^*(t)\,dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform | Time-scale analysis |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Biomedical Application | MRA Role |
|---|---|
| **ECG Denoising** | Threshold detail coefficients $d_{j,k}$ to remove muscle noise |
| **EEG Band Extraction** | Levels of MRA approximate delta ($\delta$), theta ($\theta$), alpha ($\alpha$), beta ($\beta$) |
| **QRS Detection** | Sharper QRS at fine scales, suppressed at coarse scales |
| **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** | Energy at each scale gives frequency-band power |
| **EMG Analysis** | Decompose motor unit action potentials into wavelets |
| **PCG (Heart Sound) Analysis** | Separate S1, S2, murmurs at appropriate scales |
| **Image Compression (MRI/CT)** | Discard small detail coefficients — JPEG2000 uses MRA |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Scale Relation

### Step 1 — Express $\phi(t)$ in $V_{-1}$
Since $\phi(t) \in V_0$ and $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, the function $\phi(t)$ can be expanded in the orthonormal basis $\{\phi_{-1,n}(t) = \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t - n)\}$ of $V_{-1}$:

$$
\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} c_n \, \phi_{-1,n}(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} c_n \, \phi(2t - n)
$$

Here $c_n$ are the expansion coefficients.

### Step 2 — Identify the Coefficients
Take the inner product with $\phi(2t - m)$ on both sides:

$$
\langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} c_n \langle \phi(2t-n), \phi(2t-m) \rangle
$$

Because $\{\phi(2t-n)\}$ is orthonormal, $\langle \phi(2t-n), \phi(2t-m) \rangle = \delta_{n,m}$. Thus:

$$
\langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle = \sqrt{2}\,c_m
$$

This gives $c_m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle$. By definition, this is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} h[m]$ where:

$$
h[m] = \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle
$$

### Step 3 — Final Two-Scale Equation
Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)}
$$

This is the famous **refinement equation** or **dilation equation**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: QMF Relationship Between $h[n]$ and $g[n]$

### Step 1 — Use Orthogonality of $\phi_{j,k}$ Across Scales
The basis functions at different scales must be orthogonal. The detail space $W_j$ is the orthogonal complement of $V_j$ in $V_{j-1}$.

### Step 2 — Inner Product Constraint
We need $\langle \psi(t), \phi(t-k) \rangle = 0$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. Substituting the two-scale relations:

$$
\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n g[n]\,\phi(2t-n), \quad \phi(t-k) = \sqrt{2}\sum_m h[m]\,\phi(2(t-k)-m)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Orthonormality of $\phi(2t-n)$
Using $\langle \phi(2t-n), \phi(2t-2k-m) \rangle = \delta_{n,2k+m}$:

$$
\langle \psi(t), \phi(t-k) \rangle = 2 \sum_n \sum_m g[n]\,h[m]\,\delta_{n, 2k+m} = 2 \sum_m g[2k+m]\,h[m] = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Change of Index
Let $n = 2k + m$, then $m = n - 2k$:

$$
\sum_n g[n]\,h[n-2k] = 0 \quad \text{for all } k \neq 0
$$

This is the **cross-orthogonality condition**. The simplest solution is:

$$
\boxed{g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]}
$$

This is the **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF)** relationship. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Decomposition Algorithm (Mallat's Algorithm)

### Step 1 — Signal Projection onto $V_j$ and $W_j$
The approximation coefficient at level $j$ is:

$$
a_{j,k} = \langle f, \phi_{j,k} \rangle = \int f(t)\,2^{-j/2}\phi(2^{-j}t - k)\,dt
$$

### Step 2 — Relate $a_{j,k}$ to $a_{j-1,k}$
Using the two-scale relation $\phi_{j,k}(t) = \sum_n h[n]\,\phi_{j-1, 2k+n}(t)$:

$$
a_{j,k} = \sum_n h[n]\,a_{j-1, 2k+n}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in Convolution Form
Let $a_{j-1} = \{a_{j-1, m}\}_{m \in \mathbb{Z}}$. Then:

$$
a_{j,k} = \sum_n h[n]\,a_{j-1, 2k+n} = (h \star a_{j-1})[2k]
$$

This is **convolution followed by downsampling by 2**. Similarly for detail coefficients:

$$
d_{j,k} = \sum_n g[n]\,a_{j-1, 2k+n} = (g \star a_{j-1})[2k]
$$

### Step 4 — Final Decomposition Formulas

$$
\boxed{a_{j,k} = \sum_n h[n-2k]\,a_{j-1, n}, \quad d_{j,k} = \sum_n g[n-2k]\,a_{j-1, n}}
$$

And the **reconstruction** (inverse DWT):

$$
\boxed{a_{j-1, n} = \sum_k h[n-2k]\,a_{j,k} + \sum_k g[n-2k]\,d_{j,k}}
$$

This gives the famous **analysis-synthesis filter bank**. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Haar MRA

The simplest MRA is the **Haar MRA**. The scaling function is:

$$
\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 1 — Find $h[n]$
From the two-scale relation $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$, the support of $\phi(2t-n)$ is $[n/2, (n+1)/2)$. To cover $[0,1)$, we need $n=0$ and $n=1$. Equating both sides on $[0,1)$:

$$
1 = \sqrt{2}(h[0]\cdot 1 + h[1]\cdot 1)
$$

Also orthogonality: $\sum_n h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ gives $h[0]^2 + h[1]^2 = 1$. Solving:

$$
h[0] = h[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Find $g[n]$
Using QMF: $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$:

$$
g[0] = (-1)^0 h[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad g[1] = (-1)^1 h[0] = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

So $g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$. ✓

### Step 3 — Decompose a Simple Signal
Let $f = [1, 2, 3, 4]$ (four samples). Then:

- **Approximation (low-pass) at level 1:** $a_1[k] = h[0]\,a_0[2k] + h[1]\,a_0[2k+1]$
  - $a_1[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + 2) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
  - $a_1[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 + 4) = \frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}$
- **Detail (high-pass) at level 1:** $d_1[k] = g[0]\,a_0[2k] + g[1]\,a_0[2k+1]$
  - $d_1[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 - 2) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
  - $d_1[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 - 4) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

Result: $a_1 = \left[\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$, $d_1 = \left[-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The approximation captures the **average** (slow trend), and the detail captures the **change** (fast variation). In ECG, the approximation might be the baseline, and the detail might be the QRS.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Multiresolution Analysis (MRA). List the four essential properties that a sequence of subspaces must satisfy to form an MRA.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Multiresolution Analysis (MRA), introduced by Stephane Mallat in 1989, is a hierarchical framework in $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ that represents a signal as a limit of successive approximations at multiple resolutions using a scaling function and wavelet function.

**Four Essential Properties:**

1. **Nested Subspaces:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ for all $j \in \mathbb{Z}$
2. **Completeness:** $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
3. **Separation:** $\bigcap_j V_j = \{0\}$
4. **Scale Invariance:** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$

Additionally, there must exist a scaling function $\phi(t) \in V_0$ that generates an orthonormal basis $\{\phi(t-k)\}$ for $V_0$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a scaling function? Write the two-scale (dilation) equation and explain the role of the coefficients $h[n]$.**

**Model Answer:**

A **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ is the fundamental function in MRA that generates the orthonormal basis for all approximation subspaces $V_j$. It is also called the **father wavelet** and acts as a low-pass filter — it preserves the low-frequency content (averages) of the signal.

**Two-Scale Equation:**
$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Role of $h[n]$:** The coefficients $h[n]$ are the **scaling filter coefficients** (low-pass filter taps) that determine the shape of the scaling function. They satisfy:
- Normalization: $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$
- Orthogonality: $\sum_n h[n]\,h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$
- They act as the impulse response of a low-pass analysis filter in Mallat's algorithm.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the two-scale relation (refinement equation) for the scaling function $\phi(t)$ in a Multiresolution Analysis framework. Also derive the frequency-domain equivalent.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $\phi(t) \in V_0$ and $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, the scaling function can be expressed in the basis $\{\phi_{-1,n}(t) = \sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\}$ of $V_{-1}$:

$$
\phi(t) = \sum_{n} c_n \, \phi_{-1,n}(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n c_n\,\phi(2t - n) \quad \cdots (1)
$$

**Step 2:** Take inner product with $\phi(2t - m)$ on both sides. Since $\{\phi(2t-n)\}$ is orthonormal:

$$
c_m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} h[m] \quad \cdots (2)
$$

where $h[m] = \langle \phi(t), \phi(2t-m) \rangle$.

**Step 3:** Substituting (2) into (1):

$$
\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)} \quad \cdots (3)
$$

**Step 4 (Frequency Domain):** Take the Fourier transform of (3). Recall $\mathcal{F}\{\phi(2t-n)\} = \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega n/2} \hat{\phi}(\omega/2)$:

$$
\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega n/2} \hat{\phi}(\omega/2)
$$

Defining $H(\omega) = \sum_n h[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ (DTFT of $h[n]$), and noting $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\boxed{\hat{\phi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \hat{\phi}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)} \quad \cdots (4)
$$

This is the **frequency-domain dilation equation**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the relationship between MRA and the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) using Mallat's algorithm. Show how approximation and detail coefficients are computed.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MRA Decomposition:**
In MRA, the signal space is decomposed as $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$, where $V_j$ captures the **approximation** (low-frequency content) and $W_j$ captures the **detail** (high-frequency content).

**Step 2 — Projection onto $V_j$ and $W_j$:**
$$a_{j,k} = \langle f, \phi_{j,k} \rangle, \quad d_{j,k} = \langle f, \psi_{j,k} \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Previous Level:**
Using the two-scale relations $\phi_{j,k}(t) = \sum_n h[n]\,\phi_{j-1, 2k+n}(t)$ and similarly for $\psi$:

$$
a_{j,k} = \sum_n h[n-2k]\,a_{j-1,n}, \quad d_{j,k} = \sum_n g[n-2k]\,a_{j-1,n} \quad \cdots (5)
$$

**Step 4 — Filter Bank Interpretation:**
Equation (5) is **convolution with $h$ and $g$ followed by downsampling by 2**. This is the **analysis filter bank**.

**Step 5 — Reconstruction (Synthesis):**
$$a_{j-1, n} = \sum_k h[n-2k]\,a_{j,k} + \sum_k g[n-2k]\,d_{j,k} \quad \cdots (6)$$

This is **upsampling by 2 followed by filtering and addition** — the **synthesis filter bank**.

**Conclusion:** Mallat's algorithm implements DWT as a cascade of two-channel filter banks. The MRA provides the theoretical foundation, and DWT is the practical computation. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**For the Haar wavelet, derive the scaling function $\phi(t)$, the wavelet function $\psi(t)$, the filter coefficients $h[n]$ and $g[n]$. Verify the perfect reconstruction property.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Haar Scaling Function:**
$$\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a unit-height pulse on $[0,1)$.

**Step 2 — Find $h[n]$:**
From the two-scale equation: $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$. The function $\phi(2t-n)$ is supported on $[n/2, (n+1)/2)$. To cover $[0,1)$:
- For $t \in [0, 1/2)$: $\phi(t) = 1 = \sqrt{2} h[0] \cdot 1 \Rightarrow h[0] = 1/\sqrt{2}$
- For $t \in [1/2, 1)$: $\phi(t) = 1 = \sqrt{2} h[1] \cdot 1 \Rightarrow h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$

Check orthogonality: $h[0]^2 + h[1]^2 = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Haar Wavelet Function:**
$$\psi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \le t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Find $g[n]$:**
Using QMF: $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$:
- $g[0] = (+1)(1/\sqrt{2}) = 1/\sqrt{2}$
- $g[1] = (-1)(1/\sqrt{2}) = -1/\sqrt{2}$

So $g = [1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}]$.

**Step 5 — Verify Perfect Reconstruction:**
For any $a = [a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3]$:
- $a_1[0] = (a_0 + a_1)/\sqrt{2}$, $d_1[0] = (a_0 - a_1)/\sqrt{2}$
- $a_1[1] = (a_2 + a_3)/\sqrt{2}$, $d_1[1] = (a_2 - a_3)/\sqrt{2}$

Reconstruction: $a_0' = h[0]a_1[0] + g[0]d_1[0] = (1/\sqrt{2})[(a_0+a_1)/\sqrt{2}] + (1/\sqrt{2})[(a_0-a_1)/\sqrt{2}] = a_0$ ✓

Similarly for $a_1, a_2, a_3$. **Perfect reconstruction is verified.** ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain how MRA is applied to ECG signal denoising. Show the step-by-step algorithm and discuss the choice of wavelet.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why MRA for ECG?**
ECG signals contain:
- Low-frequency baseline wander (0–0.5 Hz)
- P, QRS, T waves (0.5–40 Hz)
- High-frequency EMG noise (40+ Hz)
- 50/60 Hz powerline interference

A **single-resolution** method cannot separate all these. **MRA naturally decomposes** ECG into bands corresponding to these components.

**Step 2 — Wavelet Selection:**
The **Daubechies-6 (db6)** or **Symlet-8 (sym8)** wavelets are commonly used because:
- Higher vanishing moments → smoother approximation
- Near-symmetric phase → minimal distortion
- Compactly supported → efficient computation

**Step 3 — Decomposition Algorithm:**
1. Apply Mallat's algorithm to decompose ECG into $J = 5$ levels
2. Obtain detail coefficients $d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4, d_5$ and final approximation $a_5$
3. **Threshold the detail coefficients** using soft thresholding:
$$d_{j,k}^{thr} = \begin{cases} \text{sign}(d_{j,k})(\vert d_{j,k} \vert - \lambda), & \vert d_{j,k} \vert > \lambda \\ 0, & \vert d_{j,k} \vert \le \lambda \end{cases}$$
where $\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}$ is the universal threshold (Donoho's rule).

**Step 4 — Noise Identification at Each Level:**
- $d_1$ (highest scale, finest detail): mostly muscle noise → **threshold aggressively**
- $d_2$: residual muscle noise → **moderate threshold**
- $d_3, d_4$: QRS complex energy → **preserve**
- $d_5, a_5$: P, T waves and baseline → **preserve**

**Step 5 — Reconstruction:**
Apply inverse DWT using modified $d_{j,k}^{thr}$ to obtain the **denoised ECG** $\hat{f}(t)$.

**Step 6 — Performance Metrics:**
- **SNR improvement:** typically 8–12 dB
- **PRD (Percentage Root-mean-square Difference):** < 5% for clinical acceptance
- **Preservation of QRS morphology:** verified by correlation > 0.95 ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_j$ direction:** Many students write $V_j \subset V_{j+1}$ — it is the OPPOSITE. Higher $j$ = coarser (larger) space.
> 2. **Missing the $\sqrt{2}$ factor:** The two-scale relation MUST have $\sqrt{2}$, not $1/\sqrt{2}$, depending on convention. KTU follows the convention $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h[n]\phi(2t-n)$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Riesz basis condition:** Orthonormal basis is required, not just any spanning set.
> 4. **Confusing CWT and DWT:** CWT uses continuous parameters; DWT is MRA-based with discrete dyadic scales.
> 5. **Not verifying PR condition:** Always check $\sum h[n]^2 = 1$ for low-pass normalization.
> 6. **Wrong QMF sign:** $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ — students often write $g[n] = (-1)^n h[n]$ which is incorrect.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- [x] **MRA Definition:** Hierarchical framework by Mallat (1989) using nested subspaces in $L^2(\mathbb{R})$
- [x] **Six MRA Axioms:** Nested, Density, Separation, Scale invariance, Translation, Basis existence
- [x] **Scaling function** $\phi(t)$: Father wavelet, $\int \phi(t)dt = 1$, low-pass character
- [x] **Wavelet function** $\psi(t)$: Mother wavelet, $\int \psi(t)dt = 0$, band-pass character
- [x] **Two-scale relation:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h[n]\phi(2t-n)$ — **MUST MEMORIZE**
- [x] **QMF relation:** $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ — high-pass from low-pass
- [x] **Subspace decomposition:** $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ (orthogonal direct sum)
- [x] **Mallat's algorithm:** Analysis = filter + downsample; Synthesis = upsample + filter + add
- [x] **DWT coefficients:** $a_{j,k}$ (approximation) and $d_{j,k}$ (detail)
- [x] **Reconstruction formula:** $a_{j-1,n} = \sum_k h[n-2k]a_{j,k} + \sum_k g[n-2k]d_{j,k}$
- [x] **Haar is the simplest MRA:** $h = [1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}]$, $g = [1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}]$
- [x] **Filter constraints:** $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$, $\sum h[n]h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$
- [x] **Power symmetry:** $\vert H(\omega)\vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi)\vert^2 = 2$
- [x] **MRA vs STFT:** MRA = variable resolution (good for ECG/EEG); STFT = fixed resolution
- [x] **Biomedical Applications:** ECG denoising, QRS detection, EEG band analysis, EMG decomposition, PCG analysis
- [x] **Daubechies wavelets (dbN):** Most commonly used in biomedical signal processing due to compact support and orthogonality
- [x] **Thresholding rule:** $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\log N}$ (Donoho's universal threshold for denoising)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway:** *Multiresolution Analysis decomposes a signal into a hierarchy of approximations and details at dyadic scales using scaling and wavelet functions, enabling optimal time-frequency localization critical for biomedical signal processing.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wavelet Transform (WT)** is a mathematical transform that decomposes a time-domain signal into time-frequency representations using a family of basis functions called **wavelets**. Unlike the Fourier Transform, which provides only global frequency information, the Wavelet Transform offers **localized frequency information** in both time and scale (frequency) domains, making it exceptionally well-suited for the analysis of **non-stationary biomedical signals** such as **ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG** signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The Wavelet Transform is a signal processing technique that uses small, oscillating wave-like functions (wavelets) of finite duration, scaled and translated to analyze signals at different resolutions. It overcomes the fixed resolution limitation of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT).

**Mathematical Form (Continuous Wavelet Transform - CWT):**

$$CWT(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ = Input biomedical signal
- $\psi(t)$ = **Mother Wavelet** (the prototype analyzing function)
- $a$ = Scaling parameter (controls frequency resolution)
- $b$ = Translation parameter (controls time localization)
- $\psi^*( \cdot )$ = Complex conjugate of the mother wavelet

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Microscope Analogy for Wavelets:**
> Imagine you are a pathologist examining a tissue sample under a microscope. The **Fourier Transform** is like a single photograph — it tells you *what* frequencies are present but not *when* they occurred. The **Wavelet Transform** is like a *zoom lens* that lets you examine the signal at:
> - **High zoom (small scale 'a')** → fine time resolution → ideal for detecting sharp QRS complexes in ECG.
> - **Low zoom (large scale 'a')** → fine frequency resolution → ideal for analyzing baseline wander or low-frequency artifacts.
>
> In essence, **wavelets give you a mathematical "zoom lens" for time-frequency analysis of biomedical signals.**

### 1.3 Why Wavelets for Biomedical Signals?

Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are inherently **non-stationary** — their statistical properties (mean, variance, spectral content) change with time. Standard Fourier methods fail to capture these dynamics. Wavelets solve this through **Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)**, allowing simultaneous time and frequency localization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Wavelets vs. STFT
> - **STFT**: Uses a *fixed window size* → fixed time-frequency resolution.
> - **Wavelets**: Uses *variable window size* (short at high frequencies, long at low frequencies) → adaptive resolution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mother Wavelet: The Fundamental Building Block

A **Mother Wavelet** $\psi(t)$ is the prototype function from which all wavelet basis functions (daughter wavelets) are generated via **scaling and translation**:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

**Admissibility Condition** (must be satisfied for any valid mother wavelet):

$$C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{|\Psi(\omega)|^2}{|\omega|} d\omega < \infty$$

Where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> This condition guarantees that $\psi(t)$ has **zero mean** and is **bandpass** in nature, ensuring successful inversion of the CWT.

### 2.2 Properties of Mother Wavelet

- **Compact Support**: Energy is concentrated in a finite time interval.
- **Zero Mean (DC Removal)**: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \psi(t) dt = 0$
- **Finite Energy**: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} |\psi(t)|^2 dt < \infty$
- **Oscillatory Behavior**: Must be wave-like (hence the name *wavelet*).

### 2.3 Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) — Detailed Theory

The CWT correlates the signal with scaled and shifted versions of the mother wavelet:

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi_{a,b}^*(t) \, dt$$

**Inverse CWT (Reconstruction):**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} CWT_x(a,b) \cdot \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$$

### 2.4 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)

For computational efficiency and biomedical real-time applications, the CWT is discretized by sampling the scale-translation plane:

$$a = a_0^m, \quad b = n \cdot b_0 \cdot a_0^m$$

**Common Choice (Dyadic Sampling):**

$$a_0 = 2, \quad b_0 = 1$$

$$a = 2^m, \quad b = n \cdot 2^m$$

Where $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integers).

$$DWT(m,n) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^m}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^*\left(\frac{t - n \cdot 2^m}{2^m}\right) dt$$

### 2.5 Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) — The Heart of DWT

MRA expresses a signal as a limit of successive approximations. At each level, the signal is decomposed into:

- **Approximation Coefficients** ($cA_n$): Low-frequency content (coarse scale).
- **Detail Coefficients** ($cD_n$): High-frequency content (fine scale).

**Scaling Function (Father Wavelet)** $\phi(t)$ satisfies the **two-scale equation**:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k h(k) \, \phi(2t - k)$$

**Wavelet function** is related to scaling function via:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k g(k) \, \phi(2t - k)$$

Where $g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)$ (Quadrature Mirror Filter relationship).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous Wavelet Transform | $CWT(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int x(t) \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | Time-frequency map of ECG/EEG |
| Inverse CWT | $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \iint CWT(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$ | Perfect reconstruction |
| Admissibility Condition | $C_\psi = \int \frac{\vert\Psi(\omega)\vert^2}{\vert\omega\vert} d\omega < \infty$ | Validity of mother wavelet |
| Daughter Wavelet | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | Basis function family |
| Dyadic Scales | $a = 2^m, \quad b = n \cdot 2^m$ | Efficient discrete sampling |
| Two-Scale (Dilation) Equation | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k h(k) \phi(2t - k)$ | MRA foundation |
| QMF Relation | $g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)$ | High-pass from low-pass filter |
| Energy Conservation (Parseval-like) | $E = \sum_m \sum_n \vert DWT(m,n) \vert^2$ | Wavelet domain energy |
| Reconstruction | $x(t) = \sum_n cA_n \phi_n(t) + \sum_m \sum_n cD_{m,n} \psi_{m,n}(t)$ | Multi-resolution synthesis |
| Signal Energy | $E_x = \sum_m \sum_n \vert cD_{m,n} \vert^2 + \sum_n \vert cA_L,n \vert^2$ | DWT energy decomposition |

### 2.7 Real-World Biomedical Applications

1. **ECG Signal Denoising**: Thresholding wavelet coefficients removes powerline interference (50/60 Hz) and muscle noise while preserving QRS morphology.
2. **EEG Feature Extraction**: Wavelet coefficients serve as input features for seizure detection and motor imagery classification in BCI systems.
3. **PCG (Phonocardiogram) Analysis**: Wavelets extract S1, S2 heart sounds and detect murmurs.
4. **EMG Signal Decomposition**: Identifying motor unit action potentials.
5. **R-peak Detection in ECG**: Sharp localization using `db4` or `sym4` wavelets.
6. **Heart Rate Variability (HRV) Analysis**: Multi-scale entropy using wavelets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CWT from Fourier Transform Framework

We derive the CWT starting from correlation between the signal $x(t)$ and scaled/translated wavelets.

**Step 1: Define the Inner Product**
The CWT coefficient at scale $a$ and translation $b$ is the inner product of $x(t)$ with the daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT_x(a,b) &= \langle x(t), \psi_{a,b}(t) \rangle \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi_{a,b}^*(t) \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Daughter Wavelet Definition**
Substituting $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT_x(a,b) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical form of the CWT.

**Step 3: Show the Fourier Domain Relationship**
By Parseval's theorem, the inner product equals the inner product of Fourier transforms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int x(t) \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt &= \int X(\omega) \cdot \Psi^*(a\omega) \cdot e^{j\omega b} d\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus:

$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{\sqrt{a}}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega) \cdot \Psi^*(a\omega) \cdot e^{j\omega b} d\omega
$$

This shows CWT acts as a **bandpass filter** with center frequency inversely proportional to scale $a$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse CWT (Reconstruction Formula)

**Step 1: Start with the CWT definition and the goal of reconstructing $x(t)$.**

We seek to invert:

$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \int x(t) \psi_{a,b}^*(t) dt
$$

**Step 2: Multiply both sides by $\psi_{a,b}(t')$ and integrate over $a$ and $b$:**

$$
\iint CWT_x(a,b) \cdot \psi_{a,b}(t') \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2} = \iint \left[ \int x(t) \psi_{a,b}^*(t) dt \right] \psi_{a,b}(t') \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2}
$$

**Step 3: Swap the order of integration:**

$$
= \int x(t) \left[ \iint \psi_{a,b}^*(t) \psi_{a,b}(t') \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2} \right] dt
$$

**Step 4: Resolve the Reproducing Kernel**

Using the admissibility condition, the inner double integral simplifies to $C_\psi \cdot \delta(t - t')$, giving:

$$
\iint CWT_x(a,b) \cdot \psi_{a,b}(t') \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2} = C_\psi \cdot x(t')
$$

**Step 5: Final Reconstruction Formula:**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \iint CWT_x(a,b) \cdot \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Two-Scale Relation and Filter Bank

The **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ satisfies:

$$
\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} h(k) \phi(2t - k)
$$

**Step 1: Take Fourier Transform of Both Sides**

$$
\Phi(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \Phi\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

Where $H(\omega) = \sum_k h(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ is the **low-pass filter**.

**Step 2: Iterate the Equation**

$$
\Phi(\omega) = \prod_{j=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H\left(\frac{\omega}{2^j}\right) \Phi(0)
$$

This shows that $\phi(t)$ is the limit of cascading low-pass filters.

**Step 3: Wavelet Function in Frequency Domain**

$$
\Psi(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} G\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \Phi\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

Where $G(\omega) = \sum_k g(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ is the **high-pass filter**.

**Step 4: Quadrature Mirror Filter Condition**

$$
\boxed{g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)}
$$

This ensures that $|H(\omega)|^2 + |H(\omega + \pi)|^2 = 2$ (perfect reconstruction).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: 3-Level DWT Decomposition of ECG Signal

**Given**: An ECG signal $x[n]$ of length $N = 1024$ samples, sampled at 360 Hz.

**Step 1: Level-1 Decomposition**
Apply low-pass $h[k]$ and high-pass $g[k]$ filters, then downsample by 2:

$$
cA_1[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[2n - k], \quad cD_1[n] = \sum_k x[k] g[2n - k]
$$

Result: Two sequences of length $512$ each.

**Step 2: Level-2 Decomposition (decompose $cA_1$ only)**

$$
cA_2[n] = \sum_k cA_1[k] h[2n-k], \quad cD_2[n] = \sum_k cA_1[k] g[2n-k]
$$

Result: Two sequences of length $256$ each.

**Step 3: Level-3 Decomposition (decompose $cA_2$ only)**

$$
cA_3[n] = \sum_k cA_2[k] h[2n-k], \quad cD_3[n] = \sum_k cA_2[k] g[2n-k]
$$

Result: Two sequences of length $128$ each.

**Final DWT Decomposition Tree:**

| **Coefficient** | **Frequency Band (Hz)** | **Length** | **Biomedical Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $cA_3$ | 0 – 22.5 | 128 | Baseline wander, DC offset |
| $cD_3$ | 22.5 – 45 | 128 | T-wave, P-wave morphology |
| $cD_2$ | 45 – 90 | 256 | QRS complex high-frequency |
| $cD_1$ | 90 – 180 | 512 | Sharp QRS transitions, noise |

**Step 4: Reconstruction (Inverse DWT)**

$$
x[n] = \sum_n cA_3[n] \phi_{3,n}(t) + \sum_{m=1}^{3} \sum_n cD_m[n] \psi_{m,n}(t)
$$

This allows selective removal (e.g., thresholding $cD_1$ removes high-frequency EMG noise while preserving QRS).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DWT Filter Bank Decomposition

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputSignal["ECG Input Signal x n"] --> L1["Low-pass Filter h k"]
    InputSignal --> H1["High-pass Filter g k"]
    L1 --> D1["Downsample by 2"]
    H1 --> D2["Downsample by 2"]
    D1 --> cA1["Approximation cA1"]
    D2 --> cD1["Detail cD1"]
    cA1 --> L2["Low-pass Filter h k"]
    cA1 --> H2["High-pass Filter g k"]
    L2 --> D3["Downsample by 2"]
    H2 --> D4["Downsample by 2"]
    D3 --> cA2["Approximation cA2"]
    D4 --> cD2["Detail cD2"]
    cA2 --> L3["Low-pass Filter h k"]
    cA2 --> H3["High-pass Filter g k"]
    L3 --> D5["Downsample by 2"]
    H3 --> D6["Downsample by 2"]
    D5 --> cA3["Final Approximation cA3"]
    D6 --> cD3["Detail cD3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This architecture represents a **3-level Mallat decomposition tree**. Each level halves the bandwidth, yielding logarithmic frequency resolution — perfect for biomedical signals whose features span broad frequency ranges.

### 4.2 Multi-Resolution Analysis Time-Frequency Tiling

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "High Frequency / Fine Time Resolution"
        A1["Level 1 Detail cD1"]
        A2["Level 2 Detail cD2"]
    end
    subgraph "Low Frequency / Fine Frequency Resolution"
        A3["Level 3 Detail cD3"]
        A4["Level 3 Approximation cA3"]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Wavelet-Based ECG Denoising Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartA["Raw ECG Signal x t"] --> StepA1["Step 1: Choose Mother Wavelet db4"]
    StepA1 --> StepA2["Step 2: Perform 5-level DWT"]
    StepA2 --> StepA3["Step 3: Apply Soft Thresholding"]
    StepA3 --> StepA4{"Step 4: Coefficient Decision"}
    StepA4 -->|"Coef less than Threshold"| Zero["Set to Zero"]
    StepA4 -->|"Coef greater than Threshold"| Shrink["Shrink by Threshold"]
    Zero --> StepA5["Step 5: Inverse DWT Reconstruction"]
    Shrink --> StepA5
    StepA5 --> OutA["Denoised ECG Signal y t"]
```

### 4.4 Common Mother Wavelets for Biomedical Signals (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["Mother Wavelet Selection"]
    Root --> BranchA["Daubechies db4 db6"]
    Root --> BranchB["Symlet sym4 sym8"]
    Root --> BranchC["Coiflet coif4"]
    Root --> BranchD["Haar"]
    Root --> BranchE["Morlet"]
    Root --> BranchF["Mexican Hat"]
    BranchA --> AppA["Application: ECG QRS detection"]
    BranchB --> AppB["Application: EEG seizure detection"]
    BranchC --> AppC["Application: EMG decomposition"]
    BranchD --> AppD["Application: Simple thresholding baseline"]
    BranchE --> AppE["Application: PCG frequency analysis"]
    BranchF --> AppF["Application: Spike detection"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For ECG processing, `db4` and `sym4` are the gold standard due to their compact support and similarity to the QRS complex shape. The Haar wavelet is too coarse and rarely used for clinical applications.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Wavelet Transform. List any two advantages of Wavelet Transform over Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) for biomedical signal analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Wavelet Transform** is a mathematical tool that decomposes a signal into a set of basis functions called wavelets, obtained by scaling (dilation) and translating (shifting) a prototype function known as the **mother wavelet**.

**Advantages over STFT:**

1. **Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA):** Wavelets provide variable time-frequency resolution — good time resolution at high frequencies and good frequency resolution at low frequencies. STFT uses a fixed window, giving uniform resolution everywhere.

2. **Better suited for non-stationary signals:** Biomedical signals like ECG and EEG are non-stationary. Wavelets adaptively localize sharp transients (e.g., QRS complexes) more effectively than STFT.

3. **Compact support:** Wavelets have finite duration, allowing localized analysis, unlike the infinite-duration sinusoids used in Fourier analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a mother wavelet? State the admissibility condition for a function to qualify as a mother wavelet.**

**Model Answer:**

A **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$ is the prototype analyzing function from which all daughter wavelets are generated by scaling and translation:

$$
\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)
$$

**Admissibility Condition:**

$$
C_\psi = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{|\Psi(\omega)|^2}{|\omega|} \, d\omega < \infty
$$

This condition requires that the mother wavelet has **zero mean** (DC component = 0) and finite energy, ensuring that the CWT is invertible.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) with its mathematical expression. Discuss the role of scaling parameter 'a' and translation parameter 'b'. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the implementation of Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) using filter banks. Show how an ECG signal can be decomposed up to 3 levels. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

Where:
- $\psi(t)$ = Mother wavelet
- $a \in \mathbb{R}^+ \setminus \{0\}$ = **Scaling parameter** (inversely proportional to frequency)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ = **Translation parameter** (time localization)

**Role of Scale Parameter 'a':**
- Small $a$ (e.g., $a = 1$): Compressed wavelet → analyzes **high-frequency components** with **fine time resolution**.
- Large $a$ (e.g., $a = 1024$): Stretched wavelet → analyzes **low-frequency components** with **fine frequency resolution**.

**Role of Translation Parameter 'b':**
- Shifts the wavelet along the time axis.
- Determines the **time location** being analyzed.
- As $b$ varies, the wavelet "slides" across the signal, computing correlation at each instant.

**Mathematical Interpretation:**
The CWT measures the **similarity (correlation)** between the signal $x(t)$ and the daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$. Large coefficient values indicate strong correlation, revealing the presence of a particular frequency at a specific time.

**Biomedical Significance:** In ECG analysis, the QRS complex (high-frequency, sharp transient) produces strong CWT coefficients at small $a$, while the T-wave (low-frequency, smooth) yields large coefficients at large $a$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The **DWT** is implemented using a cascade of **two-channel filter banks** consisting of a low-pass filter $h[k]$ and a high-pass filter $g[k]$, followed by **downsampling by 2**.

**Block Diagram:**

```
       x[n]
        |
   -----+-----
   |       |
  [h]     [g]      <-- Low-pass and High-pass filters
   |       |
  [↓2]    [↓2]     <-- Downsampling
   |       |
  cA1     cD1
```

**Algorithm for 3-level decomposition of ECG signal:**

**Step 1:** Filter $x[n]$ with $h[k]$ and $g[k]$, then downsample:
- $cA_1[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[2n-k]$ (approximation, length $N/2$)
- $cD_1[n] = \sum_k x[k] g[2n-k]$ (detail, length $N/2$)

**Step 2:** Apply the same filter pair to $cA_1[n]$:
- $cA_2[n]$, $cD_2[n]$ (each of length $N/4$)

**Step 3:** Apply the filter pair again to $cA_2[n]$:
- $cA_3[n]$, $cD_3[n]$ (each of length $N/8$)

**Reconstruction (Inverse DWT):**

```
 cA3 cD3 cD2 cD1
   |   |   |   |
   |  [↑2, g_inv] |
   |   +   +    |
   +---↑2, h_inv--+
        |
     Reconstructed x[n]
```

**Quadrature Mirror Filter Condition:**

$$
g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)
$$

**For ECG at 360 Hz sampling rate (3-level DWT):**

| Coefficient | Frequency Band (Hz) | Biomedical Feature |
|---|---|---|
| $cA_3$ | 0 – 22.5 | Baseline wander |
| $cD_3$ | 22.5 – 45 | P-wave, T-wave |
| $cD_2$ | 45 – 90 | QRS complex |
| $cD_1$ | 90 – 180 | QRS edges, high-freq noise |

By thresholding $cD_1$ coefficients, EMG noise is removed while QRS morphology is preserved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a) Explain Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) using wavelets. Derive the two-scale (dilation) equation. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a flowchart, explain the steps involved in wavelet-based denoising of an ECG signal. Mention any two mother wavelets suitable for ECG processing. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)** expresses a signal $x(t)$ as a limit of successive approximations at increasing levels of detail. At resolution $2^{-j}$, the signal is approximated by projection onto a subspace $V_j$ of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$.

**Key Properties of MRA:**

1. **Nesting:** $V_j \subset V_{j-1}$ (coarser spaces are subspaces of finer ones).
2. **Completeness:** $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$.
3. **Scaling property:** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$.
4. **Orthonormality:** Exists a scaling function $\phi(t)$ such that $\{\phi_{j,k}(t)\}$ forms an orthonormal basis for $V_j$.

**Derivation of Two-Scale Equation:**

Since $\phi(t) \in V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, it can be expressed in terms of the basis of $V_{-1}$:

$$
\phi(t) = \sum_k c_k \phi_{-1,k}(t) = \sum_k c_k \sqrt{2} \phi(2t - k)
$$

Defining $h(k) = c_k$:

$$
\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k h(k) \phi(2t - k)}
$$

This is the **two-scale (dilation) equation** that defines the relationship between the scaling function at scale 0 and scale -1.

**Wavelet Function:**

Similarly, $\psi(t) \in W_0$ (orthogonal complement of $V_0$ in $V_{-1}$):

$$
\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k g(k) \phi(2t - k)
$$

Where $g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)$ (QMF relation).

**Reconstruction of signal:**

$$
x(t) = \sum_n cA_{L,n} \phi_{L,n}(t) + \sum_{m=1}^{L} \sum_n cD_{m,n} \psi_{m,n}(t)
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Flowchart for Wavelet-Based ECG Denoising:**

```
   ┌─────────────────────────┐
   │ Step 1: Input ECG Signal│
   │           x(t)          │
   └────────────┬────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 2: Choose Mother    │
   │ Wavelet (db4 / sym4)     │
   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 3: Perform N-level  │
   │ DWT decomposition        │
   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 4: Compute Threshold│
   │ λ = σ √(2 log N)         │
   │ (Universal threshold)    │
   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 5: Apply Threshold  │
   │ Function (Soft/Hard)     │
   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 6: Inverse DWT to   │
   │ Reconstruct ECG          │
   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
   │ Step 7: Output Denoised  │
   │ ECG Signal y(t)          │
   └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

**Step 1:** Read the raw ECG signal contaminated with powerline interference (50/60 Hz) and EMG noise.

**Step 2:** Select an appropriate mother wavelet:
- **Daubechies (db4)** — most widely used; compact support, near-symmetric.
- **Symlet (sym4)** — more symmetric than Daubechies; better for preserving QRS shape.

**Step 3:** Apply 4–5 level DWT decomposition (Mallat algorithm).

**Step 4:** Estimate noise variance and compute threshold (e.g., Donoho's universal threshold):

$$
\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply soft thresholding to detail coefficients:

$$
\hat{cD}_{m,n} = \begin{cases} \text{sign}(cD_{m,n})(|cD_{m,n}| - \lambda), & \text{if } |cD_{m,n}| > \lambda \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6:** Reconstruct the ECG via inverse DWT using modified coefficients.

**Step 7:** The output is a denoised ECG with preserved diagnostic features.

**Biomedical Justification:** Wavelet denoising preserves QRS morphology and ST-segment features critical for diagnosing myocardial infarction, making it superior to traditional IIR/FIR low-pass filtering.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$ normalization** in the CWT formula — Examiners specifically look for this term. Missing it = 1 mark lost.
>
> 2. **Confusing CWT with STFT:** CWT has *variable* resolution; STFT has *fixed* resolution. Students often write "wavelets have better resolution" without specifying "at high frequencies for time, at low frequencies for frequency."
>
> 3. **Wrong filter relationship:** Writing $g(k) = -h(k)$ instead of $g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)$. The latter is the correct **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF)** relation.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $cA_n$ and $cD_n$:** $cA$ = Approximation (low-frequency), $cD$ = Detail (high-frequency). Reversing these = lose 2 marks easily.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning compact support** when defining the mother wavelet. This is a KTU-favorite keyword worth 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping the admissibility condition** in CWT questions — guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 7. **In 14-mark questions, students often forget to draw the block diagram** for DWT filter bank. Without diagram, expect at least 3-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wavelet Transform**

✅ **Definition:** Wavelet Transform = time-frequency decomposition using scaled and translated wavelets.

✅ **Mother Wavelet ($\psi(t)$):** Prototype, must satisfy **admissibility condition**, **zero mean**, **compact support**, **finite energy**.

✅ **Daughter Wavelet:** $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \psi\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$

✅ **Scaling parameter $a$:** Controls frequency — *small $a$ = high frequency, large $a$ = low frequency*.

✅ **Translation parameter $b$:** Controls time location.

✅ **CWT formula:** $CWT(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int x(t) \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$

✅ **Inverse CWT:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_\psi} \iint CWT(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$

✅ **DWT:** Uses dyadic sampling $a = 2^m$, $b = n \cdot 2^m$.

✅ **MRA:** Decomposes signal into approximation ($cA$) + details ($cD$) at multiple levels.

✅ **Two-Scale Equation:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_k h(k) \phi(2t - k)$

✅ **QMF Condition:** $g(k) = (-1)^k h(1-k)$

✅ **DWT Implementation:** Mallat algorithm using low-pass + high-pass filters + downsampling.

✅ **Admissibility Condition:** $C_\psi = \int \frac{|\Psi(\omega)|^2}{|\omega|} d\omega < \infty$

✅ **Biomedical Applications:** ECG denoising, R-peak detection (db4), EEG feature extraction (seizure), PCG analysis (S1/S2 sounds), EMG decomposition.

✅ **Best Wavelets for ECG:** `db4`, `db6`, `sym4` — compact support matches QRS shape.

✅ **Advantage over STFT:** Adaptive resolution (multi-resolution analysis).

✅ **Denoising Steps:** DWT → Threshold detail coefficients → Inverse DWT → Denoised signal.

✅ **Universal Threshold (Donoho):** $\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalization of the Short-Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generalization of the Short-Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is a time-frequency analysis technique that decomposes a non-stationary signal $x(t)$ into a family of localized frequency components by multiplying the signal with a sliding window function $w(t-\tau)$ before applying the Fourier Transform. The **Generalization of the STFT** extends this classical definition beyond fixed-resolution analysis by unifying STFT, filter banks, Gabor expansions, and bilinear time-frequency distributions into a single mathematical framework governed by the **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle**.

The classical STFT is mathematically defined as:

$$X(\tau,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^*(t-\tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

where $w^*(t-\tau)$ is the complex conjugate of the window function centered at time $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization Theorem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement):**
> The STFT generalizes into four key frameworks: (i) the **spectrogram** $\vert X(\tau,\omega) \vert^2$, (ii) the **Gabor expansion** (signal reconstruction via bi-orthogonal basis), (iii) the **filter bank interpretation**, and (iv) the **Cohen class of bilinear distributions** (e.g., Wigner-Ville).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moving Spotlight" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Concert Searchlight**
> Imagine you are at a large outdoor concert, and you are trying to identify the different musical instruments being played. The orchestra is continuously changing — violins rise, drums fade, flutes enter. You cannot analyze the entire orchestra at once because the music is non-stationary.
>
> - A **stationary Fourier Transform** is like turning on the stadium lights — you see everything at once, but you cannot tell *when* each instrument played.
> - A **basic STFT** is like sweeping a narrow searchlight across the stage. You capture a "snapshot" of the spectrum at each instant.
> - The **Generalized STFT** lets you change the *width* of the spotlight dynamically — a wide beam for low frequencies (where you can afford coarse time precision) and a narrow beam for high frequencies (where you need fine time precision). This is the foundation of time-frequency analysis.
>
> For biomedical signals like the **ECG (electrocardiogram)**, the QRS complex is a short transient requiring fine time resolution, while baseline wander is a slow phenomenon requiring fine frequency resolution. Generalization provides this adaptive ability.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The fundamental metric governing all time-frequency analysis is the **time-bandwidth product** (also called the **Heisenberg constant**), which has a lower bound for Gaussian windows:

$$\sigma_t \, \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

where $\sigma_t$ and $\sigma_f$ are the standard deviations of the window in time and frequency domains, respectively. Equality holds **only** for the **Gaussian window**, making it the optimal choice for joint localization.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Examiners:** A *narrow* window gives **good time resolution, poor frequency resolution**; a *wide* window gives **good frequency resolution, poor time resolution**. This is the heart of the STFT generalization trade-off.
> 

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation of the Generalization

**Step 1 — The Limitation of the Classical Fourier Transform**
- The standard FT $X(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ assumes signal stationarity. It tells us *what* frequencies exist but not *when* they occur.
- For biomedical signals (EEG, ECG, EMG, PCG), this is a fatal limitation.

**Step 2 — Localizing the Analysis (Birth of STFT)**
- Dennis Gabor (1946) proposed multiplying $x(t)$ by a sliding window $w(t-\tau)$ to make the signal "locally stationary."
- The parameter $\tau$ provides the *time localization* axis.
- The Fourier integral over the windowed segment provides the *frequency localization* axis.

**Step 3 — Why Generalize?**
- STFT has **fixed resolution** (same window for all frequencies). The Heisenberg principle makes it impossible to achieve arbitrarily fine resolution in *both* time and frequency.
- A generalized framework allows: variable windows, non-uniform tilings, and energy distributions.

**Step 4 — Four Pillars of the Generalized Framework**
- **Pillar A — Spectrogram:** $\vert X(\tau,\omega) \vert^2$. Energy density, always non-negative.
- **Pillar B — Gabor Expansion:** $x(t) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} C_{m,n} \, g(t-mT) \, e^{jn\Omega t}$. Coefficients $C_{m,n}$ form a discrete lattice.
- **Pillar C — Filter Bank Interpretation:** STFT = output of a bank of bandpass filters (one per frequency bin) followed by envelope detection.
- **Pillar D — Cohen's Class:** A unified family of bilinear distributions $C_x(t,\omega) = \iiint \phi(\theta,\tau) \, x(u+\tau/2) \, x^*(u-\tau/2) \, e^{-j\theta u} e^{-j\tau\omega} e^{j\theta t} \, du \, d\tau \, d\theta$ with kernel $\phi(\theta,\tau)$.

**Step 5 — From STFT to Wavelets (The Multi-Resolution Leap)**
- The continuous wavelet transform (CWT) uses a *scalable* mother wavelet $\psi((t-\tau)/s)$, replacing the fixed STFT window with an adaptive one — direct descendant of the STFT generalization.

**Step 6 — Practical Engineering Utility**
- **ECG analysis:** QRS detection via spectrogram ridges.
- **EEG rhythms:** Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz) — extracted by time-varying spectral analysis.
- **Heart sound (PCG) analysis:** S1/S2 murmur separation using spectrograms.
- **EMG fatigue studies:** Median frequency shift in time-frequency maps.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are board-exam critical. Memorize the boxed equations and units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Classical STFT (Forward) | $X(\tau,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) w^*(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | $\tau$ in seconds, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 2 | STFT (Discrete-time) | $X(m,k) = \sum_{n} x(n) w(n-mH) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | $H$ = hop size, $N$ = FFT length |
| 3 | Spectrogram (Energy) | $S_x(\tau,\omega) = \vert X(\tau,\omega) \vert^2$ | Non-negative, real-valued |
| 4 | Gabor Expansion (Synthesis) | $x(t) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_{m,n} g(t-mT) e^{jn\Omega t}$ | $T\Omega \leq 2\pi$ (Balian-Low) |
| 5 | Gabor Coefficient | $C_{m,n} = \int x(t) \gamma^*(t-mT) e^{-jn\Omega t} dt$ | $\gamma$ = dual window (bi-orthogonal) |
| 6 | Heisenberg Uncertainty | $\sigma_t \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | Equality for Gaussian window |
| 7 | STFT as Filter Bank | $X(\tau,\omega) = (x(\tau) * h_\omega(\tau))$, $h_\omega(t) = w(-t) e^{j\omega t}$ | Impulse response of bandpass filter |
| 8 | Wigner-Ville Distribution | $W_x(t,\omega) = \int x(t+\tau/2) x^*(t-\tau/2) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$ | Bilinear, no windowing artifacts |
| 9 | Cohen's Class | $C_x(t,\omega,\phi) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \iiint e^{j\theta(s-t)} \phi(\theta,\tau) x(s+\tau/2) x^*(s-\tau/2) e^{-j\omega\tau} ds\, d\tau\, d\theta$ | $\phi$ = kernel (Wigner = $\phi=1$) |
| 10 | Time-Frequency Tile | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \text{constant}$ (fixed for STFT) | Area preserved (uncertainty) |
| 11 | Spectrogram vs. WVD | $S_x(t,\omega) = \iint W_x(s,\xi) W_w(t-s,\omega-\xi) ds\, d\xi$ | Spectrogram = smoothed WVD |
| 12 | Reconstruction (Inverse STFT) | $x(t) = \frac{1}{E_w} \int\!\!\int X(\tau,\omega) w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$ | $E_w = \int \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often confuse the **spectrogram** with the **Wigner-Ville distribution**. The spectrogram is the *magnitude squared STFT* and is always non-negative, but suffers from the time-frequency trade-off. WVD has superior resolution but contains *cross-terms* (interference) for multi-component signals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: STFT as a Filter Bank Output

We start from the classical STFT definition and show that it is mathematically equivalent to passing $x(t)$ through a bandpass filter, then sampling the output at the time index $\tau$.

**Starting Point:**

$$X(\tau,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^*(t-\tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the substitution $u = t - \tau$, so $t = u + \tau$ and $dt = du$:**

$$X(\tau,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(u+\tau) \, w^*(u) \, e^{-j\omega(u+\tau)} \, du$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the constant phase term $e^{-j\omega\tau}$ (it is independent of the integration variable $u$):**

$$X(\tau,\omega) = e^{-j\omega\tau} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(u+\tau) \, w^*(u) \, e^{-j\omega u} \, du$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integral as a convolution evaluated at $\tau$. Recall the definition of convolution: $(x * h)(\tau) = \int x(\tau - u) h(u) du$. Let $h_\omega(u) = w(-u) e^{j\omega u}$. Then the integral becomes a convolution between $x$ and $h_\omega$:**

$$X(\tau,\omega) = e^{-j\omega\tau} \, (x * h_\omega)(\tau)$$

**Step 4 — The filter interpretation. The function $h_\omega(t) = w(-t) e^{j\omega t}$ is the impulse response of a bandpass filter centered at frequency $\omega$, with envelope shaped by the time-reversed window $w(-t)$. Therefore:**

$$\boxed{X(\tau,\omega) = e^{-j\omega\tau} \, \left[ x(t) * \left( w(-t) e^{j\omega t} \right) \right]_{t=\tau}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The STFT is the complex envelope of the bandpass-filtered version of $x(t)$. The full STFT is equivalent to running $x(t)$ through a parallel bank of bandpass filters tuned to $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_K$ and recording each output's envelope at each time $\tau$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Spectrogram as a Smoothed Wigner-Ville Distribution

This derivation proves the foundational relationship that connects the STFT generalization to bilinear time-frequency analysis.

**Starting Point:** Define the **Wigner-Ville Distribution (WVD)** of $x(t)$:

$$W_x(t,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^*\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Express the WVD of the windowed signal $x(t) w(t-\tau)$. The short-time section around time $t_0$ is $x_t(u) = x(u) w(u - t_0)$:**

$$W_{x_t}(t,\omega) = \int x\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) w\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2} - t_0\right) x^*\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) w^*\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2} - t_0\right) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Make the substitution $\theta = t - t_0$ (the offset from window center). Recognize that the resulting integral is a two-dimensional convolution in the time-frequency plane between $W_x$ and $W_w$:**

$$S_x(t_0, \omega) = \iint W_x(s, \xi) \, W_w(t_0 - s, \omega - \xi) \, ds \, d\xi$$

**Step 3 — Equivalently, using the symbol $\ast\ast$ to denote 2-D convolution in $(t,\omega)$:**

$$\boxed{S_x(t,\omega) = W_x(t,\omega) \ast\ast W_w(t,\omega)}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight.** The spectrogram is the **2-D convolution** of the WVD of the signal with the WVD of the window. This means the spectrogram *smooths* the WVD, eliminating the oscillatory cross-terms at the cost of reduced joint resolution. The Gaussian window is the unique choice for which the WVD remains non-negative everywhere — the *only* window for which smoothing cannot introduce negative values.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Gabor Expansion Synthesis Equation

The Gabor expansion reconstructs a signal from its time-frequency coefficients on a discrete lattice.

**Starting Point:** Define the set of Gabor coefficients:

$$C_{m,n} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \gamma^*(t - mT) \, e^{-jn\Omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Postulate the expansion ansatz. A signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as a doubly-weighted sum of shifted and modulated copies of the *synthesis* window $g(t)$:**

$$x(t) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_{m,n} \, g(t - mT) \, e^{jn\Omega t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the coefficient expression into the synthesis formula. We aim to find conditions on $\gamma(t)$ and $g(t)$ that make this identity true for any $x(t)$. This requires a *bi-orthogonality* condition:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t) \, \gamma^*(t - mT) \, e^{jn\Omega t} \, dt = \delta_{m} \delta_{n}$$

**Step 3 — Taking the 2-D Fourier transform of both sides of the bi-orthogonality condition yields the **Balian-Low Theorem** constraint:**

$$\boxed{T \cdot \Omega \leq 2\pi}$$

with critical sampling achieved at $T\Omega = 2\pi$. For $T\Omega < 2\pi$, the expansion is *over-complete* (redundant); for $T\Omega > 2\pi$, it is *under-complete* (lossy).

**Step 4 — Practical biomedical example.** In ECG analysis, choosing $T = 10$ ms and $\Omega = 2\pi(100)$ rad/s gives $T\Omega = 2\pi$ (critical sampling), allowing perfect reconstruction from 100 Gabor coefficients per second per Hz — ideal for compressed ECG storage.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Time-Frequency Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions asking to "compare and discuss" time-frequency methods.

| Property | STFT (Spectrogram) | Wigner-Ville | Wavelet Transform | Gabor Expansion |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Resolution Type** | Fixed (uniform tiling) | Optimal (no window) | Adaptive (multi-resolution) | Fixed lattice |
| **Time-Freq Tile** | Rectangle ($\Delta t \times \Delta f$ = const) | Point-like | Variable area ($\Delta t \Delta f$ = const) | Rectangle |
| **Cross-terms** | None (smoothing removes them) | Severe (oscillatory) | Low | Low |
| **Marginal Conditions** | Approximate | Exactly satisfied | Not satisfied | Exact (with care) |
| **Reconstruction** | Yes (with overlap) | No (not invertible directly) | Yes (CWT with admissibility) | Yes (exact) |
| **Biomedical Use** | Heart sound spectrograms, EEG bands | Vibration analysis of implants | QRS detection, seizure spikes | Compressed ECG storage |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (FFT-based) | Medium (2 FFTs) | Medium (filter bank) | High (bi-orthogonal search) |
| **Negative Values?** | No (it's an energy) | Yes (interference) | No (modulus) | No (coefficients can be complex) |
| **Kernel Function** | $W_w$ (Wigner of window) | $\phi = 1$ | $\psi$ (mother wavelet) | $\gamma$ (dual window) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
