### 4.1 Block Diagram: STFT as a Filter Bank Interpretation

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how the STFT is realized in hardware/software as a parallel bank of bandpass filters followed by envelope detectors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Input Signal x of t] --> fbankA{Filter Bank}
    fbankA --> filt1A[Bandpass Filter 1 - omega 1]
    fbankA --> filt2A[Bandpass Filter 2 - omega 2]
    fbankA --> filt3A[Bandpass Filter 3 - omega 3]
    fbankA --> filtNA[Bandpass Filter N - omega N]
    filt1A --> env1A[Envelope Detector]
    filt2A --> env2A[Envelope Detector]
    filt3A --> env3A[Envelope Detector]
    filtNA --> envNA[Envelope Detector]
    env1A --> out1A[STFT Bin X tau omega 1]
    env2A --> out2A[STFT Bin X tau omega 2]
    env3A --> out3A[STFT Bin X tau omega 3]
    envNA --> outNA[STFT Bin X tau omega N]
    out1A --> displayA[Time Frequency Representation S tau omega]
    out2A --> displayA
    out3A --> displayA
    outNA --> displayA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Each filter is centered at a different frequency $\omega_k$ with bandwidth determined by the window $w(t)$. The output of each filter, when squared and summed across filters at a given time $\tau$, yields the short-time energy at that instant. This is the Filter Bank Summation Method (FBSM) of time-frequency analysis.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the Generalized STFT Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    signalA[Biomedical Signal x of t - ECG EEG EMG] --> preA[Preprocessing - DC removal - Bandpass filtering]
    preA --> winA[Window Selection - Hamming - Hanning - Gaussian - Kaiser]
    winA --> stftCoreA[STFT Engine - Sliding FFT - Hop size H]
    stftCoreA --> specA[Spectrogram - Magnitude Squared]
    stftCoreA --> gaborA[Gabor Coefficients - Bi-orthogonal]
    stftCoreA --> wvdA[Wigner-Ville - Bilinear]
    specA --> visA[Visualization - 2D heatmap - 3D surface]
    gaborA --> recoA[Signal Reconstruction - Inverse Gabor]
    wvdA --> smoothA[Cohen Class Smoothing - Choi-Williams kernel - Born-Jordan kernel]
    smoothA --> visA
    recoA --> evalA[Performance Metrics - RMSE - PRD - Compression ratio]
    visA --> diagA[Clinical Diagnosis - QRS detection - Seizure onset - Murmur ID]
```

---

### 4.3 Time-Frequency Tiling Diagram (Conceptual Schematic)

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the conceptual difference in time-frequency plane coverage between STFT and Wavelets — a frequently tested concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STFT_TILE[STFT TILING - FIXED RESOLUTION]
        direction LR
        stftTile1A[Low Freq Wide Time] --- stftTile2A[Mid Freq Wide Time] --- stftTile3A[High Freq Wide Time]
    end
    subgraph WT_TILE[WAVELET TILING - ADAPTIVE RESOLUTION]
        direction LR
        wtTile1A[Low Freq Wide Time Narrow Freq] --- wtTile2A[Mid Freq Medium Time Medium Freq] --- wtTile3A[High Freq Narrow Time Wide Freq]
    end
    STFT_TILE ~~~ WT_TILE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Rule:** STFT tiles are **uniform rectangles** of equal area (since $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ is constant). Wavelet tiles are **variable** — tall and narrow at high frequencies (good time resolution), short and wide at low frequencies (good frequency resolution). This is the *raison d'être* of the STFT-to-Wavelet generalization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of a discrete-time signal $x(n)$. What is the role of the window function $w(n)$ in the transform?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The discrete-time STFT is defined as:

$$X(m, k) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, w(n - mH) \, e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

where $m$ is the discrete time index, $k$ is the discrete frequency index, $H$ is the hop size (samples between successive windows), and $N$ is the FFT length.

**Role of the window function $w(n)$ (1 mark each point):**
1. The window **localizes** the signal in time, extracting a finite-duration segment around index $mH$ so that the local spectral content can be analyzed.
2. The window **controls the trade-off** between time resolution and frequency resolution. A narrow window provides fine time localization but coarse frequency localization, while a wide window provides the opposite.
3. The window **tapers the signal** to zero at the segment boundaries, reducing spectral leakage (Gibbs phenomenon) caused by the implicit rectangular truncation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and explain the Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty principle for the STFT. Mention the window that achieves the lower bound.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty principle states that the product of the time spread $\sigma_t$ and the frequency spread $\sigma_f$ of a windowed signal is bounded below by a constant:

$$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- $\sigma_t$ is the standard deviation (RMS duration) of the window $w(t)$, defined as $\sigma_t^2 = \int (t - \mu_t)^2 \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt / E_w$.
- $\sigma_f$ is the corresponding standard deviation in the frequency domain.
- The inequality is a fundamental mathematical property of the Fourier Transform pair; it cannot be circumvented by any choice of window.
- **Equality is achieved if and only if the window is a Gaussian:** $w(t) = e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$.
- This makes the Gaussian the *optimal* window for joint time-frequency localization and is the basis of the **Gabor transform**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the continuous-time STFT definition, derive the **filter bank interpretation** of the STFT. Show that the STFT at frequency $\omega$ is the complex envelope of $x(t)$ passed through a bandpass filter with impulse response $h_\omega(t) = w(-t) e^{j\omega t}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ with $f_0 = 50$ Hz, sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz, and analyzed with a Hamming window of length $N = 64$ samples, compute:
   (i) the spectrogram value at $t = 0$ and $f = 50$ Hz,
   (ii) the spectrogram value at $t = 0$ and $f = 100$ Hz.
   Discuss the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Filter Bank Interpretation — Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — Starting definition:**

$$X(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^*(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $u = t - \tau$:**

$$X(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(u + \tau) \, w^*(u) \, e^{-j\omega(u + \tau)} \, du$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant $e^{-j\omega\tau}$:**

$$X(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega\tau} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(u + \tau) \, w^*(u) \, e^{-j\omega u} \, du$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the convolution form. Define $h_\omega(u) = w(-u) e^{j\omega u}$, so $h_\omega^*(u) = w^*(-u) e^{-j\omega u}$. The integral becomes $(x * h_\omega^*)(\tau) = \int x(\tau - u) h_\omega^*(u) du$. Substituting $\tau - u = \tau + s$ (with $s = -u$) leads to:**

$$X(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega\tau} \left[ x(t) * h_\omega(t) \right]_{t = \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** $h_\omega(t)$ is the impulse response of a bandpass filter centered at $\omega$ with envelope determined by the time-reversed window $w(-t)$. The term $e^{-j\omega\tau}$ is a complex phase factor. Hence, the magnitude $\vert X(\tau, \omega) \vert$ is the *envelope* of the bandpass-filtered signal at frequency $\omega$ and time $\tau$. (Full 7 marks awarded for complete derivation + interpretation.)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $x(t) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t)$, $f_s = 500$ Hz, $N = 64$.

**Step 1 — Normalize frequencies.** $f_0 = 50$ Hz corresponds to bin $k_0 = f_0 / f_s \cdot N = 50/500 \cdot 64 = 6.4$ bins (non-integer, leakage expected).

**Step 2 — Discrete STFT at $m = 0$ (centered at $t = 0$).** The windowed segment is $x(n) w(n)$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, 63$.

**Step 3 — Spectrogram at $f = 50$ Hz (bin $k = 6.4$, nearest integer $k = 6$ or $k = 7$):**
Since the signal is a pure cosine centered in the window, the FFT magnitude at $k = 6$ (corresponding to 46.875 Hz) is close to the peak. The Hamming window energy $E_w \approx 0.397 \cdot N$ for unity-amplitude cosine input. The expected peak magnitude is:

$$\vert X(0, k_0) \vert \approx \frac{N \cdot A}{2} \cdot \text{(window coherent gain)} = \frac{64 \cdot 1}{2} \cdot 0.54 \approx 17.3$$

**Step 4 — Spectrogram value at $f = 50$ Hz (after peak picking):**

$$S_x(0, 50) = \vert X(0, 6.4) \vert^2 \approx 17.3^2 \approx 299.3 \text{ (linear units)}$$

In dB: $S_x = 10 \log_{10}(299.3) \approx 24.8$ dB.

**Step 5 — Spectrogram at $f = 100$ Hz (bin $k = 12.8$).** Since the input contains no 100 Hz component, ideally $S_x(0, 100) = 0$. However, due to spectral leakage from the Hamming window's sidelobes ($-46$ dB peak sidelobe level), there will be a small non-zero value:

$$S_x(0, 100) \approx 0 + \text{sidelobe leakage} \approx 10^{-46/10} \cdot 299.3 \approx 0.001$$

**Step 6 — Discussion.** The 50 Hz tone produces a strong spectrogram peak (~24.8 dB), while the 100 Hz location contains only sidelobe leakage (≈ $-22$ dB below the peak). This confirms the STFT's ability to correctly identify the frequency content of a stationary tone. For non-stationary biomedical signals, the spectrogram is plotted as a 2-D image of $\vert X(m, k) \vert^2$ versus $(m, k)$, revealing time-varying spectral content such as QRS complexes in ECG or seizure spikes in EEG.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the necessary mathematical expressions, explain the **Gabor expansion** of a signal. State the Balian-Low theorem and discuss its implications for bi-orthogonal window design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz, design a Gabor lattice to extract features in the 0.5–40 Hz band. Choose appropriate time and frequency sampling intervals $T$ and $\Omega$, and justify the choice. How many Gabor coefficients per second are needed? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gabor Expansion — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Gabor expansion** represents a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ as a weighted sum of time-shifted and frequency-modulated versions of a synthesis window $g(t)$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_{m,n} \, g(t - mT) \, e^{jn\Omega t}$$

The coefficients $C_{m,n}$ are obtained via the *analysis* equation using a dual window $\gamma(t)$:

$$C_{m,n} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \gamma^*(t - mT) \, e^{-jn\Omega t} \, dt$$

**Bi-orthogonality condition (required for perfect reconstruction):**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t) \, \gamma^*(t - mT) \, e^{jn\Omega t} \, dt = \delta_m \delta_n$$

**Balian-Low Theorem (2 marks):** For an orthogonal Gabor system (i.e., $g = \gamma$ up to scaling) at the critical density $T \cdot \Omega = 2\pi$, the synthesis window $g(t)$ **cannot** be well-localized in both time and frequency. Specifically, if $g \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and $\{g(t - mT) e^{jn\Omega t}\}$ is an orthonormal basis, then either $\int t^2 \vert g(t) \vert^2 dt = \infty$ (bad time localization) or $\int \omega^2 \vert \hat{g}(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega = \infty$ (bad frequency localization).

**Implications (2 marks):**
1. A single well-localized orthonormal Gabor basis **does not exist** at critical density. This is a direct consequence of the Balian-Low theorem combined with the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.
2. In practice, one must choose: (a) use bi-orthogonal windows (different analysis and synthesis), (b) work at over-critical density ($T\Omega < 2\pi$, i.e., redundant), or (c) sacrifice perfect reconstruction for better localization.
3. Modern biomedical applications (e.g., compressed ECG sensing) use **oversampled Gabor frames** with smooth, well-localized B-spline or Gaussian-derived windows.

---

**(b) Gabor Lattice Design for ECG (0.5–40 Hz):**

**Step 1 — Frequency resolution requirement.** To resolve features in the 0.5–40 Hz band, the frequency spacing $\Omega/(2\pi)$ must be small enough to capture the lowest frequency variation. A typical choice is $\Omega/(2\pi) = 0.5$ Hz spacing, giving:

$$\Omega = 2\pi \cdot 0.5 = \pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Time resolution requirement.** ECG features (QRS complex ≈ 80–120 ms) require time resolution of ~10 ms. Choose $T = 10$ ms = 0.01 s.

**Step 3 — Check Balian-Low constraint.**

$$T \cdot \Omega = 0.01 \cdot \pi \approx 0.0314 \text{ rad}$$

Compare to $2\pi \approx 6.28$ rad. Since $T\Omega \ll 2\pi$, the system is **over-complete** (redundant) — about $200\times$ over-sampling. This is acceptable and provides robustness to noise in biomedical signals.

**Step 4 — Number of Gabor coefficients per second.** Coefficients per second in time: $1/T = 100$ coefficients/s. Coefficients per second in frequency (within 0.5–40 Hz band, 40 Hz wide): $40 \text{ Hz} / 0.5 \text{ Hz} = 80$ frequency bins.

$$\boxed{\text{Total coefficients per second} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{40}{\Omega/(2\pi)} = 100 \cdot 80 = 8000 \text{ coefficients/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Justification.** The over-complete design (200× redundant) trades coefficient count for **robustness against noise and artifacts** typical in ECG recordings. The redundancy allows denoising by thresholding small coefficients and supports the recovery of QRS complexes, P and T waves with high fidelity. For a 10-second ECG strip, this yields 80,000 Gabor coefficients — a manageable number for real-time DSP implementation on modern microcontrollers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up the Wigner-Ville definition:** The correct WVD uses $x(t+\tau/2)x^*(t-\tau/2)$, not $x(t+\tau)x^*(t-\tau)$. The factor of 1/2 inside the argument is critical and frequently forgotten.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** When computing spectrograms, students often omit the normalization by window energy $E_w = \sum \vert w(n) \vert^2$, leading to incorrect magnitudes.
> 3. **Misstating the Balian-Low Theorem:** It is a statement about *orthonormal* Gabor systems, not all Gabor systems. Bi-orthogonal systems with different analysis/synthesis windows can evade the theorem.
> 4. **Confusing spectrogram with the periodogram:** The spectrogram uses a sliding window; the periodogram is the squared magnitude of the full-signal FFT (no localization).
> 5. **Wrong Balian-Low inequality direction:** It is $T\Omega \leq 2\pi$, not $\geq 2\pi$. Critical density occurs at equality; over-sampling (redundancy) is at $<2\pi$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final Read Before Exam)**

- [x] **STFT definition:** $X(\tau,\omega) = \int x(t) w^*(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ — three variables: time $\tau$, frequency $\omega$, and signal $t$.
- [x] **Spectrogram = $\vert \text{STFT} \vert^2$** — always non-negative, real-valued, energy density.
- [x] **Heisenberg bound:** $\sigma_t \sigma_f \geq 1/(4\pi)$; equality only for Gaussian window.
- [x] **Narrow window = good time, bad frequency.** Wide window = good frequency, bad time.
- [x] **Gabor expansion:** doubly-discrete lattice $(mT, n\Omega)$; Balian-Low at $T\Omega = 2\pi$.
- [x] **WVD:** bilinear, no window artifacts, but has cross-terms for multi-component signals.
- [x] **Spectrogram = 2-D convolution of WVDs** of signal and window in $(t,\omega)$ plane.
- [x] **Filter bank interpretation:** STFT = output of bandpass filter bank with envelope detection.
- [x] **Cohen's class:** unified family parameterized by kernel $\phi(\theta, \tau)$; WVD = $\phi = 1$.
- [x] **STFT tiling:** fixed-area rectangles; Wavelet tiling: variable area (multi-resolution).
- [x] **Biomedical relevance:** ECG QRS, EEG rhythms, EMG fatigue, PCG murmurs — all analyzed via generalized STFT.
- [x] **Reconstruction:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{E_w} \iint X(\tau,\omega) w(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$ — provided windows overlap properly.
- [x] **In biomedical engineering:** spectrograms are the *primary* clinical visualization for time-varying spectral content of physiological signals.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Transform and Discrete Filter Banks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Transform and Discrete Filter Banks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wavelet Transform — Formal Definition

The **Wavelet Transform (WT)** is a mathematical time-frequency decomposition tool that represents a signal $x(t)$ as a linear combination of a set of basis functions called **wavelets**. Unlike the Fourier Transform which uses globally supported sinusoids (infinite duration), wavelets are compactly supported, oscillatory, and localized in both **time** and **frequency**.

The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** is formally defined as:

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

where:
- $\psi(t)$ = **mother wavelet** (basis function)
- $a$ = **scale parameter** (inverse of frequency, $a \in \mathbb{R}, a \neq 0$)
- $b$ = **translation parameter** (time shift, $b \in \mathbb{R}$)
- $\psi^{*}$ = complex conjugate of $\psi(t)$
- $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ = normalization factor for energy preservation

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform (ICWT):**
> 
> $$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} CWT_x(a,b)\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \frac{da\,db}{a^2}$$
> 
> provided the **admissibility condition** holds:
> 
> $$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega}\, d\omega < \infty$$
> 
> where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Microscope of Signals"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Musical Score vs. Audio Recording:**
> 
> Imagine listening to a symphony. A **Fourier Transform** tells you *which notes* are being played (the frequencies), but loses information about *when* they are played. 
> 
> The **Wavelet Transform** is like having a **musical score** — it tells you both *which note* (frequency content) and *at what time* (localization) it appears. Even better, the **scale parameter** acts like a **zoom lens on a microscope**: 
> - **Small $a$ (high frequency)** → zoom in to see fine, rapid transients (like the percussive strike of a drum, or the QRS complex in an ECG).
> - **Large $a$ (low frequency)** → zoom out to see slow, global trends (like baseline drift in an ECG, or the respiratory envelope).
> 
> This **multi-resolution property** is the single most important advantage of wavelets in biomedical signal processing, where signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) contain both sharp transients and slow oscillations simultaneously.

---

## 1.3 Discrete Filter Banks — Formal Definition

A **Filter Bank** is a set of band-pass filters that split a signal $x(n)$ into several sub-band components, each occupying a different frequency region. In the context of wavelets, we focus on the **Two-Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank**, which consists of:

- A **Low-Pass Filter** $H_0(z)$ → extracts the **approximation** (low-frequency content)
- A **High-Pass Filter** $H_1(z)$ → extracts the **detail** (high-frequency content)

The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** is implemented precisely by cascading such two-channel filter banks with **dyadic downsampling**, as formalized in **Mallat's Algorithm (1989)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank Relation:**
> 
> $$H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$$
> 
> This means the high-pass filter's magnitude response is the **mirror image** of the low-pass filter about the frequency $\omega = \pi/2$. The QMF condition is the cornerstone of alias cancellation in perfect reconstruction systems.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** | **Hz** (e.g., 360 Hz for ECG) | Governs Nyquist limit |
| **Scale Parameter ($a$)** | Dyadic: $a = 2^j$ | $j \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| **Translation ($b$)** | $b = k \cdot 2^j$ | $j,k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| **Filter Order ($N$)** | Integer | Determines transition band |
| **Reconstruction Error ($E_{PR}$)** | $0$ (ideal) | Measures perfect reconstruction |
| **Regularity Order** | $\geq 1$ | Smoothness of scaling function |
| **Number of Vanishing Moments** | Integer $p$ | Polynomial suppression order |
| **Computation Complexity (DWT)** | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Linear in signal length |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Wavelet Transform? — Limitations of STFT and FT

The classical **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** uses a fixed window $w(t)$:

$$STFT_x(\tau,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

Its critical limitation: a **fixed window size** means uniform time-frequency resolution — you cannot simultaneously localize short transients and long oscillations. The **Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty principle** enforces:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

The Wavelet Transform sidesteps this by using **variable-sized windows** — short windows for high frequencies and long windows for low frequencies — giving **logarithmic (multi-resolution) frequency resolution**.

---

## 2.2 Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) — The Theoretical Backbone

A **Multi-Resolution Analysis** of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ consists of a sequence of closed subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ satisfying:

1. **Nested:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ (finer scale is contained in coarser scale)
2. **Density:** $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
3. **Separation:** $\bigcap_j V_j = \{0\}$
4. **Scale Invariance:** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$
5. **Translation Invariance:** $f(t) \in V_0 \iff f(t-k) \in V_0$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$

There exists a **scaling function** $\phi(t) \in V_0$ such that $\{\phi(t-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis for $V_0$. The **wavelet function** $\psi(t)$ is then defined in the **detail space** $W_j$ (orthogonal complement of $V_{j+1}$ in $V_j$):

$$V_j = V_{j+1} \oplus W_{j+1}$$

---

## 2.3 The Two-Scale (Dilation) Equation

The scaling function and wavelet function satisfy the **two-scale relation**:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_0[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_1[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

where $h_0[n]$ and $h_1[n]$ are the **low-pass** and **high-pass** filter coefficients, respectively. The wavelet coefficients are recovered via the **inner product**:

$$c_j[k] = \langle x(t), \phi_{j,k}(t) \rangle, \quad d_j[k] = \langle x(t), \psi_{j,k}(t) \rangle$$

with basis functions $\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}t - k)$ and $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j}t - k)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE — Commit to Memory**

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | KTU Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\,\psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | Wavelet basis function | CWT definition |
| 2 | $C_{\psi} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Admissibility condition | Mother wavelet validity |
| 3 | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | QMF condition (analysis) | Filter bank design |
| 4 | $G_0(z) = 2\,H_1(-z)$, $\;G_1(z) = -2\,H_0(-z)$ | Synthesis filters (PR-QMF) | Perfect reconstruction |
| 5 | $H_0(z)\,G_0(z) + H_1(z)\,G_1(z) = 2z^{-l}$ | Perfect reconstruction (no alias) | PR condition (1) |
| 6 | $H_0(z)\,G_0(-z) + H_1(z)\,G_1(-z) = 0$ | Alias cancellation | PR condition (2) |
| 7 | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega-\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | Power symmetry (CQF) | Daubechies / orthogonal wavelets |
| 8 | $h_1[n] = (-1)^n h_0[N-1-n]$ | High-pass from low-pass (QMF) | Filter coefficient relation |
| 9 | $a = 2^j,\; b = k\cdot 2^j$ | Dyadic sampling (DWT) | Discrete wavelet grid |
| 10 | $c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n h_0[n-2k]\, c_j[n]$ | Mallat's algorithm (decomposition) | DWT computation |
| 11 | $d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n h_1[n-2k]\, c_j[n]$ | Mallat's algorithm (detail) | DWT computation |
| 12 | $c_j[n] = \sum_k \big( g_0[n-2k]\, c_{j+1}[k] + g_1[n-2k]\, d_{j+1}[k] \big)$ | Inverse DWT (reconstruction) | IDWT computation |
| 13 | $\int \psi(t)\, dt = 0$ | Zero mean of wavelet | Band-pass nature |
| 14 | $\int t^m \psi(t)\, dt = 0$, $\;m = 0,1,\dots,p-1$ | $p$ vanishing moments | Polynomial suppression |
| 15 | $E_{PR} = \sum_n \big(x[n] - \hat{x}[n]\big)^2$ | Reconstruction error | Filter bank evaluation |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

In **biomedical signal processing**, wavelets and filter banks are indispensable because biological signals are inherently **non-stationary**:

- **ECG Denoising:** Wavelet thresholding (Donoho's soft/hard thresholding) removes powerline interference (50/60 Hz) and muscle noise while preserving QRS morphology.
- **EEG Rhythm Extraction:** Dyadic decomposition isolates delta (0.5–4 Hz), theta (4–8 Hz), alpha (8–13 Hz), beta (13–30 Hz), gamma (>30 Hz) bands via cascading QMF banks.
- **PCG/Heart Sound Analysis:** Detail coefficients at scale $2^3$–$2^5$ capture S1, S2, murmurs, and clicks.
- **EMG Decomposition:** Wavelet packet trees separate motor unit action potentials.
- **Feature Extraction for Classification:** Energy, entropy, and statistical moments of wavelet sub-bands serve as ML-ready features for arrhythmia, seizure, and sleep-stage detection.
- **Compression:** Sub-band coding via filter banks enables efficient storage/transmission of Holter and polysomnographic data (e.g., JPEG2000 uses CDF 9/7 wavelet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation Between CWT and STFT

**Goal:** Show that the CWT is equivalent to a *bank of STFTs* with adaptive window size.

**Step 1 — Start with the CWT definition.**

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the change of variable $\tau = (t-b)/a$, so that $t = a\tau + b$ and $dt = a\, d\tau$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT_x(a,b) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(a\tau + b)\, \psi^{*}(\tau)\, a\, d\tau \\
&= \sqrt{\vert a \vert} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(a\tau + b)\, \psi^{*}(\tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $\psi(\tau)$ acts as a window function.** If $\psi(\tau)$ has effective support $\Delta\tau$, then in the original $t$-domain, the window support is $a \cdot \Delta\tau$. Hence the effective time-window **scales with $a$**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** CWT is mathematically equivalent to an STFT where the analysis window is **scaled inversely with frequency**, providing adaptive (multi-)resolution. This is the formal justification for wavelets' superior performance on non-stationary biomedical signals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Scale Equation from MRA

**Goal:** Derive the dilation equation $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_0[n] \phi(2t-n)$.

**Step 1 — Since $\phi(t) \in V_0$ and $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, expand $\phi(t)$ in the orthonormal basis of $V_{-1}$.**

The basis of $V_{-1}$ is $\{2^{1/2}\phi(2t-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$, since $V_{-1}$ is the *finer* scale (one level of refinement above $V_0$).

**Step 2 — Write the orthogonal expansion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(t) &= \sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} \langle \phi(t),\, 2^{1/2}\phi(2t-k) \rangle \cdot 2^{1/2}\phi(2t-k) \\
&= \sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} \left( \sqrt{2} \int \phi(\tau)\, \phi^{*}(\tau \cdot 2 - k)\, d\tau \right) \sqrt{2}\phi(2t-k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the inner product coefficient as $h_0[k]$.**

$$h_0[k] = \sqrt{2} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \phi(\tau)\, \phi^{*}(2\tau - k)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the two-scale (refinement) equation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h_0[n]\, \phi(2t - n)\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Fourier transform to both sides to obtain the spectral form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\omega) &= \int \phi(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt = \int \sqrt{2}\sum_n h_0[n]\phi(2t-n) e^{-j\omega t} dt \\
&= \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_0[n] \int \phi(2t-n) e^{-j\omega t} dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $u = 2t-n \Rightarrow t = (u+n)/2 \Rightarrow dt = du/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\omega) &= \sqrt{2}\sum_n h_0[n] \int \phi(u)\, e^{-j\omega(u+n)/2} \frac{du}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sum_n h_0[n] e^{-j\omega n/2} \int \phi(u) e^{-j(\omega/2)u} du \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sum_n h_0[n] e^{-j\omega n/2} \Phi(\omega/2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the low-pass filter transfer function in $z$-domain with $z = e^{j\omega/2}$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\Phi(\omega) = H_0(e^{j\omega/2})\, \Phi(\omega/2)\;}
$$

This is the **spectral product form** of the dilation equation. Iterating $J$ times yields the cascade algorithm for computing the scaling function.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Perfect Reconstruction Condition for a Two-Channel QMF Bank

**Goal:** Derive the conditions under which the analysis-synthesis filter bank perfectly reconstructs the input.

**Block diagram convention:** Input $X(z)$ → split via $H_0(z)$ and $H_1(z)$ → **downsample by 2** (denoted $\downarrow 2$) → process or transmit → **upsample by 2** ($\uparrow 2$) → reconstruct via $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$ → $\hat{X}(z)$.

**Step 1 — Apply multirate identity for downsampler–filter.**

After $H_0(z)$ and $\downarrow 2$, the signal in $z$-domain is:
$$V_0(z) = \frac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(z^{1/2})\, X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2})\, X(-z^{1/2}) \big]$$

Similarly for $V_1(z)$ using $H_1$.

**Step 2 — After upsampling by 2 and synthesis filtering $G_0(z)$, $G_1(z)$.**

$$
\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) V_0(z^2) + G_1(z) V_1(z^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify (no-aliasing and distortion terms).**

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \big] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) \big] X(-z)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify two terms:**

- **Distortion term:** $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z)]$ — must equal $z^{-l}$ (pure delay).
- **Aliasing term:** $A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)]$ — must equal $0$.

**Step 5 — Apply the QMF relation $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ and verify alias cancellation.**

$$
A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_0(z) G_1(z) \big] \equiv 0
$$

This is satisfied if we choose **synthesis filters:**

$$G_0(z) = H_0(z) \quad \text{and} \quad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) = -H_1(z)$$

**Step 6 — Verify the distortion term becomes a pure delay.**

$$
T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0^2(z) - H_1(z) H_0(-z) \big] = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z) \big]
$$

For the **Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF)** or **Orthogonal** case, this evaluates to $z^{-l}$ where $l = N-1$ is the filter order delay. The **Smith-Barnwell condition** provides the necessary and sufficient design constraint:

$$
\boxed{\;H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2\;}
$$

This is the **power-symmetric** condition that yields PR orthogonal wavelets such as the **Daubechies D4, D6, …, D20** and the biorthogonal **Cohen-Daubechies-Feauveau (CDF) 9/7** filters.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vanishing Moments and Polynomial Suppression

**Goal:** Show that $p$ vanishing moments of $\psi(t)$ suppress polynomials of degree $< p$ in the wavelet coefficients.

**Step 1 — Definition of $p$ vanishing moments.**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} t^m \psi(t)\, dt = 0, \quad m = 0, 1, \dots, p-1$$

**Step 2 — Consider a polynomial input $x(t) = t^m$ for $m < p$.**

The wavelet coefficient at scale $a$ and shift $b$ is:

$$d(a,b) = \int t^m \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $u = (t-b)/a$, so $t = au + b$ and $dt = a\, du$.**

$$
d(a,b) = \sqrt{a} \int (au+b)^m \psi^{*}(u)\, du
$$

**Step 4 — Expand $(au+b)^m$ via binomial theorem (each term is a polynomial in $u$ of degree $\leq m$).**

For $m < p$, each term is annihilated by $\int u^k \psi^*(u)\, du = 0$ (vanishing moments), so $d(a,b) = 0$.

**Conclusion:** Wavelet coefficients are **identically zero on polynomial regions** of degree $< p$, meaning wavelets act as efficient *edge / transient detectors* on smooth backgrounds. This is exploited in **ECG baseline removal** and **EEG spike detection**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Single-Level DWT Computation

**Problem:** Given a finite signal $x = [4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18]$ and Haar filters $h_0 = [1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}]$, $h_1 = [1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}]$, compute the single-level DWT approximation and detail coefficients.

**Step 1 — Apply convolution with $h_0$ (low-pass) followed by $\downarrow 2$.**

**Step 2 — Apply convolution with $h_1$ (high-pass) followed by $\downarrow 2$.**

We demonstrate with $x[0\dots 7]$, periodically extended to handle boundary.

For Haar:
- Approximation: $c_1[k] = (x[2k] + x[2k+1])/\sqrt{2}$
- Detail: $d_1[k] = (x[2k] - x[2k+1])/\sqrt{2}$

**Step 3 — Compute approximations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1[0] &= (4+6)/\sqrt{2} = 10/\sqrt{2} \approx 7.071 \\
c_1[1] &= (8+10)/\sqrt{2} = 18/\sqrt{2} \approx 12.728 \\
c_1[2] &= (12+14)/\sqrt{2} = 26/\sqrt{2} \approx 18.385 \\
c_1[3] &= (16+18)/\sqrt{2} = 34/\sqrt{2} \approx 24.042
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute details.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1[0] &= (4-6)/\sqrt{2} = -2/\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 \\
d_1[1] &= (8-10)/\sqrt{2} = -2/\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 \\
d_1[2] &= (12-14)/\sqrt{2} = -2/\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 \\
d_1[3] &= (16-18)/\sqrt{2} = -2/\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification of perfect reconstruction.**

Using synthesis $g_0 = h_0$, $g_1 = -h_1 = [-1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}]$:

$$
x[2k] = (c_1[k] - d_1[k])/\sqrt{2}, \quad x[2k+1] = (c_1[k] + d_1[k])/\sqrt{2}
$$

Check for $k=0$: $x[0] = (7.071 - (-1.414))/\sqrt{2} = 8.485/1.414 = 6.0$ — wait, this should be **4**. The reconstruction formula must account for ordering. Correct reconstruction with Haar synthesis:

$$x[2k] = (c_1[k] + d_1[k]) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = c_1[k] + d_1[k]$$

Re-checking: $c_1[0] + d_1[0] = 7.071 - 1.414 = 5.657$; $\times \sqrt{2} = 8.0$. Hmm, accounting for all factors:

The original signal value $4 = (c_1[0] + d_1[0])\cdot 1 = (7.071-1.414) = 5.657 \neq 4$.

> **Note:** The exact scaling depends on whether the Haar filter uses normalization $1/2$ or $1/\sqrt{2}$. With $h_0 = h_1 = [1/2, 1/2]$ and $[1/2, -1/2]$, $c_1[0] = 5, d_1[0] = -1, c_1[0]+d_1[0] = 4 = x[0]$ ✓ (the standard convention used in Mallat's algorithm).

This example illustrates the **direct sum decomposition** $V_0 = V_1 \oplus W_1$ in action.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Filter Bank Properties Summary

| Filter Family | PR? | Linear Phase? | Orthogonal? | Vanishing Moments | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Haar** | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1 | Education, simple QRS detection |
| **Daubechies D4** | Yes | No | Yes | 2 | General biomedical denoising |
| **Daubechies D8** | Yes | No | Yes | 4 | High-resolution ECG/EEG |
| **Symlet (Sym 8)** | Yes | Near | Yes | 8 | Near-symmetric, low artifact |
| **Coiflet (Coif 4)** | Yes | Near | Yes | 4 (both $\phi$ and $\psi$) | Compression |
| **CDF 9/7 (Biorth.)** | Yes | Yes | No | 4 (analysis) / 4 (synth.) | JPEG2000, medical imaging |
| **Battle-Lemarie** | Yes | No | Yes | Spline-defined | Spline-based denoising |
| **Meyer** | Yes | No | Yes | Infinite | Theoretical benchmarks |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Level Two-Channel Analysis-Synthesis Filter Bank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal X of n"] --> B["Low Pass H0 of z"]
    A --> C["High Pass H1 of z"]
    B --> D["Downsample by 2"]
    C --> E["Downsample by 2"]
    D --> F["Approximation C1 of k"]
    E --> G["Detail D1 of k"]
    F --> H["Process or Transmit"]
    G --> I["Process or Transmit"]
    H --> J["Upsample by 2"]
    I --> K["Upsample by 2"]
    J --> L["Low Pass G0 of z"]
    K --> M["High Pass G1 of z"]
    L --> N["Sum Junction"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Reconstructed X hat of n"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **sum junction** combines the upsampled, low-pass-filtered and high-pass-filtered branches to yield the output $\hat{X}(z)$. For PR, $\hat{X}(z) = z^{-l} X(z)$ — the output equals a delayed copy of the input.

---

## 4.2 Three-Level Wavelet Decomposition Tree (Mallat's Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Signal x at level 0"] --> H0a["H0 Low Pass"]
    S0 --> H1a["H1 High Pass"]
    H0a --> D2a["Down by 2"]
    H1a --> D2b["Down by 2"]
    D2a --> A1["Approximation c1 of k"]
    D2b --> D1["Detail d1 of k"]
    A1 --> H0b["H0 Low Pass Level 2"]
    A1 --> H1b["H1 High Pass Level 2"]
    H0b --> D2c["Down by 2"]
    H1b --> D2d["Down by 2"]
    D2c --> A2["Approximation c2 of k"]
    D2d --> D2["Detail d2 of k"]
    A2 --> H0c["H0 Low Pass Level 3"]
    A2 --> H1c["H1 High Pass Level 3"]
    H0c --> D2e["Down by 2"]
    H1c --> D2f["Down by 2"]
    D2e --> A3["Approximation c3 of k"]
    D2f --> D3["Detail d3 of k"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tree Interpretation:** At each level, the input is decomposed into a **coarse approximation** $c_j$ and a **detail** $d_j$. The approximation is fed forward to the next level (further decomposition), while details at all levels are retained. This creates a **logarithmic frequency band partition**: $d_1$ = [$\pi/2$, $\pi$], $d_2$ = [$\pi/4$, $\pi/2$], $d_3$ = [$\pi/8$, $\pi/4$], and so on.

---

## 4.3 MRA Subspace Nesting — V0 ⊃ V1 ⊃ V2 ⊃ ... ⊃ Vj

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0["V0 Coarsest Scale Span of phi0k"]
    V1["V1 Span of phi1k"]
    V2["V2 Span of phi2k"]
    V3["V3 Finest Scale Span of phi3k"]
    W1["W1 Detail Space Level 1"]
    W2["W2 Detail Space Level 2"]
    W3["W3 Detail Space Level 3"]
    V0 -->|contains| V1
    V1 -->|contains| V2
    V2 -->|contains| V3
    V0 -.->|orthogonal complement| W1
    V1 -.->|orthogonal complement| W2
    V2 -.->|orthogonal complement| W3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Decomposition Identity:** $V_j = V_{j+1} \oplus W_{j+1}$. Iterating from $V_0$:
> $$V_0 = V_J \oplus W_J \oplus W_{J-1} \oplus \dots \oplus W_1$$
> The signal is represented as a **single coarse approximation** $c_J$ in $V_J$ plus a sum of $J$ detail layers $d_1, \dots, d_J$ in the $W_j$ spaces.

---

## 4.4 Wavelet Transform Application Pipeline in Biomedical Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1 Acquire Biomedical Signal ECG EEG EMG PCG"] --> P2["Step 2 Preprocess Band Limit and Remove Baseline"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3 Apply DWT via Filter Bank Mallat"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4 Extract Sub Band Coefficients c_j and d_j"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5 Feature Engineering Energy Entropy Variance"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6 Threshold Denoising Donoho Soft Hard"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7 Inverse DWT for Reconstruction"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8 Classification or Diagnosis"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **end-to-end pipeline** is the canonical workflow in KTU 2024 lab/project modules for biomedical signal analysis. Every stage corresponds to a specific course outcome (CO).

---

## 4.5 Frequency Response Partition of a Dyadic DWT (8-Point Signal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["Full Band 0 to pi Hz"] --> S1["d1 Band pi slash 2 to pi High Freq"]
    F0 --> S2["Remaining 0 to pi slash 2 Hz"]
    S2 --> S3["d2 Band pi slash 4 to pi slash 2"]
    S2 --> S4["Remaining 0 to pi slash 4 Hz"]
    S4 --> S5["d3 Band pi slash 8 to pi slash 4"]
    S4 --> S6["c3 Approximation 0 to pi slash 8"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The bandwidth of detail coefficients *halves* at each level, while the time resolution *doubles*. This is the **constant-Q property** of the wavelet transform — the ratio of center frequency to bandwidth $\Delta f / f_0$ is constant across scales, mimicking the human auditory system's cochlear filter bank.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A tests: direct recall, definitions, and concise comparisons. Aim for 3–4 sentence answers with the key equation.

---

### **Question 1. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) and explain the significance of the scale parameter $a$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

where $\psi(t)$ is the mother wavelet, $a$ is the scale parameter, and $b$ is the translation parameter.

**Significance of $a$:** The scale parameter $a$ controls the **dilation** of the wavelet. **Small $a$** corresponds to a *compressed* wavelet, which detects **high-frequency** components (rapid transients). **Large $a$** corresponds to a *stretched* wavelet, which detects **low-frequency** components (slow trends). The factor $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ ensures energy normalization across scales. This adaptive resolution makes CWT superior to STFT for non-stationary biomedical signals such as ECG and EEG.

---

### **Question 2. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the QMF condition and its role in perfect reconstruction.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) condition** states that the high-pass analysis filter $H_1(z)$ is the mirror image of the low-pass analysis filter $H_0(z)$ about the quarter-Nyquist frequency ($\omega = \pi/2$):

$$H_1(z) = H_0(-z) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad H_1(e^{j\omega}) = H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)})$$

**In time domain (for FIR filter of order $N$):**

$$h_1[n] = (-1)^n\, h_0[N - 1 - n]$$

**Role in PR:** The QMF structure guarantees that the **aliasing term** $A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)]$ can be **completely cancelled** by choosing synthesis filters $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_1(z)$. This is the foundation of perfect reconstruction filter banks used in DWT.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B Module 2 typically offers internal choice. Two parallel 14-mark questions are provided. Each is divided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks sub-parts, mirroring KTU's modular splitting pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the two-scale (refinement) equation for the scaling function $\phi(t)$ from the Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) framework. State the corresponding spectral form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain Mallat's algorithm for fast DWT computation. With a neat block diagram, describe the 3-level decomposition of a discrete signal $x[n]$ and write the equations for the approximation and detail coefficients at each level.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Recall MRA axioms.** Let $\{V_j\}_{j\in\mathbb{Z}}$ be a multi-resolution analysis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with scaling function $\phi(t) \in V_0$ such that $\{\phi(t-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis of $V_0$.

**Step 2 — Use the nesting $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$.** Since $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, every function in $V_0$ can be expressed in the orthonormal basis of $V_{-1}$, which is $\{2^{1/2}\phi(2t-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$.

**Step 3 — Orthogonal projection.**

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{k\in\mathbb{Z}} \langle \phi(t), \sqrt{2}\phi(2t-k)\rangle \cdot \sqrt{2}\phi(2t-k)$$

**Step 4 — Define the inner product as the filter coefficient.**

$$h_0[k] = \sqrt{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \phi(\tau)\phi^*(2\tau - k)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Two-scale equation.**

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n} h_0[n]\, \phi(2t-n)}$$

**Step 6 — Spectral (Fourier) form.** Taking Fourier transform:

$$\boxed{\Phi(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} H_0(e^{j\omega/2})\, \Phi(\omega/2)}$$

where $H_0(e^{j\omega/2}) = \sum_n h_0[n] e^{-j\omega n/2}$.

**Physical meaning:** The product form shows that $\Phi(\omega)$ is the infinite cascade of the low-pass filter $H_0$ acting on its own dilated versions — this is the basis of the **cascade algorithm** for computing $\phi(t)$ numerically.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Mallat's Algorithm (1989):** Mallat showed that DWT decomposition and reconstruction are equivalent to a **cascaded two-channel filter bank** with downsampling and upsampling, operating on the discrete signal $x[n]$.

**Block Diagram:**

```
x[n] → [H0 ↓2] → c1[k] → [H0 ↓2] → c2[k] → [H0 ↓2] → c3[k]
        ↓                  ↓                 ↓
       [H1 ↓2] → d1[k]   [H1 ↓2] → d2[k]   [H1 ↓2] → d3[k]
```

**Equations — Decomposition (Analysis):**

$$c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} h_0[n - 2k]\, c_j[n]$$

$$d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} h_1[n - 2k]\, c_j[n]$$

**Equations — Reconstruction (Synthesis):**

$$c_j[n] = \sum_{k\in\mathbb{Z}} \big( g_0[n - 2k]\, c_{j+1}[k] + g_1[n - 2k]\, d_{j+1}[k] \big)$$

**Three-Level Decomposition of $x[n]$:**

| Level | Approximation Coefficients | Detail Coefficients |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $c_1[k]$ (length $N/2$) | $d_1[k]$ (length $N/2$) |
| 2 | $c_2[k]$ (length $N/4$) | $d_2[k]$ (length $N/4$) |
| 3 | $c_3[k]$ (length $N/8$) | $d_3[k]$ (length $N/8$) |

**Complexity:** Each level takes $\mathcal{O}(N)$ operations, giving total $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for the full DWT — this is the celebrated **linear-time complexity** advantage over FFT-based time-frequency methods at comparable resolution for transient signals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the two conditions for perfect reconstruction (PR) in a two-channel QMF filter bank. Show that alias cancellation and amplitude distortion elimination are both required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With respect to ECG signal denoising, explain how the discrete wavelet transform is used. Discuss the role of thresholding (Donoho's soft and hard thresholding) and the choice of mother wavelet.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Setup:** Consider the standard two-channel analysis-synthesis system. Input $X(z)$ passes through analysis filters $H_0(z), H_1(z)$, is downsampled by 2, processed, upsampled by 2, and reconstructed via $G_0(z), G_1(z)$ to yield $\hat{X}(z)$.

**Step 1 — Derive the output expression.** Using the noble identities for downsamplers and upsamplers:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z) \big] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\big[ H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z) \big] X(-z)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two terms:**
- **Distortion term:** $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z)]$
- **Aliasing term:** $A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)]$

**Step 3 — PR Condition 1 (No Aliasing):** The second term must vanish for all $z$:

$$\boxed{H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z) = 0}$$

**Step 4 — PR Condition 2 (No Amplitude Distortion):** The first term must be a pure delay:

$$\boxed{H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z) = 2z^{-l}}$$

where $l$ is the system delay (typically $l = N - 1$ for an $N$-tap filter).

**Step 5 — Synthesis filter solution.** Given QMF $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$, the unique PR solution is:

$$G_0(z) = H_0(z), \quad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) = -H_1(z)$$

**Step 6 — Smith-Barnwell / CQF condition.** Substituting and simplifying condition (2) yields the **power-symmetric** constraint on the low-pass filter:

$$H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z)H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

This is the design equation for **Daubechies, Symlet, and Coiflet** orthogonal wavelets. Its satisfaction guarantees perfect reconstruction.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**ECG Denoising Pipeline Using DWT:**

**Step 1 — Decomposition.** Apply a 4- to 5-level DWT (e.g., Daubechies D6 or Symlet 8) to the noisy ECG $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n]$, where $s[n]$ is the clean signal and $\eta[n]$ is high-frequency noise (EMG/MAINS).

**Step 2 — Sub-band characterization:**
- $c_5$ → baseline wander, low-frequency drift (often discarded or filtered)
- $d_5, d_4$ → low-frequency noise, motion artifact
- $d_3$ → P, T waves; baseline wander residue
- $d_2$ → QRS complex region (most energy concentrated)
- $d_1$ → high-frequency EMG and powerline noise

**Step 3 — Donoho Thresholding.** Compute the universal threshold:

$$\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}$$

where $\sigma$ is estimated as $\text{median}(\vert d_1 \vert)/0.6745$ (robust median absolute deviation estimator).

**Step 4 — Hard Thresholding:**

$$\hat{d}_j[k] = \begin{cases} d_j[k], & \vert d_j[k] \vert \geq \lambda \\ 0, & \vert d_j[k] \vert < \lambda \end{cases}$$

**Soft Thresholding:**

$$\hat{d}_j[k] = \text{sign}(d_j[k]) \cdot \max\big( \vert d_j[k] \vert - \lambda,\; 0 \big)$$

Soft thresholding is preferred for ECG because it preserves QRS morphology and avoids Gibbs oscillations. Hard thresholding preserves sharp edges (R-peak) better but introduces more artifacts.

**Step 5 — Reconstruction.** Apply inverse DWT using Mallat's synthesis with the modified coefficients $\{\hat{c}_j, \hat{d}_j\}$ to obtain the denoised ECG $\hat{s}[n]$.

**Step 6 — Mother wavelet choice.** For ECG:
- **Daubechies D6 / D8** offer good balance of smoothness and vanishing moments.
- **Symlet 8** is near-symmetric, minimizing QRS phase distortion.
- **Coiflet 4** has 4 vanishing moments for both $\phi$ and $\psi$, useful for precise feature extraction.
- **Biorthogonal 6.8 (CDF 9/7-like)** preserves linear phase, critical for preserving ST-segment morphology.

**Result:** SNR improvement of 8–15 dB is typical; QRS detection accuracy exceeds 99.5% in MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database benchmarks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — HEED THIS BEFORE WRITING YOUR EXAM**
>
> 1. **Missing the normalization factor $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$** in the CWT definition. Examiners *specifically* check for the **admissibility constant** in the inverse CWT — losing 1–2 marks if forgotten.
>
> 2. **Confusing $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (analysis QMF) with the synthesis relation $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$.** Many students apply the QMF condition to synthesis filters, which is incorrect. The QMF is between **analysis** filters; the synthesis pair introduces a **negative sign** for proper alias cancellation.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the aliasing term** in the perfect reconstruction derivation. Even if you write $T(z) = z^{-l}$, you must also write $A(z) = 0$ explicitly. Both conditions are necessary.
>
> 4. **Confusing the order of downsampling and filtering.** The standard Mallat structure is **filter → downsample**, not the reverse. Some students incorrectly write the analysis equation as $c_{j+1}[k] = h_0[k]\, c_j[2k]$ (a common but wrong simplification).
>
> 5. **Vanishing moments ≠ number of zero crossings.** Examiners often ask the *implication* of $p$ vanishing moments — answer: *suppression of polynomial trends up to degree $p-1$*, not just zero crossings.
>
> 6. **Unit confusion:** The scale parameter $a$ is **dimensionless** (a ratio), not in seconds or Hz. Frequency relation: $f \propto f_c / a$, where $f_c$ is the mother wavelet's center frequency.
>
> 7. **Two-scale equation scaling factor:** The $\sqrt{2}$ factor is critical; some texts use 2 instead. Verify the convention from the *orthonormality* constraint $\int \phi(t-k)\phi(t-l) dt = \delta_{kl}$ before the exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ QUICK-REVISION BULLET POINTS — Print This Section!**
>
> **Core Concepts**
> - Wavelet Transform = time-frequency decomposition with **adaptive resolution** (small window for high freq, large for low freq).
> - CWT uses continuous $(a,b)$; DWT uses **dyadic** $a = 2^j, b = k\cdot 2^j$.
> - Admissibility condition: $C_\psi < \infty \Rightarrow \Psi(0) = 0$ (zero mean).
>
> **Filter Bank Fundamentals**
> - Analysis: $H_0$ (LP), $H_1$ (HP) → QMF: $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$.
> - Synthesis (PR): $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$, $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$.
> - PR requires: (1) $H_0G_0 + H_1G_1 = 2z^{-l}$ and (2) $H_0(-z)G_0 + H_1(-z)G_1 = 0$.
> - Power-symmetric (CQF/Smith-Barnwell) condition: $H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z)H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$.
>
> **MRA Essentials**
> - Nested subspaces $V_j$ with $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$; detail spaces $W_{j+1} = V_j \ominus V_{j+1}$.
> - $V_0 = V_J \oplus W_J \oplus \dots \oplus W_1$.
> - Two-scale (refinement) equation: $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_0[n]\phi(2t-n)$.
> - Spectral form: $\Phi(\omega) = H_0(e^{j\omega/2}) \Phi(\omega/2) / \sqrt{2}$.
>
> **Mallat's Algorithm**
> - Decomposition: $c_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n h_0[n-2k]\, c_j[n]$; $\;d_{j+1}[k] = \sum_n h_1[n-2k]\, c_j[n]$.
> - Reconstruction: $c_j[n] = \sum_k (g_0[n-2k]\,c_{j+1}[k] + g_1[n-2k]\,d_{j+1}[k])$.
> - Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — **linear time** — far superior to STFT for non-stationary signals.
>
> **Wavelet Properties**
> - $p$ vanishing moments ⇒ suppresses polynomials of degree $< p$.
> - Energy normalization: $\int \vert \psi(t) \vert^2 dt = 1$.
> - Constant-Q property: $\Delta f / f_0 = $ const across scales.
>
> **Biomedical Applications**
> - ECG: DWT + Donoho thresholding for QRS-preserving denoising; Symlet 8 / Daubechies D6 preferred.
> - EEG: Multi-level decomposition isolates delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma rhythms.
> - PCG/EMG: Wavelet packets for sub-band feature extraction.
> - Compression: CDF 9/7 biorthogonal wavelets (JPEG2000 standard).
>
> **Famous Wavelets to Remember**
> - **Haar** (simplest, $p=1$), **Daubechies D4–D20** (max $p$ for given support), **Symlet** (near-symmetric Daubechies), **Coiflet** (vanishing moments for both $\phi$ and $\psi$), **Meyer** (band-limited, theoretical), **CDF 9/7** (biorthogonal, linear phase, JPEG2000).
>
> **Key Constants / Conventions**
> - Sampling convention: $a = 2^j$ (dyadic), $b = k\cdot 2^j$.
> - Filter order $N$ ⇒ PR delay $l = N-1$.
> - Universal threshold: $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\log N}$; $\sigma \approx \text{median}(\vert d_1 \vert)/0.6745$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Pursuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching Pursuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: MATCHING PURSUIT — SECTION 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Matching Pursuit (MP)** is a *greedy, iterative, sparse signal decomposition algorithm* introduced by **Mallat and Zhang (1993)** that approximates an input biomedical signal $f \in \mathbb{R}^N$ as a weighted linear expansion of waveforms (called **atoms**) selected from a highly redundant, over-complete **dictionary** $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$, where each atom $g_{\gamma}$ is normalized to unit norm ($\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1$). At every iteration, the algorithm picks the atom that maximizes the *absolute inner product* (correlation) with the current signal residual, subtracts its contribution, and repeats on the residual until a stopping criterion (energy threshold, maximum iterations) is satisfied.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Surgical Toolkit"

Imagine a cardiologist trying to explain an abnormal heartbeat pattern to a junior doctor. Instead of showing the entire 24-hour ECG recording as one messy, undifferentiated waveform, the cardiologist **reaches into a toolkit (the dictionary)** that contains elementary "wave-parts" — a sharp QRS-like spike, a slow T-wave hump, a baseline drift, a 50 Hz mains-noise ripple, a wavelet-like transient, etc. 

Now imagine the doctor performs this ritual:
1. Look at the ECG.
2. Pick the **single wave-part from the toolkit that best matches a piece of the current heartbeat pattern** (the *biggest dot product*).
3. **Subtract** that wave-part's contribution from the ECG — what's left is the "unexplained remainder" (the *residual*).
4. Repeat steps 2–3 on the residual. The doctor's notes record *which part was used* and *how strong it was*.

After, say, 30 iterations, the doctor has a compact, interpretable "shopping list": **{QRS-spike at scale s, strength 4.2; T-wave at scale t, strength 2.1; …}**. This is exactly what Matching Pursuit does — it produces a **sparse, interpretable, parametric representation** of a complex biomedical signal using a few chosen atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Greedy"?** Because at every step the algorithm makes the *locally optimal* choice (the best-matching atom right now) without worrying about whether that choice will be globally optimal 50 iterations later. This is fast but not perfect — a better-but-not-best early pick can never be revisited.

### 1.2 Core Components of Matching Pursuit

| # | Component | Mathematical Symbol | Description |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Input Signal | $f$ | The biomedical signal to be decomposed (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, etc.) |
| 2 | Dictionary | $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$ | A redundant collection of prototype waveforms indexed by $\gamma$ |
| 3 | Atom | $g_{\gamma}$ | A single normalized prototype waveform |
| 4 | Index Set | $\Gamma$ | Continuous or discrete parameter set (e.g., scale, translation, frequency, phase) |
| 5 | Coefficient | $c_n$ | Scalar weight assigned to the $n^{\text{th}}$ chosen atom |
| 6 | Residual | $R^n f$ | Unexplained part of $f$ after $n$ iterations |
| 7 | Stopping Criterion | $\epsilon$ or $N_{\max}$ | Energy threshold or maximum iteration count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** All atoms $g_{\gamma}$ in the dictionary are conventionally **normalized to unit Euclidean norm** so that the inner product directly reflects correlation strength without scale bias. The dictionary size $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \gg N$ (over-complete) is the **fundamental reason** MP can produce sparse, flexible representations that no single orthogonal basis can match.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The MP Algorithm, Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-conditions:**
> - Input signal $f \in \mathbb{R}^N$ with finite energy $\Vert f \Vert^2 < \infty$.
> - Dictionary $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$ with $\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1$ for all $\gamma \in \Gamma$.
> - Initialize residual $R^0 f = f$.

### 2.2 The Five-Step Iteration Logic

**Step 1 — Compute the Inner Product Map (Correlation Profile).**
For every atom in the dictionary, compute the inner product with the current residual:
$$
\langle R^n f, g_{\gamma} \rangle = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} R^n f[k] \cdot g_{\gamma}[k]
$$

- *Why?* The inner product measures **similarity** (alignment in shape and phase) between the residual and each candidate atom. A larger magnitude means the atom captures more of the residual's structure.

**Step 2 — Select the Best-Matching Atom (Greedy Maximization).**
Choose the index $\gamma_n$ that maximizes the absolute inner product:
$$
\gamma_n = \arg\max_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \vert \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma} \rangle \vert
$$

- *How?* In practice, this is a search over a structured dictionary (e.g., Gabor atoms parameterized by scale $s$, translation $u$, and frequency $\xi$). The "best" atom contributes the most to explaining the residual's energy in a single step.

**Step 3 — Compute the Coefficient.**
The coefficient is the magnitude of the chosen inner product:
$$
c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle
$$

- *Why?* Because $g_{\gamma_n}$ has unit norm, the coefficient $c_n$ is exactly the projection of the residual onto that atom.

**Step 4 — Update the Residual (Subtract the Captured Component).**
Remove the contribution of the selected atom from the residual:
$$
R^{n+1} f = R^n f - c_n \, g_{\gamma_n}
$$

- *Why?* This ensures that subsequent iterations operate only on the **unexplained** portion of the signal, enforcing the greedy sparsity property.

**Step 5 — Check Stopping Criterion.**
Stop when either:
- The residual energy falls below a threshold: $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 < \epsilon$, or
- The maximum number of iterations is reached: $n = N_{\max}$.

**Final Sparse Expansion.** After $M$ iterations:
$$
f \approx \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, g_{\gamma_n} + R^M f
$$
where $R^M f$ is the final un-explained residual (ideally small in energy).

### 2.3 The Energy Decay Guarantee (Why MP Converges)

A beautiful consequence of the greedy choice is the **energy decay theorem**:

$$
\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means **the residual energy is monotonically non-increasing**, and after $M$ iterations the total energy captured equals the sum of squared coefficients:
> $$
> \Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2
> $$
> This is a *Parseval-like* energy conservation identity, and is the formal guarantee that MP converges (in the sense of energy) to a representation of $f$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $f \approx \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, g_{\gamma_n} + R^M f$ | Final sparse decomposition |
| 2 | $\gamma_n = \arg\max_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \vert \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma} \rangle \vert$ | Greedy atom selection rule |
| 3 | $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle$ | Atom coefficient (projection) |
| 4 | $R^{n+1} f = R^n f - c_n \, g_{\gamma_n}$ | Residual update |
| 5 | $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2$ | Energy decay per iteration |
| 6 | $\Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2$ | Energy conservation (Parseval-like) |
| 7 | $\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1, \quad \forall \gamma \in \Gamma$ | Atom normalization convention |
| 8 | $g_{\gamma}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}} \, g\!\left(\frac{t-u}{s}\right) e^{\,j\xi t}$ | Gabor atom (typical biomedical dictionary) |
| 9 | $\text{Sparsity ratio} = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | Lower is sparser (typical MP uses $M \ll N$) |
| 10 | $\text{Approximation error} = \dfrac{\Vert R^M f \Vert^2}{\Vert f \Vert^2} \times 100\%$ | Percentage energy un-explained |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility (KTU Application Spotlight):**
> In biomedical engineering, MP is used to:
> - **Decompose ECG beats** into a few morphologically meaningful atoms (P, QRS, T) for low-bit-rate **ECG compression**.
> - **Detect and characterize EEG transients** (spikes, sleep spindles, K-complexes) by selecting atoms whose parameters map directly to clinical features.
> - **Denoise EMG/PCG signals** by reconstructing only the high-energy atoms and discarding the residual.
> - **Time-frequency analysis** of non-stationary biomedical signals — the chosen atoms' parameters (scale $s$, frequency $\xi$) yield a built-in time-frequency map.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Decay Identity

This is the **single most important derivation** in Matching Pursuit theory. The KTU board examiner expects students to be able to write this out clearly.

**Statement to prove:** After the $n^{\text{th}}$ MP iteration, the residual satisfies
$$
\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2
$$

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Proof

**Starting point** — the residual update rule:
$$
R^{n+1} f = R^n f - c_n \, g_{\gamma_n}
$$

**Step 1 — Square both sides** (i.e., take the squared $\ell^2$-norm, which equals the inner product of a vector with itself):
$$
\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \langle R^{n+1} f, R^{n+1} f \rangle
$$

Substituting the update rule:
$$
= \langle R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n}, \; R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n} \rangle
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the inner product using bilinearity** ($\langle a+b, c+d \rangle = \langle a,c \rangle + \langle a,d \rangle + \langle b,c \rangle + \langle b,d \rangle$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n}, R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n} \rangle
&= \langle R^n f, R^n f \rangle \\
&\quad - c_n \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle \\
&\quad - c_n \langle g_{\gamma_n}, R^n f \rangle \\
&\quad + c_n^2 \langle g_{\gamma_n}, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Line-by-line reasoning:* Term 1 is the original residual's norm squared. Term 2 is $-c_n$ times the inner product defining the coefficient. Term 3 is the conjugate (real case: identical to term 2). Term 4 is $c_n^2$ times the atom's norm squared.

**Step 3 — Apply the three key identities** of the MP algorithm:

- $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle$ (definition of coefficient, **Eq. 3**)
- $\langle g_{\gamma_n}, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle = \Vert g_{\gamma_n} \Vert^2 = 1$ (atom normalization, **Eq. 7**)
- Inner products are real (assuming real biomedical signals and real dictionaries): $\langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle = \langle g_{\gamma_n}, R^n f \rangle = c_n$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2
&= \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - c_n^2 - c_n^2 + c_n^2 (1) \\
&= \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - c_n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining the two $-c_n^2$ terms and applying the normalization ($\Vert g_{\gamma_n} \Vert^2 = 1$) gives the **final identity**:
$$
\boxed{\;\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 \;=\; \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 \;-\; \vert c_n \vert^2\;}
$$

This proves that the **residual energy decreases by exactly $\vert c_n \vert^2$** at every iteration, which is the cornerstone of MP's convergence.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Energy Conservation Identity

**Statement:** Over $M$ iterations, $\Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Telescoping the energy decay**:
Apply the energy decay identity **Eq. 5** at $n = 0$:
$$
\Vert R^1 f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^0 f \Vert^2 - \vert c_0 \vert^2 = \Vert f \Vert^2 - \vert c_0 \vert^2
$$
At $n = 1$:
$$
\Vert R^2 f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^1 f \Vert^2 - \vert c_1 \vert^2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute recursively**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert R^2 f \Vert^2
&= \bigl(\Vert f \Vert^2 - \vert c_0 \vert^2\bigr) - \vert c_1 \vert^2 \\
&= \Vert f \Vert^2 - \vert c_0 \vert^2 - \vert c_1 \vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $M$ iterations** (telescoping sum):
$$
\Vert R^M f \Vert^2 = \Vert f \Vert^2 - \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange**:
$$
\boxed{\;\Vert f \Vert^2 \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2 \;+\; \Vert R^M f \Vert^2\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Parseval-like energy identity** for MP. It guarantees that the total signal energy equals the sum of captured-atom energies plus the residual energy — no energy is "created or destroyed" during the decomposition.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Analytical Problem)

**Problem.** A 1-D biomedical signal of length $N = 4$ has samples $f = [3, 1, -1, 1]^T$ with $\Vert f \Vert^2 = 12$. A dictionary contains two unit-norm atoms: $g_1 = [1, 0, 0, 0]^T$ and $g_2 = \frac{1}{2}[1, 1, 1, 1]^T$. Run **one iteration** of Matching Pursuit and report the chosen atom, the coefficient, and the new residual.

**Step 1 — Verify normalization:**
$$
\Vert g_1 \Vert^2 = 1^2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$
$$
\Vert g_2 \Vert^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+1+1+1) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute inner products with $R^0 f = f$**:
$$
\langle f, g_1 \rangle = 3(1) + 1(0) + (-1)(0) + 1(0) = 3
$$
$$
\langle f, g_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(3 + 1 + (-1) + 1\bigr) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4) = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply greedy selection** (Eq. 2):
$$
\gamma_0 = \arg\max \{\vert 3 \vert, \vert 2 \vert\} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{atom } g_1 \text{ is chosen}
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient** (Eq. 3):
$$
c_0 = \langle f, g_1 \rangle = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Update residual** (Eq. 4):
$$
R^1 f = f - c_0 g_1 = [3,1,-1,1]^T - 3[1,0,0,0]^T = [0, 1, -1, 1]^T
$$

**Step 6 — Verify energy decay** (Eq. 5):
$$
\Vert R^0 f \Vert^2 = 12, \quad \Vert R^1 f \Vert^2 = 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 3, \quad \vert c_0 \vert^2 = 9
$$
$$
12 - 9 = 3 = \Vert R^1 f \Vert^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In one iteration, the algorithm used **9 out of 12 units (75%)** of the signal's energy, selecting the atom $g_1$ that captured the dominant first-sample component. The remaining residual $[0,1,-1,1]^T$ is ready for the next iteration.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — MP Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Signal f of length N"] --> B["Initialize R-zero-f = f"]
    B --> C["Set n = 0"]
    C --> D["Compute inner products R-zero-f with all atoms g-gamma in dictionary D"]
    D --> E{"Find gamma-n = arg max of absolute inner product"}
    E --> F["Select best atom g-gamma-n"]
    F --> G["Coefficient c-n equals inner product of R-n-f and g-gamma-n"]
    G --> H["Update residual: R-n-plus-1 f = R-n f MINUS c-n times g-gamma-n"]
    H --> I{"Stopping criterion met: residual energy below epsilon OR n equals N-max"}
    I -->|No| J["Increment n = n plus 1"]
    J --> D
    I -->|Yes| K["OUTPUT: Sparse expansion f approx sum of c-n g-gamma-n plus final residual R-M-f"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of MP-Based Biomedical Signal Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT ACQUISITION"]
        S1["ECG Electrodes"] --> S4["Analog Front End"]
        S2["EEG Electrodes"] --> S4
        S3["EMG / PCG Sensors"] --> S4
    end
    S4 --> S5["ADC Sampling at Fs Hz"]
    S5 --> S6["Preprocessing: Filtering and Baseline Removal"]
    S6 --> S7["Segmented Signal f of length N"]
    subgraph MPENGINE["MATCHING PURSUIT ENGINE"]
        S7 --> A1["Dictionary Constructor"]
        A1 --> A2["D equals Gabor Atoms: scale s, translation u, frequency xi"]
        A2 --> A3["Greedy Atom Selector"]
        A3 --> A4["Coefficient Calculator c-n"]
        A4 --> A5["Residual Updater R-n-plus-1 f"]
        A5 --> A6{"Convergence Check"}
        A6 -->|Not Converged| A3
        A6 -->|Converged| A7["Sparse Code: list of c-n, gamma-n pairs"]
    end
    A7 --> O1["Feature Extraction: scale, energy, time-frequency map"]
    A7 --> O2["Signal Compression: store only coefficients and atom indices"]
    A7 --> O3["Denoising: reconstruct using only high-energy atoms"]
    A7 --> O4["Clinical Classification: arrhythmic beats, seizure events"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Energy Decay Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1["Initial signal energy: norm f squared"] --> E2["Iteration 0: subtract c-zero squared from energy"]
    E2 --> E3["Iteration 1: subtract c-one squared from energy"]
    E3 --> E4["Iteration 2: subtract c-two squared from energy"]
    E4 --> E5["Iteration 3: subtract c-three squared from energy"]
    E5 --> F1["Final residual energy: norm R-M-f squared"]
    F1 --> F2["Total energy conserved: equals sum of c-n squared plus final residual energy"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Figure 1 shows the *algorithmic loop* of MP — the iterative greedy selection and residual update cycle. Figure 2 shows the *system-level placement* of MP in a biomedical signal processing pipeline (acquisition → preprocessing → MP decomposition → clinical output). Figure 3 visualizes the *energy conservation law* — each iteration peels off a slice of energy proportional to $\vert c_n \vert^2$ until the residual is small.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Define Matching Pursuit. What is meant by an "over-complete dictionary" in the context of MP?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Matching Pursuit (MP)** is a greedy, iterative sparse signal decomposition algorithm that approximates a signal $f$ as a linear combination of a small number of waveforms (atoms) selected from a redundant dictionary. At each iteration, it picks the atom that has the **maximum absolute inner product** with the current residual and subtracts its contribution.
>
> An **over-complete dictionary** is a collection of prototype waveforms $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$ whose cardinality $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert$ is much larger than the signal dimension $N$ (i.e., $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \gg N$), and whose atoms are **linearly dependent**. This redundancy enables MP to choose the *most appropriate* atoms for any local signal feature, leading to sparse and flexible representations that no single orthogonal basis can achieve.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for greedy concept, 1 for over-completeness explanation.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**State and explain the energy decay relation of Matching Pursuit.**

**Model Answer:**

> The energy decay relation of MP states that:
> $$
> \Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2
> $$
> This means that **after each iteration, the residual energy decreases by exactly the squared magnitude of the selected coefficient**. Because the chosen atom is the one that maximizes $\vert \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma} \rangle \vert$, the energy drop is always non-negative, guaranteeing monotonic convergence. Over $M$ iterations, the **total energy captured** is $\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2$, and the **energy conservation** identity $\Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2$ holds.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for relation, 1 for physical meaning, 1 for energy conservation consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both options below are *internally consistent* and may appear as OR-alternatives in the same question paper slot.

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question A]**

**(a)** Explain the Matching Pursuit algorithm in detail with its mathematical formulation. Derive the energy decay identity. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Algorithm Statement (1 mark):** MP decomposes a signal $f$ into a sparse linear combination of atoms from a redundant dictionary $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}$ with $\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1$.

2. **Initialization (1 mark):** $R^0 f = f$, $n = 0$.

3. **Iterative Loop (3 marks):**
   - Atom selection: $\gamma_n = \arg\max_{\gamma} \vert \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma} \rangle \vert$
   - Coefficient: $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n} \rangle$
   - Residual update: $R^{n+1} f = R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n}$
   - Stop when $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 < \epsilon$ or $n = N_{\max}$.

4. **Final sparse expansion (1 mark):**
   $$
   f \approx \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n g_{\gamma_n} + R^M f
   $$

5. **Energy decay derivation (1 mark):** as derived in Section 3.1 above:
   $$
   \Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2
   $$

**(b)** A discrete signal $f = [4, 2, -2, 2, 0]^T$ is to be decomposed using a dictionary with two unit-norm atoms $g_1 = [1, 0, 0, 0, 0]^T$ and $g_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]^T$. Perform **two complete iterations** of Matching Pursuit and report: (i) the selected atoms, (ii) the coefficients, (iii) the residual after each iteration, and (iv) the energy captured in each step. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Vert f \Vert^2$ (preliminary):**
$$
\Vert f \Vert^2 = 16 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 0 = 28
$$

Verify $\Vert g_1 \Vert^2 = 1$, $\Vert g_2 \Vert^2 = \frac{1}{5}(5) = 1$. ✓

### **Iteration 0 (n = 0):**

**Inner products with $R^0 f = f$**:
$$
\langle f, g_1 \rangle = 4(1) = 4
$$
$$
\langle f, g_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(4+2-2+2+0) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(6) = \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 2.683
$$

**Greedy selection**: $\gamma_0 = 1$ since $\vert 4 \vert > \vert 6/\sqrt{5} \vert$.

**Coefficient**: $c_0 = 4$.

**Residual update**:
$$
R^1 f = [4,2,-2,2,0]^T - 4[1,0,0,0,0]^T = [0, 2, -2, 2, 0]^T
$$

**Energy captured**: $\vert c_0 \vert^2 = 16$. Residual energy: $0+4+4+4+0 = 12$. Check: $28 - 16 = 12$ ✓

### **Iteration 1 (n = 1):**

**Inner products with $R^1 f$**:
$$
\langle R^1 f, g_1 \rangle = 0(1) = 0
$$
$$
\langle R^1 f, g_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(0+2-2+2+0) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(2) = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.894
$$

**Greedy selection**: $\gamma_1 = 2$ since $\vert 0.894 \vert > \vert 0 \vert$.

**Coefficient**: $c_1 = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.894$.

**Residual update**:
$$
R^2 f = R^1 f - c_1 g_2 = [0, 2, -2, 2, 0]^T - \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}[1,1,1,1,1]^T
$$
$$
= [0, 2, -2, 2, 0]^T - \tfrac{2}{5}[1,1,1,1,1]^T
$$
$$
= \left[-\tfrac{2}{5}, \; \tfrac{8}{5}, \; -\tfrac{12}{5}, \; \tfrac{8}{5}, \; -\tfrac{2}{5}\right]^T
$$

**Energy captured**: $\vert c_1 \vert^2 = \tfrac{4}{5} = 0.8$. Residual energy: $\tfrac{4}{25} + \tfrac{64}{25} + \tfrac{144}{25} + \tfrac{64}{25} + \tfrac{4}{25} = \tfrac{280}{25} = 11.2$. Check: $12 - 0.8 = 11.2$ ✓

### **Final Summary Table (1 mark for tabulation):**

| $n$ | Atom $g_{\gamma_n}$ | $c_n$ | $\vert c_n \vert^2$ | $\Vert R^n f \Vert^2$ | $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2$ |
|-----|----------------------|-------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| 0 | $g_1$ | $4$ | $16$ | $28$ | $12$ |
| 1 | $g_2$ | $\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$ | $0.8$ | $12$ | $11.2$ |

**Sparse expansion after 2 iterations:**
$$
f \approx 4 \cdot g_1 + \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot g_2 + R^2 f
$$

**Energy conservation check (1 mark):** $16 + 0.8 + 11.2 = 28$ ✓

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question B]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **dictionary design** in Matching Pursuit for biomedical signal processing. Compare Gabor dictionaries with wavelet dictionaries, and explain why **redundancy** is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Importance of Dictionary (2 marks):** The dictionary $\mathcal{D}$ determines the *vocabulary* of waveforms MP can use. A well-designed dictionary yields **fewer atoms** (more sparsity), **better approximation**, and **more interpretable clinical features**. In biomedical signal processing, the dictionary atoms are often chosen to mimic known physiological waveforms (QRS complexes, EEG spikes, EMG bursts).

2. **Gabor Dictionary (2 marks):** Gabor atoms are **modulated Gaussian windows**:
   $$
   g_{\gamma}(t) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{s}} \, g\!\left(\tfrac{t-u}{s}\right) e^{\,j\xi t}
   $$
   where $s$ = scale, $u$ = translation, $\xi$ = frequency. They offer **joint time-frequency localization** (Heisenberg-optimal), making them ideal for analyzing non-stationary biosignals whose frequency content changes over time (e.g., EEG transients, heart murmurs in PCG).

3. **Wavelet Dictionary (2 marks):** Wavelet atoms are **scaled and translated copies of a mother wavelet** (e.g., Morlet, Daubechies). They provide **multi-resolution analysis** and are computationally efficient via the fast wavelet transform. However, they are typically real-valued and have **fixed frequency-to-scale relationships** ($f \propto 1/s$), limiting flexibility for oscillatory biomedical signals with chirp-like behavior.

4. **Why Redundancy? (1 mark):** A redundant dictionary has $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \gg N$, meaning many atoms can approximate the same signal.
