This **flexibility** is what allows MP to choose the *most compact* representation. With an orthogonal basis, the representation is unique but may not be sparse; redundancy is the price we pay for sparsity and interpretability.

**(b)** Explain the **strengths and limitations** of Matching Pursuit in biomedical applications. How does **Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP)** address one of these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

### **Strengths of MP (3 marks):**
1. **Sparsity:** Produces compact, few-atom representations of complex biosignals.
2. **Interpretability:** Each chosen atom has physical parameters (scale, frequency, time) that map to clinical features.
3. **Flexibility:** Can use any redundant dictionary (Gabor, wavelet, chirplet, learned dictionaries).
4. **Convergence guarantee:** Energy decay identity ensures monotonic residual energy reduction.
5. **No basis assumption:** Unlike Fourier/wavelet, MP does not require the signal to be a sum of orthogonal basis functions.

### **Limitations of MP (3 marks):**
1. **Greedy sub-optimality:** A locally optimal early pick can never be revisited, leading to over-use of certain atoms.
2. **No orthogonality of residuals:** In MP, the residual is only orthogonal to the *most recently* selected atom, not to all previous ones — this causes the same atom to be re-selected in later iterations.
3. **Computational cost:** For large dictionaries, the inner-product search can be expensive (mitigated by fast MP variants).
4. **Dictionary sensitivity:** Performance depends heavily on the choice of $\mathcal{D}$.

### **OMP Solution (1 mark):**
**Orthogonal Matching Pursuit** modifies MP by performing **least-squares re-fitting** of all previously selected coefficients at every iteration, ensuring the residual remains **orthogonal to *all* selected atoms** (not just the last one). This eliminates the redundant re-selection problem and yields faster convergence, at the cost of higher per-iteration computation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting atom normalization.** Students often forget to state $\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1$ explicitly. The energy decay identity $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2$ is **only valid under this normalization**. Wrong normalization → wrong identity → full 7 marks lost on derivation.
> 2. **Confusing inner product with correlation.** The inner product $\langle f, g \rangle$ in MP is a *vector inner product*, not the *normalized* Pearson correlation. Forgetting the normalization step is a frequent error.
> 3. **Skipping the residual update equation.** Many students write the algorithm steps verbally but forget the **explicit equation** $R^{n+1} f = R^n f - c_n g_{\gamma_n}$. The board examiner allocates marks for **mathematical statements**, not just prose.
> 4. **Sign errors in numerical problems.** A classic mistake: forgetting to subtract the chosen atom's contribution, instead *adding* it. The residual update has a **minus sign** — get it wrong and the residual energy will grow, not decay.
> 5. **Not verifying the energy conservation identity.** KTU examiners love giving a "verify" question. Always end your MP solution by checking $\Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2$.
> 6. **Mixing up MP with Basis Pursuit (BP).** BP uses $\ell^1$-minimization; MP uses greedy selection. Examiners will deduct marks if the two are conflated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 12-Point MP Survival Kit for KTU 2024 Exam Day:**

- ☐ **MP is a GREEDY iterative sparse decomposition** algorithm — Mallat & Zhang, 1993.
- ☐ A **dictionary** $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$ must be **over-complete** ($\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \gg N$) and atoms must be **unit-norm** ($\Vert g_{\gamma} \Vert = 1$).
- ☐ **Three core equations per iteration:** atom selection (Eq. 2), coefficient (Eq. 3), residual update (Eq. 4).
- ☐ **Energy decay identity** (Eq. 5): $\Vert R^{n+1} f \Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f \Vert^2 - \vert c_n \vert^2$ — guarantees convergence.
- ☐ **Energy conservation identity** (Eq. 6): $\Vert f \Vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2 + \Vert R^M f \Vert^2$ — Parseval-like.
- ☐ **Stopping criteria:** residual energy $<\epsilon$ OR $n = N_{\max}$.
- ☐ **Gabor atoms** = best for time-frequency localized biosignals; **wavelet atoms** = best for multi-scale analysis.
- ☐ **Biomedical applications:** ECG compression, EEG spike detection, EMG denoising, PCG analysis, time-frequency mapping.
- ☐ **OMP upgrade:** re-orthogonalizes residual against *all* selected atoms at every iteration → eliminates redundant re-selection.
- ☐ **MP ≠ BP:** MP is greedy & fast; BP is $\ell^1$-optimization & globally optimal (but slower).
- ☐ **Sparsity ratio** = $M/N \ll 1$ is the goal of MP.
- ☐ **Final answer format for numerical problems:** always tabulate $(n, g_{\gamma_n}, c_n, \vert c_n \vert^2, \Vert R^n f \Vert^2)$ — examiners love a clean table.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications to Biomedical Signals: Analysis of ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications to Biomedical Signals: Analysis of ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG) Signal Analysis** is the systematic application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques — including filtering, feature extraction, time-frequency decomposition, and morphological analysis — to characterize the electrical activity of the heart as recorded from surface electrodes placed on the body.

The ECG is a quasi-periodic, non-stationary bioelectric signal representing the **summated action potentials** of cardiac muscle cells (myocytes) during depolarization and repolarization cycles. A standard surface ECG has a bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**, a typical amplitude range of **0.5 mV to 5 mV**, and is conventionally sampled at **360 Hz, 500 Hz, or 1000 Hz** depending on the clinical application.

The standard diagnostic ECG waveform consists of five primary morphological components:

| Component | Physiological Origin | Duration (approx.) | Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | 80–110 ms | 0.25 mV |
| **PR interval** | AV nodal conduction delay | 120–200 ms | — |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | 1.0–1.5 mV |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | 0.1–0.5 mV |
| **ST segment** | Isoelectric (ventricular plateau) | 80–120 ms | < 0.1 mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly lists ECG analysis as a *primary biomedical application*. Expect questions combining filter design, QRS detection, and frequency-domain feature extraction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart as an Orchestra"

Imagine the human heart as a **symphony orchestra** performing a precise musical score every second:
- The **P wave** is the **string section tuning up** (soft, brief preamble).
- The **QRS complex** is the **powerful brass section striking the main theme** (loudest, sharpest, most recognizable passage).
- The **T wave** is the **gentle woodwind coda** (calm, rounded ending).
- The **baseline** between beats is the **silence between movements** of the symphony.
- **Noise/artifacts** in an ECG are like unwanted background chatter, microphone hum, or a sudden cough in the audience — they obscure the music and must be filtered out.

Just as a sound engineer uses DSP (equalizers, noise gates, compressors) to clean and analyze a recording, a biomedical signal processing engineer uses **analog front-end circuits, digital filters, and detection algorithms** to extract meaningful cardiac information from the noisy ECG.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Clinical Parameters (Bold = Industry Standard):**
> - **Sampling Frequency (Fs) = 360 Hz** (clinical standard, satisfies Nyquist for 150 Hz bandwidth) or **500 Hz / 1000 Hz** for high-resolution research.
> - **ADC Resolution ≥ 12 bits** (to resolve ~5 µV resolution for ST-segment analysis).
> - **CMRR > 100 dB** for instrumentation amplifiers.
> - **QRS Energy Band: 5 Hz – 15 Hz** (dominant spectral content).
> - **Normal Resting Heart Rate: 60 – 100 BPM** (Beats Per Minute).
> - **Standard Lead Systems: 12-Lead, Frank Vectorcardiogram, Holter (2-lead ambulatory).**
> - **Standard Diagnostic Bandwidth: 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (AHA / IEC 60601-2-25).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ECG Signal Acquisition Pipeline (Block Logic)

The ECG signal processing chain is universally structured as a cascade of five sequential stages:

1. **Acquisition Stage** — Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes capture skin potential differences (typically < 5 mV).
2. **Analog Front-End** — Instrumentation amplifier (gain ≈ 1000), driven-right-leg circuit, anti-aliasing low-pass filter.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion** — Sampling at **Fs ≥ 360 Hz** with anti-aliasing protection; quantization with **12-bit or higher** resolution.
4. **Digital Pre-Processing** — Removal of powerline interference (50/60 Hz notch filter), baseline wander suppression (high-pass @ 0.5 Hz), high-frequency noise attenuation (low-pass @ 40 Hz).
5. **Feature Extraction & Classification** — QRS detection, fiducial point identification, HRV analysis, morphological interpretation.

> **Why each step matters:** Each stage is non-negotiable. A weak instrumentation amplifier design (Step 2) will saturate the ADC; insufficient anti-aliasing (Step 3) will create *aliasing artifacts* that mimic real cardiac abnormalities — a catastrophic diagnostic error.

## 2.2 Noise Sources in ECG Signals — The "Enemy Roster"

| Noise Type | Frequency Range | Physiological/Technical Source | Primary Filter Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powerline Interference** | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** | Electromagnetic coupling from mains supply | **Narrowband Notch Filter** (or adaptive) |
| **Baseline Wander** | < 0.5 Hz | Respiration, electrode motion, perspiration | **High-Pass Filter** (cutoff 0.5 Hz) |
| **High-Frequency Noise** | > 40 Hz | Muscle artifacts (EMG), thermal noise | **Low-Pass Filter** (cutoff 40 Hz) |
| **Motion Artifacts** | 0.1 – 10 Hz (transient) | Patient movement, electrode-skin interface | **Adaptive Filter** (LMS / RLS) |
| **Electrode Contact Noise** | DC – 10 Hz | Poor electrode contact, polarization | **Impedance check + high-pass** |

## 2.3 The Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (Gold Standard for QRS Detection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)** is the universally taught real-time QRS detection method in KTU-style signal processing courses. It is a six-stage cascade.

**Stage 1 — Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz):**
A cascaded **second-order low-pass (cutoff 11 Hz)** followed by **second-order high-pass (cutoff 5 Hz)** isolates the QRS energy band.

**Stage 2 — Derivative Operator:**
The five-point derivative sharpens the QRS slope:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{8T_s}\bigl(-x[n-2] - 2x[n-1] + 2x[n+1] + x[n+2]\bigr)$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period.

**Stage 3 — Squaring Function:**
Pointwise squaring ($y[n] = x^2[n]$) makes all values positive and emphasizes large slopes (dominant in QRS).

**Stage 4 — Moving-Window Integration:**
A 150 ms moving average smooths the squared signal:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=-\lfloor N/2 \rfloor}^{\lfloor N/2 \rfloor} x^2[n-k]$$

where $N \approx 0.15 \times F_s$ (i.e., 54 samples at Fs = 360 Hz).

**Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding:**
Two thresholds (signal level + noise level) are dynamically updated. A QRS is declared when the integrated waveform exceeds the signal threshold AND a refractory period of **200 ms** has elapsed.

**Stage 6 — Search Back & T-Wave Discrimination:**
If no QRS is detected for > 1.66× the current RR interval, the algorithm *searches back* to find a missed peak.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Criterion** | $F_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300$ Hz (use **360 Hz**) |
| 2 | $H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1-z^{-6})^2}{(1-z^{-1})^2}$ | Pan-Tompkins Low-Pass (11 Hz @ Fs=360) | — |
| 3 | $H_{HP}(z) = \frac{-1+32z^{-16}+z^{-32}}{1+z^{-1}}$ | Pan-Tompkins High-Pass (5 Hz @ Fs=360) | — |
| 4 | $y[n] = \frac{1}{8T_s}(-x[n-2]-2x[n-1]+2x[n+1]+x[n+2])$ | 5-point Derivative Operator | — |
| 5 | $H(z) = 1 - 2z^{-12} + z^{-24}$ | Moving Average Integrator (150 ms window) | $N = 54$ at Fs=360 |
| 6 | $\text{Threshold}_{sig} = \text{Noise}_{peak} + 0.25(\text{Signal}_{peak}-\text{Noise}_{peak})$ | Pan-Tompkins Adaptive Signal Level | Adaptive |
| 7 | $\text{HR (BPM)} = \frac{60}{\overline{RR}\,(\text{sec})}$ | Heart Rate Computation | bpm |
| 8 | $e[n] = d[n] - y[n]$ | Adaptive Filter Error (LMS) | Volts |
| 9 | $w[n+1] = w[n] + 2\mu e[n] x[n]$ | **LMS Weight Update Rule** | $\mu$ = step size |
| 10 | $\text{RMSSD} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | HRV Time-Domain Metric | ms |
| 11 | $\text{LF/HF Ratio} = \frac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}}$ | HRV Frequency-Domain Sympathovagal Balance | LF: 0.04–0.15 Hz, HF: 0.15–0.4 Hz |
| 12 | $CWT(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int x(t)\,\psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform (for QRS localization) | Scales a, translations b |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ECG analysis pipelines power **Holter monitors**, **implantable pacemakers** (real-time QRS detection), **automated external defibrillators (AEDs)**, **fitness wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit)**, and **ICU bedside monitors** — making this one of the highest-volume DSP applications in the world.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pan-Tompkins Bandpass Filter Cascade

We will derive the **complete z-domain transfer function** of the Pan-Tompkins bandpass filter used in Stage 1 of the QRS detection algorithm, assuming a sampling rate of **Fs = 360 Hz**.

### Step 1 — Low-Pass Filter Design (Cutoff ≈ 11 Hz)

The Pan-Tompkins low-pass filter is a cascaded moving-average structure with the difference equation:

$$y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + \frac{1}{12}\bigl(x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]\bigr)$$

Taking the z-transform of both sides:

$$Y(z) = 2z^{-1}Y(z) - z^{-2}Y(z) + \frac{1}{12}\bigl(X(z) - 2z^{-6}X(z) + z^{-12}X(z)\bigr)$$

Rearranging to isolate $H_{LP}(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{\frac{1}{12}\bigl(1 - 2z^{-6} + z^{-12}\bigr)}{1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}}$$

This simplifies to the canonical Pan-Tompkins form:

$$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1-z^{-6})^2}{12\,(1-z^{-1})^2}$$

The **3 dB cutoff** of this filter at Fs = 360 Hz evaluates to approximately **11 Hz**, which effectively attenuates the high-frequency EMG and 50/60 Hz interference while preserving the QRS energy.

### Step 2 — High-Pass Filter Design (Cutoff ≈ 5 Hz)

The high-pass filter is implemented as an *all-pass minus low-pass* structure. The low-pass component is a 31-tap moving average:

$$H_{LP2}(z) = \frac{1}{32}\frac{(1-z^{-32})}{(1-z^{-1})}$$

The all-pass branch is a simple unity-gain delay. Therefore:

$$H_{HP}(z) = z^{-16} - H_{LP2}(z) = z^{-16} - \frac{1}{32}\frac{(1-z^{-32})}{(1-z^{-1})}$$

Expanding the numerator algebraically:

$$H_{HP}(z) = \frac{32z^{-16} - 1 + z^{-32}}{32\,(1-z^{-1})} = \frac{-1 + 32z^{-16} + z^{-32}}{32\,(1-z^{-1})}$$

The **3 dB cutoff** evaluates to approximately **5 Hz**, suppressing baseline wander from respiration and electrode drift.

### Step 3 — Overall Bandpass Transfer Function

The full Pan-Tompkins bandpass is the **cascaded product**:

$$H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot H_{HP}(z) = \frac{(1-z^{-6})^2}{12(1-z^{-1})^2} \cdot \frac{-1+32z^{-16}+z^{-32}}{32(1-z^{-1})}$$

This is the **canonical Pan-Tompkins QRS bandpass filter** found in every clinical-grade Holter software implementation.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — LMS Adaptive Filter for Powerline Interference Cancellation

The **Least Mean Squares (LMS)** adaptive filter is the modern standard for cancelling 50/60 Hz interference. The reference input is a synthesized sinusoid at the line frequency.

### Step 1 — Filter Output

$$y[n] = \mathbf{w}^T[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k[n]\,x[n-k]$$

where $\mathbf{x}[n] = [x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-N+1]]^T$ is the reference (line-frequency sinusoid) and $\mathbf{w}[n]$ is the adaptive weight vector.

### Step 2 — Error Computation

$$e[n] = d[n] - y[n]$$

where $d[n]$ is the **primary input** (the contaminated ECG signal).

### Step 3 — Weight Update (Gradient Descent Approximation)

The LMS algorithm approximates the steepest descent by using the instantaneous squared error:

$$\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + \mu\,(-\nabla J[n]) \approx \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu\,e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]$$

where:
- $\mu$ is the **step size** (convergence parameter), bounded by $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ (where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix).
- The sign of $e[n]\mathbf{x}[n]$ is interpreted as the estimated gradient direction.

### Step 4 — Convergence Behaviour

- **Large $\mu$** → fast convergence but high misadjustment / instability.
- **Small $\mu$** → slow convergence but low steady-state error.
- **Optimal $\mu \approx 0.01$ to 0.1** for typical ECG sampling rates.

> **Physical Interpretation:** The adaptive filter *learns* the precise amplitude and phase of the contaminating 50 Hz signal and subtracts it from the ECG, leaving the clean cardiac waveform. It can even track slow variations in line frequency.

## 3.3 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — Frequency-Domain Derivation

The Power Spectral Density (PSD) of the RR-interval series is computed via the **Welch periodogram**:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{M\,U}\sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \left|\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x[n]w[n-mD]\,e^{-j2\pi fn}\right|^2$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} \vert w[n]\vert^2$ is a normalization factor and $D$ is the segment hop.

The PSD is integrated over the standard HRV bands:

$$P_{LF} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{xx}(f)\,df, \qquad P_{HF} = \int_{0.15}^{0.4} P_{xx}(f)\,df$$

The **LF/HF ratio** quantifies autonomic balance: high LF/HF indicates sympathetic dominance (stress), low LF/HF indicates parasympathetic dominance (rest).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End ECG Signal Processing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical ECG processing chain, from electrode contact through clinical decision support.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECG Electrodes Ag/AgCl] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter fc 150 Hz]
    C --> D[12-bit ADC Sampled at 360 Hz]
    D --> E[Digital Pre-Processing Block]
    E --> E1[50/60 Hz Notch Filter]
    E1 --> E2[High-Pass Filter 0.5 Hz Baseline Removal]
    E2 --> E3[Low-Pass Filter 40 Hz EMG Suppression]
    E3 --> F[Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection]
    F --> F1[Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz]
    F1 --> F2[Derivative Operator]
    F2 --> F3[Squaring Function]
    F3 --> F4[Moving Average 150 ms]
    F4 --> F5[Adaptive Thresholding]
    F5 --> G[Feature Extraction]
    G --> G1[RR Interval Series]
    G --> G2[Morphology Fiducial Points]
    G --> G3[ST Segment Analysis]
    G --> H[HRV Analysis Module]
    H --> H1[Time Domain RMSSD SDNN]
    H --> H2[Frequency Domain Welch PSD]
    H --> I[Diagnostic Decision Support]
    I --> J[Clinical Output Report]
```

## 4.2 Pan-Tompkins Algorithm — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw ECG Input xn] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Derivative Operator 5-point]
    C --> D[Squaring Function y equals x squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integration 150 ms]
    E --> F{Adaptive Threshold Check}
    F --> G1[Signal Threshold Level]
    F --> G2[Noise Threshold Level]
    G1 --> H{Refractory Period 200 ms Elapsed}
    H --> I[QRS Detected Peak]
    H --> J[Hold and Search Back]
    I --> K[Output RR Interval Stream]
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 LMS Adaptive Noise Canceller — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Input: ECG + 50 Hz Noise] --> B[Adder Summer]
    C[Reference Input: 50 Hz Sinusoid] --> D[Adaptive FIR Filter wn]
    D --> E[Filter Output yn]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Error Signal en]
    F --> G[LMS Weight Update Algorithm]
    G --> D
    F --> H[Cleaned ECG Output]
```

## 4.4 ECG Waveform Morphology — Mapped to Cardiac Events

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ECG Waveform] --> A1[P Wave: Atrial Depolarization]
    A --> A2[PR Segment: AV Node Delay]
    A --> A3[QRS Complex: Ventricular Depolarization]
    A --> A4[ST Segment: Plateau Phase]
    A --> A5[T Wave: Ventricular Repolarization]
    A --> A6[U Wave: Purkinje Repolarization optional]
    A3 --> A3a[Q Wave: Initial Negative Deflection]
    A3 --> A3b[R Wave: Main Positive Peak]
    A3 --> A3c[S Wave: Negative Deflection After R]
```

## 4.5 HRV Frequency Band Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RR Interval Tachogram] --> B[Welch Periodogram PSD]
    B --> C1[VLF Band 0.003 to 0.04 Hz]
    B --> C2[LF Band 0.04 to 0.15 Hz]
    B --> C3[HF Band 0.15 to 0.40 Hz]
    C1 --> D1[Thermoregulation Hormonal]
    C2 --> D2[Sympathetic plus Parasympathetic]
    C3 --> D3[Parasympathetic Vagal Tone]
    C2 --> E[LF/HF Ratio: Autonomic Balance]
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Clinical Interpretation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define ECG. List the standard bandwidth and sampling frequency used in clinical ECG acquisition. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
ECG (Electrocardiogram) is the recording of the electrical activity of the heart over time, captured via surface electrodes. The **standard diagnostic bandwidth is 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (as per AHA/IEC 60601-2-25). The **standard clinical sampling frequency is 360 Hz** to satisfy the Nyquist criterion (≥ 2 × 150 Hz), with high-resolution research systems using 500–1000 Hz. A minimum **12-bit ADC resolution** is required to detect microvolt-level ST-segment changes.

---

### Question 2. What is the Pan-Tompkins algorithm? Why is the 5–15 Hz bandpass filtering step essential? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (Pan & Tompkins, 1985) is a **real-time QRS complex detection algorithm** for locating R-peaks in the ECG. It cascades bandpass filtering (5–15 Hz), derivative operation, squaring, moving-window integration (150 ms), and adaptive thresholding. The 5–15 Hz bandpass is essential because the **QRS complex carries its dominant spectral energy in this range**, while P and T waves, baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz), EMG (> 40 Hz), and 50/60 Hz powerline interference all fall outside it. Isolating this band maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for accurate R-peak detection.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between full Question A and full Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Design a Pan-Tompkins bandpass filter for ECG QRS detection at Fs = 360 Hz. Derive the transfer function and explain each stage. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Low-Pass Filter (Cutoff ≈ 11 Hz):**
The Pan-Tompkins low-pass uses a cascaded moving-average structure:

$$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^2}{12(1 - z^{-1})^2}$$

The difference equation is:
$$y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + \frac{1}{12}\bigl(x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]\bigr)$$

The $(1-z^{-6})^2$ term creates notches at multiples of Fs/6 = 60 Hz, suppressing 60 Hz interference. The denominator $12(1-z^{-1})^2$ provides the low-pass roll-off with 3 dB cutoff at 11 Hz.

**Stage 2 — High-Pass Filter (Cutoff ≈ 5 Hz):**
Implemented as *all-pass minus low-pass*:

$$H_{HP}(z) = \frac{-1 + 32z^{-16} + z^{-32}}{32(1 - z^{-1})}$$

The numerator zeroes at DC (giving infinite attenuation at 0 Hz) and the all-pass delay of $z^{-16}$ centres the impulse response. The 3 dB cutoff is 5 Hz, removing baseline wander.

**Stage 3 — Overall Bandpass:**

$$\boxed{H_{BP}(z) = \frac{(1-z^{-6})^2(-1+32z^{-16}+z^{-32})}{384(1-z^{-1})^3}}$$

**Stages 4–6 (summary):** The filtered signal is differentiated by a 5-point operator, squared (amplifying large slopes), integrated over 150 ms, and compared to adaptive thresholds. A QRS is declared when the integrated signal exceeds the threshold and a 200 ms refractory period has elapsed. Search-back logic rescues missed beats when the RR interval exceeds 1.66× the current mean.

---

#### (b) Explain the LMS adaptive filter algorithm for powerline interference cancellation in ECG. Derive the weight update rule and discuss the choice of step size $\mu$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram Reference:** (See Section 4.3 above.) The primary input is the noisy ECG $d[n] = s[n] + v[n]$ where $s[n]$ is the clean ECG and $v[n]$ is the 50/60 Hz interference. The reference input $x[n]$ is a synthesized sinusoid at the line frequency, correlated with $v[n]$ but uncorrelated with $s[n]$.

**Filter Output:**

$$y[n] = \mathbf{w}^T[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k[n]\,x[n-k]$$

**Error Signal:**

$$e[n] = d[n] - y[n] = s[n] + v[n] - y[n]$$

The LMS algorithm minimizes $E[e^2[n]]$. Substituting and using the orthogonality of $s$ and $v$:

$$E[e^2[n]] = E[s^2[n]] + E[(v[n] - y[n])^2]$$

Minimizing this is equivalent to making $y[n]$ the best MMSE estimate of $v[n]$, so that $e[n] \to s[n]$.

**Weight Update (Steepest Descent Approximation):**

$$\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] - \mu\,\nabla E[e^2[n]] \approx \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu\,e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]$$

**Choice of Step Size $\mu$:**

The stability bound is:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$. For a single-frequency reference, $\lambda_{max} = \sigma_x^2/2$ (half the reference power), so $\mu \le 2/\sigma_x^2$. Typical ECG-grade implementations use $\mu \approx 0.01$ to $0.1$.

| Step Size | Convergence Speed | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small $\mu$ | Slow | Low | Stable |
| Large $\mu$ | Fast | High misadjustment | Risk of divergence |
| Optimal $\mu$ | Moderate | Balanced | Stable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $w[n+1] = w[n] + 2\mu e[n]x[n]$ but **forget the minus sign in the gradient direction** (steepest descent is *downhill*). Always show the negative gradient: $w[n+1] = w[n] - \mu \nabla J[n]$. Also, students often confuse the *convergence bound* (function of $\lambda_{max}$) with the *misadjustment* (function of $\mu \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{R})$). Lose 2 marks each time this is muddled.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the morphological components of the ECG waveform with a neat labelled sketch. Discuss the clinical significance of each component. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The standard ECG waveform (P-QRS-T) represents one cardiac cycle:

```
        P wave       PR segment        QRS              ST           T
         ___         _________         _              ____________   _
        /   \        |        |        | |            |          | / \  amplitude
   ____/     \____   |        |  /\    | |     _____ |          |/   \____
                       \____/   \/  \   | |     /     |              U (optional)
                              Q   R  S                      
   |<-P->|<--PR-->|<-----QRS----->|<--ST-->|<---T--->|
   80-110  120-200     80-120 ms     80-120   160 ms  duration
   ms       ms                       ms
```

**Clinical Significance Table:**

| Component | Duration / Amplitude | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | 80–110 ms, < 0.25 mV | Atrial depolarization; absent in atrial fibrillation; peaked in pulmonary disease |
| **PR interval** | 120–200 ms | AV conduction time; prolonged in 1st-degree AV block |
| **Q wave** | < 40 ms, < 25% R amplitude | Normal if small; pathological if wide/deep (myocardial infarction) |
| **QRS complex** | 80–120 ms | Ventricular depolarization; widened in bundle branch blocks (> 120 ms) |
| **R wave** | dominant peak | Primary fiducial point for HR/RR detection |
| **S wave** | post-R negative deflection | Deep S indicates left/right ventricular hypertrophy |
| **ST segment** | 80–120 ms, isoelectric | Elevation → acute MI; depression → ischemia |
| **T wave** | 160 ms, asymmetric | Ventricular repolarization; inverted T → ischemia; peaked T → hyperkalemia |
| **U wave** | small, often invisible | Purkinje repolarization; prominent in hypokalemia |

**QRS Axis Determination:** The mean electrical axis is computed from the net QRS area in leads I and III:

$$\text{Axis} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.732 \cdot (I + III)}{I - III}\right)$$

Normal axis lies in $-30°$ to $+90°$.

---

#### (b) With the help of a block diagram, describe the various sources of noise in ECG and the corresponding digital filtering techniques used to remove them. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Noise Source | Frequency | Cause | Removal Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powerline Interference (50/60 Hz)** | Narrowband | Mains electromagnetic coupling | **Narrowband IIR Notch Filter** (e.g., $H(z) = \frac{1-2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1}+z^{-2}}{1-2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1}+r^2 z^{-2}}$ with $r \approx 0.9$) **OR** LMS adaptive filter |
| **Baseline Wander** | < 0.5 Hz | Respiration, electrode motion | **High-Pass IIR Filter** (Butterworth, 2nd order, cutoff 0.5 Hz) — must have linear phase constraint to avoid ST distortion |
| **Muscle Artifacts (EMG)** | 20–500 Hz | Patient muscle activity | **Low-Pass Filter** (Butterworth, 2nd order, cutoff 40 Hz) |
| **Motion Artifacts** | 0.1–10 Hz transient | Electrode-skin interface change | **Adaptive LMS filter** with motion-reference signal |
| **Electrode Contact Noise** | DC | Polarization, poor contact | **High-pass filtering + impedance monitoring** |

**Recommended Cascaded Digital Filter (typical clinical pipeline):**

1. **Stage 1:** 2nd-order Butterworth high-pass, $f_c = 0.5$ Hz (baseline).
2. **Stage 2:** 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass, $f_c = 40$ Hz (EMG).
3. **Stage 3:** Notch filter @ 50/60 Hz, Q-factor 30 (line interference).

**Filter Design Equations:**

For a **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass** at cutoff $f_c$ with sampling $F_s$:

$$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$

where the pre-warped analog prototype is:

$$\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s, \quad \Omega_c = \tan(\omega_c / 2)$$

and bilinear-transformed coefficients yield $b_0, b_1, b_2, a_1, a_2$ (tabulated in standard DSP textbooks).

For the **Notch Filter** at notch frequency $f_0$:

$$H_{NZ}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(2\pi f_0/F_s)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(2\pi f_0/F_s)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$$

with $r \approx 0.9$ to control the 3 dB bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often (i) apply a **single high-pass at 0.05 Hz** which *removes the ST segment* (a critical diagnostic feature) — losing 2 marks; (ii) forget that **motion artifacts overlap with QRS frequencies**, so a fixed filter is insufficient — an **adaptive filter** is required; (iii) confuse **filter order vs. type** (Butterworth vs. Chebyshev). Always specify the filter *type* and *order* explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / ECG Analysis**

- ✅ **ECG** is a quasi-periodic, non-stationary signal; **bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz**, amplitude 0.5–5 mV.
- ✅ **Standard sampling: 360 Hz** (clinical), **≥ 12-bit ADC** resolution.
- ✅ **P wave** = Atrial depolarization; **QRS** = Ventricular depolarization; **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization.
- ✅ **Normal QRS duration: 80–120 ms**; **Normal PR interval: 120–200 ms**; **Normal HR: 60–100 BPM**.
- ✅ **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm:** 6 stages — Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Derivative → Square → Integrate (150 ms) → Adaptive Threshold → Search Back.
- ✅ **Derivative Operator** is a 5-point FIR: $y[n] = \frac{1}{8T_s}(-x[n-2]-2x[n-1]+2x[n+1]+x[n+2])$.
- ✅ **Adaptive Thresholding** uses **Signal Level** and **Noise Level** updated each beat; **200 ms refractory period** prevents double-counting.
- ✅ **LMS Adaptive Filter Equation:** $\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu e[n]\mathbf{x}[n]$, stability requires $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.
- ✅ **Notch filter** removes 50/60 Hz interference; **High-pass @ 0.5 Hz** removes baseline wander; **Low-pass @ 40 Hz** removes EMG.
- ✅ **HRV Time-Domain:** RMSSD, SDNN. **Frequency-Domain:** LF (0.04–0.15 Hz, sympathetic) and HF (0.15–0.4 Hz, parasympathetic).
- ✅ **LF/HF ratio** = autonomic balance index (sympathovagal balance).
- ✅ **Welch Periodogram** is the standard PSD estimator for HRV.
- ✅ **CWT (Continuous Wavelet Transform)** is preferred over STFT for QRS localization due to non-stationary nature.
- ✅ **Einthoven's Triangle** — three limb leads (I, II, III); **Goldberger's augmented leads** (aVR, aVL, aVF); **6 precordial leads** (V1–V6) = full 12-lead ECG.
- ✅ **Lead II** is the most commonly monitored lead in Holter / ICU because it parallels the cardiac axis.
- ✅ **CMRR > 100 dB** is mandatory for the front-end instrumentation amplifier to reject common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz pickup).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Spectral Variability of Heart Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Spectral Variability of Heart Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** refers to the beat-to-beat variation in the time interval between successive R-peaks of the QRS complex in the electrocardiogram (ECG), commonly known as the **R-R interval (or NN interval when derived from normal sinus beats)**.

**Spectral Analysis of HRV** (also called **Frequency-Domain Analysis of HRV**) is the technique of decomposing the HRV time series into its constituent frequency components using power spectral density (PSD) estimation. This decomposition reveals the autonomic nervous system (ANS) modulation of the heart — specifically the balance and dynamics between **sympathetic** (accelerator) and **parasympathetic / vagal** (brake) nervous activity.

Mathematically, given a discrete HRV signal $x[n]$ derived from successive RR intervals, the power spectral density $S_x(f)$ represents how the signal's power is distributed as a function of frequency:

$$S_x(f) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{E[\vert X_N(f) \vert^2]}{N}$$

where $X_N(f)$ is the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of the finite-length segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Spectral analysis of HRV quantifies the *oscillatory components* of heart rate fluctuations that arise from cardiovascular control loops (baroreflex, chemoreflex, thermoregulation, respiration). It is the **gold-standard non-invasive** window into autonomic regulation of the sinoatrial (SA) node.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart as a Drummer in an Orchestra"

Imagine a skilled drummer keeping the tempo for an orchestra. If the drummer is **perfectly metronomic** (zero variability), the orchestra sounds mechanical and lifeless. A *living*, responsive drummer slightly speeds up and slows down — speeding up when the music demands energy (sympathetic activation) and slowing down during calm passages (parasympathetic dominance). 

- **Time-domain HRV** = looking at *how much* the drummer's tempo drifts over minutes (overall variability).
- **Spectral HRV** = listening to *which rhythmic patterns* dominate — a fast waltz (respiration, ~0.25 Hz), a slow waltz (baroreflex, ~0.1 Hz), or a very long tide (thermoregulation, ~0.02 Hz).

By performing a **Fourier-style decomposition** of these drifts, clinicians can identify whether the drummer (the heart) is being driven by fast controllers (breathing), medium controllers (blood pressure regulation), or very slow controllers (body temperature, hormones).

---

## 1.3 Standard Spectral Frequency Bands (Task Force of the European Society of Cardiology, 1996)

| Band | Frequency Range | Physiological Origin | Typical Power Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VLF** (Very Low Frequency) | **0.0033 – 0.04 Hz** | Thermoregulation, humoral (renin-angiotensin), slow vasomotor | ms² |
| **LF** (Low Frequency) | **0.04 – 0.15 Hz** | Baroreflex control, mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic | ms² |
| **HF** (High Frequency) | **0.15 – 0.40 Hz** | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA), pure parasympathetic (vagal) | ms² |
| **Total Power (TP)** | ≤ 0.40 Hz | Sum of all above | ms² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker:** $LF/HF$ ratio is widely used as an index of **sympathovagal balance**. A high ratio implies sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise); a low ratio implies vagal dominance (relaxation, rest).

---

## 1.4 Why Spectral Analysis? Limitations of Time-Domain Metrics

Time-domain measures (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50) provide a *single number* summarizing variability, but they **cannot distinguish** between oscillations of different physiological origins. A patient with strong respiratory sinus arrhythmia (HF power) and a patient with strong baroreflex oscillations (LF power) could have the same SDNN, but their autonomic states are profoundly different. Spectral analysis **disentangles these contributions** and is therefore the preferred modality for short-term (2–5 min) and controlled physiological studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-processing Pipeline (Must Appear in Board Answers)

Before any spectral estimation, the HRV signal must be constructed and cleaned:

1. **QRS Detection** — Apply Pan-Tompkins or similar algorithm to locate R-peaks.
2. **RR Interval Extraction** — Compute successive R-R intervals: $RR_i = t_{R}(i+1) - t_{R}(i)$.
3. **Ectopic Beat Removal / Filtering** — Use median filter or Malik's "vetoing" rule (deviation > 20% of running median).
4. **Re-sampling** — Convert the irregularly-sampled RR tachogram to an **evenly-sampled series** (typically at 4 Hz) using:
   - Linear interpolation, or
   - Cubic spline interpolation, or
   - **Lomb-Scargle periodogram** (skips resampling entirely).
5. **Detrending** — Remove slow non-stationarities using a moving average or smoothness priors (Tarvainen 2002).
6. **Windowing** — Apply a Hanning or Hamming window to reduce spectral leakage.
7. **PSD Estimation** — Choose one of the three primary methods (see §2.2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Answering spectral HRV questions without mentioning pre-processing will cost you **2–3 marks** in Part B (14-mark) derivations. The examiner expects a block diagram.

---

## 2.2 The Three Primary PSD Estimation Methods

### A. Non-Parametric (Welch's Periodogram)

The signal is segmented into overlapping windows, windowed, FFT'd, and averaged:

$$S_W(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \frac{1}{L \cdot U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x^{(m)}[n] \cdot w[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$$

- **Pros:** Simple, fast, robust. Recommended by the **Task Force (1996)** for short-term recordings.
- **Cons:** Poor frequency resolution for very short recordings; assumes stationarity.

### B. Parametric (Autoregressive — AR Model)

The HRV signal is modeled as the output of an all-pole filter driven by white noise:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + w[n]$$

The PSD is then:

$$S_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

- **Model order selection:** Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) or Minimum Description Length (MDL). Typical: $p = 8–16$ for HRV.
- **Pros:** Smooth spectrum, excellent resolution on short data, separable spectral peaks.
- **Cons:** Risk of model-order mis-selection → spurious peaks.

### C. Lomb-Scargle Periodogram (for Non-Uniform Sampling)

For an irregularly-sampled series $x(t_n)$ with sampling noise $\sigma_i$:

$$P_{LS}(f) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \left\{ \frac{\left[ \sum_{n=1}^{N} (x_n - \bar{x}) \cos(2\pi f(t_n - \tau)) \right]^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \cos^2(2\pi f(t_n - \tau))} + \frac{\left[ \sum_{n=1}^{N} (x_n - \bar{x}) \sin(2\pi f(t_n - \tau)) \right]^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \sin^2(2\pi f(t_n - \tau))} \right\}$$

where the time-shift $\tau$ is:

$$\tan(2\pi f \tau) = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \sin(2\pi f t_n)}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \cos(2\pi f t_n)}$$

- **Pros:** Does NOT require interpolation, handles missing beats, statistically robust.
- **Cons:** Computationally heavier; requires understanding of sampling theory.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the entire table below. Board questions almost always ask you to derive or apply at least one of these.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean RR interval | $\bar{RR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i$ | ms |
| 2 | SDNN (time domain) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum (RR_i - \bar{RR})^2}$ | ms |
| 3 | RMSSD | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum (RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$ | ms — vagal marker |
| 4 | Total Power (TP) | $\int_{0.003}^{0.4} S(f) \, df$ | ms² |
| 5 | LF Power | $\int_{0.04}^{0.15} S(f) \, df$ | ms² — normalized: $LFn = LF/(TP-VLF) \times 100$ |
| 6 | HF Power | $\int_{0.15}^{0.4} S(f) \, df$ | ms² — normalized: $HFn = HF/(TP-VLF) \times 100$ |
| 7 | LF/HF Ratio | $LF_{abs} / HF_{abs}$ | Dimensionless — sympathovagal balance |
| 8 | AR(p) PSD | $S_{AR}(f) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi fk}\vert^2$ | — |
| 9 | Welch PSD | $S_W(f) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_m \frac{1}{LU} \vert \text{FFT}(x^{(m)} w)\vert^2$ | — |
| 10 | Lomb-Scargle | See §2.2C above | Handles irregular sampling |

---

## 2.4 Physiological Interpretation of Spectral Bands (The "Why")

- **HF band (0.15–0.40 Hz):** Synchronized with the respiratory cycle. During inspiration, intrathoracic pressure drops → venous return increases → SA node firing rate transiently rises (RSA). This loop is mediated entirely by the **vagus nerve (parasympathetic)**. **Abolished** by atropine (muscarinic blocker). HF power $\uparrow$ ⇒ vagal tone $\uparrow$.

- **LF band (0.04–0.15 Hz):** Driven primarily by the **baroreflex** (stretch receptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch). The baroreflex has a delay of ~6 seconds → oscillation period ~10 s → frequency 0.1 Hz. Mediated by **both sympathetic and parasympathetic** branches. LF power $\uparrow$ during orthostatic stress, mental stress, and moderate exercise.

- **VLF band (0.0033–0.04 Hz):** Origin is poorly understood. Linked to thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin system, very slow vasomotor rhythms. Long-term VLF power is a **strong predictor of mortality** post-myocardial infarction.

- **LF/HF ratio:** Approximates the **sympathovagal balance** introduced by Pagani et al. (1986). Higher LF/HF → sympathetic dominance (fight-or-flight). Lower LF/HF → vagal dominance (rest-and-digest).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Spectral HRV is the *system identification* problem of the cardiovascular control system. The PSD is the system's transfer characteristic. The LF and HF peaks are the **resonance modes** of two negative-feedback loops (baroreflex and respiratory reflex) with different bandwidths.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Critical Care Monitoring:** Continuous HRV spectral monitoring in ICU detects **sepsis** 12–24 hours before clinical signs (decreased HF power, increased LF power).
- **Cardiology:** Reduced HRV (esp. VLF) is a strong independent predictor of **mortality after myocardial infarction** (Kleiger et al., 1987).
- **Sports Science / Wearables:** Smart-watches (Apple Watch, Fitbit) now expose LF/HF ratios as "stress scores."
- **Neurodegeneration:** Reduced HRV in Parkinson's disease and multiple system atrophy.
- **Diabetes:** Early autonomic neuropathy detection (loss of HF power).
- **Mental Health:** Anxiety, depression, and PTSD correlate with specific HRV spectral signatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between AR Model and Yule-Walker Equations

The AR($p$) model of an HRV signal is:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + w[n]$$

Multiplying both sides by $x[n-m]$ and taking the expectation $E\{\cdot\}$:

$$E\{x[n] \cdot x[n-m]\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, E\{x[n-k] \cdot x[n-m]\} + E\{w[n] \cdot x[n-m]\}$$

Recognize that the left side is the autocorrelation $r(m)$ at lag $m$, and the cross-term $E\{w[n] x[n-m]\}$ is zero for $m > 0$ (white noise is uncorrelated with past samples), and equals $\sigma_w^2$ for $m = 0$:

For $m = 0$:

$$r(0) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r(-k) + \sigma_w^2$$

For $m \geq 1$:

$$r(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r(m-k)$$

Since autocorrelation is an even function, $r(-k) = r(k)$, and we can write the **Yule-Walker equations** in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\ r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r(p-1) & r(p-2) & \cdots & r(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} r(1) \\ r(2) \\ \vdots \\ r(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

This system can be solved efficiently using the **Levinson-Durbin recursion** — a classic 14-mark KTU derivation question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Levinson-Durbin Recursion (Step by Step)

**Goal:** Solve the Yule-Walker system in $O(p^2)$ instead of $O(p^3)$.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$a_0^{(0)} = 1, \quad E_0 = r(0)$$

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient at order $m$:**

$$\kappa_m = -\frac{1}{E_{m-1}} \left( r(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} r(m-k) \right)$$

**Step 3 — Update AR coefficients:**

$$a_m^{(m)} = \kappa_m$$

For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, m-1$:

$$a_k^{(m)} = a_k^{(m-1)} + \kappa_m \, a_{m-k}^{(m-1)}$$

**Step 4 — Update prediction error power:**

$$E_m = E_{m-1} (1 - \kappa_m^2)$$

**Step 5 — Increment $m$ and repeat** until $m = p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key identity $E_m = E_{m-1}(1 - \kappa_m^2)$** guarantees that the prediction error power decreases monotonically. It also provides an automatic order-selection criterion: stop when $\vert \kappa_m \vert^2$ falls below a significance threshold (e.g., $2/N$).

This algorithm is **directly applicable** to estimate AR models of HRV from short (e.g., 5 min) recordings.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of PSD Method — When to Use What

| Method | Best for | Min Data Length | Resolution | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Welch Periodogram | 5-min standard clinical HRV | ≥ 256 samples (≈ 64 s @ 4 Hz) | Moderate (Δf ≈ 0.015 Hz with 256-pt FFT) | Low |
| AR(p) Model | Very short segments (1–2 min) | ≥ 100 beats | High (smooth peaks) | Moderate |
| Lomb-Scargle | Irregular / missing-beat tachograms | ≥ 50 beats (theoretically) | Depends on frequency grid | Moderate–High |
| FFT of Cumulated Periodogram (Bartlett) | Quick estimates | Long | Lower than Welch | Low |
| Welch + AR (Hybrid) | High-precision research | ≥ 5 min | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: 5-Min HRV Spectrum (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A 5-minute ECG recording yields 300 RR intervals. After re-sampling at 4 Hz, you obtain $N = 1200$ samples. Compute the spectral resolution if you use a 256-point Welch segment with 50% overlap and Hanning window.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N_{FFT}} = \frac{4 \text{ Hz}}{256} = 0.015625 \text{ Hz}$$

The LF band (0.04–0.15 Hz) is covered by $\frac{0.15 - 0.04}{0.0156} \approx 7$ frequency bins. The HF band (0.15–0.40 Hz) is covered by $\frac{0.40 - 0.15}{0.0156} \approx 16$ bins — sufficient to resolve the respiratory peak.

The total number of overlapping segments with 50% overlap is:

$$M = 2 \cdot \frac{N - N_{FFT}/2}{N_{FFT}} - 1 = 2 \cdot \frac{1200 - 128}{256} - 1 \approx 7.4 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ segments}$$

Averaging 7 independent periodograms reduces the variance by a factor of $1/7$, yielding a stable PSD estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing frequency resolution with sample rate. Resolution $\Delta f = f_s / N_{FFT}$ is **not** $f_s / N$. Always use the FFT length, not the total samples, in the denominator.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cumulative Band Power from a Discrete PSD

Given a discretized PSD $S[k]$ obtained from FFT (length $K = N_{FFT}$), the absolute power in the LF band is approximated by:

$$P_{LF} = \frac{f_s}{K} \sum_{k : f_k \in [0.04, 0.15]} S[k]$$

The summation index covers integer frequency bins between the LF limits. For $f_s = 4$ Hz and $K = 256$:

- LF lower bin: $k_{LF,low} = \lceil 0.04 \cdot 256 / 4 \rceil = \lceil 2.56 \rceil = 3$
- LF upper bin: $k_{LF,high} = \lfloor 0.15 \cdot 256 / 4 \rfloor = \lfloor 9.6 \rfloor = 9$

So $P_{LF} = 1 \cdot \sum_{k=3}^{9} S[k]$ in units of ms² (since the $\Delta f = 1/64$ Hz factor, multiplied by $f_s = 4$, gives unity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Always show the bin-mapping calculation** in your KTU answers. The examiner is watching for it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Note
All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). No reserved keywords are used as node IDs. Labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown formatting tags.

## 4.2 End-to-End Block Diagram: HRV Spectral Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid

    node4 --> node5{DATA TYPE?}
    node5 -->|UNIFORM| node6[RESAMPLE AT 4 HZ SPLINE]
    node5 -->|IRREGULAR| node7[LOMB SCARLGLE PERIODOGRAM]
    node6 --> node8[DETREND MOVING AVERAGE]
    node7 --> node9[PSD DIRECT]
    node8 --> node10[WINDOW HANNING]
    node10 --> node11A[NON PARAMETRIC WELCH]

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Physiological Origin of Each Band

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AUTONOMIC[Autonomic Nervous System]
        nodeS[SYMPATHETIC ACCELERATOR]
        nodeP[PARASYMPATHETIC VAGAL BRAKE]
    end

    subgraph CENTRAL[Central Controllers]
        nodeB[BAROREFLEX LOOP T 6 S]
        nodeR[RESPIRATORY GATING T 4 S]
        nodeT[THERMOREGULATION T 30 S]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[HRV Spectral Output]
        nodeVLF[VLF BAND 0.0033 TO 0.04 HZ]
        nodeLF[LF BAND 0.04 TO 0.15 HZ]
        nodeHF[HF BAND 0.15 TO 0.40 HZ]
    end

    nodeS --> nodeLF
    nodeS --> nodeVLF
```

```mermaid
    q1 -->|BETWEEN 2 AND 5 MIN| q3{DATA REGULAR?}
    q2 -->|YES| welch[WELCH PERIODOGRAM 256 PT HANNING]
    q2 -->|NO| lomb[LOMB SCARLGLE]
    q3 -->|YES| welch
    q3 -->|NO| lomb
    ar --> done[ESTIMATE SPECTRAL BAND POWERS]

    done --> fin[COMPUTE LF HF NORMALIZED LF TO HF]
```
