## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **statistical self-similarity** of a function of time. Write the defining equation using the Hurst exponent $H$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stochastic process $x(t)$ is said to be **statistically self-similar** with Hurst exponent $H$ if its statistical properties (finite-dimensional distributions) are preserved under a scaling of the time axis. Mathematically:

$$x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t), \quad \forall \, a > 0, \quad H \in (0,1)$$

Here $a$ is a positive scaling factor and $H$ governs the amplitude scaling relative to time scaling. This property indicates that the process has **no characteristic time scale** — its appearance is statistically similar across multiple time scales, which is the hallmark of fractal signals such as HRV and EEG.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for interpretation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the **clinical significance** of the Hurst exponent in HRV analysis. What does $H \to 0.5$ indicate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hurst exponent $H$ quantifies the long-range dependence (fractal behavior) of heart rate variability time series:

- **Healthy adults:** $H \approx 0.7 - 0.85$ (persistent, long-range correlated).
- **Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) patients:** $H \approx 0.5 - 0.6$ (loss of fractal complexity).
- **Fetal distress:** $H$ drops toward $0.5$.

A value of $H \to 0.5$ indicates that the HRV signal behaves like **uncorrelated random noise** (Brownian motion). The pathological loss of long-range correlations suggests the **breakdown of neuro-cardiac regulatory feedback loops** (sympathetic/parasympathetic balance), making $H$ a powerful non-invasive biomarker for cardiac autonomic dysfunction.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for healthy range, 1 mark for pathological range, 1 mark for physiological interpretation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **power-law relationship** $S_x(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$ for a statistically self-similar process, starting from the defining equation $x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$. State the relationship between $\beta$ and $H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how the **Rescaled Range (R/S) method** is used to estimate the Hurst exponent $H$ of a biomedical signal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Power-Law PSD (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Self-similarity axiom.** The signal $x(t)$ satisfies:

$$x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Autocorrelation of rescaled process.** For wide-sense stationary increments (fractional Gaussian noise):

$$R_{x(at)}(a\tau) = a^{2H} R_x(\tau)$$

**Step 3 — Form of the fGn autocorrelation function:**

$$C(\tau) \propto \tau^{2H-2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Wiener-Khinchin theorem:**

$$S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{C(\tau)\}$$

**Step 5 — Use the standard Fourier pair** $\mathcal{F}\{\vert \tau \vert^{-\alpha}\} \propto \vert f \vert^{\alpha - 1}$ with $\alpha = 2 - 2H$:

$$S_x(f) \propto f^{(2-2H) - 1} = f^{1-2H}$$

**Step 6 — Power-law form.** Rewriting:

$$S_x(f) \propto \frac{1}{f^{\beta}}, \quad \text{where } \beta = 2H + 1$$

Thus, a log-log plot of $S_x(f)$ versus $f$ yields a straight line of slope $-\beta$, and $H$ is obtained as $H = (\beta - 1)/2$.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–4, 1.5 marks for Step 5, 1.5 marks for Step 6]**

**(b) R/S Method for Hurst Exponent Estimation (7 marks):**

The R/S method is implemented as follows:

1. **Acquire a biomedical time series** $x[1], x[2], \ldots, x[N]$ (e.g., R-R intervals from ECG).
2. **Divide** the signal into $K$ non-overlapping windows of size $n$ each.
3. **For each window**, compute:
   - Mean: $\bar{x}_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x[i]$
   - Mean-adjusted series: $y[i] = x[i] - \bar{x}_n$
   - Cumulative deviate: $Z[k] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} y[i]$
   - Range: $R = \max(Z) - \min(Z)$
   - Standard deviation: $S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum y[i]^2}$
   - Rescaled range: $R/S$
4. **Average** $R/S$ over all $K$ windows for each window size $n$.
5. **Repeat** for multiple values of $n$.
6. **Fit a straight line** in the log-log domain:

$$\log(R/S) = H \log(n) + \text{const}$$

7. **Slope of the line** is the estimate $\hat{H}$.

The block diagram representation is as follows:

```
Signal x[n] --> [Windowing] --> [Mean Removal] --> [Cumulative Sum] --> [Range R]
                                                                       |
                                                                       v
                                  [Log Log Regression] <-- [Std Dev S] <--+
                                          |
                                          v
                                       Estimate H
```

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for Step 1, 1.5 marks each for Steps 2–3 (mean removal, range computation), 1.5 marks for log-log fitting, 1.5 marks for block diagram]**

---

### 5.3 Alternative Part B Question (Internal Choice B)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of self-similarity** in biomedical signals. Discuss the **three levels of self-similarity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **clinical applications** of self-similarity analysis in cardiology and neurology. Include typical Hurst exponent values for healthy and pathological conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a)** (7 marks)

- **Definition of self-similarity** with formal equation: $x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$. (2 marks)
- **Coastline analogy** and physiological justification (regulatory feedback). (1 mark)
- **Three levels:**
  1. *Exact* — strict replica (e.g., Weierstrass function) (1 mark)
  2. *Quasi* — distorted replica (Koch snowflake) (1 mark)
  3. *Statistical* — only statistics preserved (biomedical signals) (2 marks)

**(b)** (7 marks)

- **Cardiology:** HRV fractal analysis; healthy $H \approx 0.7 - 0.85$; CHF $H \approx 0.5 - 0.65$. (3 marks)
- **Neurology:** EEG seizure detection; normal awake $H \approx 0.6 - 0.75$; epileptic $H \approx 0.45 - 0.55$. (2 marks)
- **Other applications:** gait analysis in Parkinson's, sleep staging, aging studies. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing exact and statistical self-similarity:** Many students incorrectly claim ECG/EEG are *exactly* self-similar. They are *statistically* self-similar. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the equality type:** Writing $x(at) = a^H x(t)$ instead of $x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$. The $\overset{d}{=}$ (statistical equality) is critical. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing $D$ and $H$:** $D = 2 - H$ for **1-D time series**; do not confuse with the 2-D relation $D = 3 - H$ used in image analysis. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Log-log plot slope sign:** The slope of $\log S_x$ vs. $\log f$ is **negative** ($-\beta$), not positive. (-1 mark)
> 5. **H range for fBm:** $H$ must lie in $(0, 1)$ for standard fractional Brownian motion; values outside are non-physical in this context. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps:** Examiners deduct marks for jumping directly from $x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$ to $S_x(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$ without intermediate steps. Always show the autocorrelation form. (-2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Self-Similarity & Functions of Time)**

- [x] **Self-similarity equation:** $x(at) \overset{d}{=} a^H x(t)$ with $H \in (0,1)$.
- [x] **Statistical (not exact) self-similarity** is the relevant class for biomedical signals.
- [x] **Power-law PSD:** $S_x(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$ with $\beta = 2H + 1$.
- [x] **Fractal dimension relation (1-D):** $D = 2 - H$.
- [x] **Hurst exponent interpretation:** $H < 0.5$ anti-persistent, $H = 0.5$ random, $0.5 < H < 1$ persistent.
- [x] **Clinical benchmarks:** Healthy HRV $H \approx 0.7 - 0.85$; CHF HRV $H \approx 0.5 - 0.6$.
- [x] **Estimation methods:** R/S analysis, DFA (Detrended Fluctuation Analysis), PSD slope fitting, Higuchi FD.
- [x] **fGn autocorrelation:** $C(\tau) \propto \tau^{2H-2}$.
- [x] **Key applications:** HRV, EEG, EMG, gait analysis, sleep staging.
- [x] **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** bridges autocorrelation and PSD — must be invoked explicitly in derivations.
- [x] **Log-log regression** is the standard graphical estimation method; slope = $-\beta$ for PSD, slope = $H$ for R/S plot.
- [x] **Loss of fractal complexity** ($H \to 0.5$) is a hallmark of pathology, not a sign of "cleaner" data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Advanced Biomedical Signal Processing, a **Theoretical Signal Having Statistical Similarity** refers to a class of **stochastic (random) signals** that, although their instantaneous values are unpredictable, can be completely described and compared using **time-invariant statistical descriptors** such as the *mean, variance, autocorrelation function, and power spectral density (PSD)*. Two such signals are said to possess "statistical similarity" when their **first- and second-order moments are matched**, irrespective of the specific waveform morphology observed at any given instant.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEBMT635 - Module 3), this topic forms the theoretical bridge between deterministic biomedical waveforms (e.g., a clean ECG) and real-world noisy physiological recordings, where exact waveform reproduction is impossible but the *statistical fingerprint* remains consistent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A signal is theoretically "similar" to another in the statistical sense when both signals belong to the same **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** family and share an **identical autocorrelation sequence** or **equivalent power spectral density (PSD)**. This is the foundational premise behind noise modeling, Wiener filtering, and adaptive filtering of biomedical signals.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine two patients' **electroencephalogram (EEG)** recordings taken under identical experimental conditions (eyes closed, same lighting, same scalp electrodes). The actual voltage trace of Patient A will *never* match Patient B trace-for-trace. However, if both patients are healthy, the **average power in the alpha band (8–13 Hz)** and the **average background noise variance** will be remarkably similar.

Think of it like this:

- A **specific coffee cup** is unique (deterministic — you know its exact shape, weight, design).
- A **"coffee cup in a Starbucks store"** is a statistical class — you don't know the exact cup, but you *do* know that, on average, it holds **350 ml ± 20 ml**, is made of **ceramic (95%) or paper (5%)**, and weighs around **300 grams**.

**Theoretical signals having statistical similarity** are precisely this: we cannot predict the next sample, but we *can* describe the *class* of signals they belong to using numerical statistical parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Statistical similarity is **NOT** about waveform matching — it is about matching the underlying probability distributions and second-order statistics (autocorrelation/PSD). This is why WSS white noise generators can simulate muscle artifact in ECG, and pink noise can model baseline wander.

## 1.3 Categories of Theoretical Signals with Statistical Similarity

| Class of Signal | Statistical Descriptor | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **White Gaussian Noise (WGN)** | Zero mean, flat PSD, $\sigma^2$ variance | Thermal noise in electrodes |
| **Coloured Noise (Pink / Brown / Blue)** | PSD $\propto 1/f^\alpha$ | Baseline wander in ECG |
| **Poisson Process** | Rate parameter $\lambda$ (events/sec) | Neuronal spike trains |
| **Gaussian Random Process** | Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, full PDF | EEG background activity |
| **Binary / Bernoulli Process** | $P(1) = p, P(0) = 1-p$ | Thresholded nerve firing |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (used in thermal noise variance)
- **Thermal Noise PSD:** $N_0 = k_B T$ (W/Hz) — at body temperature $T = 310$ K
- **Sampling Rate Range for Biomedical Signals:** **250 Hz – 10 kHz**
- **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** **0.05** (for hypothesis testing of stationarity)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Statistical Signal Classes

A **random process** $X(t)$ is an indexed family of random variables. Statistical similarity theory is built on the following hierarchical structure of assumptions:

### Step 1 — Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS)
A process is SSS if its joint probability distribution is invariant under a shift in time origin:

$$f_{X(t_1), X(t_2), \ldots, X(t_n)}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = f_{X(t_1+\tau), X(t_2+\tau), \ldots, X(t_n+\tau)}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

> **Why this matters:** SSS guarantees *all* statistical moments (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis) are time-invariant. This is the strongest form of statistical similarity.

### Step 2 — Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)
A process is WSS if only the **first two moments** are time-invariant:

$$E[X(t)] = \mu \quad \text{(constant, independent of } t\text{)}$$

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)] = R_X(\tau), \quad \tau = t_2 - t_1$$

> **How:** This is the practical working definition in KTU biomedical modules. Mean is constant; autocorrelation depends only on the *lag* $\tau$, not on absolute time.

### Step 3 — Ergodic Process
A process is **ergodic** if its **time averages equal its ensemble averages**:

$$\mu = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} x(t) \, dt$$

$$R_X(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau) \, dt$$

> **Why:** Ergodicity allows us to estimate statistics from a *single* long recording (which is essential in clinical practice, where only one patient's ECG/EEG is available).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean (Ensemble) | $\mu_X(t) = E[X(t)]$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Autocorrelation Function (ACF) | $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$ | V² |
| 3 | Autocovariance | $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ | V² |
| 4 | Variance | $\sigma_X^2 = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2$ | V² |
| 5 | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $S_X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_X(\tau)\}$ | V²/Hz |
| 6 | Wiener–Khinchin Theorem | $S_X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_X(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ | Bilateral PSD |
| 7 | White Noise PSD | $S_X(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ (bilateral) | Constant for all $f$ |
| 8 | White Noise ACF | $R_X(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)$ | Dirac delta at $\tau=0$ |
| 9 | Cross-Correlation | $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t+\tau)]$ | V² |
| 10 | Coherence Function | $\gamma_{XY}^2(f) = \frac{\vert S_{XY}(f)\vert^2}{S_X(f) S_Y(f)}$ | $0 \le \gamma^2 \le 1$ |
| 11 | SNR | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 12 | Ergodicity Time Average (Mean) | $\langle x(t) \rangle = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)dt$ | Equivalent to $\mu_X$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline to Prove Statistical Similarity in KTU exams:**
> 1. Verify $\mu_X(t) = \mu_Y(t)$ (equal means) ✓
> 2. Verify $R_X(\tau) = R_Y(\tau)$ (equal autocorrelations) ✓
> 3. If both hold $\Rightarrow$ signals are WSS and statistically similar in the wide sense.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG Noise Modelling:** Powerline interference (50/60 Hz) is a *deterministic* signal with statistical similarity to a pure sinusoid of fixed amplitude — modelling it as a narrowband random process with similar PSD helps in adaptive notch filtering.
- **EEG Background Modelling:** Alpha rhythms of healthy subjects across a population share statistically similar PSDs in the **8–13 Hz** band, enabling **template matching** and **coherence-based seizure detection**.
- **EMG Noise in ECG:** High-frequency muscle artifact is modelled as **band-limited white Gaussian noise** with statistical similarity to thermal noise.
- **Monte-Carlo Simulation:** Generating statistically similar noise replicas allows testing of biomedical denoising algorithms before clinical deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Autocorrelation of a White Noise Process

A **White Gaussian Noise (WGN)** process $W(t)$ is defined by three properties:
1. $E[W(t)] = 0$ (zero mean)
2. $E[W(t)W(t+\tau)] = \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)$ (uncorrelated samples)
3. Gaussian amplitude distribution at every $t$.

**Step 1 — State the definition of the autocorrelation function:**

$$R_W(\tau) = E[W(t)\,W(t+\tau)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the defining property of white noise (samples are uncorrelated except at $\tau = 0$):**

For $\tau \neq 0$, the two samples are statistically independent. Since $E[W(t)] = 0$:

$$R_W(\tau) = E[W(t)] \cdot E[W(t+\tau)] = 0 \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{for } \tau \neq 0$$

**Step 3 — At $\tau = 0$:**

$$R_W(0) = E[W(t)^2] = \sigma_W^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \delta(0) \to \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)$$

Combining the two cases using the Dirac delta representation:

$$R_W(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the PSD using the Wiener–Khinchin theorem:**

$$S_W(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_W(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

$$S_W(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Apply the sifting property of the Dirac delta:**

$$S_W(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}\, e^{-j2\pi f \cdot 0} = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

> **Interpretation:** The PSD is *flat* (constant) across all frequencies — hence the name **"white"** noise (analogous to white light containing all visible frequencies).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Spectral Density of a Coloured Noise (Pink Noise, $\alpha = 1$)

Pink noise (1/f noise) is critical in biomedical signal processing because it models **baseline wander** in ECG and **long-range correlations** in HRV (Heart Rate Variability) signals.

**Step 1 — Define the pink noise PSD directly:**

$$S_{\text{pink}}(f) = \frac{K}{f}, \quad f > 0$$

where $K$ is a positive constant (W/Hz²) and $f$ is frequency in Hz.

**Step 2 — Compute the autocorrelation via inverse Fourier transform:**

$$R_{\text{pink}}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{K}{f} \cos(2\pi f \tau)\, df$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integral form (this is a known integral identity):**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\cos(ax)}{x}\, dx = -\ln(a) - \gamma \quad \text{for } a > 0$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the **Euler–Mascheroni constant**.

**Step 4 — Substitute $a = 2\pi \tau$:**

$$R_{\text{pink}}(\tau) = K \cdot \left[-\ln(2\pi \tau) - \gamma\right] = -K \ln(2\pi \tau) - K\gamma$$

**Step 5 — Write the final compact form:**

$$R_{\text{pink}}(\tau) = -K \ln\!\left(2\pi e^{\gamma}\, \tau\right) \quad \text{for } \tau > 0$$

> **Interpretation:** Unlike white noise, the autocorrelation of pink noise decays **logarithmically** (not as a delta function), indicating *long-range statistical similarity* — a property exploited in **fractal analysis of HRV signals**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cross-Correlation for Statistically Similar Signals

Given two WSS processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ that are *individually* statistically similar (same mean $\mu$, same variance $\sigma^2$) but *jointly* correlated with cross-covariance $C_{XY}(\tau)$:

**Step 1 — Express the cross-correlation:**

$$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t+\tau)]$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the identity $C_{XY}(\tau) = R_{XY}(\tau) - \mu_X \mu_Y$:**

$$R_{XY}(\tau) = C_{XY}(\tau) + \mu_X \mu_Y$$

**Step 3 — The cross-spectral density via Wiener–Khinchin:**

$$S_{XY}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XY}(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XY}(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — The coherence function, which measures *degree* of statistical similarity at each frequency:**

$$\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{XY}(f)\vert^{2}}{S_{X}(f)\, S_{Y}(f)}$$

> **Physical Meaning:**
> - $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = 1$ : Perfect linear statistical similarity at frequency $f$.
> - $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = 0$ : Complete statistical independence at frequency $f$.
> - $0 < \gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) < 1$ : Partial statistical similarity (very common in real biomedical signals).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Similarity Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Time/Frequency Domain | What It Measures | Typical Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | $\mu$ | Time | DC component offset | Baseline DC removal |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Time | Instantaneous power fluctuation | Noise floor estimation |
| Autocorrelation | $R_X(\tau)$ | Time | Self-similarity vs. lag | Template matching (ECG) |
| Cross-correlation | $R_{XY}(\tau)$ | Time | Lag between two signals | Fetal ECG extraction |
| PSD | $S_X(f)$ | Frequency | Power distribution | EEG band analysis |
| Coherence | $\gamma_{XY}^2(f)$ | Frequency | Frequency-wise similarity | Brain connectivity |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y)$ | Joint PDF | Non-linear dependence | Non-Gaussian biosignals |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Statistical Similarity Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL X of t"] --> nodeB["PRE-PROCESSING MODULE\nBandpass filtering, DC removal"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SEGMENTATION BLOCK\nSliding window of length L"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["MOMENT ESTIMATOR\nCompute mean and variance"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["AUTOCORRELATION ESTIMATOR\nCompute R_X of tau"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["PSD ESTIMATOR\nWelch periodogram"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["FEATURE VECTOR ASSEMBLY\nmu, sigma squared, R tau, S f"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["SIMILARITY METRIC ENGINE\nCoherence and cross-correlation"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["DECISION MODULE\nThreshold and classification"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["OUTPUT LABEL\nStatistically similar or dissimilar"]
    nodeA2["REFERENCE SIGNAL Y of t"] --> nodeH
```

> **Caption:** Block-level architecture illustrating how two biomedical signals (X(t) and Y(t)) are statistically compared using moment estimators, autocorrelation, PSD, and coherence metrics. This is the canonical KTU examination block diagram for "signals having statistical similarity."

## 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart — Classifying Signals by Statistical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["START: Random Process X of t"] --> nodeQ1{"Is mean constant in time?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "Yes" --> nodeQ2{"Does ACF depend only on lag tau?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "No" --> nodeNS["NON-STATIONARY\nExample: Growing ECG T-wave"]
    nodeQ2 -- "Yes" --> nodeWSS["WIDE-SENSE STATIONARY (WSS)\nStatistically similar class"]
    nodeQ2 -- "No" --> nodeNS
    nodeWSS --> nodeQ3{"Time average equals ensemble average?"}
    nodeQ3 -- "Yes" --> nodeERG["ERGODIC\nEstimate from single recording"]
    nodeQ3 -- "No" --> nodeNERG["NON-ERGODIC WSS\nNeed multiple recordings"]
```

> **Caption:** Decision-flow chart mapping the statistical classification tree of a random biomedical signal. WSS + Ergodic = the canonical "theoretical signals having statistical similarity" used in KTU Module 3 problems.

## 4.3 Schematic — The Wiener–Khinchin Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Time Domain\nR_X of tau"] -- "Fourier Transform F" --> nodeB["Frequency Domain\nS_X of f"]
    nodeB -- "Inverse Fourier F inverse" --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Autocorrelation\n(statistical similarity in time)"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Power Spectral Density\n(statistical similarity in frequency)"]
```

> **Caption:** The Wiener–Khinchin theorem establishes a one-to-one Fourier bridge between the time-domain autocorrelation and the frequency-domain PSD — two equivalent representations of statistical similarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the concept of a "Wide-Sense Stationary" (WSS) random process. State its two key defining conditions. Why is WSS preferred over Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS) in practical biomedical signal analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

A random process $X(t)$ is **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** if it satisfies two conditions:

1. **Constant Mean (First Moment):**
$$E[X(t)] = \mu_X \quad \text{(independent of } t\text{)}$$

2. **Lag-Dependent Autocorrelation (Second Moment):**
$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)] = R_X(\tau), \quad \tau = t_2 - t_1$$

**Why WSS is preferred in biomedical analysis:**
- SSS requires the *entire* joint probability distribution to be time-invariant — this is mathematically demanding and rarely verifiable in clinical recordings.
- WSS requires only the *first two moments*, which can be estimated from a finite-length recording using sample averaging.
- Most biomedical noise processes (thermal noise, muscle artifact) can be assumed WSS over short analysis windows of **2–5 seconds**.

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for two conditions, 1 for justification)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the statistical similarity of two signals? Define the coherence function and state its range of values.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statistical similarity** of two signals $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ refers to the degree to which they share identical or nearly-identical second-order statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation, and/or power spectral density).

The **coherence function** is a frequency-domain measure of statistical similarity:

$$\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{XY}(f)\vert^{2}}{S_{X}(f)\, S_{Y}(f)}$$

**Range:** $0 \le \gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) \le 1$ for all $f$.

- $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = 1$ indicates perfect linear statistical similarity at frequency $f$.
- $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = 0$ indicates complete statistical independence at frequency $f$.

> *(3 marks: 1 for similarity definition, 1 for coherence formula, 1 for range & interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the autocorrelation function and power spectral density of a **White Gaussian Noise** process. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two EEG signals $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ are recorded from the left and right occipital lobes of the same patient. Their autocorrelation and cross-spectral density are estimated. Given that $R_X(\tau) = R_Y(\tau)$ and $S_{XY}(f) = S_X(f) = S_Y(f)$, comment on the **statistical similarity** of the two signals. Compute the **coherence function** and interpret. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Autocorrelation and PSD of WGN (7 marks)

**Assumptions of WGN:**

1. Zero mean: $E[W(t)] = 0$
2. Uncorrelated samples: $E[W(t_1)W(t_2)] = 0$ for $t_1 \neq t_2$
3. Gaussian amplitude distribution
4. Stationary in the wide sense

**Step 1 — Definition of autocorrelation:**

$$R_W(\tau) = E[W(t)\,W(t+\tau)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the uncorrelated-samples property:**

For $\tau \neq 0$, the two samples are independent. Using $E[W] = 0$:

$$R_W(\tau) = E[W(t)] \cdot E[W(t+\tau)] = 0 \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — At $\tau = 0$:**

$$R_W(0) = E[W(t)^2] = \sigma_W^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compact form using the Dirac delta:**

$$R_W(\tau) = \sigma_W^{2}\, \delta(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\, \delta(\tau)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Wiener–Khinchin to find PSD:**

$$S_W(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2}\, \delta(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

Using the sifting property $\int f(\tau)\delta(\tau)d\tau = f(0)$:

$$S_W(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \quad \text{(constant for all } f\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

The PSD is *flat* (constant) for all frequencies $f \in (-\infty, \infty)$. This means white noise has equal power at every frequency — analogous to white light containing all visible wavelengths.

> *(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 2 for ACF derivation, 2 for PSD via Wiener–Khinchin, 1 for interpretation, 1 for unit check)*

---

#### Part (b) — Coherence and Statistical Similarity (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $R_X(\tau) = R_Y(\tau)$ (equal autocorrelations)
- $S_{XY}(f) = S_X(f) = S_Y(f)$ (equal auto- and cross-spectra)

**Step 2 — Apply the coherence function definition:**

$$\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{XY}(f)\vert^{2}}{S_{X}(f)\, S_{Y}(f)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $S_X = S_Y = S_{XY} = S(f)$:**

$$\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S(f)\vert^{2}}{S(f) \cdot S(f)} = \frac{S(f)^{2}}{S(f)^{2}} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion on statistical similarity:**

Since $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = 1$ for all $f$, the two EEG signals are **perfectly statistically similar** in the wide-sense.

**Step 5 — Biomedical interpretation:**

- Both occipital lobes exhibit identical second-order statistics — this is consistent with **bilateral symmetry** of healthy alpha rhythms.
- The signals are linearly related at every frequency: $Y(f) = H(f) X(f)$ for some linear transfer function $H(f)$.
- Such high coherence in the **alpha band (8–13 Hz)** is a hallmark of resting-state eyes-closed EEG in healthy adults.

> *(7 marks: 1 for substitution, 2 for computing coherence, 2 for interpretation, 2 for clinical/biomedical meaning)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define the **autocorrelation function** of a random process. With suitable derivations, explain how the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** establishes statistical similarity in both time and frequency domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-second EMG signal is sampled at $f_s = 1$ kHz. The signal is modelled as a **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** coloured noise process with autocorrelation:
$$R_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 \, e^{-\alpha \vert \tau \vert}$$
where $\sigma^2 = 10\,\mu V^2$ and $\alpha = 50$ rad/s.
**(i)** Compute the **variance** of the signal.
**(ii)** Derive the **power spectral density** $S_X(f)$.
**(iii)** State the **3-dB bandwidth** of the process. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Autocorrelation and Wiener–Khinchin (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define autocorrelation:**

The autocorrelation function (ACF) of a random process $X(t)$ is:

$$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t+\tau)]$$

It measures the **statistical similarity of a signal with a time-shifted version of itself**.

**Step 2 — Properties of the ACF:**

- $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)] = \sigma_X^2 + \mu_X^2$ (instantaneous power)
- $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ (even function)
- $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)$ (maximum at $\tau = 0$)

**Step 3 — State the Wiener–Khinchin theorem:**

$$S_X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_X(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_X(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

$$R_X(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S_X(f)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_X(f)\, e^{j2\pi f \tau}\, df$$

**Step 4 — Demonstrate the bidirectional bridge:**

Two signals $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ are **statistically similar** if and only if either of these equivalent conditions holds:
- $R_X(\tau) = R_Y(\tau)$ in the time domain, **OR**
- $S_X(f) = S_Y(f)$ in the frequency domain

The Fourier transform provides a *one-to-one* mapping — therefore, statistical similarity can be verified in **either domain**.

> *(7 marks: 2 for ACF definition + properties, 3 for Wiener–Khinchin statement, 2 for the time-frequency equivalence argument)*

---

#### Part (b) — Coloured Noise EMG Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2 = \sigma^2 \cdot e^{0} - 0 = \sigma^2 = 10\,\mu V^2$$

**Step 2 — Derive the PSD using Wiener–Khinchin:**

$$S_X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \sigma^2\, e^{-\alpha \vert \tau \vert}\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

Split the integral at $\tau = 0$:

$$S_X(f) = \sigma^2 \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{\alpha \tau} e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha \tau} e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau \right]$$

Compute the first integral (let $u = -\tau$):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha u} e^{j2\pi f u} du = \frac{1}{\alpha - j2\pi f}$$

By conjugate symmetry, the second integral is:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha \tau} e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau = \frac{1}{\alpha + j2\pi f}$$

Add and combine:

$$S_X(f) = \sigma^2 \left[ \frac{1}{\alpha - j2\pi f} + \frac{1}{\alpha + j2\pi f} \right] = \sigma^2 \cdot \frac{2\alpha}{\alpha^2 + (2\pi f)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$S_X(f) = \frac{2 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 50}{50^2 + (2\pi f)^2} = \frac{10^{-9}}{2500 + 4\pi^2 f^2} \quad \text{(in V}^2\text{/Hz)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 3-dB bandwidth:**

The 3-dB point is where $S_X(f)$ drops to half its maximum value $S_X(0)$:

$$S_X(0) = \frac{2\sigma^2}{\alpha} = \frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12}}{50} = 4 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$$

Setting $S_X(f_{3dB}) = \frac{1}{2} S_X(0)$:

$$\frac{2\alpha}{\alpha^2 + (2\pi f_{3dB})^2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\alpha}$$

$$(2\pi f_{3dB})^2 = \alpha^2 \Rightarrow f_{3dB} = \frac{\alpha}{2\pi} = \frac{50}{2\pi} \approx 7.96\,\text{Hz}$$

> *(7 marks: 1 for variance, 3 for PSD derivation, 1 for numerical substitution, 2 for 3-dB bandwidth calculation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Dirac delta with the Kronecker delta.** In *continuous-time* white noise, $R_W(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$. In *discrete-time* (DSP), $R_W[k] = \sigma^2 \delta[k]$. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "$-\mu^2$" term** when computing variance from autocorrelation. The correct formula is $\sigma^2 = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2$, not simply $R_X(0)$.
>
> 3. **Missing units** in PSD problems. Always write $S_X(f)$ in **V²/Hz** and $R_X(\tau)$ in **V²**. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
>
> 4. **Assuming ergodicity without justification.** When a problem says "estimate from a single recording," *state* that the process is assumed ergodic — do not assume it implicitly.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in the inverse Fourier transform.** Remember the sign convention: $R_X(\tau) = \int S_X(f) e^{+j2\pi f \tau} df$ (positive exponent), while $S_X(f) = \int R_X(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ (negative exponent).
>
> 6. **Confusing statistical similarity with waveform similarity.** Two signals can be statistically similar (matching PSD/ACF) but have completely different instantaneous waveforms. KTU Module 3 emphasizes this distinction heavily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity"**

- ✅ **Statistical similarity** is governed by *second-order moments* (mean and ACF), not waveform shape.
- ✅ A signal is **WSS** if $E[X(t)] = \mu$ (constant) and $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$ (depends only on lag).
- ✅ A signal is **ergodic** if time averages equal ensemble averages — enables single-recording statistics.
- ✅ **White noise** has flat PSD $S_W(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ and impulse ACF $R_W(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$.
- ✅ **Pink (1/f) noise** has $S_{\text{pink}}(f) = K/f$ and logarithmic ACF — models ECG baseline wander.
- ✅ **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**: $S_X(f) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_X(\tau)$ — Fourier pair.
- ✅ **Variance** is always $\sigma^2 = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2$.
- ✅ **Coherence function**: $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{XY}(f)\vert^{2}}{S_X(f)S_Y(f)}$ measures frequency-wise similarity.
- ✅ **Coherence range**: $0 \le \gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) \le 1$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Thermal noise PSD**: $N_0 = k_B T$ — at body temperature $T = 310$ K, $N_0 \approx 4.28 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz.
- ✅ **SNR formula**: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications**: ECG denoising, EEG coherence analysis, EMG spectral modelling, HRV fractal analysis.
- ✅ **Cross-correlation** $R_{XY}(\tau)$ detects time delay between two statistically similar signals (used in fetal ECG extraction from maternal abdominal recordings).
- ✅ **Sliding window approach**: Real biomedical signals are WSS only over **short windows** (2–5 s for EEG, 10–30 s for HRV).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Biomedical Signal Processing (PEBMT635)**, *Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals* constitute a family of quantitative, mathematically rigorous metrics used to evaluate the degree of resemblance, correspondence, or alignment between two (or more) stochastic biomedical signals — such as ECG, EEG, EMG, or PCG recordings. These measures operate on the underlying statistical properties of the signals (mean, variance, covariance, spectral density, and probability distributions) rather than on instantaneous sample-by-sample amplitude identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> A *similarity measure* is a scalar, bounded, often normalized, real-valued function $S(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies the statistical agreement between two real-valued discrete-time biomedical signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$, where higher values typically indicate greater similarity and lower values indicate divergence or dissimilarity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic voice analyst** comparing two audio recordings of the same person saying the word "hello." The two recordings will not be *identical* sample-by-sample (due to background noise, microphone distance, breathiness), but they share the *same pattern of energy variation*. Statistical similarity measures are like the **mathematical magnifying glass** that quantifies "how close the patterns are," even if every individual number differs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Mapping to Biomedical Context:**
> - *Template matching in QRS detection* → "How closely does this heartbeat segment match a known normal heartbeat template?"
> - *Patient-to-patient ECG comparison* → "How statistically similar is the rhythm of Patient A to Patient B?"
> - *Noise removal validation* → "How similar is the filtered signal to the clean reference signal?"

## 1.3 Classification of Similarity Measures (KTU Taxonomy)

| Category | Core Property | Typical Range | Primary Use |
|----------|--------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Correlation-Based** | Linear dependence | $[-1, +1]$ | Template matching, alignment |
| **Error-Based** | Amplitude deviation | $[0, \infty)$ | Filter performance, reconstruction |
| **Spectral-Based** | Frequency-domain coherence | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-channel EEG, evoked potentials |
| **Geometric / Vector** | Directional alignment | $[-1, +1]$ | Feature-space clustering, PCA validation |
| **Information-Theoretic** | Probabilistic divergence | $[0, \infty)$ | Nonlinear biosignal comparison |

## 1.4 Why These Measures Are Central to Biomedical Signal Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Driving Reasons for KTU 2024 Emphasis:**
> 1. **Diagnostic Decision Support** — Automated arrhythmia detection, seizure detection, and gait analysis all depend on similarity scoring.
> 2. **Quality Assurance** — Validating denoising algorithms, compression codecs, and feature extractors requires an objective similarity benchmark.
> 3. **Adaptive & Reference-Based Systems** — Adaptive filters (LMS, RLS) literally *minimize* an error-based similarity measure in real time.

---

## 1.5 Engineering & Clinical Utility

The real-world applications of statistical similarity measures in B.Tech biomedical projects, KTU final-year capstone work, and clinical engineering include:

- **ECG Template Matching (Pan-Tompkins Algorithm):** Cross-correlation of detected QRS complexes against a learned template.
- **EEG Coherence Mapping:** Inter-hemispheric and intra-hemispheric coherence for cognitive and neurological disorder studies.
- **EMG Pattern Recognition:** Similarity between muscle activation envelopes for prosthetic control.
- **Motion Artifact Rejection:** SNR-based gating of usable versus corrupted biosignal windows.
- **Biometric Authentication:** Correlation-based matching of PPG or ECG signatures for secure identification.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Framework — Assumptions

For all measures discussed in this module, the following standard assumptions apply:

- Both signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ are **real-valued**, **discrete-time**, and **finite-energy or finite-power**.
- Signals are treated as **jointly wide-sense stationary (WSS)** random processes, allowing time-averaged estimates to equal ensemble averages (ergodicity).
- Sample length is $N$, indexed by $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$.
- Estimated moments are computed via the standard unbiased estimators.

## 2.2 First-Moment & Second-Moment Estimators (Foundation Block)

These four estimators form the *building blocks* of every similarity measure discussed below:

- **Sample Mean (Expectation Estimate):**
$\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$, and analogously $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n]$.

- **Sample Variance (Power Estimate, Biased Form):**
$\sigma_x^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n] - \bar{x}\right)^{2}$, and analogously $\sigma_y^{2}$.

- **Sample Covariance (Cross-Second-Moment):**
$\sigma_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n] - \bar{x}\right)\left(y[n] - \bar{y}\right)$.

- **Sample Cross-Correlation at Zero Lag (Unnormalized):**
$R_{xy}(0) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, y[n]$.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Similarity Measures Required for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)**

| # | Measure Name | Mathematical Expression | Range | Optimal Value | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC)** $\rho_{xy}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | $+1$ | Linear template matching |
| 2 | **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** | $\dfrac{R_{xy}(0)}{\sqrt{R_{xx}(0)\, R_{yy}(0)}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | $+1$ | Image/signal registration |
| 3 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left(x[n]-y[n]\right)^{2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Filter & compression design |
| 4 | **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Same units as signal |
| 5 | **Normalized RMSE (NRMSE)** | $\dfrac{\text{RMSE}}{\max(x)-\min(x)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ | Scale-invariant quality |
| 6 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum x^{2}}{\sum (x-y)^{2}}\right)$ dB | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $+\infty$ dB | Denoising benchmarks |
| 7 | **Peak SNR (PSNR)** | $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{x_{\text{peak}}^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ dB | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $+\infty$ dB | Image/signal fidelity |
| 8 | **Magnitude Squared Coherence (MSC)** | $\dfrac{\vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2}}{S_{xx}(f)\, S_{yy}(f)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Frequency-domain coupling |
| 9 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\dfrac{\langle x, y \rangle}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | $+1$ | Feature-vector comparison |
| 10 | **Percentage Root Mean Square Difference (PRD)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x-y)^{2}}{\sum x^{2}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $[0, 100\%]$ | $0\%$ | ECG compression assessment |
| 11 | **Cross-Correlation Coefficient at Lag $k$** | $\dfrac{\sum_{n} (x[n]-\bar x)(y[n+k]-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum (x-\bar x)^{2} \sum (y-\bar y)^{2}}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | $+1$ | Time-delay estimation |
| 12 | **Correlation Dimension (D2)** | $\displaystyle\lim_{r \to 0} \dfrac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Problem-specific | Nonlinear complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the *magnitude* (modulus) of a complex quantity — used inside the Coherence formula. The square of this magnitude is a real, non-negative number bounded by $[0, 1]$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Each Measure (Engineering Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Each Measure Matters in Real Engineering Pipelines:**

- **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC)** is the *workhorse* of QRS detection in the Pan-Tompkins algorithm and in adaptive heartbeat classifiers. It is invariant to DC offset and scale, making it robust across patients.
- **MSE** is the *loss function* behind virtually every Wiener filter, Kalman filter, and adaptive filter. KTU students studying adaptive filtering will see it again in the LMS algorithm.
- **Coherence** answers *"do these two EEG channels share rhythmic activity at frequency $f$?"* — critical for diagnosing epilepsy and studying cognitive load.
- **SNR / PSNR** are the *universal currency* for reporting biomedical signal enhancement performance in IEEE-style journals and KTU project reports.
- **Cosine Similarity** dominates *machine learning* pipelines — it is the similarity metric inside $k$-Nearest Neighbors, $k$-means clustering, and most deep metric learning architectures.
- **PRD** is the **gold standard in ECG compression research** (the paper by Jalaleddine et al. established the $\text{PRD} < 9\%$ benchmark for clinically acceptable compression).

## 2.5 The Underlying "Why" — Statistical Interpretation

- **PCC** measures the *standardized linear covariance* — it tells you how well a straight-line relationship describes the joint variation of $x$ and $y$.
- **MSE** measures *second-moment energy of the error signal* $e[n] = x[n] - y[n]$ — the average squared deviation.
- **Coherence** is a *frequency-domain analogue of the squared correlation* — at every frequency, it quantifies linear relationship strength.
- **Cosine Similarity** is a *geometric dot-product in $N$-dimensional space* — it ignores magnitude and captures only directional alignment.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC)

The PCC is derived from the principle of *normalizing* the sample covariance by the geometric mean of individual variances. The motivation: covariance alone depends on signal scale (a signal in volts and the same signal in millivolts yield different covariance), making cross-patient comparisons meaningless. Normalization solves this.

**Step 1 — Define the Unnormalized Cross-Covariance:**

The sample cross-covariance is the average product of the *centered* (mean-removed) signals:

$$
\sigma_{xy} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n] - \bar{x}\right)\left(y[n] - \bar{y}\right)
$$

*Explanation:* Centering (subtracting the mean) removes the DC component, ensuring the measure reflects *covarying variation* rather than constant offsets.

**Step 2 — Define Individual Variances:**

$$
\sigma_x^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n] - \bar{x}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(y[n] - \bar{y}\right)^{2}
$$

*Explanation:* Variance is the second central moment — it quantifies the spread of each signal around its own mean.

**Step 3 — Apply Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality:**

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in the inner-product space, the following bound always holds:

$$
\vert \sigma_{xy} \vert \;\leq\; \sigma_x \, \sigma_y
$$

*Explanation:* The magnitude of cross-covariance can never exceed the product of individual standard deviations, because the maximum occurs when $x$ and $y$ are perfectly linearly related.

**Step 4 — Normalize to Obtain a Dimensionless, Bounded Quantity:**

$$
\rho_{xy} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x \, \sigma_y}
$$

*Explanation:* Dividing by the geometric mean of the standard deviations removes unit dependence and bounds the result in $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 5 — Equivalent Expanded Form (Direct Computation):**

Expanding the centered products yields the computationally efficient form:

$$
\rho_{xy} \;=\; \frac{ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, y[n] \;-\; N\, \bar{x}\, \bar{y} }{ \sqrt{ \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^{2}[n] - N \bar{x}^{2} \right) \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y^{2}[n] - N \bar{y}^{2} \right) } }
$$

*Explanation:* This form is preferred in MATLAB/Python implementations because it requires only one pass to compute the sums of products, squares, and cross-products.

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Case — Zero Variance:** If either $\sigma_x = 0$ or $\sigma_y = 0$ (constant signals), $\rho_{xy}$ is undefined. In practice, add a small $\epsilon$ to the denominator to avoid division by zero.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) from a Statistical Decision Perspective

The MSE is the *expected squared error* when treating $y[n]$ as a noisy estimate of $x[n]$:

**Step 1 — Define the Error Signal:**

$$
e[n] = x[n] - y[n]
$$

*Explanation:* $e[n]$ is the instantaneous deviation at sample $n$.

**Step 2 — Define Instantaneous Squared Error:**

$$
e^{2}[n] = \left(x[n] - y[n]\right)^{2}
$$

*Explanation:* Squaring (rather than taking absolute value) emphasizes large errors, penalizes outliers, and yields a smooth, differentiable objective — which is critical for gradient-based optimization in adaptive filters.

**Step 3 — Take the Expected Value (Ensemble Average):**

$$
\text{MSE} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{2}[n]\,\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(x[n] - y[n]\right)^{2}\right]
$$

*Explanation:* For WSS ergodic signals, the ensemble average equals the time average.

**Step 4 — Replace Expectation with Time Average (Ergodicity):**

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n] - y[n]\right)^{2}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the practical, computable form used in DSP code and KTU lab assignments.

**Step 5 — Expand the Quadratic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSE} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x^{2}[n] - 2\,x[n]y[n] + y^{2}[n] \right) \\
&= \underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum x^{2}[n]}_{P_x} - 2\underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]y[n]}_{R_{xy}(0)} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum y^{2}[n]}_{P_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The MSE decomposes into the *power of $x$* plus the *power of $y$* minus *twice their cross-correlation*. This decomposition is foundational in Wiener filter theory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Magnitude-Squared Coherence (MSC)

The MSC extends the time-domain correlation concept into the frequency domain, and is derived from the Wiener–Khinchin theorem and the concept of *cross-spectral density*.

**Step 1 — Define the Cross-Spectral Density (CSD):**

$$
S_{xy}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ X^{*}(f)\, Y(f) \right]
$$

*Explanation:* The CSD captures the *frequency-resolved cross-correlation* between two signals. $X^{*}(f)$ is the complex conjugate of the Fourier transform of $x$.

**Step 2 — Define the Auto-Spectral Densities (Power Spectral Densities):**

$$
S_{xx}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \vert X(f) \vert^{2} \right]
$$

$$
S_{yy}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \vert Y(f) \vert^{2} \right]
$$

*Explanation:* The PSD measures the power distribution of each signal across frequencies.

**Step 3 — Define the MSC (Frequency-Domain Normalized Correlation):**

$$
\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = \frac{ \vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2} }{ S_{xx}(f)\, S_{yy}(f) }
$$

*Explanation:* The squared magnitude of the CSD is divided by the product of the two PSDs. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in the frequency domain, this ratio is always in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Interpretation of Boundary Values:**

- $\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = 1$ → The two signals are *perfectly linearly related* at frequency $f$.
- $\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = 0$ → The two signals are *linearly independent* at frequency $f$.
- $0 < \gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) < 1$ → Partial linear coupling, often due to noise, nonlinearities, or uncorrelated signal components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall — Common Misconception:** Coherence close to $1$ does *not* mean "the two signals are identical." It only means that one can be *linearly predicted* from the other with small mean-square error at that frequency. Nonlinear coupling will reduce coherence even when signals are functionally related.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Computing PCC, MSE, and SNR

**Problem:** Given two finite-length biosignals $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $y[n] = \{1.1, 1.9, 3.2, 3.8, 5.1\}$ (samples at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$), compute (a) Pearson Correlation Coefficient, (b) MSE, and (c) SNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pearson Correlation Coefficient:**

Step 1 — Means:
$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3.0$
$\bar{y} = \frac{1.1+1.9+3.2+3.8+5.1}{5} = 3.02$

Step 2 — Centered signals:
$(x - \bar{x}) = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$
$(y - \bar{y}) = \{-1.92, -1.12, 0.18, 0.78, 2.08\}$

Step 3 — Cross-product sum:
$\sum (x-\bar x)(y-\bar y) = (-2)(-1.92) + (-1)(-1.12) + (0)(0.18) + (1)(0.78) + (2)(2.08) = 3.84 + 1.12 + 0 + 0.78 + 4.16 = 9.90$

Step 4 — Variance of $x$:
$\sum (x-\bar x)^{2} = 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10.0$

Step 5 — Variance of $y$:
$\sum (y-\bar y)^{2} = 3.6864 + 1.2544 + 0.0324 + 0.6084 + 4.3264 = 9.908$

Step 6 — PCC:
$\rho_{xy} = \dfrac{9.90}{\sqrt{10.0 \times 9.908}} = \dfrac{9.90}{\sqrt{99.08}} = \dfrac{9.90}{9.954} \approx 0.9946$

**Result:** $\rho_{xy} \approx 0.995$ → The two signals are *almost perfectly linearly correlated*, consistent with $y$ being a slightly noisy version of $x$.

**(b) Mean Squared Error:**

Error signal: $e[n] = x[n] - y[n] = \{-0.1, 0.1, -0.2, 0.2, -0.1\}$

Squared errors: $\{0.01, 0.01, 0.04, 0.04, 0.01\}$

Sum: $0.11$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{0.11}{5} = 0.022
$$

**Result:** $\text{MSE} = 0.022$ → Average squared deviation is small, consistent with high similarity.

**(c) Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

Signal power: $P_x = \frac{1}{5}(1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25) = \frac{55}{5} = 11.0$

Noise power: $P_e = \text{MSE} = 0.022$

$$
\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_x}{P_e}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.0}{0.022}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(500) \approx 26.99\ \text{dB}
$$

**Result:** $\text{SNR} \approx 27\ \text{dB}$ → Very high signal fidelity, consistent with the correlation and MSE results.

---

## 3.5 Derivations Summary Table

| Measure | Foundational Principle | Bounded? | Translation-Invariant? | Scale-Invariant? |
|---------|------------------------|----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| PCC | Cauchy–Schwarz on centered inner product | Yes ($[-1,1]$) | Yes | Yes |
| MSE | Squared L2-norm of error | No ($[0,\infty)$) | No | No |
| RMSE | Square root of MSE | No | No | No |
| NRMSE | RMSE / signal range | Yes ($[0,1]$) | Yes | Yes |
| SNR | Log-ratio of powers | No | No | No |
| Coherence | Frequency-domain Cauchy–Schwarz | Yes ($[0,1]$) | Yes | Yes |
| Cosine Similarity | Geometric dot-product | Yes ($[-1,1]$) | No | Yes |
| PRD | Normalized squared-error | Yes ($[0\%,100\%]$) | No | Yes |

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — End-to-End Similarity Evaluation Pipeline

This diagram captures the **engineering workflow** for evaluating similarity between two biosignals, as it would be implemented in a MATLAB-based KTU mini-project or a Python biomedical research script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigX[/Acquire Signal x of length N/] --> pre1[Preprocessing: DC Removal, Bandpass Filter]
    sigY[/Acquire Signal y of length N/] --> pre2[Preprocessing: DC Removal, Bandpass Filter]
    pre1 --> est1[Estimate Statistical Moments]
    pre2 --> est2[Estimate Statistical Moments]
    est1 --> compute[Compute Similarity Metrics]
    est2 --> compute
    compute --> m1[Pearson Correlation]
    compute --> m2[MSE and RMSE]
    compute --> m3[SNR Calculation]
    compute --> m4[Coherence via Welch Method]
    compute --> m5[Cosine Similarity]
    m1 --> decision{Threshold Comparison}
    m2 --> decision
    m3 --> decision
    m4 --> decision
    m5 --> decision
    decision --> match[Signals are SIMILAR]
    decision --> nomatch[Signals are DISSIMILAR]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Coherence Computation Pipeline (Welch Method)

The Welch-based coherence estimation is the most frequently asked frequency-domain similarity procedure in KTU papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX[Input Signal x] --> seg1[Segment into K overlapping windows]
    inY[Input Signal y] --> seg2[Segment into K overlapping windows]
    seg1 --> win1[Apply Window Function Hann]
    seg2 --> win2[Apply Window Function Hann]
    win1 --> fft1[FFT of each segment]
    win2 --> fft2[FFT of each segment]
    fft1 --> psdX[Estimate Sxx Average of periodograms]
    fft2 --> psdY[Estimate Syy Average of periodograms]
    fft1 --> csd[Estimate Sxy Cross periodogram]
    fft2 --> csd
    csd --> mag[Compute magnitude squared of Sxy]
    psdX --> denom[Denominator Sxx times Syy]
    psdY --> denom
    mag --> ratio[Divide magnitude squared by denominator]
    denom --> ratio
    ratio --> out[Output Coherence Curve vs Frequency]
```

## 4.4 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual 2-D Representation)

The diagram below illustrates how similarity metrics partition the 2-D feature space. Signals whose feature vectors fall *inside* the bounded region are classified as similar.

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Feature Dimension 1] --> origin[Origin 0 0]
    axisY[Feature Dimension 2] --> origin
    region1[Circular Similarity Region radius equals threshold] --> origin
    region2[Hyperbolic PCC contour] --> origin
    region3[Linear Iso MSE contours] --> origin
    legend[Legend: Inside circle is similar. On PCC equals 1 is perfect. On MSE equals 0 is identical.] --> region1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** MSE contours are *concentric circles* in the 2-D error space. PCC contours are *straight lines* in the 2-D feature space (because PCC measures linear relationship). Coherence is a *1-D curve* indexed by frequency.
