# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]`

**Define the Pearson Correlation Coefficient. State its mathematical expression and the range of values it can take. Mention one biomedical application.**

**Model Answer:**

The Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) is a normalized measure of the *linear statistical dependence* between two real-valued signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$.

$$
\rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(x[n]-\bar{x}\right)\left(y[n]-\bar{y}\right)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left(x[n]-\bar{x}\right)^{2}} \, \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left(y[n]-\bar{y}\right)^{2}}}
$$

**Range:** $\rho_{xy} \in [-1, +1]$, where $+1$ indicates perfect positive linear correlation, $-1$ indicates perfect negative linear correlation, and $0$ indicates no linear correlation.

**Biomedical Application:** Template matching in QRS complex detection in ECG signals — the detected beat is compared against a stored normal-beat template using PCC, and beats with $\rho \geq 0.9$ are classified as normal sinus rhythm.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]`

**Explain the significance of Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in biomedical signal processing. Write the formula for SNR when $x$ is the clean signal and $e = x - y$ is the noise signal.**

**Model Answer:**

SNR quantifies the *level of the desired biosignal relative to the background noise* introduced by filtering, acquisition, or compression. A high SNR indicates that the signal of interest dominates the noise floor, which is critical for accurate clinical diagnosis.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^{2}[n]}{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{2}[n]} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} \right)
$$

**Significance in Biomedical Signal Processing:**

- It is the **standard benchmark** for evaluating denoising algorithms (e.g., wavelet denoising, Kalman filtering, empirical mode decomposition) in research papers and KTU project reports.
- Clinical ECG monitors typically require $\text{SNR} \geq 20$ dB for reliable automated ST-segment analysis.
- In compression research, an ECG compressed with $\text{PRD} < 9\%$ corresponds to $\text{SNR} > 21$ dB, the threshold for clinical acceptability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Strict):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified Module 3 Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Magnitude Squared Coherence (MSC) function. Derive its mathematical expression from the cross-spectral density. Explain the physical interpretation of values $0$, $1$, and intermediate values. State the application of MSC in EEG signal analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two ECG segments are recorded for the same patient at different time instants:
$x[n] = \{0.5, 1.2, 2.1, 1.8, 0.9\}$ and $y[n] = \{0.6, 1.4, 2.0, 1.7, 1.0\}$
Compute (i) the Pearson Correlation Coefficient, (ii) the Mean Squared Error, and (iii) the SNR in dB. Comment on the similarity of the two signals.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Magnitude Squared Coherence is the **frequency-domain analogue of the squared correlation coefficient**, defined for two jointly WSS random processes $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ as:

$$
\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2}}{S_{xx}(f)\, S_{yy}(f)}
$$

where $S_{xy}(f)$ is the cross-spectral density and $S_{xx}(f)$, $S_{yy}(f)$ are the auto-spectral densities (PSDs).

**Derivation Steps:**

(i) The cross-spectral density is defined as the Fourier transform of the cross-correlation function: $S_{xy}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xy}(\tau)\}$.

(ii) The auto-spectral density is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation: $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$.

(iii) By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** in the frequency domain, $\vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2} \leq S_{xx}(f) S_{yy}(f)$, ensuring the ratio is bounded in $[0, 1]$.

**Physical Interpretation:**

- $\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = 1$ → At frequency $f$, $x$ and $y$ are *perfectly linearly related* (e.g., one is a scaled, delayed, or filtered version of the other without noise).
- $\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = 0$ → At frequency $f$, $x$ and $y$ are *linearly independent*; no linear model can predict one from the other.
- $0 < \gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) < 1$ → Partial linear coupling — caused by additive noise, nonlinear distortions, or uncorrelated signal components.

**EEG Application:** In multi-channel EEG recordings, MSC is used to measure *inter-hemispheric* and *intra-hemispheric* coupling between different brain regions. It is the foundation of cognitive neuroscience studies, seizure localization in epilepsy, and the analysis of event-related potentials (ERPs). For example, high frontal-parietal coherence in the alpha band (8–13 Hz) is associated with attention and working memory tasks.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the means:**

$\bar{x} = \frac{0.5 + 1.2 + 2.1 + 1.8 + 0.9}{5} = \frac{6.5}{5} = 1.3$

$\bar{y} = \frac{0.6 + 1.4 + 2.0 + 1.7 + 1.0}{5} = \frac{6.7}{5} = 1.34$

**Step 2 — Centered signals:**

$(x - \bar{x}) = \{-0.8, -0.1, 0.8, 0.5, -0.4\}$

$(y - \bar{y}) = \{-0.74, 0.06, 0.66, 0.36, -0.34\}$

**Step 3 — Sum of cross-products:**

$\sum (x - \bar{x})(y - \bar{y}) = (-0.8)(-0.74) + (-0.1)(0.06) + (0.8)(0.66) + (0.5)(0.36) + (-0.4)(-0.34)$

$= 0.592 - 0.006 + 0.528 + 0.180 + 0.136 = 1.430$

**Step 4 — Sums of squared centered values:**

$\sum (x - \bar{x})^{2} = 0.64 + 0.01 + 0.64 + 0.25 + 0.16 = 1.70$

$\sum (y - \bar{y})^{2} = 0.5476 + 0.0036 + 0.4356 + 0.1296 + 0.1156 = 1.232$

**Step 5 — Pearson Correlation Coefficient:**

$$
\rho_{xy} = \frac{1.430}{\sqrt{1.70 \times 1.232}} = \frac{1.430}{\sqrt{2.0944}} = \frac{1.430}{1.4472} \approx 0.988
$$

**Step 6 — Error signal and MSE:**

$e[n] = x[n] - y[n] = \{-0.1, -0.2, 0.1, 0.1, -0.1\}$

$e^{2}[n] = \{0.01, 0.04, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01\}$

$\sum e^{2}[n] = 0.08$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{0.08}{5} = 0.016
$$

**Step 7 — Signal power and SNR:**

$P_x = \frac{1}{5}(0.25 + 1.44 + 4.41 + 3.24 + 0.81) = \frac{10.15}{5} = 2.03$

$$
\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.03}{0.016}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(126.875) \approx 21.03\ \text{dB}
$$

**Comment:** The two ECG segments exhibit *very high similarity* — $\rho_{xy} \approx 0.988$ is close to unity, the MSE is small at $0.016$, and the SNR of $\approx 21$ dB exceeds the clinical ECG threshold. The patient shows consistent cardiac morphology across the two recordings.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the following similarity measures for biomedical signals: (i) Pearson Correlation Coefficient, (ii) Mean Squared Error, (iii) Cosine Similarity. Provide formulas, ranges, and discuss the suitability of each for evaluating (1) ECG template matching, (2) EEG denoising validation, and (3) patient-to-patient feature-vector comparison in a machine learning pipeline.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the Percentage Root Mean Square Difference (PRD) used in ECG compression research. A clean ECG segment $x[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 4, 2\}$ is compressed and reconstructed as $\hat{x}[n] = \{2.1, 3.9, 5.8, 4.2, 2.1\}$. Calculate the PRD and the corresponding Quality Score (QS), defined as $\text{QS} = \dfrac{\text{CR}}{\text{PRD}}$, assuming a Compression Ratio (CR) of 12. Comment on the clinical acceptability of this compression.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Aspect | Pearson CC | MSE | Cosine Similarity |
|--------|-----------|-----|-------------------|
| **Formula** | $\rho = \dfrac{\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (x-y)^{2}$ | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\langle x, y\rangle}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert}$ |
| **Range** | $[-1, +1]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[-1, +1]$ |
| **Optimal** | $+1$ | $0$ | $+1$ |
| **Translation Invariant** | Yes | No | No |
| **Scale Invariant** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Units** | Dimensionless | Signal power units | Dimensionless |

**Application Suitability:**

1. **ECG Template Matching:** *Pearson Correlation Coefficient* is the most suitable. It is *robust to baseline wander* (translation invariance), handles the natural amplitude variation between patients (scale invariance), and gives an interpretable $[-1, 1]$ score. MSE is unsuitable because it conflates amplitude mismatch with morphological mismatch.

2. **EEG Denoising Validation:** *MSE* is most suitable in this case, often expressed in normalized form (NRMSE) or as SNR. Since the goal is to verify that the *filtered EEG is close to the ground-truth clean EEG in amplitude*, MSE directly measures reconstruction error. PCC may be misleading if the denoiser introduces a small DC offset.

3. **Patient-to-Patient Feature-Vector Comparison (ML pipeline):** *Cosine Similarity* is the standard choice in machine learning. It compares the *direction* of feature vectors rather than their magnitudes, making it ideal for high-dimensional normalized feature embeddings (e.g., from PCA, autoencoders, or deep metric learning). It is also computationally efficient, requiring only a dot product and two norms.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The Percentage Root Mean Square Difference (PRD) is the **normalized** version of the RMSE, expressed as a percentage of the signal's power. It is the **gold-standard quality metric** in ECG compression literature.

$$
\text{PRD} = \sqrt{ \frac{ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \hat{x}[n] \right)^{2} }{ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^{2}[n] } } \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the error signal:**

$e[n] = x[n] - \hat{x}[n] = \{2 - 2.1,\; 4 - 3.9,\; 6 - 5.8,\; 4 - 4.2,\; 2 - 2.1\}$

$e[n] = \{-0.1,\; 0.1,\; 0.2,\; -0.2,\; -0.1\}$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of squared errors:**

$\sum e^{2}[n] = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.01 = 0.11$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum of squared original samples:**

$\sum x^{2}[n] = 4 + 16 + 36 + 16 + 4 = 76$

**Step 4 — Compute the PRD:**

$$
\text{PRD} = \sqrt{ \frac{0.11}{76} } \times 100\% = \sqrt{0.001447} \times 100\% \approx 0.0380 \times 100\% \approx 3.80\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Quality Score (QS):**

$$
\text{QS} = \frac{\text{CR}}{\text{PRD}} = \frac{12}{3.80} \approx 3.16
$$

**Step 6 — Clinical Acceptability Comment:**

According to the ECG compression literature, the **clinical acceptability threshold** is $\text{PRD} < 9\%$ (Jalaleddine et al.). Our computed $\text{PRD} = 3.80\%$ is *well below* this threshold, indicating that the reconstructed ECG is clinically indistinguishable from the original. The Quality Score of $3.16$ is moderate — typical of efficient but not aggressive ECG compression algorithms. The combination of high CR (12) and low PRD (3.80%) makes this compression scheme clinically viable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PRD with PRDN** — PRD is unnormalized; the normalized variant divides by $N$ or uses a different denominator. Verify the formula stated in the question.
> 2. **Forgetting the percentage multiplier** — Many students write $\sqrt{0.11/76}$ directly and forget to multiply by 100, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Misinterpreting the Quality Score** — QS is *higher* when both compression ratio is high *and* distortion (PRD) is low. A QS below 2 is considered poor.
> 4. **Sign of PCC confusion** — A high positive PCC ($>+0.9$) indicates similarity, not just a "high number." Students often quote $\rho$ without interpreting its sign.
> 5. **Coherence interpretation trap** — A coherence of $0.95$ does not mean 95% identical — it means 95% of the *linear* relationship is preserved at that frequency.
> 6. **MSE vs RMSE unit confusion** — RMSE is in the same units as the signal; MSE is in squared units. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC):** Normalized covariance in $[-1, +1]$. Translation- and scale-invariant. Best for template matching.
- **Cross-Correlation at Lag $k$:** Used for *time-delay estimation* between two signals (e.g., estimating fetal ECG from abdominal recordings).
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average squared deviation, always $\geq 0$. Loss function for Wiener, LMS, RLS filters.
- **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):** Square root of MSE — same units as the signal. Always report RMSE, not MSE, when units matter.
- **Normalized RMSE (NRMSE):** RMSE divided by signal range, bounded in $[0, 1]$ — scale-invariant quality metric.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR in dB):** $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{signal}} / P_{\text{noise}})$. Higher is better. ECG clinical threshold $\geq 20$ dB.
- **Peak SNR (PSNR):** Uses $x_{\text{peak}}^{2}$ in the numerator. More sensitive to outliers than SNR. Common in image-quality research.
- **Magnitude Squared Coherence (MSC):** Frequency-domain PCC, bounded in $[0, 1]$. Foundation of EEG channel-coupling analysis.
- **Cosine Similarity:** Geometric dot-product, ignores magnitude. Standard in ML pipelines and $k$-NN classifiers.
- **Percentage Root Mean Square Difference (PRD):** ECG compression benchmark. $\text{PRD} < 9\%$ is the clinical threshold.
- **Quality Score (QS):** $\text{QS} = \text{CR} / \text{PRD}$. Higher is better; combines compression efficiency and fidelity.
- **Cauchy–Schwarz Foundation:** All bounded similarity measures ($[-1,1]$ or $[0,1]$) are direct consequences of this inequality.
- **Translation Invariance:** Achieved by *mean-centering* the signals before computation.
- **Scale Invariance:** Achieved by *dividing by the standard deviation or norm* of each signal.
- **Ergodicity Assumption:** All ensemble expectations are replaced by time averages — required for practical computation.
- **Edge Cases:** If $\sigma_x = 0$ or $\sigma_y = 0$, PCC is undefined. If $P_{\text{noise}} = 0$, SNR is $+\infty$ dB. If $x = \hat{x}$, MSE = 0 and PRD = 0%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid — The 4-Step Recipe for Any Similarity Computation:**
> 1. **Center** the signals (subtract means).
> 2. **Normalize** by standard deviation / norm / range.
> 3. **Combine** using dot-product, squared error, or spectral ratio.
> 4. **Bound** the result using Cauchy–Schwarz, logarithm, or percentage scaling.
>
> Every KTU 2024 measure on this topic fits one or more of these four operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of Synthetic Fractal Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generation of Synthetic Fractal Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **synthetic fractal signal** is a numerically generated, time-domain waveform whose statistical properties (specifically, its power spectral density and amplitude distribution) remain invariant under scaling transformations of time and amplitude. These signals are mathematically constructed using **fractional Brownian motion (fBm)** models, the **Weierstrass–Mandelbrot (W–M) function**, or **spectral synthesis (FFT-based shaping)** to emulate the long-range temporal correlations observed in physiological recordings such as heart-rate variability (HRV), electroencephalograms (EEG), surface electromyograms (sEMG), and gait dynamics.

A stochastic process $X(t)$ is classified as a **statistically self-similar fractal signal** if, for any time-scaling factor $a > 0$, there exists a Hurst exponent $H \in (0, 1)$ such that:

$$X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$$

where $\stackrel{d}{=}$ denotes equality in statistical distribution. The scaling exponent $H$ uniquely characterizes the "roughness" of the trajectory, while the **fractal dimension** $D$ of its graph is given by:

$$D = 2 - H$$

The **power spectral density** of a fractal signal follows the canonical inverse-power law:

$$S_{x}(f) \;\propto\; \frac{1}{f^{\beta}}, \qquad \beta = 2H + 1$$

where $\beta \in (1, 3)$ for classical fractional Brownian motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Synthetic fractal signals are indispensable tools in biomedical engineering because they allow controlled validation of non-linear analysis algorithms (DFA, Hurst estimation, Higuchi's method) under *known* ground-truth values of $H$ and $\beta$ — something impossible to obtain from real physiological recordings.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Coastline of Norway"

Imagine measuring the **coastline of Norway** with a ruler. As you switch to a finer ruler, the measured length *grows* — not because the coast physically extends, but because more bays, fjords, and sub-bays are revealed at finer scales. Mandelbrot formalized this paradox: **the apparent complexity of the curve increases as the measurement scale shrinks.**

A synthetic fractal signal behaves identically. If you zoom into a 1-second window of an HRV trace, you see fluctuations that *statistically resemble* the 1-hour trace. This **scale-invariant self-similarity** is the defining fingerprint of fractal signals.

> **Physical Constants & Metrics (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Hurst exponent $H = 0.5$** → classical uncorrelated Brownian motion (random walk).
> - **Hurst exponent $0 < H < 0.5$** → **anti-persistent** behavior (signal tends to reverse direction; $\beta < 2$).
> - **Hurst exponent $0.5 < H < 1$** → **persistent** behavior (long-range positive correlations; $\beta > 2$).
> - **Fractal dimension $D = 2 - H$** for a 1-D waveform's graph; $D \in (1, 2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why generate synthetic fractals?**
> 1. **Algorithm benchmarking** with known $H$.
> 2. **Monte Carlo simulation** of physiological noise.
> 3. **Texture generation** for medical imaging phantoms.
> 4. **Theoretical study** of long-range dependence in neural/cardiac systems.

---

## 1.3 Biomedical Relevance Matrix

| Physiological Signal | Typical $\beta$ | Typical $H$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Healthy HRV | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.0 – 0.25 | Strong anti-persistence |
| ECG baseline wander | ≈ 2.0 | 0.5 | Pure Brownian motion |
| EEG (eyes open) | 1.5 – 2.5 | 0.25 – 0.75 | Mixed persistence |
| sEMG (sustained contraction) | 2.5 – 3.5 | 0.75 – 1.0 | Strong persistence |
| Pathological HRV (CHF) | ≈ 1.0 | ≈ 0.0 | Loss of fractal complexity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Architecture of Fractal Signals

A fractal signal is fully characterized by **three orthogonal properties**:

1. **Scale invariance** (statistical self-similarity).
2. **Long-range temporal correlation** (non-exponential decay of autocorrelation).
3. **Power-law spectral density** $S_x(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$.

The synthesis of such a signal therefore requires us to enforce *at least one* of these properties in the generation algorithm — all three follow mathematically once any one is enforced correctly.

---

## 2.2 The Three Principal Synthesis Methods

### Method A — Fractional Brownian Motion via Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD)

This is the **most commonly tested method** in KTU examinations. It is a recursive spatial-domain algorithm:

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Define endpoints $X(0) = 0$ and $X(T) = G \cdot T^{H}$, where $G \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_0^{2})$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable. The initial variance is:

$$\sigma_{0}^{2} = \frac{\Gamma(1 - 2H) \cos(\pi H)}{\pi H} \cdot T^{2H}$$

**Step 2 — Recursive Midpoint Insertion:** At recursion level $k$, with segment length $\Delta t = T / 2^{k}$, generate the midpoint value:

$$X\!\left(\frac{t_{i} + t_{i+1}}{2}\right) = \frac{X(t_{i}) + X(t_{i+1})}{2} + G_{k} \cdot (\Delta t)^{H}$$

where $G_{k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{k}^{2})$ with variance that scales as:

$$\sigma_{k}^{2} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2^{k}}\right)^{2H} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{2kH}}$$

**Step 3 — Termination:** Continue until the desired sample resolution $N = 2^{K}$ is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $(Δt)^H$ scaling in the displacement is the *only* place where the Hurst exponent enters the algorithm.** Changing $H$ from 0.3 to 0.9 drastically alters the smoothness of the trajectory.

### Method B — Weierstrass–Mandelbrot (W–M) Function Synthesis

The W–M function is a **continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere** function, making it the canonical mathematical fractal:

$$W_{H}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\gamma^{n} t + \varphi_{n}) - \cos(\gamma^{n} t)}{\gamma^{n(H + 0.5)}}$$

where:
- $\gamma > 1$ is the frequency-base multiplier (commonly $\gamma = 1.5$ or $e$).
- $\varphi_{n}$ are independent uniformly distributed phases $\varphi_{n} \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 2\pi)$.
- $H$ is the Hurst exponent controlling local roughness.

In **practical finite summation** form (used in DSP synthesis):

$$W_{H}(t) \approx \sum_{n=n_{1}}^{n_{2}} \gamma^{-n(H + 0.5)} \sin(\gamma^{n} t + \varphi_{n})$$

### Method C — Spectral Synthesis (FFT-based $1/f^{\beta}$ Shaping)

This is the **fastest** method, suitable for long-duration signals:

**Step 1:** Generate complex white Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ in the frequency domain: $Y[k]$.

**Step 2:** Multiply by the spectral-shaping filter:

$$H_{\text{shape}}[k] = \frac{1}{f_{k}^{\beta/2}} = \left(\frac{k}{N}\right)^{-\beta/2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply inverse FFT to obtain the synthetic fractal time series:

$$x[n] = \text{IFFT}\!\left\{ Y[k] \cdot H_{\text{shape}}[k] \right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\beta/2$ and not $\beta$?** Because power spectral density is the *squared magnitude* of the FFT: $|H|^2 \propto f^{-\beta}$, so $|H| \propto f^{-\beta/2}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$ | Statistical self-similarity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D = 2 - H$ | Fractal dimension of waveform graph | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\beta = 2H + 1$ | Power-law exponent (1-D fBm) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $H = (\beta - 1) / 2$ | Inverse mapping for $\beta$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S_{x}(f) = \dfrac{K}{f^{\beta}}$ | Power spectral density | V²/Hz |
| 6 | $R_{x}(\tau) = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2}\!\left(\vert\tau\vert^{2H} - 1\right)$ | Autocorrelation of fBm | V² |
| 7 | $W_{H}(t) = \sum \gamma^{-n(H+0.5)} \sin(\gamma^{n} t + \varphi_{n})$ | Weierstrass–Mandelbrot synthesis | dimensionless |
| 8 | $x_{\text{mid}} = \dfrac{x_{i} + x_{i+1}}{2} + G_{k} (\Delta t)^{H}$ | Midpoint displacement recurrence | signal units |
| 9 | $\sigma_{k}^{2} \propto (\Delta t)^{2H} \propto 2^{-2kH}$ | Variance decay per recursion | signal units² |
| 10 | $K(H) = \dfrac{\Gamma(2H+1)\sin(\pi H)}{H \pi (2H+1)}$ | Normalization constant for fBm | signal units²·sec⁻²ᴴ |

> **Note on absolute values:** Per KTU formatting rules, all absolute-value bars in tables use `\vert\;\vert` instead of `|...|` to prevent markdown parsing errors.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

Synthetic fractal signals underpin:

- **Heart-Rate Variability (HRV) modeling:** Generating surrogate HRV traces with controlled $H$ to validate DFA (Detrended Fluctuation Analysis) in the $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ regimes.
- **EEG microstate synthesis:** Replicating $1/f^{\beta}$ neural background activity for BCI benchmarking.
- **EMG fatigue modeling:** Synthetic sEMG with adjustable persistence $H$ to test fatigue estimators.
- **Compression algorithms:** Fractal compression of medical images exploits self-similarity across scales.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Spectrum of Fractional Brownian Motion

**Goal:** Show that for $B_H(t)$ (fractional Brownian motion), the PSD obeys $S(f) \propto f^{-(2H+1)}$.

### Step 1 — Definition of fBm

By Mandelbrot & Van Ness (1968), fractional Brownian motion is defined via the moving-average integral:

$$B_{H}(t) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(H + 0.5)} \int_{-\infty}^{0}\!\left[ (t - s)^{H - 0.5} - (-s)^{H - 0.5} \right] dW(s) \;+\; \frac{1}{\Gamma(H + 0.5)} \int_{0}^{t} (t - s)^{H - 0.5} dW(s)$$

where $W(s)$ is standard Wiener noise and $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the Gamma function.

### Step 2 — Stationary Increments

The key property of fBm is that the increments $\Delta B_H(\tau) = B_H(t + \tau) - B_H(t)$ are *stationary* Gaussian processes with variance:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[ \Delta B_H(\tau)^{2} \right] = V_{H} \cdot \vert\tau\vert^{2H}$$

with the constant $V_H$:

$$V_{H} = \frac{1}{\Gamma(2H+1) \sin(\pi H)} \cdot \pi \cdot \Gamma(2H+1)\sin(\pi H) / \pi = \frac{1}{\pi}\Gamma(1-2H)\cos(\pi H)$$

(omitting intermediate normalization for brevity, retaining only the $\tau^{2H}$ dependence).

### Step 3 — Autocorrelation Function of Increments

For zero-mean increments $Y_{\tau}(t) = B_H(t + \tau) - B_H(t)$:

$$R_{Y}(\Delta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ Y_{\tau}(t) \cdot Y_{\tau}(t + \Delta) \right] = \frac{V_{H}}{2}\!\left( \vert\Delta + \tau\vert^{2H} + \vert\Delta - \tau\vert^{2H} - 2\vert\Delta\vert^{2H} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Fourier Transform of Autocorrelation

By the Wiener–Khinchin theorem, $S_Y(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_Y(\Delta)\}$. For large $\vert\Delta\vert \gg \tau$, the asymptotic form is:

$$R_{Y}(\Delta) \sim V_{H} \cdot H \cdot (2H - 1) \cdot \tau^{2} \cdot \vert\Delta\vert^{2H - 2}$$

Computing the Fourier transform of $\vert\Delta\vert^{2H-2}$ (a fractional integral kernel):

$$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{ \vert\Delta\vert^{2H - 2} \right\}(f) = \frac{2 \Gamma(2H - 1) \sin(\pi(H - 0.5))}{(2\pi f)^{2H - 1}} = C(H) \cdot f^{1 - 2H}$$

### Step 5 — Final PSD Form

Multiplying constants and substituting back:

$$S_{Y}(f) = C_{1}(H, \tau) \cdot f^{-(2H - 1)} \cdot \tau^{2} \;\equiv\; \frac{K(H)}{f^{2H - 1}}$$

For the increments themselves (not their autocorrelation), the spectrum of the original fBm $B_H(t)$ is:

$$S_{B_H}(f) = \frac{K(H)}{f^{2H + 1}}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;\beta = 2H + 1\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** For $H = 0.5$ (classical Brownian motion), $\beta = 2$, recovering the well-known $1/f^{2}$ spectrum of integrated white noise. For $H \to 1$, $\beta \to 3$, corresponding to very smooth, highly persistent signals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Self-Similarity from the Power-Law Spectrum

**Goal:** Prove that $S(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$ implies $X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$ with $H = (\beta - 1)/2$.

### Step 1 — Time-Scaling of Power Spectrum

If $X(t)$ is dilated in time by factor $a$, the new process is $Y(t) = X(at)$. Its PSD transforms as:

$$S_{Y}(f) = \frac{1}{a}\,S_{X}\!\left(\frac{f}{a}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substituting the Power-Law Form

$$S_{Y}(f) = \frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{K}{(f/a)^{\beta}} = \frac{K \, a^{\beta - 1}}{f^{\beta}}$$

### Step 3 — Distributional Equivalence

For the distribution of $Y(t)$ to be identical to that of $X(t)$ up to a multiplicative amplitude factor $a^{H}$ (i.e., $Y \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X$), we require the *variance* to scale by $a^{2H}$:

$$\sigma_{Y}^{2} = a^{2H} \sigma_{X}^{2}$$

The variance equals the integral of the PSD over all frequencies:

$$\sigma_{Y}^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} S_{Y}(f)\, df = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{K a^{\beta - 1}}{f^{\beta}}\, df$$

Comparing with $\sigma_X^{2} = \int K f^{-\beta}\, df$, we obtain:

$$a^{2H} = a^{\beta - 1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2H = \beta - 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;H = \frac{\beta - 1}{2}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation makes it absolutely clear **why** the spectral-synthesis method (Method C) works: filtering white noise by $1/f^{\beta/2}$ in the frequency domain produces a time series whose PSD is $1/f^{\beta}$, whose variance scales as $a^{\beta - 1}$ under time dilation, and which therefore exhibits statistical self-similarity with Hurst exponent $H = (\beta - 1)/2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Variance of the Midpoint Displacement

**Goal:** Show that in the RMD method, the added Gaussian noise at recursion level $k$ must have variance $\sigma_k^2 = 2^{-2kH} \sigma_0^2$.

### Step 1 — Required Property

We require the variance of $X(t)$ over an interval of length $\Delta t$ to satisfy:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[ X(t + \Delta t) - X(t) \right] = V_{H} \cdot (\Delta t)^{2H}$$

### Step 2 — Recursion Geometry

At level $k = 1$, the interval is $\Delta t_1 = T/2$. The midpoint value is constructed from the two endpoints $X(0)$ and $X(T)$ as:

$$X(T/2) = \frac{X(0) + X(T)}{2} + G_{1}$$

The increment $X(T/2) - X(0)$ has variance:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[ X(T/2) - X(0) \right] = \frac{\text{Var}[X(T)]}{4} + \text{Var}[G_1]$$

By induction, $\text{Var}[X(T)] = V_H T^{2H}$, so:

$$\frac{V_H T^{2H}}{4} + \sigma_1^{2} = V_H (T/2)^{2H}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_1^2$

$$\sigma_{1}^{2} = V_H \left[ \left(\frac{T}{2}\right)^{2H} - \frac{T^{2H}}{4} \right] = V_H \, T^{2H} \left( 2^{-2H} - \frac{1}{4} \right)$$

For general recursion level $k$, the analogous derivation yields:

$$\sigma_{k}^{2} = \frac{V_H \, T^{2H}}{2} \left( 2^{-2kH} - 2^{-2k+1} \right) \;\propto\; 2^{-2kH}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{k}^{2} \;\approx\; \frac{V_H \, T^{2H}}{2^{2kH + 1}}\;}$$

confirming the fundamental scaling law that governs all recursive fractal synthesis.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Derived Relationships

| Derived Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral exponent | $\beta = 2H + 1$ | Links time-domain roughness to frequency-domain slope |
| Hurst from PSD | $H = (\beta - 1)/2$ | Inverse relation |
| Fractal dimension | $D = 2 - H$ | Embedding dimension 2 minus roughness |
| Midpoint variance | $\sigma_k^2 \propto 2^{-2kH}$ | Per-level noise amplitude in RMD |
| Spectral filter | $\vert H(f)\vert \propto f^{-\beta/2}$ | Spectral-shaping gain |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Fractal Signal Synthesis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs: Hurst Exponent H, Length N, Method] --> B{Choose Synthesis Method}
    B -->|Spatial Recursive| C[Random Midpoint Displacement RMD]
    B -->|Continuous Analytic| D[Weierstrass Mandelbrot Function]
    B -->|Frequency Domain| E[Spectral Synthesis via FFT]
    C --> F[Initialize Endpoints X0 XT]
    F --> G[Loop k = 1 to K log2 N]
    G --> H[Compute Midpoint Xmid X Xi + Xi+1 / 2]
    H --> I[Add Gaussian Noise Gk times Delta t to power H]
    I --> J[Refine Resolution Delta t Delta t / 2]
    J --> G
    G --> K[Output Fractal Signal xRMD n]
    D --> L[Initialize Phase Array phi n Unif 0 2 pi]
    L --> M[Loop n = n1 to n2]
    M --> N[Compute Term gamma to power nH + 0.5 times sin gamma to power n t plus phi n]
    N --> O[Accumulate Sum to W t]
    O --> M
    M --> P[Output Fractal Signal wH t]
    E --> Q[Generate White Gaussian Noise Yk in Frequency Domain]
    Q --> R[Apply Spectral Shaping Filter H f equal to f to power beta / 2]
    R --> S[Inverse FFT to Obtain Time Domain Signal]
    S --> T[Output Fractal Signal xSpec n]
    K --> U[Validation Block]
    P --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Estimate Hhat via DFA or R S Analysis]
    V --> W{Error between Hhat and H less than 5 percent}
    W -->|Yes| X[Accept Synthetic Signal]
    W -->|No| Y[Reject and Regenerate with Adjusted Parameters]
```

> **Caption:** The above flowchart maps the complete decision-and-data pipeline for generating synthetic fractal signals using any of the three principal methods (RMD, W–M, or Spectral Synthesis), followed by an automatic validation step in which the recovered Hurst exponent is compared to the requested value.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparative View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[RMD Method] -->|Time domain recursion| S1[Signal X1 n]
    M2[W M Method] -->|Harmonic sum| S2[Signal X2 t]
    M3[Spectral Method] -->|FFT IFFT| S3[Signal X3 n]
    S1 --> ANA[Common Analysis Stage]
    S2 --> ANA
    S3 --> ANA
    ANA --> DFA[Detrended Fluctuation Analysis]
    ANA --> PSA[Power Spectral Density Fit]
    ANA --> HUR[Hurst Exponent Estimator]
    DFA --> OUT[Recovered H and Beta]
    PSA --> OUT
    HUR --> OUT
    OUT --> VAL[Compare Estimated vs Requested H]
```

> **Caption:** All three synthesis methods converge into a unified analysis stage that recovers $H$ and $\beta$ independently, allowing empirical validation of the synthesis algorithm.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Effect of Hurst Exponent on Signal Morphology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowH[H less than 0.5 Anti Persistent]
        A1[Jagged Zigzag] --> A2[Many Zero Crossings]
    end
    subgraph MidH[H equal to 0.5 Pure Brownian]
        B1[Moderate Smoothness] --> B2[Uncorrelated Increments]
    end
    subgraph HighH[H greater than 0.5 Persistent]
        C1[Smooth Drifts] --> C2[Long Trending Episodes]
    end
```

> **Caption:** Visual intuition for how varying $H$ alters signal character — a key KTU viva question. As $H \to 1$, the synthetic signal approaches a smooth, monotonically drifting trajectory (low $\beta$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define synthetic fractal signals. What is the role of the Hurst exponent in characterizing them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Synthetic fractal signals are computer-generated time-domain waveforms that exhibit statistical self-similarity across multiple time scales. They are constructed to replicate the long-range temporal correlations and $1/f^{\beta}$ power spectra observed in physiological signals such as HRV and EEG. **Role of the Hurst exponent $H$ (2 marks for explanation):** The Hurst exponent $H \in (0,1)$ quantitatively measures the *persistence* of the signal — i.e., the tendency of the trajectory to continue in its current direction. Values $H > 0.5$ indicate persistent (positively correlated) behavior; $H < 0.5$ indicates anti-persistent (negatively correlated) behavior; $H = 0.5$ corresponds to classical uncorrelated Brownian motion. The exponent uniquely determines the power-law slope $\beta = 2H+1$ and the fractal dimension $D = 2 - H$, making it the single most informative parameter of any fractal signal.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Weierstrass–Mandelbrot function with its significance in fractal signal synthesis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Weierstrass–Mandelbrot function is given by:

$$W_{H}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\gamma^{n} t + \varphi_{n})}{\gamma^{n(H + 0.5)}}$$

where $\gamma > 1$ is a frequency-base multiplier, $\varphi_n$ are independent uniform phases in $[0, 2\pi)$, and $H$ is the Hurst exponent. **Significance (2 marks):** It is *continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere*, making it the canonical continuous-time fractal. Its self-similarity is exact rather than statistical, which is useful as a theoretical benchmark. The function has a fractal dimension $D = 2 - H$ and a power spectrum that decays as $1/f^{\beta}$ with $\beta = 2H+1$, allowing direct control of signal complexity through a single parameter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the power spectral exponent $\beta$ and the Hurst exponent $H$ for a fractional Brownian motion process $B_H(t)$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD) algorithm** for generating synthetic fractal signals. Derive the variance scaling law $\sigma_k^2 \propto 2^{-2kH}$ for the recursion levels. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $\beta = 2H + 1$ **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the self-similarity property $X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$ and the PSD of fractional Brownian motion $S_{B_H}(f) = K / f^{\beta}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the time-dilation theorem for power spectra:
$$S_{Y}(f) = \frac{1}{a}\,S_{X}\!\left(\frac{f}{a}\right) = \frac{K\, a^{\beta - 1}}{f^{\beta}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the variance by integrating the PSD:
$$\sigma_{Y}^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{K a^{\beta - 1}}{f^{\beta}}\, df = a^{\beta - 1}\!\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{K}{f^{\beta}}\, df = a^{\beta - 1} \sigma_{X}^{2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equate with the self-similarity variance scaling $a^{2H}\sigma_X^{2}$:
$$a^{\beta - 1} = a^{2H} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \beta - 1 = 2H \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\beta = 2H + 1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the inverse mapping $H = (\beta - 1)/2$ and the special case $H = 0.5 \Rightarrow \beta = 2$ (classical Brownian motion).

---

#### Part (b) — RMD Algorithm & Variance Derivation **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the algorithm with clear pseudocode-like narrative:

- Initialize endpoints $X(0) = 0$, $X(T) = G_0 \cdot T^{H}$ with $G_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_0^2)$.
- For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$:
    - At each interval of length $\Delta t = T/2^{k}$, compute midpoint:
      $$X_{\text{mid}} = \frac{X_i + X_{i+1}}{2} + G_k (\Delta t)^{H}, \quad G_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_k^2)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the required variance-matching condition:
$$\text{Var}[X(t + \Delta t) - X(t)] = V_H (\Delta t)^{2H}$$

For a midpoint at level $k$, $\Delta t = T / 2^{k}$:
$$\text{Var}[X_{\text{mid}} - X_i] = \frac{\text{Var}[X_{i+1} - X_i]}{4} + \sigma_k^2 = \frac{V_H (T/2^{k-1})^{2H}}{4} + \sigma_k^2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Equate to $V_H (T/2^k)^{2H}$ and solve:

$$\sigma_k^2 = V_H T^{2H}\!\left( 2^{-2kH} - 2^{-2(k-1)H - 2} \right) \;\propto\; 2^{-2kH}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_k^2 \approx \frac{V_H \, T^{2H}}{2^{2kH + 1}}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude with the practical implementation note — at each recursion level the displacement amplitude is scaled by $(\Delta t)^{H}$, which is the *only* location where $H$ enters the algorithm.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe the **spectral synthesis method** for generating $1/f^{\beta}$ fractal signals. Derive the form of the frequency-domain shaping filter and explain why the exponent is $\beta/2$ rather than $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Generate a synthetic fractal signal with $H = 0.7$ and length $N = 1024$ using the Weierstrass–Mandelbrot function. Compute the expected power spectral exponent $\beta$ and explain the physical meaning of this value for a biomedical signal like surface EMG. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Spectral Synthesis Method **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Generate zero-mean unit-variance complex Gaussian white noise in the frequency domain: $Y[k] \sim \mathcal{CN}(0, 1)$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The desired PSD is $S_x(f) \propto f^{-\beta}$, which equals $|X(f)|^2$. Therefore the magnitude of the shaping filter must be:
$$|H_{\text{shape}}(f)| = \sqrt{S_x(f)} \propto f^{-\beta/2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Multiply in frequency domain and inverse-transform:
$$X[k] = Y[k] \cdot k^{-\beta/2}, \qquad x[n] = \text{IFFT}\{X[k]\}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Explain the $\beta/2$ factor: because the PSD is a *power* quantity (squared magnitude), taking its square root to obtain the filter transfer function halves the exponent. Conclude that this method is computationally efficient ($O(N \log N)$), ideal for long biomedical recordings, and produces exact statistical self-similarity for any requested $H$.

---

#### Part (b) — W–M Synthesis with $H = 0.7$ **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For $H = 0.7$:
$$\beta = 2H + 1 = 2(0.7) + 1 = 2.4$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Set up the W–M series with $\gamma = 1.5$, $n \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 9\}$ for $N = 1024$ samples:
$$W_{0.7}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{9} (1.5)^{-n(1.2)} \sin\!\left((1.5)^{n} t + \varphi_n\right)$$

Draw ten independent uniform phases $\varphi_n \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 2\pi)$ and evaluate at $t = 2\pi k / 1024$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the periodogram of $W_{0.7}(t)$ and verify the slope of $\log S(f)$ vs $\log f$ is approximately $-2.4$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Biomedical interpretation:** $\beta = 2.4$ corresponds to a strongly persistent signal ($H = 0.7$). In sEMG recordings during sustained isometric contraction, such $\beta$ values reflect long-range temporal correlations between motor-unit firing patterns — consistent with the slow, cumulative recruitment of additional motor units as the muscle fatigues. The Hurst exponent near 0.7 is often quoted as a marker of *healthy* neuromuscular control, with reductions toward $H = 0.5$ observed in pathological or fatigued states.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Fractal-Signal Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\beta$ with $H$** — remember $\beta = 2H + 1$, not $H$ directly. A surprising number of students write $\beta = H$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $\beta/2$** in the spectral synthesis filter — examiners *specifically* test this.
> 3. **Omitting the $(Δt)^H$ term** in the RMD algorithm — failing to state this is treated as a missing concept (loses 2–3 marks).
> 4. **Not mentioning the inverse-power-law PSD** — every synthesis method must reference $S(f) \propto f^{-\beta}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Wrong Hurst range** — students sometimes claim $H \in (0, 2)$ or use $H > 1$. Strictly, $H \in (0, 1)$ for 1-D fractional Brownian motion.
> 6. **Skipping the variance derivation** — in 14-mark questions, the derivation carries 4–5 marks; merely stating the result is insufficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synthetic fractal signals** are computer-generated waveforms with statistical self-similarity across time scales.
- **Three principal synthesis methods:** (i) Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD), (ii) Weierstrass–Mandelbrot (W–M), (iii) Spectral Synthesis (FFT-based).
- **Hurst exponent $H \in (0, 1)$** uniquely parameterizes the fractal signal and controls its "roughness."
- **Power-law exponent $\beta = 2H + 1$** defines the slope of the PSD on a log-log plot.
- **Fractal dimension** of the waveform graph: $D = 2 - H$.
- **Self-similarity identity:** $X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$.
- **RMD midpoint recurrence:** $X_{\text{mid}} = (X_i + X_{i+1})/2 + G_k (\Delta t)^H$, with variance $\sigma_k^2 \propto 2^{-2kH}$.
- **W–M series:** $W_H(t) = \sum \gamma^{-n(H+0.5)} \sin(\gamma^n t + \varphi_n)$ — continuous but nowhere differentiable.
- **Spectral filter:** $|H(f)| \propto f^{-\beta/2}$ (note the half-exponent due to squared magnitude).
- **Biomedical applications:** HRV (β ≈ 1.0–1.5), EEG (β ≈ 1.5–2.5), sEMG (β ≈ 2.5–3.5).
- **Special cases:** $H = 0.5$ → classical Brownian motion (β = 2); $H \to 1$ → very smooth persistent signal (β → 3).
- **Validation protocol:** always estimate $H$ from the synthesized signal via DFA or PSD fitting and compare to the requested value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fractional Differencing Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fractional Differencing Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fractional Differencing Model** is a parametric time-series framework that generalizes the classical integer-order Box–Jenkins ARIMA($p,d,q$) models by allowing the differencing order $d$ to take any **real (non-integer) value**. The resulting family is commonly known as the **ARFIMA(p, d, q)** — *Autoregressive Fractionally Integrated Moving Average* — process, introduced by **Hosking (1981)** and **Granger and Joyeux (1980)**.

Mathematically, a stationary long-memory process $\{X_t\}$ satisfies the operator equation:

$$\Phi(B)\,(1-B)^{d}\,X_t \;=\; \Theta(B)\,\varepsilon_t$$

where:
- $\Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \cdots - \phi_p B^p$ is the **AR polynomial**
- $\Theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \cdots + \theta_q B^q$ is the **MA polynomial**
- $B$ is the back-shift operator ($B X_t = X_{t-1}$)
- $d \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **fractional differencing parameter**
- $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ is white noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fractional differencing operator $(1-B)^d$ is the cornerstone of *long-memory* (or *long-range dependent*) signal modelling. For biomedical signals such as EEG, HRV, and gait, the parameter $d$ quantifies the degree of persistence / self-similarity that standard ARIMA cannot capture.

### 1.2 The Fractional Differencing Operator — Binomial Series

Using the generalized binomial theorem for any real $d$:

$$(1-B)^{d} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \binom{d}{k}\,(-B)^{k} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \pi_k\,B^{k}$$

with weights

$$\pi_k \;=\; \frac{\Gamma(k-d)}{k!\,\Gamma(-d)} \;=\; \frac{d(d-1)(d-2)\cdots(d-k+1)}{k!}$$

This **infinite** expansion is what gives fractional models their "long-memory" character — every past sample influences the present, with weights decaying hyperbolically (not exponentially).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Ripple Memory" Pond**

> Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. In a **standard ARIMA** model, the ripples vanish quickly (exponentially decaying memory) — past disturbances are forgotten after a few samples. In a **fractional differencing model**, the pond surface still bears the imprint of the original stone-drop even hours later — the past "remembers" itself through an infinite, slowly-decaying trail of ripples (hyperbolic decay).

> For biomedical signals:
> - **Heart Rate Variability (HRV):** A perturbation in heart rate (e.g., a sigh or cough) influences rhythm for thousands of subsequent beats — *long memory*.
> - **EEG background rhythms:** Slow cortical potentials exhibit self-similar fractal structure across many time-scales.
> - **Gait signals:** Stride-interval time series show persistent correlations lasting many cycles.

> In all these cases, an integer $d=0$ or $d=1$ model is **inadequate**; a fractional $d \in (0, 0.5)$ captures the persistence beautifully.

### 1.4 Physical / Statistical Constants of the Model

| Quantity | Standard Value / Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $d$ | $-0.5 < d < 0.5$ | **Stationary** long-memory regime |
| $d$ | $0.5 \le d < 1$ | Non-stationary but mean-reverting |
| $d$ | $d \ge 1$ | Fully non-stationary (integrated) |
| $d = 0$ | – | Reduces to ARMA($p,q$) |
| $d = 1$ | – | Reduces to ARIMA($p,1,q$) — standard differencing |
| **Hurst exponent $H$** | $H = d + 0.5$ | $H > 0.5 \Rightarrow$ persistent; $H < 0.5 \Rightarrow$ anti-persistent; $H = 0.5 \Rightarrow$ short memory (white noise / random walk) |
| **Power-law exponent** $\alpha = 2d$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Slope of log–log PSD near zero frequency |

### 1.5 Why Fractional Differencing? — Motivation for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Many biomedical signals exhibit **$1/f^\alpha$ power spectral density** (pink / fractal noise). Standard ARMA / ARIMA models can only generate flat or rational PSDs — they **cannot** reproduce the divergent low-frequency behaviour of $1/f$ processes. Fractional differencing is the *minimal* parametric tool that achieves this.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fractional Differencing Operator — Structure

The operator $(1-B)^d$ is defined for any real $d$ via its **binomial-series expansion**:

**Step 1 — Binomial expansion:**
$$(1-B)^{d} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \binom{d}{k}(-B)^{k}$$

**Step 2 — Generalised binomial coefficient:**
$$\binom{d}{k} \;=\; \frac{d(d-1)(d-2)\cdots(d-k+1)}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Recursive computation of weights** $\pi_k = (-1)^k \binom{d}{k}$:
$$\pi_0 = 1, \qquad \pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\,\frac{d - (k-1)}{k}\quad \text{for } k \ge 1$$

**Why "How" matters:** The **hyperbolic decay** of $\pi_k \sim k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ as $k \to \infty$ is the mathematical origin of long memory. Compare with the exponential decay $\pi_k \sim \phi^k$ of an AR(1) coefficient — infinitely faster.

### 2.2 Stationarity and Invertibility Conditions

- **Stationarity:** $d < 0.5$ (i.e., $-0.5 < d < 0.5$ for causal processes)
- **Invertibility:** $d > -0.5$
- **Causality:** All roots of $\Phi(z) = 0$ lie outside the unit circle
- **Mean reversion** (even if non-stationary): $d < 1$

### 2.3 Autocorrelation Function (ACF) — Long Memory Signature

For a pure fractional noise $X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t$, the autocorrelation decays as:

$$\rho(\tau) \;=\; \frac{\Gamma(1-d)\,\Gamma(\tau + d)}{\Gamma(d)\,\Gamma(\tau + 1 - d)} \;\sim\; \tau^{2d-1} \quad \text{as } \tau \to \infty$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **hyperbolic** decay (algebraic, not geometric) is the *defining feature* of long-memory processes. The sum $\sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \rho(\tau) \vert$ **diverges** for $d \ge 0$, in stark contrast to ARMA where it converges.

### 2.4 Power Spectral Density — The $1/f$ Signature

The PSD of a fractionally differenced process is:

$$S_X(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{2\pi}\,\frac{\vert \Theta(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}{\vert \Phi(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}\;\left[2\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega}{2}\right)\right]^{-2d}$$

Near the origin ($\omega \to 0$):

$$S_X(\omega) \;\propto\; \omega^{-2d} \;\equiv\; f^{-\alpha}, \qquad \alpha = 2d$$

### 2.5 The Hurst Exponent and the Relation $H = d + 0.5$

The Hurst exponent $H$ characterises the **self-similarity** of a process $X(t)$:
$$X(t) \;\overset{d}{=}\; a^{-H} X(at)$$

For fractionally differenced processes:

$$H = d + \tfrac{1}{2}$$

| $H$ | $d$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $0 < H < 0.5$ | $d < 0$ | Anti-persistent (mean-reverting, oscillatory) |
| $H = 0.5$ | $d = 0$ | Short memory (white noise) |
| $0.5 < H < 1$ | $0 < d < 0.5$ | **Persistent / long memory** (most biomedical) |
| $H = 1$ | $d = 0.5$ | "Pink" $1/f$ noise |
| $H > 1$ | $d > 0.5$ | Non-stationary long memory |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Card for KTU 2024 Exam**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi(B)(1-B)^{d}X_t = \Theta(B)\varepsilon_t$ | ARFIMA definition | Core model |
| 2 | $(1-B)^{d} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k B^{k}$ | Binomial expansion | Show weights |
| 3 | $\pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\dfrac{d-(k-1)}{k}$ | Recursive weights | Computation |
| 4 | $\pi_k \sim \dfrac{k^{-(d+1)}}{\Gamma(-d)}$ | Asymptotic weight | Long memory |
| 5 | $\rho(\tau) \sim \tau^{2d-1}$ | ACF decay | Distinguishes from ARMA |
| 6 | $S_X(\omega) \propto \left[2\sin(\omega/2)\right]^{-2d}$ | PSD | $1/f$ behaviour |
| 7 | $S_X(\omega) \sim \omega^{-2d} \sim f^{-\alpha}$ | Low-freq asymptote | $1/f^\alpha$ noise |
| 8 | $H = d + 0.5$ | Hurst–d relation | Self-similarity |
| 9 | $\alpha = 2d$ | Spectral exponent | $1/f^\alpha$ law |
| 10 | $\sum_{\tau} \vert\rho(\tau)\vert = \infty$ for $d \ge 0$ | Long-memory criterion | Diagnose LM |
| 11 | Stationarity: $d < 0.5$; Invertibility: $d > -0.5$ | Validity bounds | Pre-check model |
| 12 | $X(ct) \overset{d}{=} c^{H} X(t)$ | Self-similarity | Derive $H$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology:** HRV analysis — $H \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ in healthy adults (persistent); drops in pathology.
- **Neurology:** EEG long-range correlations in sleep stages, anaesthesia depth monitoring.
- **Gait & Rehabilitation:** Fractal scaling of stride intervals — $H$ marker for Parkinson's, Huntington's.
- **Respiration:** Breath-to-breath intervals — fractal dynamics across time-scales.
- **Neural spike trains:** Long-memory firing patterns in cortical neurons.
- **Gait EMG:** Surface EMG exhibits $1/f^\alpha$ scaling with $\alpha \in [0.8, 1.5]$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Binomial Expansion of $(1-B)^d$

We start from the **generalised binomial theorem** valid for any real exponent $d$ and $\vert B \vert < 1$ (i.e., for causal processes, on the unit disc):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-B)^{d}
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{d}{k}(-B)^{k} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{d(d-1)(d-2)\cdots(d-k+1)}{k!}(-1)^{k}B^{k} \\
&= 1 - d\,B + \frac{d(d-1)}{2!}\,B^{2} - \frac{d(d-1)(d-2)}{3!}\,B^{3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic (row by row):**
- **Line 1 → 2:** Substitute the generalised binomial coefficient $\binom{d}{k} = \frac{d!}{k!(d-k)!}$ extended to real $d$ via the **Gamma function**: $\binom{d}{k} = \frac{\Gamma(d+1)}{\Gamma(k+1)\Gamma(d-k+1)}$. Expanding the Gamma representation in falling-factorial form yields the rising-factorial product in the numerator.
- **Line 2 → 3:** Pull $(-1)^k$ out of $B^k$ explicitly; collect sign per term.

**Recursive weight formula:** Multiplying consecutive terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_k 
&= \frac{d(d-1)(d-2)\cdots(d-k+1)}{k!} \\
&= \frac{1}{k}\,\cdot\,\frac{d(d-1)\cdots(d-(k-1)+1)}{(k-1)!} \\
&= \frac{1}{k}\,(d-k+1)\,\pi_{k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\pi_0 = 1$.

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** Compute the first **5 weights** for $d = 0.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_0 &= 1 \\
\pi_1 &= \frac{0.4}{1}\cdot\pi_0 = 0.4 \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{0.4-1}{2}\cdot\pi_1 = \frac{-0.6}{2}(0.4) = -0.12 \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{0.4-2}{3}\cdot\pi_2 = \frac{-1.6}{3}(-0.12) = 0.064 \\
\pi_4 &= \frac{0.4-3}{4}\cdot\pi_3 = \frac{-2.6}{4}(0.064) = -0.0416
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the truncated operator is $(1-B)^{0.4} \approx 1 - 0.4B - 0.12B^{2} + 0.064B^{3} - 0.0416B^{4} + \cdots$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Asymptotic Behaviour of $\pi_k$ (Stirling + Gamma)

We want to show $\pi_k \sim k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ for large $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_k 
&= \frac{\Gamma(k-d)}{\Gamma(-d)\,\Gamma(k+1)} \\
&\overset{\text{Stirling}}{=}\frac{1}{\Gamma(-d)}\,\frac{k^{-d-1/2}\,e^{-k}\,k^{k-d}}{\sqrt{2\pi}\;k^{k+1/2}\,e^{-k}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\Gamma(-d)}\,k^{-d-1}\;\frac{1}{1} \\
&= \frac{k^{-(d+1)}}{\Gamma(-d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1:** Rewrite $\binom{d}{k}(-1)^{k} = \frac{\Gamma(k-d)}{\Gamma(-d)\,\Gamma(k+1)}$ using $\binom{d}{k} = \frac{\Gamma(d+1)}{\Gamma(k+1)\Gamma(d-k+1)}$ and $(-1)^{k} = \frac{\Gamma(k-d)}{\Gamma(-d)\Gamma(d-k+1)/\Gamma(d+1)}$. The leading-order Gamma ratio yields the above.
- **Line 2:** Apply **Stirling's asymptotic** $\Gamma(n) \sim \sqrt{2\pi}\,n^{n-1/2}\,e^{-n}$ to both $\Gamma(k-d)$ and $\Gamma(k+1)$.
- **Line 3:** All $k^{k}$, $e^{-k}$, and $2\pi$ factors cancel; the surviving power of $k$ is $-d-1$.

**Conclusion:** $|\pi_k|$ decays **algebraically** (polynomially), not exponentially. This is the root of *long memory*.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autocorrelation of Pure Fractional Noise

For $X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t$ with $\varepsilon_t$ white noise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(\tau) 
&= \mathbb{E}[X_t\,X_{t-\tau}] 
= \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k\,\pi_{k+\tau} \\
&\overset{\text{asymptotic}}{=}\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\,\frac{\Gamma(1-2d)}{\Gamma(1-d)^{2}}\,\tau^{2d-1}\quad(\tau\to\infty)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1:** Use the MA($\infty$) representation $X_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k\,\varepsilon_{t-k}$, so the cross-term is $\sum_{k}\pi_k\pi_{k+\tau}\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{t-k}\varepsilon_{t-\tau-k}]=\sum_{k}\pi_k\pi_{k+\tau}\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\delta_{0,\tau}$ — generalised to autocorrelation over shift $\tau$ by Wiener–Khinchin.
- **Line 2:** Replace $\pi_k \approx k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ and evaluate the resulting sum via the **Euler–Maclaurin** formula or the **Hurwitz zeta** relation. The closed-form coefficient is $\Gamma(1-2d)/\Gamma(1-d)^{2}$.

**Key result:** $\rho(\tau) \sim \tau^{2d-1}$ — for $0 < d < 0.5$ this decays to zero, but **sum diverges**, signalling long memory.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — PSD and the $1/f$ Law

The PSD of $(1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(\omega) 
&= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\bigl\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega}\bigr\vert^{-2d} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\bigl(2\sin(\omega/2)\bigr)^{-2d} \\
&\overset{\omega\to 0}{\sim}\frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\omega^{-2d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1 → 2:** $|1 - e^{-j\omega}| = |1 - \cos\omega + j\sin\omega| = \sqrt{(1-\cos\omega)^{2}+\sin^{2}\omega} = \sqrt{2-2\cos\omega} = 2|\sin(\omega/2)|$.
- **Line 2 → 3:** Use $\sin(x) \approx x$ as $x \to 0$.

**Conclusion:** $S_X(\omega) \propto \omega^{-2d} = f^{-2d}$ with $f = \omega/(2\pi)$. Thus $\alpha = 2d$ is the **spectral exponent** of $1/f^\alpha$ noise.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Hurst Exponent from Self-Similarity

A process $\{X(t)\}$ is **self-similar** with exponent $H$ if for all $c > 0$:
$$X(ct) \;\overset{d}{=}\; c^{H} X(t)$$

For fractionally integrated white noise $X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\bigl[X(ct)\bigr] 
&= c^{2H}\,\text{Var}[X(t)] \\
\text{But also:}\quad \text{Var}\bigl[(1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon\bigr] 
&\propto \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{c-1}\pi_k^{2} \;\sim\; c^{2d+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the two power-laws: $2H = 2d+1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{H = d + \tfrac{1}{2}}$

**Conversion logic:**
- The variance of a self-similar process scales as $c^{2H}$ from the definition.
- The variance of the MA($\infty$) representation has the $\pi_k^{2}$ sum, which by Euler–Maclaurin scales as $c^{2d+1}$.
- Matching powers of $c$ yields $H$.

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An EEG epoch is modelled as ARFIMA(0, $d$, 0) with $d = 0.35$. Compute: (i) the Hurst exponent; (ii) the spectral exponent; (iii) the first three $\pi_k$ weights; (iv) state the stationarity status.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)}\quad H &= d + 0.5 = 0.35 + 0.5 = 0.85 \\
\text{(ii)}\quad \alpha &= 2d = 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{PSD} \propto f^{-0.7} \\
\text{(iii)}\quad 
\pi_0 &= 1,\quad 
\pi_1 = d = 0.35,\quad 
\pi_2 = \frac{d(d-1)}{2} = \frac{0.35(-0.65)}{2} = -0.1138 \\
\text{(iv)}\quad d = 0.35 &< 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{stationary, persistent, long-memory}
\end{aligned}
$$

    D --> D1["Estimate p and q via AIC and BIC on ARMA Residual"]

    D1 --> E["Parameter Estimation Engine"]

    E --> E1["Maximum Likelihood in Time Domain"]
