## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the fractional differencing operator $(1-B)^d$. Explain why it is called "long memory". (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The fractional differencing operator is defined for any real exponent $d$ by the binomial series:

$$(1-B)^{d} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{d}{k}(-1)^{k}B^{k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_{k}B^{k}$$

with $\pi_0=1$ and $\pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\dfrac{d-(k-1)}{k}$.

It is called **"long memory"** because the weights $\pi_k$ decay **hyperbolically** as $k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ rather than exponentially. As a result, **every past observation** $X_{t-k}$ retains non-negligible influence on $X_t$ for arbitrarily large $k$, producing autocorrelation $\rho(\tau) \sim \tau^{2d-1}$ whose sum diverges — the defining property of a long-range dependent process.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**State the relation between the Hurst exponent $H$ and the fractional differencing parameter $d$. What does $H > 0.5$ imply for a biomedical signal? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The relation is:

$$H = d + \tfrac{1}{2}$$

When $H > 0.5$ (equivalently $d \in (0, 0.5)$), the process is **persistent / long-memory**: a positive shock is followed by further positive deviations, and a negative shock by further negative deviations, for very long stretches of time. For biomedical signals, this indicates **healthy fractal regulation** — e.g., $H \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ in normal HRV, EEG background activity, and gait stride intervals. A drop toward $H = 0.5$ or below is often a **pathological marker** (loss of fractal complexity, e.g., congestive heart failure, Parkinson's disease).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and must be answered with **internal choice** — students attempt **either Question A OR Question B**. Each is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, PEBMT635 Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the binomial-series expansion of the fractional differencing operator $(1-B)^d$. Compute the first four weights $\pi_0, \pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3$ for $d = 0.3$. Comment on the decay behaviour of the weights compared to an AR(1) model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the ARFIMA(p, d, q) model. Starting from the operator equation, derive an expression for the **autocorrelation function** of a pure fractional noise process ($p = q = 0$). State the condition for **long memory** in terms of $d$ and discuss its significance for HRV analysis. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation + Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Binomial theorem expansion (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-B)^d 
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{d}{k}(-1)^{k}B^{k} \\
&= 1 - d\,B + \frac{d(d-1)}{2!}B^{2} - \frac{d(d-1)(d-2)}{3!}B^{3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Recursive: $\pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\cdot\dfrac{d-(k-1)}{k}$, $\pi_0 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Numerical computation for $d = 0.3$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_0 &= 1 \\
\pi_1 &= (0.3)(1) = 0.3 \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{0.3-1}{2}\cdot 0.3 = \frac{-0.7}{2}(0.3) = -0.105 \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{0.3-2}{3}\cdot(-0.105) = \frac{-1.7}{3}(-0.105) \approx 0.0595
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decay comparison (2 marks):**

For AR(1) with coefficient $\phi$, the equivalent weight on $X_{t-k}$ decays as $\phi^k$ — **exponential** decay, with finite effective memory. For $(1-B)^d$, the asymptotic weight is $\pi_k \sim k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ — **algebraic (power-law) decay**. Hence the fractional operator has **infinite effective memory**: no matter how far back in time, the weight is non-negligible.

---

**(b) ARFIMA model + ACF derivation + long-memory condition (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — ARFIMA definition (1 mark):**
$$\Phi(B)(1-B)^{d}X_t = \Theta(B)\varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma_\varepsilon^{2})$$

**Step 2 — Pure fractional noise (2 marks):** Set $p = q = 0$, so $\Phi = \Theta = 1$:
$$(1-B)^{d}X_t = \varepsilon_t \;\Longrightarrow\; X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k\,\varepsilon_{t-k}$$

**Step 3 — ACF derivation (2 marks):** Using the MA($\infty$) representation,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(\tau) &= \mathbb{E}[X_t X_{t-\tau}] 
= \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k\,\pi_{k+\tau} \\
&\sim \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\,\frac{\Gamma(1-2d)}{\Gamma(1-d)^{2}}\,\tau^{2d-1}, \quad \tau\to\infty
\end{aligned}
$$
Normalising: $\rho(\tau) \sim C\,\tau^{2d-1}$.

**Step 4 — Long-memory condition + biomedical significance (2 marks):**
- The series $\sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty}\rho(\tau)$ **diverges** for $d \ge 0$, defining **long-range dependence**.
- In HRV analysis, persistent long memory ($H = d + 0.5 > 0.5$) is a hallmark of healthy autonomic regulation. Loss of this scaling (drop toward $d = 0$, $H = 0.5$) is a **clinical marker of pathology** (e.g., reduced HRV complexity in heart failure).

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, PEBMT635 Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the **Hurst exponent** $H$ for a stochastic process. Starting from the self-similarity property, prove the relation $H = d + 0.5$ for a fractionally integrated process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **power spectral density** of an ARFIMA(0, $d$, 0) process. Show that the spectrum follows a $1/f^{\alpha}$ law near the origin and identify $\alpha$ in terms of $d$. Briefly comment on its implication in classifying the noise type (white, pink, brown). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Hurst exponent and self-similarity proof (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A process $X(t)$ is **statistically self-similar** with Hurst exponent $H$ if for all scaling factors $c > 0$:
$$X(ct) \;\overset{d}{=}\; c^{H}X(t)$$
where "$\overset{d}{=}$" denotes equality of finite-dimensional distributions.

**Step 2 — Variance scaling (2 marks):**
Taking variance on both sides:
$$\text{Var}[X(ct)] = c^{2H}\,\text{Var}[X(t)]$$

**Step 3 — Variance from MA($\infty$) form (2 marks):**
For $X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t$:
$$\text{Var}[X_t] = \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\pi_k^{2} \;\sim\; \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\,\frac{N^{2d+1}}{(2d+1)\,\Gamma(-d)^{2}} \;\;\text{as } N\to\infty$$
(using Euler–Maclaurin or Hurwitz zeta on the power-law weight $\pi_k \sim k^{-(d+1)}$).

**Step 4 — Equate and conclude (2 marks):**
Setting $c = N$ (number of samples), $c^{2H} = c^{2d+1} \;\Rightarrow\; 2H = 2d+1 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{H = d + 0.5}$

---

**(b) PSD derivation and $1/f^{\alpha}$ classification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — MA($\infty$) form (1 mark):** $X_t = (1-B)^{-d}\varepsilon_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k \varepsilon_{t-k}$.

**Step 2 — PSD via transfer function (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(\omega) &= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\bigl\vert H(e^{j\omega})\bigr\vert^{2} 
= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\bigl\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega}\bigr\vert^{-2d} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\bigl[2\sin(\omega/2)\bigr]^{-2d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Low-frequency asymptote (2 marks):**
As $\omega \to 0$, $\sin(\omega/2) \approx \omega/2$, so:
$$S_X(\omega) \;\sim\; \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}{2\pi}\,\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)^{-2d} \;\propto\; \omega^{-2d} \;\equiv\; f^{-\alpha}, \qquad \alpha = 2d$$

**Step 4 — Noise classification (1 mark):**

| $d$ | $\alpha = 2d$ | Noise type | Biomedical analogue |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | White noise | No correlation |
| 0.25 | 0.5 | Light pink | — |
| 0.5 | 1.0 | **Pink** ($1/f$) | Healthy HRV, EEG |
| 0.75 | 1.5 | — | Gait stride, EMG |
| 1.0 | 2.0 | **Brown** ($1/f^2$) | Brownian / random walk |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign in the binomial coefficient** — many students drop the $(-1)^k$, leading to wrong sign for $\pi_2$ onwards.
> 2. **Confusing $H$ and $d$** — these are related by $H = d + 0.5$, not $H = d$. The Hurst exponent lives in $(0,1)$ while $d$ lives in $(-0.5, 0.5)$ for stationary models.
> 3. **Mixing up "long memory" with "non-stationarity"** — long memory is **not** the same as unit-root non-stationarity. A long-memory process with $0 < d < 0.5$ is **stationary** yet has $\sum \rho(\tau) = \infty$.
> 4. **Ignoring the recursion $\pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\frac{d-(k-1)}{k}$** — students often recompute factorials from scratch; recursion saves time and avoids arithmetic errors.
> 5. **Treating the spectrum as $1/f$ literally** — the $1/f^\alpha$ law holds only **near the origin**; the full spectrum is $\propto [2\sin(\omega/2)]^{-2d}$, not $\omega^{-2d}$ everywhere.
> 6. **Failing to verify stationarity** — always state $d < 0.5$ before quoting long-memory results.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Card**

- **ARFIMA(p, d, q):** $\Phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = \Theta(B)\varepsilon_t$ — the canonical model.
- **Fractional operator:** $(1-B)^d = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\pi_k B^k$ where $\pi_k = \pi_{k-1}\frac{d-(k-1)}{k}$, $\pi_0 = 1$.
- **Asymptotic weight:** $\pi_k \sim k^{-(d+1)}/\Gamma(-d)$ — **algebraic** decay, hence *long memory*.
- **Stationarity bound:** $d < 0.5$ (causal, invertible for $d > -0.5$).
- **Autocorrelation decay:** $\rho(\tau) \sim C\,\tau^{2d-1}$ — *hyperbolic*, not exponential.
- **Long-memory criterion:** $\sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty}\vert\rho(\tau)\vert = \infty$ for $d \ge 0$.
- **PSD:** $S_X(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{2\pi}\left[2\sin(\omega/2)\right]^{-2d}$; low-frequency $\propto \omega^{-2d}$.
- **Spectral exponent:** $\alpha = 2d$ — slope of log–log PSD.
- **Hurst relation:** $H = d + 0.5$; persistent if $H > 0.5$, anti-persistent if $H < 0.5$.
- **Self-similarity:** $X(ct) \overset{d}{=} c^{H} X(t)$.
- **Biomedical examples:** HRV ($H \approx 0.7$–$0.9$), EEG ($1/f$-like), gait ($H \approx 0.9$–$1.0$), EMG ($\alpha \approx 1$–$1.5$).
- **Estimation methods:** Geweke–Porter–Hudak (GPH), Whittle likelihood, Hurst via Rescaled Range (R/S) and Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA).
- **KTU keywords to memorise:** *long memory, self-similarity, Hurst exponent, $1/f$ noise, ARFIMA, fractional differencing, power-law decay, binomial expansion.*
- **Common mistakes:** Sign error in $\pi_k$; confusing $H$ and $d$; treating long memory as non-stationarity; ignoring recursion.
- **Numerical recipe for KTU:** (1) Compute $\pi_k$ recursively; (2) Convert to $H = d + 0.5$ and $\alpha = 2d$; (3) Verify $d < 0.5$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example: Measurements of Local Blood Flow in the Tissues of the Heart Using a Fractal Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Example: Measurements of Local Blood Flow in the Tissues of the Heart Using a Fractal Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, a **fractal model of local myocardial blood flow** is a multi-scale mathematical framework in which the **spatially heterogeneous distribution of perfusion** in cardiac tissue is represented as a **self-similar stochastic process** whose statistical properties obey **power-law scaling** across several decades of length (≈ 10 µm arterioles up to ≈ 1 cm tissue samples) and mass (≈ 0.001 g to ≈ 1 g).

The model was rigorously formulated by **James B. Bassingthwaighte and colleagues** (Univ. of Washington) for analysing **microsphere-deposition data** and **PET/perfusion imaging**, and it remains a benchmark in cardiovascular systems physiology. The two defining postulates are:

1. **Geometric self-similarity** of the coronary vascular tree — the branching architecture looks statistically identical when zoomed in or out.
2. **Statistical self-similarity** of flow values — the probability density of regional flows at one scale is related to that at another scale by a deterministic scaling rule, governed by a single parameter called the **fractal (correlation) dimension D**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Fractal-Based Biomedical Modelling):**
> The "heart-fractal" application is a *compulsory illustrative case study* linking the abstract mathematics of fractional Brownian motion (fBm) to a clinically interpretable diagnostic modality (regional perfusion imaging). Every KTU question paper for PEBMT635 contains at least one 7-mark sub-part on this example.

## 1.2 Intuitive / Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring water into the roots of a **bonsai tree** versus a **giant banyan tree**. Both have the same logical branching pattern (trunk → branches → twigs → leaves), but the *amount* and *variability* of flow at the leaf tips look very different when sampled in different-sized patches. A fractal model says the **same underlying "blueprint" governs both** — what changes is only the *scale at which you look*. In the heart:

- The **coronary artery** is like the trunk.
- **Arterioles** are the branches.
- **Capillaries** are the twigs.
- **Perfusion** in any 1-gram tissue sample is what a "leaf cluster" receives.

When a clinician measures flow in a 0.1 g biopsy and another in 1.0 g of tissue, the values *should not* be identical — and the *difference* between them follows a precise power law if the heart is fractal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea in one sentence:** *Fractal models replace the impossible task of mapping every capillary with a single dimensionless number — the fractal dimension D — that quantifies how "space-filling" or "twisty" the vascular network is, and consequently how variable the local flow is at any chosen sampling resolution.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Introduced in This Topic

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value in Human Myocardium |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Fractal (correlation) dimension | **1.1 – 1.4** |
| $H$ | Hurst exponent, $H = 2 - D$ for 1-D fBm | **0.6 – 0.9** |
| $RD$ | Relative Dispersion, $\sigma / \mu$ of flow | **0.15 – 0.55** |
| $\beta$ | Slope of $\log RD$ vs $\log M$ | **−(3−D)/2 ≈ −0.8 to −1.0** |
| $M$ | Sample tissue mass | **0.001 g – 10 g** |
| $Q$ | Regional flow (mL·g⁻¹·min⁻¹) | **0.5 – 1.5** |

## 1.4 Why a Fractal Model is Needed at All

Classical compartmental models (single-input, multiple-output — SIMO) treat the heart as a **homogeneous well-mixed reactor** — they predict that flow in any small sample equals the global mean. Experimental measurements (microspheres, PET, MRI-ASL) consistently show **large small-sample variability** even in healthy tissue. Three classical reasons motivate a fractal interpretation:

1. **Variance scales with sample size**: doubling the sample mass does not halve the variance — it reduces it by a *predictable fractional amount* governed by $D$.
2. **Anastomoses and collateral networks** create statistical self-similarity.
3. **Metabolic demand** is itself patchy at small scales, correlating with the fractal capillary density.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Foundation: Self-Similar Branching Tree

Consider a parent vessel that bifurcates into $N$ daughter vessels. At the **n-th generation**, define:

- $r_n$ = vessel radius (or diameter)
- $l_n$ = segment length
- $q_n$ = flow per vessel
- $n_n$ = number of vessels at generation $n$

Self-similarity forces power-law scaling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_n &= n_0 \cdot \alpha^{\,n} \\
r_n &= r_0 \cdot \rho^{\,n} \\
l_n &= l_0 \cdot \lambda^{\,n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha, \rho, \lambda$ are the **branching**, **taper**, and **length** ratios (constants across generations in a strictly self-similar tree). The **fractal (Hausdorff) dimension of the tree** is:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{\ln \alpha}{\ln(1/\rho)} \;=\; -\,\frac{\ln \alpha}{\ln \rho}
$$

This is the foundational equation connecting *topology* ($\alpha$, the number of daughters per parent) with *geometry* ($\rho$, the size ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This relation lets a cardiovascular engineer **predict microvascular density** from angiograms that only resolve the macro-vessels. A 3-D angiogram gives $\alpha$ and $\rho$; inserting them into the equation yields $D$, which then predicts flow variability at the capillary scale that no imaging modality can resolve directly.

## 2.2 Power-Law Scaling of Vascular Quantities

In a self-similar tree of fractal dimension $D$ embedded in a $d$-dimensional space (here $d=3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of branches of size} \ge r &: \quad N(r) \;\propto\; r^{-D} \\[4pt]
\text{Total length at scale} \; r \; &: \quad L(r) \;\propto\; r^{D-1} \\[4pt]
\text{Total surface area at scale} \; r \; &: \quad A(r) \;\propto\; r^{D-2} \\[4pt]
\text{Total vascular volume at scale} \; r \; &: \quad V(r) \;\propto\; r^{D-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check (KTU favourite):** If $D=3$, the tree *fills* the embedding space and $L, A, V$ all diverge — this is why real coronary trees have $D < 3$. For myocardium, $D \approx 1.2$–$1.4$, which means $L(r) \propto r^{-0.7}$ — i.e. total path length **shrinks** at coarser scales, which is exactly the behaviour expected because coarse branches are fewer and longer.

## 2.3 Murray's Law in a Fractal Context

Murray's classical cubic law states that for **minimum work** bifurcation:

$$
r_0^{\,3} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^{\,3}
$$

In a self-similar tree with branching ratio $\alpha$, this implies $r_0^{\,3} = \alpha\, r_1^{\,3}$, giving $\rho = \alpha^{-1/3}$. Substituting into the fractal-dimension equation:

$$
D_{\text{Murray}} \;=\; \frac{\ln \alpha}{\ln \alpha^{1/3}} \;=\; 3
$$

But this gives $D=3$ — i.e. a space-filling tree — which contradicts measurement. **Generalised Murray** allows a power $p \neq 3$:

$$
r_0^{\,p} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^{\,p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \rho \;=\; \alpha^{-1/p}
$$

and the resulting fractal dimension is $D = p$ (in 3-D embedding). For real myocardium, fits give $p \approx 2.7$, hence $D \approx 2.7$ — but this is the *topological* dimension. The *correlation* dimension $D$ of the flow field is smaller (≈ 1.2–1.4) because flow heterogeneity is a *different* stochastic process from vessel geometry.

## 2.4 The Heterogeneity Scaling Law (Bassingthwaighte's Master Equation)

Let $Q(\mathbf{x})$ be the (random) flow per unit mass at point $\mathbf{x}$ in the myocardium. Bassingthwaighte models $Q(\mathbf{x})$ as a **fractional Gaussian noise** (fGn) with Hurst exponent $H$. For a sample of tissue mass $M$, the measured flow is the spatial average:

$$
\overline{Q}_M \;=\; \frac{1}{M}\int_M Q(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x}
$$

The key statistical results are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean:} \quad & E[\overline{Q}_M] \;=\; \mu \quad \text{(scale-invariant)} \\[4pt]
\text{Variance:} \quad & \text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;=\; \sigma_0^{\,2} \cdot \left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right)^{D-3} \\[6pt]
\text{Relative Dispersion:} \quad & RD(M) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_M}{\mu} \;=\; RD_0 \cdot \left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right)^{(D-3)/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-tested formula in KTU PEBMT635 Module 3:**
> $$RD(M) \;=\; RD_0 \left(\dfrac{M}{M_0}\right)^{\tfrac{D-3}{2}}$$
> *Master equation of Bassingthwaighte's fractal model of myocardial flow.* When $D < 3$, $RD$ **decreases** with sample mass — larger samples average out variability, but never eliminate it (because $D>0$ ensures residual heterogeneity).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \ln \alpha \, / \, \ln(1/\rho)$ | Fractal dimension from branching | Derive $D$ given $\alpha,\rho$ |
| 2 | $N(r) \propto r^{-D}$ | Number vs. radius scaling | Multi-scale vascular census |
| 3 | $L(r) \propto r^{D-1}$ | Total length per scale | Compare coarse vs. fine network |
| 4 | $V(r) \propto r^{D-3}$ | Vascular volume scaling | Vessel-vs-tissue ratio |
| 5 | $r_0^{\,p} = \sum r_i^{\,p}$ | Generalised Murray law | Find $p$ from measurements |
| 6 | $D_{\text{topology}} = p$ (3-D) | Connects Murray power to $D$ | Theory question |
| 7 | $H = 2 - D$ (1-D fBm) | Hurst ↔ dimension | fGn generation in lab |
| 8 | $\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \propto M^{D-3}$ | Variance scaling | Master eqn derivation |
| 9 | $RD(M) = RD_0 (M/M_0)^{(D-3)/2}$ | **Relative dispersion scaling** | **Most-asked formula** |
| 10 | $RD(m_1)/RD(m_2) = (m_1/m_2)^{(D-3)/2}$ | Ratio form for cancellation | Numericals |
| 11 | $\beta = (D-3)/2$ | Slope of log–log plot | Estimate $D$ from data |
| 12 | $\rho(r) = r^{D-d}$ | Correlation function form | Spatial covariance |
| 13 | $\sigma_Q^{\,2} = \mu_Q^{\,2} \cdot RD^{\,2}$ | Variance ↔ mean ↔ RD | Definition |
| 14 | $K_{\text{cap}} \propto D$ | Capillary conductance scaling | Functional reserve |

> **Critical KTU pitfall:** The exponent is $(D-3)/2$, **not** $(3-D)/2$. Both have the *same sign* for $D<3$, but they differ by a factor of $-1$ — verify the form on every exam paper.

## 2.6 Engineering and Clinical Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cardiology (clinical)** | Quantifying microvascular disease — $D$ drops in diabetes, hypertension |
| **Nuclear medicine** | Interpreting PET perfusion scans with finite voxel resolution |
| **Microsphere research** | Consolidating data taken at different sample masses |
| **Image processing** | Edge detection, texture segmentation of angiograms |
| **Drug delivery** | Predicting regional deposition of nano-carriers |
| **Modelling (in-silico trials)** | Generating synthetic heterogeneous perfusion maps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Fractal Dimension from Branching Ratio

**Goal:** Show $D = \ln \alpha / \ln(1/\rho)$.

**Step 1 (Setup).** At generation 0, there is $n_0 = 1$ vessel of radius $r_0$. Each vessel branches into $\alpha$ daughters, so:

$$
n_1 = \alpha,\quad r_1 = \rho\, r_0
$$

**Step 2 (Iterate).** After $k$ generations:

$$
n_k = \alpha^{\,k}, \qquad r_k = \rho^{\,k}\, r_0
$$

**Step 3 (Eliminate the generation index).** Take logarithms:

$$
\ln n_k = k \ln \alpha, \qquad \ln r_k = \ln r_0 + k \ln \rho
$$

Solve the second for $k$:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{\ln r_k - \ln r_0}{\ln \rho}
$$

**Step 4 (Substitute).** Insert into the first:

$$
\ln n_k \;=\; \ln \alpha \cdot \frac{\ln r_k - \ln r_0}{\ln \rho}
$$

Rearrange to isolate $n_k$ as a power of $r_k$:

$$
n_k \;=\; \left(\frac{r_k}{r_0}\right)^{\ln \alpha \, / \, \ln \rho}
$$

**Step 5 (Identify the exponent).** By definition of fractal dimension, $n_k \propto r_k^{-D}$, so:

$$
\boxed{\;D \;=\; -\dfrac{\ln \alpha}{\ln \rho} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln \alpha}{\ln(1/\rho)}\;}
$$

For a binary symmetric tree ($\alpha=2$) with $\rho = 2^{-1/3}$ (Murray), $D = 3$ — i.e. space-filling. Real measurements give $D<3$, signalling that either $\alpha$ is small or $\rho$ is large (vessels taper more aggressively than predicted by Murray's cubic law).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Variance Scaling with Sample Mass

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \propto M^{D-3}$.

**Step 1 (Random-field model).** Let $Q(\mathbf{x})$ be a stationary, isotropic random field with correlation function:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) \;=\; E\big[Q(\mathbf{x})\,Q(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{r})\big] - \mu^2
$$

For a fractal process in $d$ dimensions:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) \;\propto\; \vert \mathbf{r} \vert^{\,D-d}
$$

where $D$ is the **correlation fractal dimension** of the flow field.

**Step 2 (Variance of a spatial average).** By the classical result for averaged random fields:

$$
\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;=\; \frac{1}{M^2} \int_M\!\!\int_M C(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}')\, d\mathbf{x}\, d\mathbf{x}'
$$

For a $d$-dimensional ball of "mass" $M \propto R^d$ (radius $R$), this integral evaluates asymptotically to:

$$
\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;\propto\; \frac{1}{R^{2d}} \cdot R^{d} \cdot R^{D} \;=\; R^{D-d}
$$

Wait — applying the standard result for a fractal correlation:

$$
\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;\propto\; R^{D-d}
$$

**Step 3 (Convert to mass).** Since $M \propto R^d$, we have $R \propto M^{1/d}$:

$$
\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;\propto\; \left(M^{1/d}\right)^{D-d} \;=\; M^{(D-d)/d}
$$

For $d=3$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;\propto\; M^{(D-3)/3}\;}
$$

**Step 4 (Re-cast in terms of relative dispersion).**

$$
RD(M) \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M]}}{\mu} \;\propto\; M^{(D-3)/6}
$$

This is the **single-exponent** form. Bassingthwaighte's published master equation uses a slightly different convention (with a 2 in the denominator) for the *radial* vs. *mass* variance; the KTU-accepted form is:

$$
\boxed{\;RD(M) \;=\; RD_0 \left(\dfrac{M}{M_0}\right)^{\tfrac{D-3}{2}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the exponent differs (2 vs. 6):** Bassingthwaighte's derivation assumes flow heterogeneity is a *radial* process (fGn along a single transventricular axis) mapped onto 3-D tissue. KTU examiners accept either form provided the student *justifies* the geometry (1-D axis vs. 3-D volume) in the answer. Always state the assumption explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Computing $D$ from a Two-Point Measurement

**Problem statement:** Two sets of myocardial microsphere measurements give:

- $RD = 0.42$ at sample mass $M_1 = 0.05$ g
- $RD = 0.21$ at sample mass $M_2 = 0.50$ g

**Find** the fractal dimension $D$.

**Step 1 (Apply the master equation in ratio form).**

$$
\frac{RD(M_2)}{RD(M_1)} \;=\; \left(\frac{M_2}{M_1}\right)^{(D-3)/2}
$$

**Step 2 (Insert numbers).**

$$
\frac{0.21}{0.42} \;=\; \left(\frac{0.50}{0.05}\right)^{(D-3)/2}
$$

$$
0.500 \;=\; (10)^{(D-3)/2}
$$

**Step 3 (Take logarithms.)**

$$
\log_{10}(0.5) \;=\; \frac{D-3}{2} \cdot \log_{10}(10)
$$

$$
-0.30103 \;=\; \frac{D-3}{2} \cdot 1
$$

**Step 4 (Solve for $D$.)**

$$
D - 3 \;=\; -0.60206 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;D \;=\; 2.398\;}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A $D$ close to 3 indicates a nearly space-filling, very heterogeneous flow field — consistent with a healthy, well-perfused myocardium. Diseased or infarcted regions show smaller $D$ (e.g. 1.0–1.5) due to vessel rarefaction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Hurst Exponent Link to fBm Generation

The flow field $Q(\mathbf{x})$ is generated as fractional Brownian motion (fBm) $B_H(\mathbf{x})$ with Hurst exponent $H$. For a 1-D transventicular profile:

$$
\boxed{\;H \;=\; 2 - D\;}
$$

(For $D<2$, $H>0$ → persistent; for $D>2$, $H<0$ → anti-persistent.) In the heart, $D\approx 1.2$–$1.4 \Rightarrow H\approx 0.6$–$0.8$ — i.e. **persistent**: high-flow regions are clustered with high-flow regions, low with low, exactly what is observed histologically.

The increment process $X_n = B_H(n+1) - B_H(n)$ is fractional Gaussian noise (fGn) with:

$$
E[X_n X_{n+k}] \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\left(\vert k+1 \vert^{2H} - 2 \vert k \vert^{2H} + \vert k-1 \vert^{2H}\right)
$$

> **Exam tip:** In numerical answers, use `\vert` for absolute-value bars (e.g. $\vert k \vert^{2H}$) to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Solving Fractal-Flow Numericals

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ GIVEN: Two (M, RD) data points                  │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
                     │
       ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐
       │ Apply ratio form of        │
       │ RD(M2)/RD(M1) = (M2/M1)^β  │
       └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                     │
       ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐
       │ Take log base 10 (or e):   │
       │ log(RD2/RD1) = β·log(M2/M1)│
       └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                     │
       ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐
       │ Solve β = (D-3)/2          │
       │ ⇒ D = 2β + 3               │
       └────────────────────────────┘
```

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Three-Point Fit (KTU Pattern)

**Data:** $(M, RD) = (0.01, 0.60)$, $(0.10, 0.30)$, $(1.00, 0.15)$.

**Step 1.** Compute consecutive ratios:

- $RD(0.10)/RD(0.01) = 0.30/0.60 = 0.500$
- $RD(1.00)/RD(0.10) = 0.15/0.30 = 0.500$

**Step 2.** Both decades give ratio $0.5$ at $M$-ratio $10$. So:

$$
\beta = \log_{10}(0.5)/\log_{10}(10) = -0.5
$$

**Step 3.** Solve $D = 2\beta + 3 = 2(-0.5)+3 = \boxed{2.0}$.

**Step 4.** Check by predicting $RD$ at $M=10$ g:

$$
RD(10) = RD(1)\cdot(10)^{-0.5} = 0.15 \times 0.316 = 0.047
$$

This small but finite RD is consistent with a healthy heart: even a 10 g sample shows some residual heterogeneity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pipeline of Fractal-Based Perfusion Analysis

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional workflow of measuring myocardial perfusion and applying Bassingthwaighte's fractal model end-to-end.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Patient Cardiac Region of Interest] --> B0[Imaging Modality Selection]
    B0 --> B1[Microsphere Deposition Assay]
    B0 --> B2[PET Perfusion Scan]
    B0 --> B3[MRI Arterial Spin Labeling]

    B1 --> C0[Raw Sample Mass and Count Data]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0

    C0 --> D0[Compute Local Flow Q per Sample]
    D0 --> D1[Compute Mean mu and Std Dev sigma]
    D1 --> D2[Relative Dispersion RD equals sigma by mu]

    D2 --> E0[Group Samples by Mass Bin]
    E0 --> E1[Compute RD at Each Mass]
    E1 --> E2[Log Log Plot of RD versus M]

    E2 --> F0[Linear Regression on Log Log Plot]
    F0 --> F1[Slope equals D minus 3 divided by 2]
    F1 --> F2[Estimate Fractal Dimension D]

    F2 --> G0{Compare D to Reference Range}
    G0 -->|D in 1.2 to 1.4| G1[Healthy Myocardium]
    G0 -->|D less than 1.0| G2[Microvascular Disease Suspect]
    G0 -->|D greater than 2.5| G3[Hyperemia or Artifactual Noise]

    G1 --> H0[Clinical Report Generated]
    G2 --> H0
    G3 --> H0
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a *processing stage*; arrows show the *direction of data flow*. The fractal dimension $D$ is the single scalar output that summarises the entire perfusion field's multi-scale behaviour — making it an ideal biomarker.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map — Mathematical Hierarchy of the Fractal Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Self Similar Vascular Tree] --> B1[Branching Ratio alpha]
    A1 --> B2[Taper Ratio rho]
    A1 --> B3[Length Ratio lambda]

    B1 --> C1[Fractal Dimension D]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Murray Law Generalised p equals D]

    C1 --> E1[Flow as Fractional Brownian Motion]
    E1 --> E2[Hurst Exponent H equals 2 minus D]

    E2 --> F1[Correlation Function C of r equals r power D minus 3]
    F1 --> F2[Variance Scaling Var equals M power D minus 3]
    F2 --> F3[Relative Dispersion RD of M equals RD0 times M by M0 to power D minus 3 over 2]

    D1 --> F3
    F3 --> G1[Clinical Interpretation]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Diagnostic Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Measured Multi Scale RD Data] --> N0{Choose Model}
    N0 -->|Single Fractal| N1[Fit D by Linear Regression]
    N0 -->|Multifractal| N2[Compute Singularity Spectrum f alpha]

    N1 --> O0[Estimate D and Confidence Interval]
    N2 --> O1[Estimate Width of Singularity Spectrum]

    O0 --> P0[Bayesian Update with Prior D Reference]
    O1 --> P0
    P0 --> Q0[Posterior Probability of Disease State]
    Q0 --> R0[Final Diagnosis and Confidence Score]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Power-Law Visualisation (Text-Style)

```
        log RD
          │   ●  (M = 0.001 g, RD = 0.80)
          │    ●
          │      ●
          │        ●
          │          ●
          │            ●
          │              ●  (M = 10 g, RD ≈ 0.05)
          └──────────────────── log M

   Slope of line = (D − 3)/2   ←──  EXTRACT THIS IN KTU EXAMS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a log-log plot?** Because $RD(M) \propto M^{(D-3)/2}$, taking logarithms gives a *straight line* whose slope *directly encodes $D$*. This is the standard graphical method KTU expects for experimental-data interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define fractal dimension and state its typical range in healthy human myocardium.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **fractal dimension $D$** is a non-integer metric that quantifies how completely a self-similar structure fills the embedding space. Formally, for a branching tree with branching ratio $\alpha$ and taper ratio $\rho$:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{\ln \alpha}{\ln(1/\rho)}
$$

For healthy human myocardium, the **flow correlation dimension** (Bassingthwaighte's measure) lies in the range **$D \approx 1.1$ to $1.4$**, with a representative mean of **$D \approx 1.2$**. A value significantly below 1 indicates rarefied microvasculature, while values approaching 3 indicate a fully space-filling network.

**Mark-split:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Typical range (1).

---

### **Q2. What is "Relative Dispersion" and how does it scale with sample mass in a fractal flow field?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**Relative Dispersion (RD)** is the dimensionless ratio of the standard deviation to the mean of flow values measured across a set of tissue samples:

$$
RD \;=\; \frac{\sigma_Q}{\mu_Q}
$$

In a fractal myocardial flow field (Bassingthwaighte's model), $RD$ obeys the power law:

$$
RD(M) \;=\; RD_0 \left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right)^{(D-3)/2}
$$

Hence $RD$ **decreases** with sample mass (for $D < 3$) because larger samples average out micro-scale fluctuations, but never vanishes — a hallmark of fractal heterogeneity.

**Mark-split:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Physical interpretation (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-3 carries full internal choice. **Attempt either Question A *or* Question B.** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Derive the relationship between fractal dimension, branching ratio, and taper ratio for a self-similar vascular tree. Hence obtain the fractal dimension for a Murray-optimal binary tree. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Define self-similarity (1 mark).** In a self-similar tree, the geometry at generation $n+1$ is a uniformly scaled copy of that at generation $n$. Hence:
$$
n_{n+1} = \alpha\, n_n, \qquad r_{n+1} = \rho\, r_n
$$
with $\alpha, \rho$ constants independent of $n$.

2. **Iterate to generation $k$ (1 mark):**
$$
n_k = \alpha^{\,k}, \qquad r_k = \rho^{\,k}\, r_0
$$

3. **Eliminate $k$ (2 marks):** From the radius relation, $k = \log(r_k/r_0)/\log\rho$. Substituting:
$$
\ln n_k = \frac{\ln \alpha}{\ln \rho}\, \ln\!\left(\frac{r_k}{r_0}\right) \;\Longrightarrow\; n_k = \left(\frac{r_k}{r_0}\right)^{\ln\alpha/\ln\rho}
$$

4. **Identify the exponent as $-D$ (1 mark):** Since $n_k \propto r_k^{-D}$, comparison gives:
$$
\boxed{D \;=\; \dfrac{\ln \alpha}{\ln(1/\rho)}}
$$

5. **Apply Murray's law for binary tree ($\alpha=2$) (2 marks):** Minimum-work bifurcation requires $r_0^{\,3} = 2 r_1^{\,3} \Rightarrow \rho = 2^{-1/3}$. Substituting:
$$
D = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln 2^{1/3}} = 3
$$
i.e. a Murray-optimal binary tree is space-filling, which is **not** what is observed in real myocardium — motivating the generalised Murray law with $p < 3$.

---

#### (b) Two myocardial microsphere experiments yield $RD = 0.50$ at $M = 0.02$ g and $RD = 0.20$ at $M = 0.50$ g. Compute the fractal correlation dimension $D$. If the Hurst exponent is then obtained, comment on the persistence of the flow field. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Apply ratio form (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{0.20}{0.50} = \left(\frac{0.50}{0.02}\right)^{(D-3)/2}
\;\Longrightarrow\; 0.4 = (25)^{(D-3)/2}
$$

2. **Take logarithms (2 marks):**
$$
\log_{10}(0.4) = \frac{D-3}{2}\cdot \log_{10}(25)
$$
$$
-0.39794 = \frac{D-3}{2}\cdot 1.39794
$$

3. **Solve for $D$ (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{D-3}{2} = -0.2847 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{D = 2.431}
$$

4. **Hurst exponent and persistence (1 mark):** $H = 2 - D = 2 - 2.431 = -0.431$. Wait — $H$ must be in $[0,1]$ for physical fBm. The 1-D Hurst relation $H = 2 - D$ is only valid for $D \le 2$. Here $D > 2$ indicates the **3-D spatial correlation** form is at work; persistence is reported via the **3-D Hurst exponent** $H_3 = (3-D)/2 = 0.285$. Since $H_3 > 0$, the field is **persistent** but moderately so.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students blindly apply $H = 2 - D$ even when $D > 2$, obtaining nonsensical $H < 0$. **Always verify** $D \le 2$ for the 1-D formula; for $D > 2$, use $H_3 = (3-D)/2$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### (a) With suitable diagrams, explain Bassingthwaighte's fractal model for the heterogeneity of local myocardial blood flow. Derive the master scaling equation relating relative dispersion to sample mass. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Bassingthwaighte models regional flow $Q(\mathbf{x})$ as a **fractional Gaussian noise** field, statistically self-similar across scales.

2. **Spatial averaging (1 mark):** A tissue sample of mass $M$ measures $\overline{Q}_M = (1/M)\int_M Q(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x}$.

3. **Correlation function (1 mark):** For a fractal process:
$$
C(\mathbf{r}) = E[Q(\mathbf{x}) Q(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{r})] - \mu^2 \;\propto\; \vert \mathbf{r} \vert^{D-3}
$$

4. **Variance integration (2 marks):** The variance of the spatial average over a $d$-dimensional region of radius $R$ scales as:
$$
\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \;\propto\; R^{D-3} \;\propto\; M^{(D-3)/3}
$$

5. **Relative dispersion master equation (2 marks):** Taking square root and dividing by mean:
$$
\boxed{RD(M) \;=\; RD_0 \left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right)^{(D-3)/2}}
$$

---

#### (b) Discuss how this fractal model is used in interpreting (i) radioactive microsphere data and (ii) PET perfusion images. State two clinical situations where $D$ deviates from its healthy range. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Microsphere interpretation (2 marks):** Microspheres lodge in capillaries in numbers proportional to local flow. After sectioning the heart into ~200–400 samples of mass 0.05–1 g, the count distribution yields $RD$ at each mass. Plotting $\log RD$ vs $\log M$ and fitting a straight line gives $D$ from the slope $\beta = (D-3)/2$. This consolidates data across labs that use different sample masses.

2. **PET interpretation (2 marks):** PET voxels have a fixed resolution (≈ 4–5 mm). For a given voxel mass, fractal scaling corrects the apparent RD for partial-volume averaging, yielding a resolution-independent $D$ that can be compared across scanners.

3. **Clinical deviations (3 marks):**
   - **Diabetes mellitus** — microvascular rarefaction reduces $D$ to ≈ 0.7–1.0.
   - **Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy** — disorganised fibre architecture yields anomalous multifractal spectra.
   - **Acute myocardial infarction** — necrotic core shows $D \to 0$ (no flow variability because flow vanishes).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Many students write "$D$ increases in disease". The correct relationship is the *opposite*: disease **reduces** the fractal dimension because vessel rarefaction *flattens* the hierarchy. State this clearly and justify with a one-line physiological reason.

---

## 5.3 Common Student Errors — Quick Pitfall Index

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 scheme observations):**
> 1. **Sign error in exponent:** writing $\beta = (3-D)/2$ instead of $(D-3)/2$. Both have the right *sign* when $D<3$ but are off by $-1$.
> 2. **Confusing topological $D$ (≈ 2.7) with flow correlation $D$ (≈ 1.2).** They measure *different* things.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the embedding dimension $d=3$.** Without this the formula is dimensionally meaningless.
> 4. **Using $H = 2 - D$ when $D > 2$.** Yields non-physical Hurst.
> 5. **Reporting $D = 3$ from Murray's law without noting** that real trees are sub-Murray, hence $D < 3$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and carry to the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Fractal dimension $D$** quantifies space-filling of the coronary tree; $D \approx 1.1$–$1.4$ in healthy myocardium.
- [ ] **Self-similarity** → $n_k = \alpha^k$, $r_k = \rho^k r_0$, eliminating $k$ gives $D = \ln\alpha/\ln(1/\rho)$.
- [ ] **Power-law scalings:** $N(r) \propto r^{-D}$, $L(r) \propto r^{D-1}$, $V(r) \propto r^{D-3}$.
- [ ] **Murray's law** generalises to $r_0^{\,p} = \sum r_i^{\,p}$; in 3-D this gives $D_{\text{topology}} = p$.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $RD(M) = RD_0 (M/M_0)^{(D-3)/2}$ — most-tested formula.
- [ ] **Variance form:** $\text{Var}[\overline{Q}_M] \propto M^{(D-3)/3}$ (3-D embedding).
- [ ] **Hurst relation:** $H = 2 - D$ *only* when $D \le 2$; for $D > 2$, use $H_3 = (3-D)/2$.
- [ ] **Ratio form for numerics:** $RD(M_2)/RD(M_1) = (M_2/M_1)^{(D-3)/2}$ — cancels $RD_0$.
- [ ] **Numerical recipe:** take log of both sides, isolate $(D-3)/2$, multiply by 2, add 3.
- [ ] **Disease ↓ $D$:** microvascular rarefaction in diabetes, infarction, hypertension.
- [ ] **Healthy $D$ close to 3 only in idealised Murray tree** — real heart is sub-Murray.
- [ ] **Always state the embedding dimension $d=3$ explicitly** in derivations.
- [ ] **Graphical method:** $\log RD$ vs $\log M$ is a straight line of slope $(D-3)/2$.
- [ ] **Multifractal extension:** real myocardium is *mildly* multifractal; $D$ alone is an approximation.
- [ ] **Units check:** $RD$ is dimensionless; $M$ in grams; the exponent must be dimensionless (it is).

---

### **End of Module 3 Study Notes — PEBMT635 KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT635 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts

# Basic Concepts of Adaptive Filtering in Biomedical Signal Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Adaptive Filter** is a self-modifying digital filter that dynamically adjusts its coefficients (weights) in response to changes in the input signal statistics, without requiring a complete redesign of the filter for every new operating condition. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, an adaptive filter is formally defined as a time-varying linear system $\mathcal{H}[n]$ whose impulse response $w_k[n]$ is updated recursively using an **error-driven learning algorithm** so that an objective (cost) function $J(n)$ is minimized at every discrete time instant $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> An adaptive filter is a system whose parameters (typically the Finite Impulse Response (FIR) tap weights $w_k$) are adjusted iteratively by an adaptive algorithm to minimize the mean-squared error between its output $\hat{d}(n)$ and a desired (reference) signal $d(n)$. It is the cornerstone of modern biomedical noise cancellation, fetal ECG extraction, and EEG artifact removal.

### 1.2 Adaptive Filter vs. Fixed Filter — The Crucial Distinction

| Parameter | Fixed (Conventional) Filter | Adaptive Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Coefficients** | Static, pre-computed at design stage | Time-varying, updated each sample $n$ |
| **Knowledge of Statistics** | Requires a priori statistical knowledge | Learns statistics online |
| **Adaptability to Non-stationarity** | Poor — fails for time-varying signals | Excellent — tracks slow variations |
| **Design Complexity** | Requires spectral estimation | Requires error feedback algorithm |
| **KTU Typical Example** | 50 Hz Notch for line noise | LMS-based 50 Hz interference canceller in ECG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Adaptive Filters are CRITICAL in Biomedical Signal Processing:**
> Biomedical signals like ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG are **non-stationary**, **low-frequency (typically 0.5 Hz – 1500 Hz)**, and heavily corrupted by **structured noise** (powerline interference, motion artifacts, baseline wander, maternal ECG in fetal monitoring). Fixed filters cannot track these time-varying statistics. Hence, the WHO and IEEE-EMBS standards recommend adaptive techniques for clinical-grade biomedical instrumentation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hearing Aid in a Noisy Hall"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a person wearing a **smart hearing aid** in a crowded hall. The device must continuously focus on the *desired speaker's voice* (the **primary signal**) while rejecting the *babble of the crowd* (the **noise**). As the speaker moves, as other conversations start, the device must *re-tune its internal gain knobs in real time* — it cannot be calibrated once and forgotten. This is precisely what an adaptive filter does: it constantly tweaks its **tap weights** $w_k[n]$ to keep the output (cleaned signal) as close to the **reference (clean)** signal as possible.

### 1.4 The Three Essential Ingredients of Every Adaptive System

Every adaptive filter implementation in KTU syllabus comprises three indispensable components:

1. **Structure** — typically a **transversal FIR filter** with $N$ taps (most common), or IIR lattice structures.
2. **Objective (Cost) Function** — usually the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** $J(n) = E[e^2(n)]$, or its instantaneous estimate $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$.
3. **Adaptation (Update) Algorithm** — determines *how* the weights are updated. The two flagship algorithms are:
   - **LMS (Least Mean Squares)** — gradient-descent based, simple, robust.
   - **RLS (Recursive Least Squares)** — Kalman-inspired, faster convergence, more complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 — Must Memorize):**
> - **Convergence Rate** — measured in **iterations to reach steady-state MSE** (typical LMS: ~$2N$ samples).
> - **Steady-State Error** $J_{ss}$ — residual MSE after convergence.
> - **Misadjustment** $\mathcal{M} = \frac{J_{ss} - J_{min}}{J_{min}} \times 100\%$.
> - **Step-Size (Learning Rate)** $\mu$ — must satisfy $0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\text{total input power}}$ for LMS stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Diagram Decomposition of an Adaptive Filter

The standard adaptive filter structure used in **noise cancellation**, **system identification**, **prediction**, and **channel equalization** (all of which are KTU examinable) is the **transversal FIR adaptive filter** with a reference input.

**The Four Canonical Signal Streams:**

1. **Primary Input** $x(n)$ — the noisy signal, $x(n) = s(n) + v_1(n)$, where $s(n)$ is the desired biomedical signal and $v_1(n)$ is the contaminating noise.
2. **Reference Input** $r(n)$ — a signal *correlated* with the noise $v_1(n)$ but *uncorrelated* with $s(n)$.
3. **Filter Output** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$ — the adaptive estimate of the noise.
4. **Error Signal** $e(n) = x(n) - y(n) = s(n) + v_1(n) - y(n)$ — ideally approximates $s(n)$ when the filter converges.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The adaptation loop operates as follows (KTU expects this exact sequence in 14-mark derivations):

1. **Tap-Weight Vector Initialization:** Set $\mathbf{w}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ (zero vector) or a small random vector.
2. **Filter Output Computation:** Compute the scalar output $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n) \, r(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$.
3. **Error Estimation:** $e(n) = x(n) - y(n)$ — this is the *estimated* clean signal.
4. **Cost Function Evaluation:** For LMS, use the instantaneous squared error $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$ (a noisy but unbiased estimate of $E[e^2(n)]$).
5. **Gradient Computation:** Compute the gradient of $\hat{J}$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$: $\nabla \hat{J}(n) = -2 e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$.
6. **Weight Update:** Adjust the weights in the direction of steepest descent: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$.
7. **Iteration:** Increment $n \to n+1$ and return to Step 2 until convergence criterion is met (e.g., $|e(n)| < \epsilon$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The LMS update equation $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$ is the **single most important equation** in Module 4. It will appear in 7-mark sub-questions on derivation, and the stability bound for $\mu$ will appear in 3-mark questions.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Operational Step

- **Why use instantaneous squared error instead of true MSE?** Because true MSE requires knowing the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{r}(n) \mathbf{r}^T(n)]$, which is unknown in real time. The instantaneous estimate $e^2(n)$ is stochastic but has zero mean gradient — a property exploited by Widrow & Hoff (1960).
- **Why does the update use $+2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$ and not $-2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$?** Because we want to *minimize* the error, and the negative gradient direction ($-2 e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$) points toward the minimum. The *update* is therefore *opposite* to the gradient: $\Delta \mathbf{w} = -\mu \nabla \hat{J} = +2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$.
- **Why FIR and not IIR for biomedical adaptive filters?** FIR structures are **unconditionally stable** (no poles), have linear phase (preserves signal morphology — critical for ECG QRS complex), and avoid numerical instabilities in fixed-point DSP hardware used in medical devices.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n) r(n-k)$ | FIR filter output (dot product form) | $y$ in mV (for ECG) |
| 2 | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Error signal (system ID) or $e(n) = x(n) - y(n)$ (noise cancel.) | Volts |
| 3 | $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$ | Instantaneous MSE estimate | $V^2$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$ | **LMS Weight Update Rule** (the master equation) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | LMS stability bound ($\lambda_{max}$ = largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$) | s/sample (normalized) |
| 6 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})} = \dfrac{2}{\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} E[r^2(n-k)]}$ | Practical LMS stability bound | — |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$ | Wiener-Hopf optimal solution (Wiener filter) | — |
| 8 | $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$ | Minimum achievable MSE | $V^2$ |
| 9 | $\tau_{LMS} \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \mu \lambda_{min}}$ | LMS time constant (slow mode of convergence) | iterations |
| 10 | $\mathcal{M} = \mu \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Misadjustment of LMS | dimensionless (%) |
| 11 | $e_{RLS}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n)$ | A priori error (RLS) | $V$ |
| 12 | $\mathbf{P}(n) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \left[ \mathbf{P}(n-1) - \dfrac{\mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n) \mathbf{r}^T(n) \mathbf{P}(n-1)}{\lambda + \mathbf{r}^T(n) \mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n)} \right]$ | RLS inverse correlation matrix update | — |
| 13 | $0 < \lambda < 1$ | RLS forgetting factor (typical: 0.98 – 0.995) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $SNR_{out} = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}^{out}}$ | Output SNR (dB) | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Tip for KTU Exam Sheets:** All these formulas are exam-ready. The single most-tested equation is **#4** (LMS update) and **#5** (stability bound). For 7-mark derivation questions, derive the LMS update by differentiating $J(n) = E[e^2(n)]$ and applying steepest descent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Biomedical Application | Adaptive Filter Configuration | KTU Module Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Fetal ECG (FECG) Extraction** | Noise canceller with maternal ECG as reference | Module 4, Adaptive Filters |
| **50 Hz Powerline Interference Removal from ECG** | Adaptive notch (LMS-based) | Module 4 |
| **Motion Artifact Removal in Holter Monitoring** | Adaptive noise cancellation | Module 4 |
| **Cancellation of Maternal Respiration in Fetal HR Detection** | Reference input from chest belt | Module 4 |
| **EEG Artifact Removal (EOG, EMG)** | LMS / RLS adaptive filter | Module 4 |
| **Cochlear Implant Speech Enhancement** | Spectral subtraction + LMS | Module 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMS Weight Update Equation

This is the **flagship derivation** for KTU Module 4 14-mark questions.

### Step 1: Define the Adaptive Filter Output

Consider a transversal FIR adaptive filter with $N$ taps. Let the input reference vector at time $n$ be:

$$\mathbf{r}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} r(n) \\ r(n-1) \\ \vdots \\ r(n-N+1) \end{bmatrix}$$

Let the tap-weight vector be:

$$\mathbf{w}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} w_0(n) \\ w_1(n) \\ \vdots \\ w_{N-1}(n) \end{bmatrix}$$

The filter output is the inner product:

$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \, \mathbf{r}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n) \, r(n-k)$$

*Explanation:* This expresses the filtered output as a weighted sum of the current and past $N-1$ input samples. Each weight $w_k$ is the gain applied to the delayed sample $r(n-k)$.

### Step 2: Define the Error Signal

For the **system identification / noise cancellation** configuration:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$$

*Explanation:* The error is the difference between the *desired response* $d(n)$ (the clean signal we want) and the filter's estimate $y(n)$. The goal of the algorithm is to make this error as small as possible.

### Step 3: Define the Cost Function (Objective)

The Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function is:

$$J(n) = E[e^2(n)] = E[(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n))^2]$$

Expanding the square:

$$J(n) = E[d^2(n)] - 2 \mathbf{w}^T(n) E[\mathbf{r}(n) d(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T(n) E[\mathbf{r}(n) \mathbf{r}^T(n)] \mathbf{w}(n)$$

Define the standard statistical quantities:

$$\sigma_d^2 = E[d^2(n)] \quad ; \quad \mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{r}(n) d(n)] \quad ; \quad \mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{r}(n) \mathbf{r}^T(n)]$$

So:

$$J(n) = \sigma_d^2 - 2 \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}(n)$$

*Explanation:* We have expressed the MSE as a quadratic function of the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$. The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite, guaranteeing that $J(\mathbf{w})$ has a single global minimum (the Wiener solution).

### Step 4: Compute the Gradient of $J(\mathbf{w})$

Differentiating $J$ with respect to the weight vector:

$$\nabla J(n) = \frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 \mathbf{p} + 2 \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}(n)$$

*Explanation:* This is a vector whose direction points toward the steepest *increase* of $J$. For minimization, we must move in the *opposite* direction.

### Step 5: The Wiener-Hopf (Optimal) Solution

Setting the gradient to zero:

$$\mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$$

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Wiener filter solution** — the globally optimal weight vector that minimizes the MSE. However, computing $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ requires knowledge of the input statistics, which is not available in real time. Hence we need an *iterative* algorithm.

### Step 6: Steepest-Descent Update

The steepest-descent method updates weights along the negative gradient:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mu (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{p})$$

*Explanation:* At each step, weights move by a small amount $\mu$ opposite to the gradient direction. This is the most fundamental of all gradient-descent procedures.

### Step 7: Replace True Gradient with Instantaneous Estimate (LMS Trick)

This is the **crucial LMS innovation**. Instead of computing the true gradient $-2(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{p})$, we use the *instantaneous* estimate based on a single sample:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$$

The instantaneous squared error is:

$$\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$$

Its gradient is:

$$\nabla \hat{J}(n) = \frac{\partial e^2(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2 e(n) \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$$

*Explanation:* We have dropped the expectation $E[\cdot]$ — this makes the algorithm cheap, but introduces stochastic noise into the gradient estimate. Despite the noise, the algorithm converges *on average* to the Wiener solution.

### Step 8: The Final LMS Update Equation

Substituting into steepest descent:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla \hat{J}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$$

*Explanation:* The negative gradient becomes *positive* when we substitute $\nabla \hat{J} = -2 e(n) \mathbf{r}(n)$. This is the **final LMS update rule**.

### Step 9: LMS Stability Condition

For convergence, the step-size $\mu$ must satisfy:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{max}(\mathbf{R})}$$

Since $\lambda_{max}$ is hard to estimate, the practical bound used in KTU examinations is:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})} = \frac{2}{\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} E[r^2(n-k)]} = \frac{2}{N \sigma_r^2}$$

*Explanation:* If $\mu$ is too small, convergence is slow; if too large, the algorithm diverges (weights blow up). The trace of $\mathbf{R}$ equals the total input signal power, which can be estimated online.

## 3.2 Derivation of the RLS A Priori Error Update

### Step 1: Define the Exponentially Weighted Cost Function

$$J_{RLS}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} e^2(i)$$

*Explanation:* The RLS algorithm minimizes a *weighted least-squares* error, where past errors are exponentially forgotten via the forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0,1]$. This makes RLS a *sliding-window* estimator that tracks non-stationarities.

### Step 2: Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$\frac{\partial J_{RLS}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2 \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} e(i) \mathbf{r}(i) = 0$$

Define the time-averaged correlation matrices:

$$\mathbf{R}_{RLS}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{r}(i) \mathbf{r}^T(i) \quad ; \quad \mathbf{p}_{RLS}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{r}(i) d(i)$$

The optimal RLS weights are:

$$\mathbf{w}_{RLS}(n) = \mathbf{R}_{RLS}^{-1}(n) \, \mathbf{p}_{RLS}(n)$$

### Step 3: Recursive Formulation

The matrix inversion lemma (Woodbury identity) gives the recursive update:

$$\mathbf{P}(n) = \mathbf{R}_{RLS}^{-1}(n)$$

$$\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ \mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n) \mathbf{r}^T(n) \mathbf{P}(n-1)}{\lambda + \mathbf{r}^T(n) \mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n)} \right]$$

The weight update is:

$$\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n) \, e_{RLS}(n)$$

where the gain vector is:

$$\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{r}^T(n) \mathbf{P}(n-1) \mathbf{r}(n)}$$

*Explanation:* RLS uses second-order statistics through the matrix $\mathbf{P}(n)$, which makes convergence **an order of magnitude faster** than LMS for the same steady-state error, at the cost of $O(N^2)$ computations per iteration versus $O(N)$ for LMS.

## 3.3 Convergence Analysis — LMS vs RLS Comparison

| Property | LMS Algorithm | RLS Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (depends on eigenvalue spread) | Fast (an order of magnitude faster) |
| **Computational Cost** | $2N+1$ multiplications / sample | $2N^2 + 4N$ multiplications / sample |
| **Misadjustment** | Higher (gradient noise) | Lower |
| **Stability Bound** | Simple: $0 < \mu < 2 / \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | No $\mu$; just $0 < \lambda < 1$ |
| **Tracking Capability** | Poor for abruptly changing signals | Excellent due to forgetting factor |
| **KTU Use Case** | ECG denoising, real-time Holter | Fetal ECG extraction, EEG analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:**
> For a 16-tap adaptive filter denoising an ECG sampled at 500 Hz:
> - LMS: requires ~$2N = 32$ samples (~64 ms) to converge.
> - RLS: requires ~$2N$ *but with eigenvalue-spread independence*, so practically 10-50 samples.
> - Steady-state SNR improvement: LMS gives ~15-20 dB, RLS gives ~25-30 dB on clean ECG datasets from the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
